author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Peach and Cucumber Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Tasty, easy, crisp, and attractive early summer salad. I used torn fresh basil and it really went well. The trick is to get the Halloumi browned and crisp. The exact proportions of peach, cucumber, and onion really don't matter; what matters is that they are fresh.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Chops With Buttered Corn Recipe
commentBody: If you pull pork chops at 130 they’ll carry over to about 140.  You’ll have a nicely dried out, overcooked pork chop. Pull at 120 (even 118).  You’ll get a nice medium rare pink, juicy pork chop.

commentBody: I find blending cows milk yogurt with feta really detracts from the feta's flavor. Make sure you use yogurt made with sheep or goat milk, traditional Greek yogurt, or just us a little sheep's milk instead of the yogurt.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jim Johnson Or use plain coconut yogurt.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jim Johnson wouldn't have thought of that but thank you. I now buy sheep's milk yogurt in addition to my usual Fage Greek. My local market carries bellwether farms, sheeps and goats milk, yogurt, and trader Joe's has goat milk yogurt as well. I like having the two really different flavor profiles at hand and I can see that cheap or goat's milk yogurt would be really Superior with this recipe. it's also thinner and I bet the texture will be perfect. thanks for posting this!

It’s nice to have variations and try new things and if you don’t want to then don’t, but allow others to enjoy it who haven’t been 40+ years eating the same salad and enjoying it - others may enjoy something different🙏🏽

commentBody: Just curious - who rates recipes?  I had always assumed it was cooks who commented, but this recipe has 5 stars & only one comment, which is actually a question.  I’ve just made this & I think there’s too much dressing.  It’s tasty but a little too salty & gloppy. I like it overall, but think next time I’ll cut way down on the dressing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @YiaYia any user can leave a rating regardless of whether they also add a comment or not. I leave a rating more for myself (as a reminder that yes, I did cook this one, and I should do it again!) and only leave comments if I have something to add to the conversation that may be useful for others.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Morgan at the end of each recipe, there’s a spot next to “rating” that’s says “Mark as cooked” that you can check off as well! I always forget that feature is there, but it’s a helpful feature to track what you’ve made if you can remember to tick it off. :)

author: Ali Slagle

Pretty easy summery veggie side. Would make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I haven't made this recipe, but I love green beans, and cook them often. I strongly recommend that you start checking the green beans for doneness around 3 to 3 1/2 minutes,and keep checking every 30 seconds. In my experience, except for the rare excessively fibrous types, most green beans get to tender crisp in around 4 minutes, so 5 or 6 minutes could overcook them.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @karen I tried this on broccoli (blanched) and it worked. I added a glug of honey to the dressing. It really sung when paired with lemon dressed potatoes. I've been enjoying the leftover green beans and broccoli all week.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Per Laura B, I used asparagus.  The miso sauce as written is too much for asparagus. Make half ( or even a fourth) of the miso mix in the recipe.  Use half of that while mixing in asparagus.  Add more miso sauce as needed so as not to overpower the asparagus.  Less is more. Lovely.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Instead of blanching and chilling the green beans I just steamed them in the microwave. They were a bit watery when I mixed them with the whisked dressing which created a perfect texture. I agree with others that you could use the same amount of dressing for a bigger quantity of green beans- it was quite thick and I had a bit left over. When I first tasted the dressing I blinked a few times- it is very high intensity flavor. But then it was absolutely irresistible to keep eating.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this using Trader Joe's Dijon Mustard which is relatively spicy and this dish turned out excellent!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: What a surprise. I made this as a main dish lunch for two - - mostly because I had all the ingredients on hand and it sounded good. Also, let's face it: the prep is simple. Well, it was surprisingly good. It will be a go-to for this summer, both as a lunch and a side dish. I used mint as the herb addition, which resulted in a bright, summery taste. I did not use all the oil called for in the recipe, and agree with the comment that this amount of dressing was on the heavy side. My solution for that next time will be to use three, not two, zucchini. Enjoy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delightful with fresh garden herbs. So simple to make but such elevated summer flavor. I did not add the additional oil to the zucchini in the dish, as I felt the seared zucchini and halloumi brought enough oil with them. My other tips would be to take care not to overcook the zucchini. A few of my thinner ones were on the mushier side but they taste much better with some bite to them. Also don’t add too much salt, I forgot how salty halloumi already is! Will absolutely be making again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Cooked the zucchini and halloumi on the grill and served this over Charlie Bird’s Farro Salad with grilled pita on the side. Great summer meal

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty. I'd cut the amount of dressing in half next time - just a little too much liquid in the finished dish for my taste.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was really delicious!  No notes.  We had it with some grilled chicken and toasted pita but it could easily go over rice.  I cut the halloumi too small so I didn’t get as much browning but that’s my own mistake.  Will definitely cook this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This dish has fantastic flavors but the texture of the crisped halloumi and marinated garlic truly make it! I used Dodoni Authentic which caramelized well in the pan and a little over half the vinegar and oil that was called for. Turned out great. Will make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I wish ALL recipes had nutritional info.  It helps those of us watching calories or other things.  When I make it, I will drizzle balsamic glaze.

commentBody: @zoya Hey! Are you on the app or are you using a browser? I have seen this comment come up before, and I think maybe there is a different user experience between the two. I only use the app, and can always see the nutrition information. Hope that helps!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: @soya it’s kind of hidden in very light gray text at the bottom of the recipe when viewed from the app. That’s one of the first things I like to look at if a recipe looks good. I wish it was a bit less hidden.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I prepped the platter where I placed the pan grilled zucchini with a bed of baby arugula. I think this addition really completes the dish. Placed the dressing on top, but did not mix-yet. Grilled shards of Queso de Freir (that’s what was available), placed on top of the zucchini and then I tossed the entire mixture in the platter. Topped with chopped mint and cilantro. Perfecto!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Run, don’t walk, to make this! Delicious, bright, a gorgeous addition to the table, and easy. What more could one ask for? I cut and salted the zucchini ahead of time to reduce some of the moisture and they fried up easily.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was terrific. I used burrata rather than halloumi, as it needed to be eaten. Delicious. But halloumi is up next. Really a simple way to add great flavor to zucchini.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic flavor, but the texture of the crispy halloumi and marinated garlic really make this dish! I used Dodoni Authentic which caramelized well in the pan and a little over half the vinegar and oil that was called for. Turned out great. Will make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I'm usually not drawn to zucchini, have a prejudice that it will be boring and mushy. This is my new favorite recipe. I forgot to add the olive oil to the platter and that ended up making the dressing perfect for me. I only added parsley because I didn't have anything else on hand and found that it was plenty flavorful just like that.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This dish was as simple and delicious as everyone commented. My husband and I voted it into the regular meal plan.  I did finish the dish off with 2 Tbsp of red wine vinegar and 2 Tbsp olive oil tossed with salt and finely chopped mint from the garden.  Next time I will add more mint and let the dish set for a few minutes to let the flavours co-mingle before serving.   The tastiest bits were the ones left sitting at the bottom of the dish that I ate while cleaning the kitchen.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Will try with feta (uncooked, of course), less salt, and less liquid as recommended).

Treat for the tastebuds!

author: Ali Slagle

commentBody: Incredibly simple and delicious. After making it as written a couple times, I tried adding farro and chickpeas to turn it into a meal. Made for an excellent lunch!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was meh… needed some more crunch and something to brigyten the flavor. If Inmake it again, Id add pomegranite or oranges possibly sauteed briefly with some red onion and toasted pistachio hazelnut or sliced almonf as a topping. I also served on a bed of mixed greens. The idea was good but the recipe is seriously missing balance and texture.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Any way to do some of this ahead of time?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I used the small amounts of herbs I had on hand (chives, dill and parsley) and reduced the dressing by half as other commenters noted. I agree with the commenter that said to keep the zucchini on the thicker side to keep some bite to it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: How can something this easy be this delicious?! Ali Slagle is a magician! I had zucchini from the CSA, lots of herbs from our garden, and conveniently some halloumi in the fridge. Serves 2 as a main, and 4 if other dishes available.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: DELICIOUS.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Divine! I used an extra zucchini and a few sliced mushrooms, and grated the garlic. Cold leftovers were also delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Good, but needs something else, like an extra herb or a punch spice…

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I should have drained the halloumi before adding it to the hot oil. Water hitting the oil splatters!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious but cooks beware: if you have a regular sized pan, you'll spend a fair bit of time browning the zucchini in batches. This is a tremendous side, but not a quick one.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Any advice for substituting the vinegar here?  I usually opt for citrus.  Wondering what would pair best overall.  Lemon?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: @One Human I used 1 TB of red wine and 1 TB of apple cider vinegar.  Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I love halloumi but I find it difficult to brown without melting. Any suggestions?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: @NNYer oil and heat promotes browning. If that doesn’t work, it could be the package of halloumi you’re using (manufacturing error e.g. I bought vanilla yoghurt that was thick on top and soup on the bottom. I can’t imagine that’s normal)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Saw the recipe today and had all of these ingredients. What a hit!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Added 2 cans of butter beans, amazing!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Wow! Delicious. Grilled the zucchini and halloumi in thick strips on the grill then chopped and assembled with a big handful of mint. Mint is a must! Didn’t have garlic but honesty didn’t miss it. 10/10!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I just finished licking the bowl…too delicious!  Super easy and quick.  I had some cherry tomatoes that were languishing, so I halved and roasted them in the toaster oven, to toss in at the end.  As others mentioned, be judicious in salting, and I was happy I’d heeded the advice to skip the additional oil when dressing it at the end.  Can’t wait to have this again…and again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Honestly this took 15 minutes. Was amazing for a light lunch.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was just ok for me. I think someone mentioned adding a little apple cider vinegar which would have helped with the bitterness for me.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: i thought I was getting zucchini adn squash in my farm share, Got broccoli instead. I cooked that with garlic scrapes, onion, olive oil in a Dutch oven - everything else the same - plus farro - and wow!! Delightful.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Wow! So easy and yummy! I used one zucchini and one yellow squash. I made the dressing in a little bowl using minced garlic & two teaspoons each of the red wine vinegar and olive oil. Put the browned veggies in a shallow bowl, seasoned with salt and pepper, and drizzled with some of the dressing. Added the browned halloumi and topped with the remaining dressing. Served as dinner for two with pita bread. No leftovers. A definite do-over!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I liked this recipe immensely. The salty fried halloumi is a revelation and if thin enough not squeaky. As usual, having made it as the recipe, I have tried variations and have subsequently added finely sliced radishes which add a texture and found that sliced (not too thin but so they are still slices and not chunks) and quartered cucumber can do the same.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: So yummy! Used 6oz block of garlic bread cheese and skipped the garlic. It’s quite a bit cheaper at my store and really similar. Added mint, lemon thyme, and pineapple sage from the garden.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious.  Cut the recipe in half because there are only two of us but regretted it!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Mild is a euphemism.  Bland. Zucchini is just bland.  And it’s too bland to be as ubiquitous as it is this time of year.  This recipe is fine. And, I suppose it doesn’t seem too much like one of the countless recipes existing only to find new things to do with the ridiculous bounty of zucchini that marks this time of year.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Pretty meh!  I wanted a grain base and used toasted farro. The base was not the issue. The zucchini halloumi part was very oily and combined with the farro base it was not a stellar combo even after letting it sit and mingle for a while.  Bleah!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Can it be served at room temp or because of the cheese does it need to be served hot?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Tasty but zucchini, no matter how seared will always be mushy. Perhaps patting dry and salting beforehand can result in some crispiness?  Used a combo of parsley and dill and some left over green spring onion.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I had the same issue-it all tasted fine but the zucchini was mushy even though browned.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Delish

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I almost never cook anything like this, frying in hot oil, spattering my stovetop, clothing, nearby floor.  But this was absolutely worth it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Loved this - wow. I never comment on recipes, but this was so wonderful I am compelled. I used ghee instead of olive oil for the cooking of the courgette and cheese, which worked well. I added lemon zest at the end, which gave a nice lift. We served with a rotisserie chicken that we bought (from Harley’s in Hampstead, for anyone in London!). Anyway, it was a lot of bang for the effort here. And a surprising summer meal (while watching the World Cup). I was initially concerned about the amount of dressing - but I added half at the point called for in the recipe), and then half at the end. I think it was the perfect amount. I loved letting some of the cheese bits crisp more than others; the different textures made it even more interesting in the mix. This will become a staple recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: After trying with paneer because that's what I had, and then halloumi in another occasion, I have to say it's the halloumi that makes this dish. Without it, it's not nearly as exciting. The zucchini is just zucchini.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written, aside from soaking and drying the halloumi prior to cooking to remove some salt and help it brow. It was delicious. I could've eaten the whole bowl myself. I didn't, but I sure was tempted!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. I paired it with the chicken. mint meatballs with tzaziki from Bon Apetit and my family pilaf recipe. it was a delicious dinner.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Not a fan of nori, so I'd leave that out, but I've made salads like this for ages. It's great on pasta also. Use any peanut butter you happen to have. You'll be adding peanuts anyway, so why not use crunchy? I also recommend adding red bell pepper for that little sweet + color.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Tried and found it made enough for good-sized dinner four nights in a row. I switched it up every night: added a bit of extra cabbage the first night, tossed in a bit of Chile crisp the second night. The third night, I added fried shallots. And more cucumbers, some basil and a little extra rice vinegar. Each night was great… and slightly different. Tons of fun!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Your several versions are very inspirational -- THANKS!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Didn't use Nori (family member won't eat) and reduced garlic but otherwise made as directed.  Absolutely delicious.  Used squeeze ginger (1 TBSP) and minced most of a large garlic clove.  Had smaller bag of slaw so will use rest of dressing in other ways (happily).  Hoping extra dressing keeps in the refrigerator for around a week - will add warm water to loosen if needed.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious but missing something nonetheless: sesame oil? (I think.) Easy enough to put together, I followed the recipe precisely. But couldn't taste the nori, it was nice and crunchy, but...salted peanuts, perhaps would have helped.? The sauce just needs something. Guess I said that already.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @Carol S. Fish Sauce - just a dash (or 2)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @Carol S. Yes - definitely needed tweaking.  I doubled nearly everything in the dressing (except for the peanut butter), plus added a couple dashes fish sauce, 2 T maple syrup, a couple tablespoons of lime juice and half a teaspoon of gochuchang paste for heat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Our family loved this salad. I agree with other reviewers to 1.5 x the dressing. I also agree that the dressing needs a little extra kick - I added juice from half a lime, a rough 2 tsp of fish sauce, 1 tbsp honey, and 1/2 tsp of chile crisp. I also swapped in a tbsp of thinly sliced mint for the nori (it gets soggy once the dressing hits it). It changes the flavor profile to something  more on the Thai side of things, and it was tasty….!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Loved this dish - I would up the sauce a little, 1.5x the recipe, add a little sesame oil and fish sauce, and I doubled the cumbers, used slaw blend, and used salted peanuts (why use unsalted? makes no sense). Absolutely delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: This recipe needs cilantro and mint in the salad and a pinch of sugar in the dressing and a squeeze of lime at the end and then it is perfect and would eat it for every meal.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @SR agree with this - remembering for next time I make it

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: OK, good recipe but needs a few tweaks. Not enough sauce-could easily double. Add 1-2 tablespoons of maple syrup.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I agree that a few somethings were missing.  I doubled the sauce and the salad was still very dry (yes, I measured all the ingredients and followed the directions).  There was so much leftover that I made another double batch of the sauce to deal with it.  Probably won't make again.  Disappointed.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: A note for those looking for a peanut butter substitute - as someone with a lifelong life-threatening peanut allergy married to someone who loves peanut butter, sunbutter has been our house alternative! It “tastes just like peanut butter” and nobody dies - win win!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I used regular soy sauce, as that's what I had on hand. I started out using 1.5 TBS, but the sauce was too bland, so added in the remaining .5 TBS. Used 2x the garlic/ginger. Added thin sliced red bell pepper. Excellent!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe. Couple tips after making it twice:

-Double sauce quantity
-Add 1/2 tsp toasted sesame oil to sauce
-make the sauce in a nutribullet if you have one (just add the water before you blend)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Great cold/room temp salad that's still hearty. Had it with cold quinoa, which didn't take over any of the flavor profile or texture of the salad. Suggest making extra sauce in order to adjust before serving, especially if prepping in advance or storing overnight (salad is drier the next day). I increased the ginger and vinegar to get the right balance in the sauce, as well as chili crisp for a bit of bite. I wondered if making the sauce in advance would help it the flavors come out more.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight without the nori (sorry). Very good, similar to Martha Rose Schulman's Indonesian-Style Chicken Salad, also on this site. For this one, I felt like it needed a small hint of something sweet to balance the vinegar. Maybe a a bit of brown sugar or something. I served fresh mango on the side, which also helped.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Great base for a salad slaw but in addition to sesame oil & a splash of Fish Sauce this definitely needs sliced Green onions!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @Maya scallions are in the recipe.  scallions= green onions

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: so so good.  tetris meal for me. had everything here.  love the way the warm poached chicken wilted the napa further.  used peanut butter powder and the sauce is drinkable!   thank you, ali, for creating and sharing with us.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe.. Make a little more sauce than is called for.  Yes, it keeps well in the fridge. If you poach the chicken, massage some sauce into the warm slices before tossing with the salad... Oh, strain and save that poaching liquid! Make rice or soup with it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe! Would have grated the garlic and ginger into the sauce instead of chopping so it blended better but otherwise great.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @Mariel this is such a good and helpful tip!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @Mariel GREAT idea!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Had high hopes for this, but it came out so bland. The dressing on its own is fab (definitely making again to use in other recipes!) I think this is a situation where the core ingredients of chicken, cabbage, and cucumber have so little of their own flavor that they suck up the dressing's, in a bad way. I even stirred in a few spoonfuls of chili crisp at the end, which helped, but not enough for this to be a repeat. That said, highly recommend adding fish sauce + lime juice to the dressing!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I have to agree. The dressing tasted fabulous before it was added to the salad... but then, once mixed in, all the flavor seemed to go right down the flavor drain. (The only component that really came through... and in not a pleasing way... was the raw garlic.) I did serve with lime wedges, which helped somewhat. but I wondered if more of the dressing (like maybe double the recipe), possibly with the modifications you suggest, would help?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Took some shortcuts and it was still delicious - bought packages of Applegate Naturals Shredded Chicken Breasts instead of shredding chicken myself, used 1 T of ginger paste instead of fresh ginger, and used a bag of slaw blend. No mini cucumbers available so used an English cucumber.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Nice to also sprinkle with toasted sesame seeds.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Plus, serve with a wedge of lime.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Great summer salad and great for meal prepping/making lunch ahead of time. Added fresh herbs like basil for added flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I also thought sauce was missing something.  Sesame oil?  More soy sauce?  Might try adding to leftovers.  Couldn’t taste the nori, but it looked pretty.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: It was good and I will definitely make it again. We didn't care for the nori and will leave that out next time. It is a good base recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I made it exactly as instructed. Very good!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Really good and very simple. Squeezed a little lime over the top. Next time I think I’ll add some red bell pepper and Fresno chile, and maybe use furikake seasoning instead of the Nori flakes. Will definitely make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: This is very good, though it was not good the next day, so plan to finish it the day it's made! Though I like Nori, I don't think its addition added much here. Instead, I would add a splash of lime juice and chopped cilantro and/or mint before serving. I used 1.5 lbs. of chicken thighs marinated in equal parts olive oil, vinegar, soy sauce plus garlic and baked and sliced thin.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe. When I tasted the dressing it was overpowered by vinegar (could have been my TJ’s rice vinegar) so I added a few drops of fish sauce as recommended here. That did the trick. Next time I may add red pepper flakes or a little sriracha. Delicious salad, easily came together with a bag of Wegmans precut Asian slaw.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Used napa cabbage and sliced it super thin in my food processor, and it kinda turned to mush. But I am dedicated to this recipe and will try again, either with non-Napa cabbage or not in the processor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Subbed tahini for the peanut butter b/c that’s what I had in the pantry. Tasted great and I think matched the flavor profile of the recipe. Increased salt all around by using Korean gim, salted peanuts, and full salt soy sauce and increasing amount to 2 T. At the cabbage salting step, added a sprinkle of sugar. Served with chicken on top versus mixed in the slaw.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Nori gave it a nice umami flavor reminiscent of sushi. Best suggestions here were to add chopped red pepper and mint. I also added avocado. Low salt peanuts are better imho.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I liked the texture and flavor of this salad.  I used the poached chicken breast.  I also doubled the tamari (I used tamari rather than low-salt soy sauce) and rice wine in the dressing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I served this at a casual dinner to my family and friends. It wasn’t well liked. When I asked for suggestions and thoughts they said it was bland, it didn’t have much flavor, needed more sauce, and  they couldn’t taste the nori. I’ve been trying to eat the leftovers and I’ve added more rice vinegar to give it more pep but I agree- we won’t be making this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I agree the sauce is missing some umami (and salt) so a dash of fish sauce, splash of sriracha and cilantro did the trick.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Easy and very good recipe especially with the nori - the more nori the better.  However, we felt it needed a bit more oomph and the next time I will add radishes for that little extra bite.  And I actually used "bought" cabbage slaw and less than called for in the recipe since I could only find a 10oz package and the dressing was a little too sparse.  Will go 1-1/2 times for the dressing next time.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: A very healthy low glycemic salad especially for diabetics but it needs a lift as most reviews state. Next time I will double the sauce and perhaps add diced preserved lemon.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Hi,  We don't do well w peanuts or even almonds...  what  would yu sub?  I am wondering about Miso.  many thanks!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Tahini?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: For a vegan option, subbing shredded tofu is absolutely enough

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: We didn't think the nori really went with the other flavors so we left that out and added fresh basil. We also added a little lime juice, sambal and fish sauce to the dressing.  We liked it on it's own, but also with glass noodles and as sandwich filling.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Double the dressing and cucumbers. Add lime?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Anyone tried it with tofu or a vegetarian version?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Yes, you can make this recipe with relatively firm tofu. First try to squeeze out the water by pressing the tofu gently between two plates + by blotting out water with plenty of paper towels (or a clean dish towel). Then, cut the tofu into cubes (no more than 3/4") and mix + coat generously with some of the dressing before adding to the salad. We also shred carrot into the slaw + top with B+W toasted sesames. If the tofu seems likely to crumble, just pile it on top, instead of stirring it in.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I was thinking tofu as well. Cubed and fan oven or airfry in similar seasoning.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I followed readers' suggestions by adding sesame oil and fish sauce to punch up the dressing, plus thinly sliced red peppers for color. Didn't have nori, so omitted it. Salad was delicious and a big hit at a potluck, but it took a loooong time to assemble (shredding cabbage, shredding chicken, grating ginger, chopping peanuts, etc.)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Followed various suggestions and added cilantro and mint, a dash of sesame oil, and finished off with a squeeze of lime. Yum.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody:
A word about the raw GARLIC: 

If you don't mind totally raw garlic that will linger in your mouth long after the meal, then execute that part of the recipe as written.  

But we don't care for that experience...so, in the future, we will do what we typically do with raw chopped/pressed garlic in a dressing (or similar): Before mixing it in, we let it sit in a small bowl or cup for at least 10 minutes in enough vinegar to cover, mixed well with a good grind of salt, to "cure" it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: If you're not a fan of peanuts - - - -  has anyone made this with almond butter as a substitute, and if so, how did it come out?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: just wondering if you tried the recipe with almond butter already. my son is allergic to peanuts

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Are sheets of nori that one would use to roll sushi the same as nori snacks? if not, what are nori snacks?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: @Susan B Yes, the same. I believe the suggestion of Nori snacks is because of the volume or smaller amount that comes in the snack pouch. Nori snacks are dried seaweed in small pouches. They can be flavored as sea salt, wasabi and such. My granddaughter loves them, we include them in her lunch daily. But, yes they are the same as nori sheets.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Does this hold up in the fridge for a few days or must it be eaten that day for best results?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: As per the recipe, you can prep the slaw up to 1 day ahead. I can’t see any reason why the dressing couldn’t be made and kept for at least a few days (refrigerated) - though you may need to add a splash of water once it’s at room temperature to loosen it up again. I would wait to add the remaining ingredients (and dress the salad) until day of eating, to avoid it becoming too soggy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Would this be ok with almond butter instead of peanut butter?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: What are nori snacks, and where do I get them?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I was so excited to see this recipe until I saw the ingredient list - I hate peanut butter! (I know how odd that is.) Any ideas on a good substitute? Maybe just another "Asian" style salad dressing?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: This one didn’t work for me. The sauce lacked zing, and the use of rotisserie chicken left the salad mushy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Sorry. Worst thing I ever made. Had to throw the whole thing out. Love your recipes and won’t  let this shade my opinion of NYT cooking/recipes.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: ?Have you made this with salmon??

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: The chicken turned extra- mushy, not sure why. I used a rotisserie chicken, followed the recipe, and it was....ok. The consistency of the meat was the biggest issue. I wonder whether the liquid ingredients had anything to do with it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Watch out for salt. Skip salting the cabbage, use unsalted peanuts and peanut butter. Use more ginger than recommended. Add lime juice and cilantro. Add something red (bell pepper) for looks. i didn't have nori on hand and didn't miss it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Agree with other reviewers - a bit more sauce, maybe will try lime juice, fish sauce next time.  Big picture - a yummy recipe that can always be tweaked for your diners.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: I was hoping for a fresh tasting, light summer dish. I found this to be very heavy and bland tasting. There was no zing to it and the nori made the entire dish taste fishy. I think this is the first recipe from NYT that I haven't liked.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was disappointing.  I do love how fast it came together and no oven, especially on a hot day.  Strange how I love all the ingredients, but they didn’t work well together for me.  As others have commented, I tried to kick up the flavor.  I added maple syrup, pochang sauce, lime juice; I also used apple cider vinegar instead of the rice vinegar.  But it still fell flat.  Definitely needed salt.  This recipe may also work with grilled shrimp or salmon instead of the chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Allergic to peanuts any recommendations for substitutions?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: So good! Serve it on toast with apple slices

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone use almond butter and almonds?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Increase dressing by 50%, but keep PB around 1/4 cup. Roasted thin cut chicken breasts and cup up when making salad. Good meal prep and leftovers recipe

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Followed all the suggestions and still found it was missing something. I have some leftover so I’ll try with  making more sauce ., more garlic, fish sauce, lime juice and maple syrup. Maybe better, maybe worse.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Nori is definitely an acquired taste (not mine!). I made 1.5x the recipe and glad I did. I also added an extra clove of garlic, a little more ginger than suggested, 1 Tbsp. maple syrup (I'm Canadian, eh), 1/2 orange and the juice of one lime. Oh, and salted peanuts and a bit of chili pepper. Will never eat Waldorf Salad again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Tried and found it made enough for good-sized dinner four nights in a row. I switched it up every night: added a bit of extra cabbage the first night, tossed in a bit of Chile crisp the second night. The third night, I added fried shallots. And more cucumbers, some basil and a little extra rice vinegar. Each night was great… and slightly different. Tons of fun!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Great combination of flavors and textures. (I made it with packaged broccoli slaw. I agree with the previous commenters who said there needs to be more dressing and it needs to be more ... assertive.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody:
I just took a star off (leaving 4) because the recipe says you can mix chicken, cabbage slaw, and cucumbers 1 day before serving. I found this NOT to be true.

 I live alone, so I made a half recipe, ate half of it, and refrigerated the above-described 3-ingredient mix for the next day, when I added the garnishes. Well, like the spearmint on the bedpost, it loses its flavor overnight. It was so blah that it lessened my enthusiasm for the recipe.

Moral: Eat it fresh.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Would this work with shrimp or salmon?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I added a bit of sesame oil and still found it a bit dry, so I’ll increase dressing and cucumbers. Chili crisp is a great idea too!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: How did the chicken get cooked without cooking?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: Made without chicken and chopped the cucumbers made for a great slaw to top fish tacos.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: The amount of dressing called for in the recipe is fine. I made it with extra dressing (1.5) and it was too much.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
commentBody: This dish just tasted very "meh" to me. It's any chicken salad with a twist, and in this case I thought the twist was OK but not especially impressive. I'll try it again with some minor tweaks.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Thank you for recipes that use a full box of pasta,

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Loved this meal. Used impossible meat, which worked well. Makes a lot of food!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this for dinner this evening and thought it was delicious. I used fresh peas, not frozen, added some dill because I didn’t have basil, did not add the butter, and forgot the Parmigiana. Still, I don’t think it could have been better. I have enough asparagus and peas to make it again for tomorrow’s dinner—it was that good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made the recipe again for dinner this evening. I found morels in the market this morning, which I immediately bought. I added the morels after the sausage and onions were fried, along with the asparagus and peas. I remembered the cheese this evening, too—and the result was mouth-watering. It is a perfect recipe for spring.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Margie Gibsonhow can you possibly say it could be better when you didn’t add three items in the recipe . It probably would have been better if you added those items.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Nori I think you misread @Margie Gibson's original post.  She said "I DON'T THINK. it could have been better."  She wasn't disparaging the recipe -- she said it was as good as it could be.  You and she are on the same side!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Good, but not great. Leveling up options for next go round, wound maybe using a little white wine to deglaze the sausage and to double the lemon juice at the end. Added some red pepper flakes which added a needed kick. Will definitely attempt again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this exactly according to the recipe and it was fabulous!! It will remain in my rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Very solid but I found it lacked the flavour I was expecting. Might play around with maybe some capers or anchovies and some more citrus. Feels like it should work!
I also added chili's for some extra spice that helped also.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Reg S.I added a cup of homemade pesto sauce and it really brought this recipe together.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: PLEASE give us some weights for the asparagus. The asparagus in my garden does not arrive in bunches.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: PLEASE use your best judgment for the amount of asparagus. Too much or too little will not affect the outcome of this recipe. If you like asparagus (why else would you grow it?), grab a big bunch from your garden. If you don't care for asparagus, grab less. The recipe calls for 1-1/2 cups of peas. The 1 inch pieces of asparagus could be about the same -- or more or less -- depending on your love for asparagus.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Corlan Johnson Where I live, asparagus is generally sold in 1 pound bunches. Lucky you, having the fresh stuff!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: A bunch of asparagus is typically 1 pound.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this for Mother’s Day. 10/10. Used about 2 cups pasta water. 1 was not enough.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yummy delicious. I used half the called for pasta (8oz) and thought it was the perfect ratio. Also subbed the vegan sausage - added a bit more oil while cooking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The joy of NYT Cooking website is those little bars that say recently viewed or similar recipes. That's what happened here. I planned to make this as written, then I saw Melissa Clarks recipe. Similar, but mint was a NO. Loved the idea of a creamy cheese sauce. Ultimately meat (sausage) wins the day. I combined the two recipes and it was a perfect marriage. Yes, I'm one of those people who complain about other people altering a recipe. Hmm. I guess that's why there's a comment section.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Perfect spring greens pasta salad! 
Seasoned Clonakilty traditional Irish sausages with sage, black pepper and thyme. 
Would increase basil and reduce the amount of pasta next time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Excellent,but honestly couldn’t taste the basil. I think you would be 100% fine without it. Also only used half the amount of pasta, sausage, olive oil and butter and it was absolutely delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious and fresh tasting. Feels healthy and indulgent at the same time. Perfect spring recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Please give a weight for the asparagus-our ‘bunches’ may not be the same size. Thank you

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Use as much -- or as little -- asparagus as suits you.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's a keeper.  No asparagus at the market, so I used sugar snap peas and mushrooms.  Just the right amount of garlic and lemon.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Agree with Bee, deglazing with white wine or stock would be great before adding the veggies. 
Made this with sliced snow peas and they were a great swap for regular peas. Added 4 oz of mascarpone with the pasta water and it helped everything come together. Also recommend serving with crushed chili flakes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Decent but not stellar recipe on its own. If following the recipe exactly, it will taste a bit bland. Need to use the zest and juice of a very large lemon, and add additional spices like black pepper and more salt at the end. I also added half a teaspoon of MSG, which helped significantly.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so easy and perfect for spring produce. Used parsley instead of basil and spring onions instead of shallots and it turned out great.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this pretty much as written and it was a huge hit in our house. Spicy Italian sausage added kick without any additional pepper flakes. I look forward to making variations throughout growing season. We’ll be mixing up the sausage options, too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: to A and Corlan: I've noticed that asparagus bunches--at least where I shop--weigh a pound, or very close to it. I think you could assume that this recipe calls for a pound of asparagus.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Perfect dish to add garlic scapes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Took advice of a few capers and used a little white wine to deglaze. Everyone loved it!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Didn't have basil; so I subbed in arugula.  Didn't have a fresh lemon either so I subbed lemon pepper - a goodly amount.  Delish!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this with the lemon pasta from Trader Joe’s. It was wonderful! I did add a little extra red pepper.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Just made this almost exactly as written. Used zest of an entire lemon and was liberal with the salt but not too much if topping with Parmesan. Everyone liked it!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I’m a very lousy cook. This recipe is fail proof and profoundly delicious.  Leftovers are just as tasty the next day.  

Be a bit careful with the timing of the pea and asparagus additions. It’s easy to overcook them losing their crunchiness and their bright green-ness.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: You are getting better presenting fresh and accessible ingredients and simplicity in your recipes. Most importantly, those couples with young families who Door Dash regularly have a reachable  and cheap alternative. Easy healthy recipes for all. Please keep up your good work.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was fairly bland, which surprised me given the list of ingredients. I think it would be better using half the amount of the pasta, but keeping the other ingredients the same.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Mine was not bland at all, and I used half the amount of pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Super excited about all the new recipes being posted recently but please provide nutritional info as well for us! Love NYT Cooking!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can I make this in a regular pan since I don’t have a Dutch oven?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great Spring pasta recipe. Sautéed some mushrooms first, then put to the side. Followed the recipe from there and put the mushrooms in after the pasta was stirred back in. Skipped the basil and mixed in some grated parmesan. We loved it!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made it tonight. Used a half pound of chicken Italian sausage for its lower fat content and so as not to overpower the vegetables. Turned out great. Will make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious! Though I did think the dish needed a creamy element. I used sugar snaps from the garden--fun to eat food that I grew myself. I chose the hot Italian sausage but also added a jalapeno with the veins and seeds removed, making it spicy but not too hot. I'll make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I love this recipe. And yet, like some other cooks hopefully have done, after following this original recipe exactly as it is written, I tweaked it by adding mushrooms and cherry tomatoes. Both iterations, the original as well as the tweaked variety, are so satisfying and unctuously delicious that we make it twice a month.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fast, easy, and tasty. Added cherry tomatoes because they were there. Deglazed the pan with white wine. Otherwise followed the recipe. Delightful!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Perfect dish for early spring. I forgot the basil, so used baby spinach with a pinch of oregano instead. I’ll try it with basil next time, but this was still very good. I appreciate that the recipe calls for using the peas frozen, so they’re not mushy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yum! So good! I added two cups of frozen sliced shiitakes with the green veggies and that was a great addition. Love Ali Slagle’s easy, family friendly weeknight recipes like this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
AMAZING! Finally a veggie-filled pasta that also contains meat and isn’t overly heavy. Going in my regular rotation for sure. 

Also, life is short. Use the whole lemon ;)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We found it bland. Perhaps because had to use gluten-free pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We liked this more and more as we ate it and appreciated the pops of sweetness from the peas, the silkiness of the butter, and the accents of the basil.  I used a full box of Barilla Protein Plus farfalle, and it was fine.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The main flaw in the recipe is excessive pasta.  The flavor of the other ingredients is unnecessarily diluted.  Next time we’ll cut the pasta to 1/2 pound. We made the recipe as directed but substituted 1.5 cups of chopped leeks for the peas and added some hot pepper flakes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Excessive pasta? It's a pasta dish. Go to Rome someday and order a pasta dish. Ask the waiter to "hold" half the pasta. Let us know the response!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The whole didn’t transcend the sum of its parts.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So much better reheated.  The pasta retains the sauce which is rich and creamy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I thought I had the ingredients on hand for this recipe until I started cooking and I am not very good at improvising on recipes, yet, necessity is the mother of invention! Had almost zero frozen peas so searched for veggies.  Two very small tomatoes, dried tomatoes (small handful), handful of shredded cabbage, phew! I added pasta water earlier to hydrate the dried tomatoes. Added Aleppo pepper flakes. It was delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I had lemon basil pork sausages in the freezer when I saw this recipe and thought they would go with it perfectly…and they did! We did not have asparagus, but substituted green beans in their place and it turned out wonderfully. The only thing I might do differently is to cut back on the amount of pasta used.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this tonight and followed the recipe with one exception: I used the rind of an enter large lemon. It is perfect for a rainy, dull spring day. The ample vegetables lighten it. Fusilli, not rotini, is essential. So good that everyone wanted seconds. Next time, I’ll drain the sausage fat off after crisping.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Didn’t drain it, but probably should have.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made in the amounts suggested, but did sub one cup white wine for a cup ogthe pasta water, let it cook off and then added the cup of pasta water. Don’t skip the basil. It’s great with the lemon and is full of antioxidants. A little red pepper and a bit more salt at the end. This is a nice bright change from a favorite, more wintry orechiette, sausage and broccoli dish I make. Thanks, Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What can  I use instead of asparagus ?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Sheila C 
Maybe a different recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Broccoli?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sheila C I would try broccolini.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Recipe would work great if all asparagus were the same size. For larger ones you need with withhold the peas, since the asparagus may need twice as long to reach the point described. I learned the hard way, not the end of the world, just surprised I’m paying for a recipe that didn’t bother to offer a wide range to match our varying agricultural products.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Anyone know how much a bunch of asparagus typically weighs? I see big variations where I live.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Use whatever quantity you think you and your family will eat, allowing for leftovers (or not). When a recipe isn't precise, you can use your best judgement.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Attractive, simple recipe. I just need to grab some basil then I can give it a go.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I cook the pasta last!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious! I halved the amount of pasta per another reader and I needed just 1 C of pasta water . Family asked for second helpings.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Shallots helped.  Used True Lemon powder instead of expensive lemons - that citrus kick matters - or it can be a bit bland.  Also added some dark soy sauce, kitchen bouquet. Not bad; not the best on NYT culinary.  Basic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was very bland, which surprised me, given the list of ingredients. I had to add quite a bit of salt, more basil and even some pesto. I think there is far too much pasta - it should probably be cut in half, but keep the amounts of the other ingredients.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: this was easy, quick and delicious.  Made it with chicken sausage because that is what I had.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wow, this is good! Don't be afraid to be heavy handed with the amount of peas or lemon zest/juice used. Came together fast, makes a ton, and the amount of veggies is great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Has anyone used chickpea noodles (like Banza, or the like)?

Giving this recipe a try later this week.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So simple, yet tasty - hearty and fresh at the same time.  It does make a lot of food. I used all the reserved pasta water, KEEP IT SAUCY & GLOSSY! Beyond brand veggie sausage worked really well in this

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great recipe! Threw the lid on the dutch oven for about 2 mins to move the asparagus along, cooked as written and my 5 year old told me this was "a great dinner, mama." Hooray!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was incredibly bland. Maybe b/c I used hot chicken sausage instead of pork.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe calls for a whole pound of pasta to make 4 to 6 servings. All my pasta containers say that a pound serves 8. We made the full recipe for two diners so we would have leftovers, and boy, did we have leftovers! Opted for  sweet sausage and otherwise stuck exactly to the recipe. We found it bland and unfortunately it did not reheat well. So much for leftovers - the asparagus flavor dominated the mild taste of the dish, especially after reheating. Oh, well, a rare meh dish from NYT Cooking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My husband is allergic to green peas and green beans. What would be a reasonable substitute for the green peas?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I've subbed fresh corn for the peas and it worked well. You get the sweetness that peas would add with a little more texture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Most past recipes don't, but I think this one could work with whole wheat pasta--a longer noodle though because a mouthful of whole-wheat fusilli might make me gag.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Try Delallo whole wheat fusilli.  Much better than other brands.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'm eating this now with whole wheat pasta (reginetti from Sfoglini) and it's delish.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I only used one clove of garlic, a bit less pasta (8.8 ounce package), and no shallots. It tasted great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you are going to add fresh morels to this dish, please make sure they are cooked for at least 10 minutes. They are toxic when not cooked well and can send people to the hospital. Add to the sausages when browned for a couple of minutes to release their liquid. When liquid is gone, continue recipe with shallots and garlic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Nice taste, a little bland. Might be zippier with spicy sausage.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I generally followed and my mods were adding cherry tomatoes (semi blistered) and chopped broccolini. Added peas a bit later so they didn’t overcook. 
This type of recipe is perfect for using veggies on hand. I also used 2 lemons and added ~1/4 cup Italian parsley.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this tonight and just loved it. I used orecchiette, as that was the pasta I had on hand. Also, as suggested, I used half a pound of pasta instead of the whole box. We will still have loads of leftovers for the two of us. Otherwise, I followed the recipe as written. It took a bit longer than 40 minutes, but preparation was still less than an hour with minimal cutting of vegetables - a plus! Highly recommend this lovely spring-ey pasta dinner.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's good! Easy too. Didn't take the casings of the sausage, just cut it in smaller pieces.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We have an asparagus garden and I am always in need of recipes where a lot of asparagus can be used.  This recipe exceeded expectations, but for our preference, half a pound of fusilli was used. We bought the Rao pasta, from Italy with two ingredients only, and it is quite heavy. Hubby bought ordinary store brand hot sausages; I added two shakes of anise seeds and one shake of hot pepper flakes while it cooked. Other than that I followed the recipe exactly and this will be in our meal rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Delicious and easy! I had made homemade pesto earlier today (thanks to my prolific sweet basil plant), so  then followed the recipe with shallots, garlic, and three links of Aldi’s organic Italian chicken sausage. 

I made this with penne pasta, saved the pasta water and added the knob of unsalted butter as directed, and then added about half a cup of wine wine. I swirled in several generous tablespoons of pesto and topped with red pepper flakes, oregano, and Parmesan cheese.

It was  divine and will definitely make again!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: WHEN USING gluten-free pasta, it seems not "firm enough" after a while, even if cooked al dente, and looses it's shape and taste especially, when I add the cooked pasta back in to vegetables & sausage. Any suggestions?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Two thumbs up, even though I used chopped onions and dried basil instead of what was in the recipe. It is delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Two thumbs up, very delicious, although I made it with chopped onions instead of shallots, dried basil, and bottled lemon juice instead of the recipe ingredients.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great easy meal.  Since I had zucchini, I substituted that for the asparagus.  I also bought garlic-basil sausage instead of Italian.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Oh. This was a celebration on the tongue. I used about twice the garlic, but followed the rest of recipe as written. OMG.  Amazing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wish I could taste more of the flavors in this dish. I don’t taste the basil or the shallots — just a lot of sausage. Wondering how to deepens all the flavors — just add more?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: we used beyond hot italian sausage for this and love it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Didn't have any lemon, but I added a couple of spoonfuls of pesto at the end.  Delish!  Oh and I halved the recipe since there were just two of us (we measured out 3 servings of pasta instead of using half the box).  There's a serving left for lunch tomorrow - yay!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hal, try Impossible's spicy sausage in the red tube...makes it even better!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Ditto--the salmon was overcooked yet raw after 8 minutes so I dropped it to 350 and moved it lower in the oven, and it was good after another 5-7 minutes. Next time I would season it. I made black lentils with some broth and leeks and used that instead of canned. Used just a couple tablespoons oil from the anchovy tin, and some extra anchovies. So, made some changes but the overall effect--crisped salmon, lentils, herby umami lemony sauce--was good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Why not just sear the salmon, skin side down in a cast iron pan? The toaster oven will retain a fish smell.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This dish was superb but I made some changes. I used black beluga lentils instead of canned. I also pan seared the salmon. You want the oil hot enough to shimmer, then place the salmon skin-side down and immediately lower to medium heat. Press each fillet down for 10 seconds so the skin stays flat and crisps evenly, then cook about 7 minutes until nearly done. Flip for 90 seconds on the flesh side, then flip back skin-side down and give it one final minute over medium-high heat for extra crispy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My salmon took much longer than 6-10 minutes.  I had to move it lower in the oven and adjust the broiler temperature a bit lower as well.  In the end the skin was a bit charred and crispy.  I served it skin side down, and just put the lentils on top of the meat.  It was truly delicious, it will be a keeper in my house.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I don’t use canned lentils. Are the lentils lentils de puy or are these lentils as used in dah?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It would be good with any sort of cooked lentil.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Made this tonight with plain, old green lentils from TJ's that I cooked myself. It only takes 20 minutes! I added a small carrot to the pot about halfway through, and then sliced it into the lentils once they were drained. 
 
You don't need a full 1/2 C of o. oil, but use a good one. Broiled the salmon until the skin was (a little too) crispy, then moved it to a lower rack and reduced the oven to 400. Total time about 13 mins. This was quite delicious, I'll make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Instrad of anchovies can yiu use capers?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used bigger fillets and it took longer but was delicious and very simple to make. I lowered the fish as recommended and it came out perfectly.  I roasted cherry tomatoes and served the fish over the tomatoes to increase acidity and sweetness. This will go into regular meal  rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Four stars, as modified.  I really like lentils, but salmon is not my preferred fish.  I made this with arctic char, sautéed, an excellent choice for me.  I thought the lentil salsa verde was lacking.  I prepared lentils du puy and then sauced the legumes as directed.  I must have had a very juicy lemon as there was too much sauce for me.  More importantly, there wasn’t enough flavor.  I wound up adding some Dijon mustard to the lentils.  If I made this again, I’d double the amount of crushed red peppers and anchovies.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Such a wonderful and easy week night recipe! Followed the recipe as directed, however did season the fish with a little Dijon and salt and pepper before broiling. I think the cook time was a few more minutes in my oven. I served this with some sautéed Swiss chard with pistachios and dried cherries and it was the perfect meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Is there a substitute for anchovies?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Canned lentils, is that a thing? I don't think I have ever seen them.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: An easy, delicious meal for a warm night when you don't want to cook.  I used smoked mackerel since that's what I had and added capers, halved cherry tomatoes, and slivers of red onion for a sweet/salty supper.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Completely elevated the bland frozen dumplings we had at home. I can't wait to make this again! 
A few suggestions based on changes I made: 
- Try spinach instead of kale (about 500g for 20 dumplings)
- Double the ginger and garlic
- Before serving, I added sliced scallions and white sesame seeds, then drizzled with soy sauce, sesame oil, ponzu, chili crisp and a few drops of la-yu (Japanese chili oil)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
After deribbing the kale leaves, place the leaves in the freezer (bagged) for at least 24 hours.
When ready to use, remove from freezer and massage the frozen kale into small pieces. This process breaks down the tough cell structures and tenderizes the kale.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: 20 minutes to make -- Don't believe it!  It takes 20 to 30 minutes just to prep the kale, ginger and garlic. Once you have everything ready to go it takes another 20 to 25 minutes to cook.  A fine recipe but give yourself 50 minutes to make it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Paul, although they don't make this information especially clear, the time posted for NYT recipes is the actual cooking time, not the prep time (chopping, grating, etc).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Paul I am a sometimes-impatient cook. I used pre-chopped bagged kale, and pre-minced (tubed) garlic and ginger. Everything turned out quite nicely. 

The only real prep I had to do was to wash and thinly slice three leeks, which added a nice flavor to the kale.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Our family LOVED this recipe! 

We used a bag of frozen chicken potstickers from Costco, which turned out just fine. Might try a bag of frozen Korean mandu next time.  

Also used a bag of pre-chopped kale, which was easy and turned out very tender.

We also added three halved and very thinly sliced (4mm food processor blade) leeks, which brought a lovely earthy tone to the otherwise bland kale.

As others have noted, we used more ginger and garlic than called for (probably about 50% more than the recipe suggested). 

I will probably double the recipe next time by using two large frying pans for the greens, because we all loved the greens so much.

I’m sure I’m going to cooking h-e-l-l because I used the tubes of pre-minced ginger and garlic, but the end result was still staggeringly good. 

I made the dipping sauce as directed, found it a bit bland. (However, that’s why chile crisp and hoisin sauce is for, in our home.) Served it with toasted sesame seeds for garnish, and they added a lovely nutty subtle crunch to the greens.

Next time we’ll add a bag of baby spinach, and maybe some baby bok choy.

We also decided that we’ll make just the greens some evening as a side dish, without the dumplings. The greens were just that good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very good. A one-pound package of dumplings (aka potstickers) was a bit too much food for my 2-person household. Next time I’ll save half for another meal and serve it over rice. By request, I will also substitute other greens for the kale.

commentBody:
*Step 2 does not include how long to “Add the dumplings on top of the kale and partially cover the skillet with a lid or baking sheet.”  before continuing to “Pour ¼ cup water into the skillet, cover completely”

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The chia seed did not blend well . They were like clumps in my smoothie

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Maya j 2 Tablespoons is a lot in my experience.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Maya j Mix the chia seeds with your milk of choice and stir until it starts to thicken into a pudding-like texture, about 5 minutes. Don’t let the chia seeds settle at the bottom. You want them to absorb the liquid evenly and avoid clumping. Once thickened, add the chia mixture to your shake ingredients and blend.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Clean your dishwasher filter weekly (or more than your usual once in a whenever). Chia seeds easily get trapped in the filter and clog it, and then you have an unwanted unintended consequence. Speaking from experience

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I like the focus of ditching the protein powders which are ultra-processed.  My favourite ingredient in protein shakes is egg whites. You can buy pasteurized egg whites and 1/3 cup has 10 gm of protein.  Top up with soy milk, throw in a bit of nut butter or hemp hearts and whatever fruit you like and you have 20 gm of protein. You can’t taste the egg.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sally the recipe intro says it's "flexible based on what you have". You have protein powder you want to use. Congratulations on your protein shake adaptation! I hope you enjoy it!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tip for frozen bananas to make blending much easier: slice up your bananas when they ripe and put them in a ziplock in the freezer with the round pieces arrayed kind of like a line of fallen dominoes. When ready to make your shakes, pull out a ziplock and they should easily break apart and be much easier blend than a whole banana. Rinse and reuse the ziplocks.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Don't see Protein ratings.   
Not sure why your other stuff tasted like cement.  Maybe too much powder. 
Try frozen bananas, blueberries.   This isn't your only source of protein, so you don't need to overdo it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I’ve read info & my Dr. also told me as well to not add fruit to my smoothies b/c:
Blending fruit for a smoothie breaks down plant cell walls, releasing trapped natural sugars and turning them into "free sugars". While fiber remains, this mechanical breakdown makes the sugar metabolically similar to added sugar, often causing a faster spike in blood sugar compared to eating whole fruit
I add a bit of vanilla extract or a FEW blueberries .. then eat my fruit in it’s natural form.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I usually do a variation of this:

1 cup unsweetened almond/nut milk
2 Tbsps Greek yogurt (whatever fat content you want)
1 Tbsp chia seeds (soaked in the nut milk for at least 15 min so they become like little jelly balls)
1 Tbsp flax meal (yay fiber!)
1 serving protein powder (I use flavored, but you can substitute with whatever protein source you want)

That’s my go to base. Then I add either a banana or a couple slices dehydrated mangos and about half a cup of frozen fruit. I’ll throw in a scoop of peanut butter if I’m hungry and out of frozen fruit. Frozen blueberries thicken the shake a lot so I use more nut milk when I do blueberries.

I don’t track my macros or anything, but just generally want a tasty and pretty healthy shake. This hits the spot every time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Meladee. Low fat versions of dairy are carb bombs and bad for diabetics and those with insulin resistance.  Fat slows down the absorption of the carbs, and removing the fat leads to insulin and glucose spikes from the now “naked” carbs.  

Fat is also a macro nutrient our bodies need. Fat is not the enemy:  sugar and ultra-processed foods are the enemy.

commentBody: I try not to lump all fats together.  The evidence is quite strong that consumption of land raised animal fat contributes to arterial disease.  See e.g. Esselstyn's work at Cleveland Clinic.  The consumption of relatively small amounts of fat from nuts, seeds, (and perhaps fatty fishes) appears protective for arterial disease.

commentBody: Why skip the protein powder? Many of us eschew dairy as lactose intolerance becomes prescient.  I swim, jog, lift weights at an advanced age; this gives me energy: Handful of chopped fresh spinach, 4-5 chunks each of pineapple, avocado, banana, all frozen) 1 T. 3-seed mix (hemp, chia, flaxseed), scoop of vanilla protein powder (35-60 grams), 1c. SF coconut milk, stevia, dashes-nutmeg, ginger, cinnamon. Can add 1/2 c. canned pumpkin, SF brown sugar, SF maple syrup, pumpkin pie seasoning.

author: Ali Slagle
