author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Bananas are particularly high in PPO (Polyphenol Oxidase). Adding bananas to smoothies introduces this enzyme, which can reduce the beneficial effects of polyphenols from berries like blueberries.  I imagine that a high percentage of protein smoothies sold or homemade contain berries and bananas.  Bananas add thickness and sweetness, but to benefit from the best nutritional component of the shake, you may want to avoid this combination (86 the bananas). Just a thought.

recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Growing up in the 50s, my family was poor, although as a kid, we didn't know it. One dish my mother made frequently was pasta with butter, cottage cheese and the grated Parmesan from Kraft that came/still comes in the green tube. Occasionally she would throw frozen green peas in at the same time as the cottage cheese. It remains comfort food for me

I am bemused and encouraged to find the NYT version of this. It is definitely going into the rotation.

recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Charles Kruger zucchini noodles would be good but try shirataki noodles (Miracle Noodles) — basically all konjac fiber and no carbs.  There are also some varieties that are part tofu (House Foods brand) which I actually prefer for a slightly better texture and taste plus a little protein.  The important thing in preparing these noodles is to rinse well then boil for about 5 minutes and drain well. They are packed in a solution with an unpleasant smell but this will remove it. I usually find shiratake noodles in the tofu section of the store.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’m not a huge fan of cottage cheese, consistency- and sodium-wise, although the consistency will not be a factor in this recipe. Still, I think I’ll try it with Eastern European “farmers’ cheese”, called tvorog in Russian. It’s sold at Slavic/Russian grocery stores, of which there are several in the DC Metro area where I live. Possibly also in Whole Foods, although not sure about that (also easy to make from scratch with milk that is starting to turn, or simply by adding some vinegar to fresh milk - then heating it until the curds separate from the whey and straining it). This type of cottage cheese is lovely for breakfast, a snack, etc., and much lower in sodium.

Farmers Cheese is ALWAYS my substitute for cottage cheese. I can usually get it at Whole Foods. Use it in cheese cakes, pancakes, lasagna and will use it in this sauce.

commentBody: I grew up eating cottage cheese and pasta. Easy recipe. When you’ve drained the pasta, put it back in the pot. Over small fire, add cottage cheese and whatever else you’d like, parmesan cheese is good. Stir and you’re finished.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been grating parmesan in a Cuisinart for roughly 50 years.  I usually do a fairly large quantity at one time, put some in a jar in the frig, and freeze the rest.  I cut the cheese into digestible chunks, start the machine and drop a few chunks at a time through the feed tube.  It works with the disk grater, but I often just use the steel blade.  The process is noisy, the Cuisinart sometimes bounces around a bit, and stay bits of cheese occasionally fly out, but it works fine.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Thought this was rather bland. Will add some red pepper flakes and maybe some chopped anchovies.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: really good, easy meal! I agree with other commenters that you don’t need that much pasta water, but I’m saving the rest to use with the leftovers. It could use a little bit of pop- maybe black pepper or crushed red pepper flakes. I’m going to experiment with the leftovers and see if either of those help

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We made this with blanched Nettles from our garden and it was DIVINE.....added a touch more garlic and a little arugula....oh and some chicken thigh cooked in marsala and onion.  Will definitely be making this again

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This recipe popped up Saturday afternoon and I wanted to try it immediately! I had to raid the cupboard and fridge, swapping in kale for the spinach and jarred Barilla pesto because I didn’t have enough basil. I was unsure how much Parmesan to use, but can you ever use too much? This was still delicious—such a forgiving recipe! I sautéed shrimp from the freezer to go with. Such a keeper!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Works with full fat Greek yogurt, too. (And for that matter, with spring mix rather than pure spinach)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Three quarters of cup of pasta water made my sauce too loose.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: One of the best NYT ever & I’ve done many.  Incredibly creamy.  Do yourself a favor & don’t shortcut with jar pesto.  Do the deal with very fresh basil & spinach.  Use only real grated Parmesan, not the shaker kind.  3/4 c pasta water is a bit much cuz it turns out a bit soupy but use a slotted spoon.  No need for long pasta.  I used Trofie pasta from Trader Joe. I think you could use this (before adding pasta water) to dress salad, veggies, fish, chicken.  Out of this world with possibilities.  And it’s cottage cheese yay!flavor bomb!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is very forgiving and adaptable with other fresh herbs. I had spinach but no basil so used tarragon, dill, and chives. It was bright and zippy! Everyone from toddler to grandparents loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: WAY too much work for an imitation pasta with pesto.  Needs some red pepper and walnuts or pine nuts to make it more interesting.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Came here to also recommend this. I roasted halved cherry tomatoes at 375 for 11 min, and the extra sweetness and color played very nicely with the slightly tart sauce. @kkm

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight, and it's very good! My only suggestion would be to use a medium lemon rather than a large one. mine was quite large, and just on the edge if overwhelming the other flavours. Definitely a keeper!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent, definitely going into the rotation, but honestly I think the amount of sauce could have dressed twice the pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe but felt it needed something more so I roasted walnuts and rough chopped them which added color, texture and protein.  Think hazelnuts would work well too.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Nutritional yeast instead of Parmesan works just as well. Blend with a little almond milk. It's my go-to cream for all pasta and soup dishes.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Cuisinart has a grater attachment that works like a charm. My Cuisinart is 40 years old and works perfectly. I always grate a large volume of parmesan with it and never had an issue.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy same here.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy Yes, the grating disc for the Cuisinart does a great job with Parmesan.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: AEC - Will it be as creamy if you use 2% cottage cheese, possibly not but, it's what I'm planning to do!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Re: Using food processor for grating cheese.....cube the parmesan, turn processor on and add a few cubes at a time as it is running. I've done this for years and never had a problem.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: For those of us who pay "some" attention to the nutrition numbers, PLEASE don't show them as per serving (4-6 servings) Which is it?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The is a great recipe. A nice way to get a creamy sauce for pasta without the heavy cream. I amped up the garlic (to the equivalent of 5 cloves) and was generous with the olive oil. Next time I will use all the pasta cooking water recommended in the recipe so that it would loosen up more. Also, I should add that I used the Beta version of the recipe (I made only a half) which resulted in leftovers for two people. The Beta version of the recipe was spot on.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This will now become a staple for me.  It's outrageously good!  I was low on fresh basil so filled in with some fresh parsley.  It has the prefect amount of creaminess cut with the lemon zest/juice and garlic.  Divine and a great weeknight reheat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!! wow. Used Fresh local spinach, in lieu of basil, garlic and olive oil I added a home made batch of petso from the freezer, which equated to 2 cups of basil, ect.  Tasted great, so assuming it was an okay substitution.  The sauce tastes so good it would be a great dip as an appetizer too!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was meh. I had to add a lot of pepper and more parmesan It also wasn't particularly filling, which was surprising for something with that much cottage cheese and parmesan. It was fine, but I wouldn't make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I've made this again and again and again. I serve it with a side of seared chicken sausage, sliced, for my kids. The leftovers are even better. This is a killer receipe and my kids love it after practice when they are starving and just out of the shower. I serve it to them in a big bowl, with a towel on their laps. I've shared it with a lot of people. A keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: 5 stars all around. This recipe f l e x e s. Our basil plants are busting, so we harvested from 4 basil plants, (finally) used a 12oz bag of frozen spinach bc that’s what we have, a tub of Violife vegan cream cheese, vegan Parmesan cheese, half a block of silken tofu (boo is lactose free intolerant), olive oil because we are lacking dairy fat, and 2 juicy, fat garlic cloves which I recommend to everyone following Ali’s recipe to a T. Flavor is off the charts, easy to make and adapts h a r d. I have 3/4 pint of additional sauce due to my adaptations. I regret nothing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Would it be possible to make this with Farmer’s cheese? I have some to use up.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I use farmer cheese or pressed cottage cheese all the time. It should be fine; just break up the cheese into chunks. Since farmer cheese is dryer than curd cottage cheese you might need to add more liquid (more pasta water or lemon juice or a bit of white wine).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried adapting this using store-bought pesto?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this with roughly equal parts cottage cheese and my frozen and thawed homemade pesto. Morning olive green in color, although my pesto did not contain any spinach. Topped it off with leftover breadcrumbs from the eggplant parm pasta recipe. Easy riff on pasta pesto and kids liked it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I would not try to great a hard cheese in a food processor. it's just going to get caught on the blades and spin around and jam it up. I do however great hard cheese in my original Vitamix. it has an extraordinarily strong motor and it's probably the only appliance I would use for something like this. Otherwise it's probably best to just hand grade it using a box grater

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’m confused about the lemon.  The recipe sounds like you toss the whole lemon into the high speed blender.  Is it the zest of one lemon and the juice of one lemon?  Please explain.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: It sounds like you zest and juice one lemon. Both things can be done to one lemon. The whole lemon does not go into the blender.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The instructions say "zest and juice the lemon into a high-powered blender or food processor." So you first zest the lemon and put that into the blender, and then squeeze the juice from the lemon into the blender. You don't put the whole lemon in.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is a fantastic sauce! I used pecorino romano instead of parm.  I served it over small raviolis.  It was a hit with the family.  Next time I will serve it over tortellini.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: All we could taste was lemon. If I could go back in time, I’d start with 1/4 lemon juice zest and gradually add in more. I ended up having to throw most of it away because my family refused to eat it. Even an extra cup of cheese couldn’t save it

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Definitely not going back to  days of cottage cheese and cantaloupe /pineapple  which everyone ate to loose weight.  And spinach ? The 80’s and 90s  “it salad”.  No thanks .  Let’s update to delicious Ricotta cheese and arugula or kale or baby anything greens.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I would love to make this tonight but I have no basil yet. I do however have a garden full of gorgeous mint. maybe mint and parsley would work?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Do you taste the spinach?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We called this "green spahetti" and it was one of my kid's favorites. We made it pretty much as the recipe says, except we used cream cheese instead of cottage cheese, and we didn't have a blender. it is truly a perfect sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with jarred pesto? I use Trader Joe’s all the time.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I have lots of frozen homemade pesto. Think I can substitute pesto for the fresh basil leaves? I don’t see why not, but haven’t made this yet. Thoughts?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is reminiscent of a much simpler recipe that my mother made when we were kids: macaroni, sour cream, and cottage cheese. It was served with salt and pepper and whatever green vegetables were in the fridge like peas, or chopped up scallions. I think basil was a little too sophisticated but this sounds great and I'll definitely give it a try.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Old guy who doesn't do much cooking:  Try it!  The "what ifs" are the mainstay and great joy of cooking!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I, for one, I know I am in the minority, don't particularly like too much lemon. For me, it cuts through the other flavors, so I would do the zest.  I also think a handful of nuts would be nice, for a little texture.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: For the Parmesan:  I run pieces through the processor with the shredding blade, then switch to the regular blade and chop up those shredded pieces.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: OMG. This is genius. Made as written. Glorious! Bonus is protein from cottage cheese!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Used 16 oz tub of cottage cheese and a 5oz tub of spinach. We like our pasta saucy. 

Not sure if it’s because I used whole wheat pasta, but the sauce didn’t really seem to stick to the noodles. Loved the flavor and texture if the sauce though, 

Next time I make it, I’ll use a penne or other tube shape to get more sauce per bite.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Yum! Made as written and topped with grilled chicken. Easy 30 minute meal. It was a hit with the whole fam. Will definitely make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Just Delicious and super easy! Will be my new go-to for an easy, healthy and yummy pasta sauce!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’m a fan of cottage cheese because of the protein,but this recipe was a big bore. I prefer Eric Kim’s Spinach Egg Bites if we’re combining cottage cheese and spinach. Otherwise I’ll forget trying to get my protein intake in a pasta recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
What a great recipe.
April is the end of the winter growing season here in Miami and our veggie garden is full of all kinds of greens that are starting to go to seed. So besides snipping the basil and spinach the recipe calls for, I also grabbed a bunch of kale, mustard greens, Swiss chard, and arugula. 
Because they were weeks past baby tender, I removed the thicker center spines and blanched the leaves quickly in the pasta water before throwing them into the food processor.
The dish was fresh, floral, and very delicious. I even have a nice container of sauce left that I’ll use for something else tomorrow.
Thank you for a great suggestion. We’ll be making this again soon.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Added pine nuts…yum!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Tasted great! I didn’t have fresh spinach so I used frozen, and I didn’t have fresh Parmesan so I used some packets from pizza delivery, and it tasted great anyway.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Who knew that grating cheese in the food processor with the blade was so easy?! It was good but will definitely add less lemon next time.  Like half.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Halved it and made it as is with Thai basil since that’s what my farmers market had. I added some red pepper flakes and it’s so good.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as written and it was delicious.  It produced enough for 4 generous servings.  I used my immersion blender and it took 5 minutes to reach the correct consistency.  Will make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed preparing this dish (much prefer weighing ingredients for accuracy).  But my immediate reaction was disappointment in how lemony this was, as in much too much.  I'll admit that the lemon I had on hand was large and very juicy.  But if everything else in the ingredients list here is measured, why not the lemon as well?  Will try again, but will really dial the lemon back next time.  P.S. Used whole wheat pasta, which worked fine.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night exactly by the recipe - including weighing all ingredients -  and did not love it.  It was too much basil for my taste.  Also needed more acid.   I threw some fresh cherry tomatoes in while eating, and that helped a bit, but still wasn't great.  Tasting for salt is a good tip - I felt like it needed a good bit of salt added.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: So good! Wouldn't change anything except add lemon to your taste. Half a lemon plus zest from one full lemon was the perfect amount without being overwhelming. Also, I like my pastas with more sauce, so I'd use this much sauce for half a pound of pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe!  Being unable to be faithful to any recipie, I did add double the olive oil and a bit more (1/3) of the greens, cottage cheese, and parm, plus 1/2 cup cream cheese.  This is  keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Hello,  I was really hoping to love this but it was a bit to vividly herbaceous for me.  It was likely user error - I got regular spinach rather than baby spinanch by mistake and just eyeballed the measurements which likely caused me to add too much of the green.  My lemon was also a bit long in the tooth.  Am posting so you can avoid my errors.  I think I will give it a second try though, maybe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Was so excited about this recipe. Everything seemed to come out great. I added the pasta water to the creamy sauce, and tossed it with the pasta, all was well. It was beautiful in fact. Then I made the mistake of following their recommendation of adding more pasta water to the mixed pasta and sauce. I added just a little water and it immediately made the beautiful creamy pasta suddenly disappeared. Awful. Maybe user error, but definitely don't follow that part of the recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night...it was very "pesto" flavored and rich...though I wish I thought to cut the recipe in half...(we are just 2 and we will be eating it for days!) It was a little "one note" for me, so perhaps next time I might add some finely chopped salted lemon and some kind of heat...But otherwise lovely!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This is superb, as is. Wow.
Make it! You won’t be disappointed.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’m in the minority, but I found this dry and bland, even after adding extra olive oil, lemon, and pasta water to the sauce. Would not make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: unfortunately sauce completely broke when i added it to the pasta and became an ugly grainy (albeit pretty tasty) mess. i suspect this is bc i used gluten-free chickpea pasta, so no starch in the pasta water to help bind, but i wish instructions for these types of recipes would include a warning or alternative steps so that these could be made with gluten free pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Rare indeed is the recipe that stands as written!

Note that it really does require a full pound of pasta, and that spaghetti or linguine is the way to go

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Very average, sorry to say.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! We had actually watched a video on pasta and looked for real Italian pasta, which made it even better. Would make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Can I freeze leftover sauce without spaghetti.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as per recipe, this was just excellent, definitely a keeper. I have only two comments: go for a medium lemon; I used a large one, and it was right on the edge of the lemon overwhelming the other flavours -- still delicious, but on the edge. Second, for goodness's sake, grate the parmesan by hand! I just don't understand why anyone would risk their blender when it takes about two minutes to grate 3.5 ozs of it. Seriously, people.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Totally - only took a sec to grate.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this with 1/2c Parmesan and a similar amount of Rana pesto and it was delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly according to the recipe - absolute Spring heaven, and so good for you - for a pasta dish! I cooked up some shrimp to go with it. I could not help myself but to have a second serving the same size as the first with zero guilt. Ugh - so good.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Since there’s a conversation about grating Parmesan, my method is with my Vitamix. 1” chunks, fire it up and in mere seconds your cheese is finely grated. Another plus is that you can use soapy water and the Vitamix cleans itself.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is DELICIOUS but I needed to add some red pepper flakes to up the flavor profile....

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was very good and also very easy and quick to make.  For cost reasons, I suggest making it in summer/early fall when basil is plentiful.  That much basic bought off-season is really expensive.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night. Had homemade pesto frozen, so defrosted and added the cottage cheese.  Was very thick, even after adding lots of lemon juice. Two hours before cooking, I marinated heirloom cherry tomatoes with EVOO, red wine vinegar and salt and pepper. Heated these a bit to top the pasta. You must heat the bowls as pesto is not cooked. Topped with grated Parmesan and Romano cheeses and served parmesan crisps on the side. Husband loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made it with orzo & added a can of cannellini beans for extra protein.  I also used Nancy's Low Fat probiotic cottage cheese with live cultures.  I was reluctant to add the 3/4 cup of pasta water into the finished pasta--but it actually worked out fine.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: made this tonight. served with crispy garlic enoki mushrooms and toasted pepitas on top. Delish!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I'd recommend Costco pesto 💯 my goto for not home made @Old guy who doesn't do much cooking

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is an easy and delicious recipe, but an entire lemon was too much in my opinion (and I like lemon a lot). Will use half a lemon in the future. It yielded quite a bit of sauce, too - didn’t need that much pasta water.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Can I use 2% cottage cheese instead of whole milk

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I just made this as is.  So, so delicious.  Creamy, bright and I can just feel the benefits of that spinach and fresh basil coursing through my veins. I’m totally serious.  This was so easy, so quick and so incredibly wonderful. Will definitely be moving into the rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is very similar to Peruvian “tallarines verdes”, a popular dish. The Peruvian version includes pecans, and uses “queso fresco” instead of cottage cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: A tip for families with little ones: we call it “Hulk Pasta” and now my 4 year old can’t get enough. Quick, easy, and delicious! Added bonus: it’s a great way to use up an abundance of greens from the garden.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Nice to find this somewhat more nutritious version of pesto, with the addition of cottage cheese and spinach.   I found that it didn't need nearly as much pasta water as it called for -- so be sparing lest the sauce get too thin.  Next time i will experiment with adding walnuts maybe some  veggies such as green beens or peas.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is phenomenal. We added a good pinch of red pepper flakes and let it rest for about 30 minutes, added some more of the warm pasta water before serving. Outrageously good.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this and couldn’t get behind it. Tried the leftovers for lunch, same thought. Then I realized I forgot the basil. 😂

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Can you explain juicing and zesting lemon in a food processor/blender? Or did I misunderstand?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @S I think you do the juicing and zesting but you do it into the food processor bowl so it’s ready to be blended with everything else.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I interpreted this to mean zest the lemon into the food processor bowl, then juice the lemon and add the juice into the bowl with the other ingredients.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @S I was also confused

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The recipe doesn't say to grate the parm using the food processor. The ingredient list calls for grated parm, i.e. cheese that has already been grated @NHMAAZTXCA

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: From the Tip at the end of the recipe: "Since you’re getting a blender or food processor dirty anyway, use it to grate the Parmesan, too: Chop the Parmesan into big chunks, then pulse in a blender until ground."

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: It does say that in the "Tip" directly below the recipe.   I for one am afraid to try, because it may ruin my Vitamix, and is just as much labor (and time) to cut the cheese into tiny pieces to use my Vitamix than it it is to simply grate the cheese by hand.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The sauce is bright (lemon and garlic) and nice but less flavorful than I wanted.  I added some sausage to give it more depth.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone else find that when incorporating the sauce into the pasta, it coagulated into a pasty, clumpy mess? We didn’t transfer the pasta to a different dish after boiling it, so we’re thinking the heat of the pot maybe caused this to happen? The sauce looked delicious before we began stirring it in!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Ella D - yes, this exact thing sadly happened to me too! I’ve been trying to figure out why. Possibly the reserved pasta water too hot? Any of you super cooks out there with an idea? I would love to make this again and have the noodles all coated in a beautiful sauce than the globs of congealed parm I ended up with.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Why couldn't one put the lemon in the blender as the recipe calls for?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @David D I think the pith would be too bitter.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I'm going to give this a try for Sunday Supper.  I was thinking that the Green Goddess Chicken meatballs recipe shown in the same article would be a nice topper for this dish.  Sadly, it's too early here for my herb garden and fresh basil so it's off to the market.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Can I use jarred pesto? I hate to even ask but I have both it and cottage cheese fresh from Costco.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I wish that the brand Costco near us sells (Shamrock) didn’t have so many additives.  I look for Daisy or Good.

recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I will try this appealing recipe for family pasta Sunday, but by substituting ricotta for cottage cheese (fiocchi di latte). Otherwise a mutiny. American born, 100% Italian, and having lived in Italy, I can assure you authentic Italian cooking does not use cottage cheese in most pasta dishes excepting occasional accommodations for lactose intolerant (eg., some lasagna dishes). Definitely a taste/texture difference. Used in salads mostly.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Thanks Pretzel, and I think you’re right; it’s not supposed to be an Italian recipe. But I only wish NYT editors would clarify by labeling it as “fusion” or “Americanized” as very popular dishes found domestically were created by early immigrants and not found in Italy. Readers deserve to know the difference.

commentBody: I loved this! After I got started,  I realized I didn’t have all of the basil called for (only 1 ounce), it still turned out great.  Someone stated they had added some Preserved Lemon. It sounded good to me, so I added 1/4 rind, well rinsed…then added another 1/4 for a little more salt and lemon - total 1/2 preserved lemon.  It really amped you the flavor in my opinion.  I make Preserved Lemons with Meyer Lemons from my tree, so I always have plenty on hand.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This was great! Only made 3 adjustments:
1. Blended a small handful of pine nuts into the pesto
2. Cooked up some sausage and half an onion and added it at the end. Totally unnecessary, but we needed to use them up.
3. Added some frozen fire roasted corn at the end (a pesto classic for our family, I would recommend).

I highly recommend this recipe. We grew up eating pesto pasta all summer, and I was afraid this would taste like a let-down in comparison. Quite the opposite. This was creamy and delicious, more substantial, and less oily. Making the sauce only takes a long as boiling and cooking by the pasta.

author: Ali Slagle

commentBody: Delicious and so pretty!  I found it easy to blend in the Nutribullet, but had to do it in 2 batches.  I used probiotic whole milk cottage cheese, protein spaghetti, and baby spinach.  I bought a large basil plant from TJ's and was surprised that it provided only about 1.5 ounces.  I added an extra ounce of spinach to make up the difference.  Next time I'll use the full amount of basil. I love that it's a whole meal in one dish. My whole family loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are totally addictive. I used Momofuku chili crunch instead of hot sauce. If you don't want it too spicy, just use 1/2 tablespoon of the crunch. PLUS >>> I am from Baltimore, and Old Bay seasoned crabs are in my DNA. I love this spice so much. Thank you, Ali, for giving me yet another non-meat way to enjoy it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great recipe. I recommend freezing the tofu ahead of time and then thawing (and wringing it out like a sponge) which helps give it a better texture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Excellent - do you drain the liquid from around the tofu before you freeze it?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Betsy nope! Just throw the whole container in the freezer

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Excellent another method would be to tear the tofu into chunks and boil it for 5 minutes in salted water.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This comment applies to ALL of your recipes featuring tofu: The best way to drain tofu is using the container it comes in, turned upside down on a plate. It has grooves/ridges that help the water move downward toward the plate. Place the slab of tofu on top, covered with a piece of wax paper or facsimile. Place a plate with weight on top of that. Wait for the weight to work its magic - about an hour or more. Perfect. No towel necessary. The slab can be easily sliced into perfect cubes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I just want to say that if you use “super firm” tofu, which is AKA “high protein” tofu (14g vs 9g per serving), all this fussy pressing, drying, freezing that folks recommend is not at all necessary. I go way back with cooking tofu, and there is no need to do all this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @JenScan I concur. Our local supermarket just started carrying super firm tofu, which still has some liquid, but it amounts to less than three tablespoons. It also crisps really nicely in the pan, or in the oven to roast. Forget extra firm.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @JenScan Agree. Also, trust the recipe if it doesn’t say you need to press. Make it without pressing it or freezing it and see how it is. I am an inconsistent presser and find the difference negligible, even with extra firm.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I did half in the air fryer, half in the oven. Air fryer for the win! They just didn’t crisp up in the oven.  Subbed rice flour for the AP flour. These were terrific with blue cheese salad dressing, roasted broccoli and jeera rice. And while my tin of Old Bay has been in the spice drawer for ages, the flavour was still great.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: How long did you cook them for in the air fryer?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Kay I did the air fryer at 400 for 25 minutes which was perfect for the bigger pieces but too much for the ones smaller than an inch. I also added a little corn meal in the coating and that was great too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Kay thanks for the info! I came to the comments to see if anyone had tried these in the air fryer. :)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you shred the tofu instead of cubing it; add a little Dijon mustard, a couple of tablespoons of melted butter, tbsp lemon juice and splash  vegan Worcestershire sauce to two beaten eggs; add half a cup of bread crumbs, panko, or cornmeal, then add the tofu and form into patties - crab cake flavored tofu patties! I added a couple of tablespoons of seaweed too Bake at 375 for 15-20 minutes per side.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy and delicious. Used siracha and opted to top off with lemon (instead of tartar sauce, ketchup or bbq sauce). Made NY Times cole slaw, which was an excellent complement.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As a fried seafood platter lovin’ Southerner, I made this as written substituting Red Chili paste for hot sauce as I’m currently out of Texas Pete! It was pretty good but next time I will add more Old Bay, less hot sauce and I will fry it!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe made me finally succeed at baking a crispy tofu!!! As an experiment I made (half) both a firm and an extra firm tofu package. Followed comments here to use Chile crisp (no Frank's in my cupboard) and add some cornmeal. I crossed my fingers and put them in the oven. They are fantastic!! I'm making the second batch now with the full 2 T of Chile crisp, a little extra spice and smaller pieces (probably actual 1 inch)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Pamela did you prefer the nuggets made with firm or extra firm tofu?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Deceptively simple and packed with lots of flavor. It’s always great to get another tofu recipe that is beyond typical Asian flavors. Tearing the tofu was a new technique for me and I’m glad to have all ragged edges to absorb the great hot sauce flavor. I may try using cornstarch the next time to see if this can still be crispy and gluten free. Put this on my meatless rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My 3-year-old is an Old Bay fanatic and I think would love these, but at this point he can't handle the hot sauce. Any suggestions for how to modify this so it isn't spicy?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sarah just use a little olive or veg oil rather than hot sauce, if that’s your concern.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Ranch salad dressing would work well as the binder and kid friendly.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Rich

Mayo too!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Had an ancient tin of Old Bay and a bit of tofu ennui so I gave the recipe a try.
Loved it! Substituted Mongolian Fire oil for the hot sauce. Served with Cole slaw.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really loved this. I used super firm tofu and Franks Buffalo sauce (since that’s what I had). Will be interested to make it with hotter sauce and more (or fresher) Old Bay. Crisped really nicely after about 35 min. I paired it with Genevieve Ko’s tartar sauce. Delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so fun! I used Old Bay hot sauce mixed with a little ranch dressing and some oil, added a little corn meal to the flour coating and air fried at 400 for 25 minutes. Next time I’ll make sure my tofu pieces are a little bigger because the smallest ones turned a little too crunchy. We had it with tartar sauce, coleslaw and buttered peas with mint.  Hubby and grandson loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very tasty! Just eyeballed the seasoning so I’m sure there was extra. Air fried at 395 for 20 minutes instead of baking (too hot in the house for the oven) and they came out nice and crunchy with a little bit of chew.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I followed the recipe to the T and they were so good, so easy, and scratched my itch for something fried-ish (even tho they are baked!). Will be making again!! Think this will be a great addition to a grain bowl or salad.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Rich-Thank you for the suggestion of ranch dressing for the binder.  I may try avocado ranch dressing since I do not like hot sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used 1 tsp Hot sauce, 1 tsp Old Bay and added 1/2 tsp salt. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Freezing your tofu first, then thawing it and squeezing the moisture out (I put it between 2 bowls and squeeze) gives the tofu a wonderful texture!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Followed the instructions exactly and it was delicious!  Served with hot sauce.  My husband (who claims to not like tofu) said, “it’s scary how much I like this.”

Think I might add a little siracha and use a little less olive oil next time.  I baked it longer than the recipe to get a nice crispy outside.  Yummy and easy!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could almond flour be used to make this GF?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Another Ali Slagle keeper. Used my beloved Frank's hot sauce and baked for 35 minutes. Would make a nice spicy "chicken" sandwich, too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Ehhhh… these still have that raw tofu taste. I followed the recipe exactly except I added a dash of corn starch. I am vegan so no stranger to tofu but I was hoping for more flavor. I think I’ll try this again but “marinade” the tofu in the hot sauce or something else. They were nice and crispy though.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Added 1 1/2 tsp. Old Bay and used cornstarch instead of flour. Also used 1 tbs. oil, 1 tbs. chile crisp and 1 tbs. of a spicy lime mayo sauce I had to make the cornstarch adhere. This is a great recipe, super easy, flexible and fast to throw together. We squeezed lemon juice on top (the best) and also opened up whatever fast food sauce packets we had in the junk drawer. Delicious! Thank you, Ali.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Extra hot sauce and old bay did the trick. Partner loved it alongside rice, broccoli and mushrooms. Nothing left!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have a tofu press which works beautifully in the recipe. Such an amazing recipe! Thanks again NYT Cooking for another winner!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Thank you, Rich for the suggestion to sub ranch dressing. I first made it with half the Frank's Red Hot (and added an extra tablespoon of oil), but the flavor was still too strong for my kids, even though it wasn't that spicy. They definitely like the ranch version better.

It was with this recipe that I discovered that Ali Slagle's all about recipes that aren't too time-consuming or complicated. Turns out I have made other recipes of hers, and they have been great! Keep 'em coming!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as written. A nice easy recipe that results in a perfect blend of complimentary addictive flavors. No need for improvements.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can these be cooked in an air fryer?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are very tasty. I try to always make a recipe exactly as written the first time, but I didn't have Frank's on-hand, so I used Tapatio instead. It was delicious. I will be making this recipe *a lot*.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Surprisingly good and quick. I used about 1/3 of the hot sauce and wheat flour. I didn't bother pressing the tofu. That seemed like an unnecessary step and they turned out almost a little dry. Might be a tad much on the garlic

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Too spicy

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: roasted crispy tofu!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What can I use in place of the hot sauce for extremely spice averse toddlers? All of the subs listed in the comments would still be too spicy I think

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So easy and delicious. We paired it with a malt vinegar based slaw. Celery seed is indeed key for that part. Will cook this one again for sure.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious! A unique take on tofu that easily comes together.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Public service announcement: Old Bay is predominantly celery salt, so if you don't like celery salt, might be a good idea to swap out that particular seasoning for something else.  The tofu preparation is great (binder, seasoned flour, baking instructions).  I'll be using it often.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It was dry and somewhat bland. Not sure if this tofu needed marinating…

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @shaunaleee - I couldn't tell the difference at all!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hmmm, my tofu did not turn out crispy at all.  I did follow the instructions as I like to do when trying out a recipe for the first time.  It could use more flavor, maybe a dash of soy sauce in the mixture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Thank you for not telling us to press the tofu first! An often repeated but often necessary instruction.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Following other suggestions I used half flour half cornmeal, increased the Old Bay to 1.5 teaspoons, and used a combo of hot sauces (one homemade, one maple habanero acquired in Canada). My pieces were somewhat smaller than 1 inch so a few seemed a bit dried out with 30 minutes cook time; will adjust sizes or timing next time I make it. We lived in Maryland for a few years in the 80s and I have a recipe in the 50th anniversary cookbook of 1989, so I have a soft spot in my heart for Old Bay.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I can second prior comments that this is truly delicious.  One of the best tofu recipes I have tried, honestly. A nice change from stir fries, curries, etc.  I have made this with extra firm and super firm tofu, and super firm is the way to go.  The nuggets or "sticks" hold their shapes better.  Turned out crispy and very tasty. Served with simple homemade tartar sauce and lemon wedges. Two big thumbs up!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I thought as written this didn't work -- the hot sauce was overpowering, and I couldn't taste the Old Bay or any other flavors. I used Cholula; maybe that was an issue. It also didn't get crispy in my oven. Would reduce the hot sauce and try the air fryer if I made it again. I do agree that the leftovers out of the fridge were weirdly addictive.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I did not have Franks so I used three tablespoons of Earth Balance stick butter, melted, with two teaspoons of gochujang paste mixed in as the binder (had a little leftover). I also used high protein tofu. Anyway, it was delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So delicious! I’ll be experimenting with this method to make other flavor combinations of tofu nuggets.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Made once as written and liked a lot. Made again using potato starch (same amount) instead of flour and baking at 375 convection mode on the middle rack and loved them!

So nice added to a salad or cut up cold and put into a chicken salad style situation the next day. 

Looking forward to trying the cornmeal recommendation.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are good but need 2x the old bay and garlic powders. I also threw in some nutritional yeast for good measure. Bakes well in air fryer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are supposed to be crispy. They did not come out crispy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I forgot to "tear" the tofu instead of just cutting it. It was fine. Not evenly coated though.  didn't have any red hot sauce so used some other habanero sauce that we had on hand, doubled the spice amount.  I cooked it for a total of about 26 minutes to make it just a tad crispier.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I love most Ali Slagle recipes, but this one was a miss for me. Not terrible, but not great. There are better tofu recipes in the NY Times Cooking section, including Ali's wonderful Lemon-Pepper Tofu and Snap Peas recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Loved this recipe! But two words of advice: potato starch. It really does the trick, getting the torn pieces perfectly crispy and not gluey. Also, advice to put on the bottom rack is spot on. 30 mins at 425 in gas oven was perfect.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Do these freeze well?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We loved these little nuggets. Made the NYT tartar sauce recipe to dip. Fantastic combination.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Ah been making these for years for breakfast.. much easier wisk eggs in small bowl. Add cheese and mix.  Melt butter in frying pan. Place tortilla in buttered pan and top with egg mix (and perhaps some canned refried beans as extra). Place second tortilla on top. When bottom tortilla starts to brown flip and cook other side to golden flip on plate and cut in half and serve hot plain or top with salsa, cilantro, fresh diced tomato, sour cream, chopped green onions, etc.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Geri.  Thank you!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you don't want to grab the tortilla from the skillet, you can slide it onto a plate, put a second plate over that, flip and then slide it back into the skillet. Or just use a spatula!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This has been a breakfast staple of my family for years and we call them armadillos.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Los armadillos.  Love the name!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Whole wheat tortillas and olive oil.  Aged sharp cheddar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @John MD That’s a quesadilla only a bolillo would eat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Actually, one can and should omit the "dexterity required" flipping technique.  When the cook "presses the tortilla to adhere", it sticks to the cheese and egg.  Use a rigid metal restaurant-type 5" long spatula and you can easilly flip like a pancake.  Then on to Step 4. as described. Easier.  Safer. And even the dexterous challenged among us can do it successfully.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Andy Hess a metal spatula is not too kind to non-stick pans. But other than that, you are spot on!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Whisking the eggs in a bowl, adding some cilantro, cumin powder, and paprika will take it to a new level. Instead of holding the tortilla by hand, simply cover the skillet with a plate, flip it, and then slide the tortilla back into the skillet.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used the technique as written (normal sized tortilla, not big burrito size), and it was super easy and not tricky or dangerous in the least. Heed the directions to use a pan that fits your tortilla snugly, and you’ll be fine! I added fresh jalapeños, cilantro, and chopped green scallions in the filling and served with avocado and Peri Peri sauce for dipping. Easy and delish!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is one of my favorite breakfasts.  One small addition that I cannot resist is some crumbled Mexican style chorizo sprinkled over the egg and cheese before folding.  I also like to put a bit of a smokey char on the tortilla by toasting it for a few seconds on over the gas burner.  Finally a big spoonful of pico de gallo goes on top of the finished quesadilla.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made it as written except used cooking spray instead of butter and a low carb tortilla (I need the fiber, sorry!) Turned out perfectly and I will be making this regularly for a quick lunch. I can see myself adding a squirt of kewpie mayo or whatever veggies are currently going bad in my fridge in the future, it’s very riffable.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: There are lots of ways to make this. You don't have to engage in the act of grabbing a hot pan and turning its contents upside down. Just cook the egg first then put on a plate. Lay the tortilla in the hot pan and sprinkle with cheese. Replace egg back on top, give it a minute or two to brown the tortilla, fold, and you're done. This is not complicated cooking here.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I make this Chinese-ish, sort of a breakfast bau. Add sliced scallions to the egg. Instead of cheese, I put a smear of hoisin sauce and maybe a drizzle of chili crisp. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I don’t add any fats to the pan for this or other grilled sandwiches because I like the toasted surface to be dry and crispy (I love butter and oils, just not in this instance) - it still works well - each to their own!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Geez, I'm 80 and have been making this since high school. Born and live in CA, so....I usually scramble the eggs in a separate pan and I almost always add chopped scallions, sometimes canned diced chilis. Butter the down side of a tortilla  and place on hot flat skillet. Put cheese on half, dump the eggs on,  (diced avocado !) add more cheese, fold the tortilla in half, flip to the cheese side for a minute to melt. Cut in half and serve w/ salsa or not. A squiggle of sriracha is fast instead.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: When I make this, I use only 14 grams of cheese to one egg. I always use corn tortillas rather than flour tortillas. Always tasty but even better if I also add some sort of vegetable, anything from lettuce to broccoli or eggplant.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My granddaughter love these, which she calls 'tias. I usually add a slice of deli ham or turkey before the cheese. When I flip it over, instead of folding it I let it cool briefly on a cutting board before slicing it into small squares for her.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'm going to try this with 2 halves of a pita.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is called and “egg-adilla” in my house and I top it with avocado and pico. I use small tortillas (like the mini Trader Joe’s ones) and a single egg and it is very easy to flip with a spatula.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's interesting that so many NYTC recipes call for non-stick pans when most people I know don't own them anymore.  Many people are opting out of coated pans. I used a high quality stainless pan for this and it worked perfectly.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Old cast iron flat skillet, well seasoned, works really well. If making for others, I use my cast iron pancake griddle, fits across 2 burners.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe is a go-to when I need something quick. I add some Sriracha on the eggs before adding the cheese. If I want to be fancy, I add sliced avocado too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Subbing the tortillas for matza as Passover comes to a close!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Staying at my boyfriend’s house and the only ingredients in his man fridge are quesadillas, eggs, and Mexican cheese. NYT saved me today by making this the featured recipe :) yummy and easy

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fun for a change! Added spinach.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Very easy and good. 
I used two slices of cheddar rather than grating.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For a little extra flavor I spread a thin layer of sun dried tomato pesto on the tortilla before placing it on the eggs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very good breakfast! I made a few changes: Add a few grinds of white pepper to the eggs along with the salt, and about 1 tablespoon of snipped chives. As others have suggested, I placed a small plate face-down on the eggs in the pan and (using pot-holders), flipped the eggs face-down onto the plate, then slid the eggs (tortilla-side down) back into the pan to cook. I'll make this often.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I make something (it may even be a NYT recipe, though I'm not quickly finding it here) with similar effect but I'd say easier technique—start by toasting a flour tortilla in a hot pan on just one side. Then basically follow this recipe, (toasted side up) but no need to cook after flipping, you can just flip it onto a plate. I do end up heating two pans (cast iron for the tortilla toasting, nonstick for the eggs), but only one gets dirty.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as written except used sliced American cheese and found it too cheesy. Prefer the Cooking section's Scallion Egg Wrap, which I know is a whole different direction but in case you haven't tried it, look it up! Otherwise, this was fine for breakfast though half was enough.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I just soft scramble an egg and put it on one side of a tortilla (which is in my counter).  Sprinkle on cheese, roll it up burrito style or fold in half quesadilla style, and return to pan to crisp on all sides in some additional melted butter.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We used to call these eggsadillas-quick breakfast for the boys when they were young.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Breakfast burrito in quesadilla form.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Like a breakfast burrito, just not folded the same. Could add breakfast meats or veggies to your liking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I recently discovered that a key ingredient for amazing quesadillas is queso Oaxaca (Oaxacan cheese).  If you have a Mexican grocery store nearby, I suggest you give it a try.  It may be behind the counter rather than with the prepackaged cheeses.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Like an omelette in a tortilla. I added chopped garlic chives w the cheese and topped it with avocado chunks and chopped tomato at the table.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's also a 2am favorite! basic recipe that other ingredients can be added.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Been making these for years! My daughters know them as "Eggy Cheesy Tortillas". The perfect lunch!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Just what I needed this morning

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I am totally confused by your directions. Step two refers to a skillet and a pan. I do not understand which is which or are they the same. Please elaborate

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Really easy, even the hot pan /hand flip!  It took me about 15 minutes of cooking to make 8 of them. 
I did add mayo and scallions , but I could say that about any recipe,,,

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Quesadilla are super easy to make, but I found the build process for this recipe to be fun and the results were an easy to work with and eat egg cheese sandwich.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I call these "eggadillas".  Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: When our daughter was an infant our babysitter would make this with egg, cheese and refried black beans, with corn tortillas.  Thole family ate them up! This reminds me of those quesadillas and for certain, add black beans or refried black beans, or really any other bean, smashed if you prefer, and then its more protein!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As someone whose breakfast diet is heavily dependent on egg & tortilla combinations, I am grateful to Ali Slagle for developing this ingenious technique. It is nearly impossible to keep the eggs from falling out of the tortilla any other way! (the eggs do kind of get loosey-goosey during the last bite, but stay in place much much better). Also, the proportions are good. Make sure you cook the eggs enough before you put the cheese & tortilla on, or the trick won't work).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this last night. Easy to do, flip and all!! Served with black beans and Hatch green chilis and a dolop of sour cream. Yum and satisfying!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
This works great with a single egg + smaller "soft taco" tortillas, too! 

In my large pan, I just eyeball the size of the tortilla to size my egg pour, then after adding the tortilla, I gently press the egg towards the edge of the tortilla so it's fully covered. This is also much easier to flip/handle for me. 

So far my favorite inclusion is a few frozen seasoned curly fries, cut small with my kitchen shears after a minute or so in the microwave to soften. I also like adding in hot sauce (Cholula!) for some extra flavor. 

Great with a side of grapes for a quick lunch.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: tasted the sauce as-written and it was a bit too acidic for my liking. I added a bit of soy sauce and toasted sesame oil to take it closer to a traditional bimbimbap sauce (referencing a recipe from Meera Sodha's East cookbook) and that helped.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I really liked this. The dressing was the perfect sweet-sour combo. I used 4 tablespoons of gochujang and a teaspoon of red chili flakes, and it was not too spicy. I didn’t skimp on the salt either. The veggies and tofu were nice and browned after roasting. Served over short grain brown rice and included sesame seeds and cilantro. Yum!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The dressing was really delicious. Next time I would use less vinegar and maybe add some sugar to thicken it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I loved how this recipe turned out. Served with pearl couscous. I used a mandoline to get the apple and shallot sliced very thin - having both raw adds a wonderful texture variation. I thought we had ACV in the pantry, but we did not, so I ended up just using white wine vinegar and didn't have any complaints. Also skipped the gochugaru (sorry!). I had a butternut squash on the larger side, and ended up with enough for 6 decently sized servings. Great ingredient combo!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: pretty good, but I wish I had used a little less vinegar. it was the most prominent flavor and I am a vinegar lover. grated the apple instead of slicing. I used sweet potatoes instead of squash.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Had this last night and then again for lunch as leftovers. I think it was a little better last night but maybe because the flavors were new and interesting. 

I used about 3 T gochujang and 2 t crushed red pepper. It was not spicy, so if you want spice, you might try turning it up a notch. I also only used 1/2 of a green apple in the sauce and that felt like enough. Served it over farro. I don't know that this would go into a regular rotation but maybe a once a year kind of recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This turned out really well, save for the sauce which we also found to be overly vinegary. Next time will reduce the vinegar and add sesame oil/soy sauce as suggested by other commenters. Doubled the recipe to feed family of 6 and ended up with a ton of leftovers. I also added Italian sausage, taken out of the casing and broken into pieces, on the baking sheet at the same time as the Brussels sprouts, it cooked fantastically. Ground chicken might also be great if you want a little meat in this.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I cooked 2 cups of pearled barley, only added one tablespoon gochujang and skipped the gochugaru.

The dish was OK but the flavor combination was a little bewildering. Not sure exactly how things are supposed to work together. I can imagine taking the sauce in a different direction and with the same vegetables could be a winning dish. 

Tofu, brussel sprouts and squash worked well together. Maybe a different fruit or dressing would brighten the dish or complement the heat of the gochujang better.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: A little too vinegary for me, and I love vinegar. Maybe a little soy and sesame and a small amount of sugar will help.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The dressing was alright - after halving the vinegar and adding a little sugar - it just didn’t pair well with brussel sprouts and squash and apples. I’d skip the dressing or make your own

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: a great base of a recipe but after reading the comments i put extra gochujang in the sauce, added maple syrup and toasted sesame sauce and did a really saucy bowl and it was delicious !

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: So delicious--my brussels overcooked slightly, perhaps because I put on convection, but otherwise perfect! I used seitan, as I discovered at the last moment I didn't have tofu, and it was great although again perhaps needs a bit less time. Endorse the addition of some sesame oil for extra umami and aroma, and I used persimmon vinegar. Served with my favorite organic short-grain brown rice from Massa Organics.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Just made this and overall good though I agree that sauce is too acidic   Next time would add less vinegar.   Adding soy sauce as suggested in earlier comment really helped balance out the flavors :)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Pretty average recipe.   The dressing needed a lot of work to make it taste good.    As noted by others the dressing was too acidic with a pretty one dimensional flavor profile.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Is there a reasonable substitute for gochujang? I don't want to keep so many ready-made sauces in the fridge, as I don't use them often. I'd really like to try this with crushed red pepper as a sub for the gochugaru.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: We ended up eating roasted veggies and tofu which was perfect. Before tossing them into marinade I tasted and decided not to mix them. Too much vinegar.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: 100% agree, these flavors were not gelling well. Might have been decent with just a dash of vinegar but it was so overpowering it completely killed it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written and will not make again. Way too much vinegar. We were disappointed, as we are always excited to see (and game to try) a one-pan vegetation sheet dinner with protein. Not worth the fuss for this one.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: delicious! i disagree with others who said that the vegetables don’t go with the dressing. i did add a tablespoon of maple syrup to the dressing, thx to comments that it was a little too acidic. but i would put this in the regular rotation for sure.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I have to respectfully disagree with most of the comments I've seen here. We loved this dish and didn't have any issues with the combination or the dressing, in fact, I wished I'd made more! The apple and onion added lovely freshness and the brown rice soaked up all the tangy residue. We didn't use gochugaru as we didn't have it. I also used a buttercup squash as it's what I had but defo think butternut would be better texturally. Be sure to add the sesame seeds if you have them. Very nice addition.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty! I used generous tablespoons of gochujang, not accounting for the gochugaru so it definitely had some heat but that didn’t stop us from readily spooning this in. I did sweeten the sauce a bit for our taste. Also, I air fried our tofu as I have a personal preference for crispy, chewy tofu cubes, It was an extra step worth doing. Served over some leftover farro so this was a perfect dish to use up odds and ends in the fridge.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: LOVED the dressing. So simple and good - I plan to use it for salads. This was a great dish! The tofu won't get crispy as written, so keep that in mind if softer tofu bothers you. I think next time I'll use sweet potatoes instead of butternut squash, but that's personal preference for the overall flavor profile.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @CL I used sweet potato instead of butternut squash and it was excellent!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Easy, interesting, great payoff for effort. Use the full 4 tablespoons gochujang!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Do the squash and tofu stay in the oven with the Brussels sprouts? Or do they just roast 10 minutes on their own and come out?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor!   The tofu was really spongy tho.   I would omit the tofu next time and add other veg.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I love vinegar and agree with others that this sauce was a little too vinegary. Added some honey to temper it which helped but it needed less vinegar to start. Otherwise, a straightforward and satisfying tofu bowl meal

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Followed 100%. Really yummy! Fingers crossed leftovers are delish too.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Took a pointer from other commenters and halved the vinegar. Perfect balance! Will definitely make again, perhaps with other proteins to switch things up.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Mouth-puckeringly tart! But also the description for how to cut the tofu seemed unnecessarily specific yet unclear: “3/4 inch cubes” would work. I love gochujang and will be giving this another go, but will halve the vinegar and pre-season the tofu.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I chose this to use up some winter vegetables I still had on hand. I loved it! I wouldn't recognize it as bimbimbap, which for me is all about the egg--and it's possible that actually egg would make a nice addition. What I liked was the combination of textures and bold, contrasting flavors. Contrary to one commenter, I did not find the dressing too acidic at all. I wish I had checked on the vegetables after 20 minutes, since at 25 the sprouts were quite brown, though not burnt. Glad I tried it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Unlike some comments I’m seeing, I love the tart taste of the apple cider vinegar! I add a splash of soy sauce and I crumble the tofu instead of cutting it to help it get crispier.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I hate to be that guy, but as written, this isn't a vinaigrette (and thus tastes too vinegary) because there's no oil in it. You need a bit of fat to cut the acid and help emulsify the dressing! 

Similar to Mehak, I added a drizzle of fish sauce and a few teaspoons of sesame oil. This made it feel much more balanced!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: New family favourite! My two-year-old loved this so much that in her bedtime prayers she said, “Thank you, God, for the new dinner.”

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Ashley Best review ever!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Ashley this is adorable! I think she’s a girl after my own heart.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Another great Ali Slagle sheet pan dinner that'll be going into the rotation at our house! I subbed in sweet potatoes for the squash because I find them easier to work with and my family likes them better anyway. For toppings we had cilantro, lime, red onion, and feta. I doubled the recipe for our family of 4 (2 adults, 2 elementary-aged kids) hoping for leftovers. But everyone liked it and we only had one thigh leftover, so I think we would have been hungry if I hadn't.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Kate C. I took your suggestion and used sweet potatoes. Also prepped veggies early and tossed in large bowl with spices. Spice wimp here so only one 1t. of chili powder. Added chicken after an hour. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I was attracted to the combination of butternut squash and black beans, and it was definitely a winner.  We used skinless-boneless thighs, and marinated the chicken and the vegetables in the seasoned oil for about an hour in advance before roasting. Although the tip for using boneless thighs suggested starting the vegetables first, I did everything together and both the chicken and the vegetables were perfectly done in just under 30 minutes. We garnished with cilantro, sour cream and pumpkin seeds. My one recommendation is to lightly toast the pumpkin seeds—it added a delicious crunch. Overall, a great dish that we will make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Used smoked paprika, pinch of cayenne, and a little extra cumin instead of chili powder. Also, mixed all the ingredients in a large bowl before spreading onto sheet pan, ensuring that the chicken was well coated with oil and spice. Absolutely delicious, and so easy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: NO comments yet? Made this last night, pretty easy, very good, was a hit (used less chili powder, we don't do spicy well). Curious what others have done to adjust the spice mix, but it was a hit as written (didn't need that much raw red onion at the end...)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Jack from Joisey For those that have issue with nightshades (chili powder comes from nightshades) try subbing Achiote aka Annato. It’s used in many regions of Mexico as a substitute and compliment to dried chili powder. Been using it for years in this capacity when we discovered in our household that nightshades upset and bloated our digestive system.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Jeff in HMB I am allergic to all nightshades and I love your suggestion! Always looking for new spices to substitute for nightshades. Thank you!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
@Jeff in HMB
I did not know that this spice came from the deadly nightshade. Thank you, I always love learning new things

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
Spectacular! Took 5 mins to  make on a busy weeknight and was absolutely delicious!
I used skinless boneless chicken breast and cut them in 1/3's. No onion at the end.
Served on whole grain farro
This is a winner!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @JennieTXS. Like the farro idea, will def try!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Like the marmalade one, this is excellent. I used curry powder instead of chile powder gave it more of an India taste.  So easy loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Nicole The marmalade one? Going to have to look for that!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was surprisingly delicious for how simple it was! Only downside is I used frozen butternut squash and it got really mushy. Flavors were so tasty.
