I would not use bone in next time — more trouble than it’s worth IMO. Also I added pomegranate seeds because I had some and thought it was a great addition.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Excellent return for the amount of efforts.  I used chickpeas, because that's what I had, and spices were more of a morrocan mix;, cooked the whole sliced onion, had boneless skinless chicken thighs only. No lime nor fresh avocado on hand. Did not matter, dropped dollop of guacamole and showered w cilantro. Delish. Side of steamed kale. Great leftovers as well.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Very good! Super fast and easy especially if you use pre-cut squash. And very high flavor to effort ratio. Served with cilantro, lime, avocado, and crema.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy, fast, and healthy! This is immediately on rotation at our house. I added smoked paprika, which pairs really nicely with the cumin (season your chicken thighs with it!). And I used frozen cubed butternut squash, thawed for a few minutes in the microwave. It's almost too easy! It's a full meal, but benefits from a side like the lemon-garlic kale salad: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015707-lemon-garlic-kale-salad

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Made this with boneless, skinless chicken breasts. I tossed everything into a large bowl and added the ingredients that way to ensure the chicken breasts were adequately coated with the oil.  I used frozen cubed butternut squash from the freezer section at the grocery and if you do this, I highly recommend opening the bag and dumping into a collander to allow to thaw at least slightly and drain any potential moisture before adding to the dish.  Super easy prep and tasty meal and leftovers!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @KC thank you - could only find frozen squash!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic flavor! And really easy to make. Will be going into the easy weeknight dinner rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious.  I tweaked a bit based on what I had on hand. I added Tajin, 1 minced garlic clove and smoked paprika in addition to listed spices. I used 2 boneless chicken breasts, each cut into 3 tenders. I mixed all ingredients in a bowl to marinate while my (horribly slow) oven preheated.  Baked at close to 425 for 20 minutes. Topped with cilantro, crumpled feta, sliced red onion, tzatziki, and squeeze of lemon juice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Jill B that sounds delicious with the feta and tzatziki!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was a delicious, easy meal that transforms chicken and veggies into something new and exciting. Based on what I had, I used boneless skin-on chicken breast and sweet potatoes. I put all of the red onion on the sheet pan and added some broccoli to provide green and round out the meal. The result was a delicious blend of flavors and textures. Some of the black beans got a little crispy which was a pleasant surprise. I will make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Crazy good. And easy. Probably the first time I ever had a NYTimes recipe actually take the given amount of time to prepare as written. I used boneless skinless chicken thighs and delicata squash (that's what I had), and added some very thin orange slices on top of the chicken thighs when I put them in to roast, but otherwise followed the recipe. Topped with red onion, toasted pumpkin seeds, avocado and a dollop of plain yogurt. A keeper for sure.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Mix everything in a bowl first.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I rarely comment on recipes, but this one is so exceptional, I must. I made it exactly as directed, using a tasty New Mexico chili powder and boneless thighs (pay attention to the note about using these). We purred as we ate it! I've made it multiple times since -- heaven.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @RootieBaga “purred as we ate it “ - can’t think of a more ringing endorsement!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Used boneless/skinless thighs. Put all the ingredients except the beans in a bowl to marinate for 2hrs. Placed the beans on a foil lined sheet pan then added the chicken and veggies that were marinating.  DIdn't feel it was too spicy.  I did add a bit more salt and about 1/2 tsp of smoked paprika plus saved only 1/4 of the red onion as a topper. Baked at 425 for 35min, the chicken and veggies were nicely browned.  Topped w the 1/4 onion, lime juice and cilantro. Served over jasmine rice. YUM!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: for the boneless, skinless thighs, what did you use as the equivalent amount in lbs?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was great! Very easy and filling. I made with boneless thighs and followed the recommended time. I used 1 tsp chili powder and 1tsp chipotle powder, which was a bit on the spicy side for my family. Next time I would probably do 3/4 of the onion in the oven and save the last 1/4 for a raw topping. The pepitas, lime, sour cream, and cilantro tied it all together. Will definitely add to the rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: We were amazed at the amount of flavour vs the low level of effort for this dish. Great recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I made it with sweet potatoes instead of squash and it was delicious. Soaked the onions in water for the garnish and made a lime crema to top it off.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Used split breasts instead of thighs and sweet potatoes instead of squash. Not the craziest swaps. Ran out of chili powder so I used cumin, cayenne, smoked paprika, and coriander. Didn't measure just went wild. This was SUPER easy and came together nicely. Skipped the sour cream and just went with avocado and lime juice. Would recommend! It made sweet potatoes palatable!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I’d love to try this, but I really just do not like chicken thighs so I was wondering if using boneless chicken breast would be possible without ruining the recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I made it tonight with boneless skinless chicken breasts. Worked out well for me! I think you can make it with whatever veggies and whatever protein you have handy. I mostly ate the veggies and my family ate the chicken (and veggies). Some of us wrapped it in a tortilla and I put mine on a bed of lettuce. I also doubled the spices and added some grated garlic to the chicken. Very good!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Amazing effort to taste ratio! I bought pre cut butternut squash so the onion was the only chopping I needed to do. Topped with sour cream, radishes, cilantro and lime. I found it too salty though so next time I will probably omit the kosher salt altogether (i used 3/4 tsp)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. Penzey's has a spice blend called Arizona Dreaming and I ended up using that, which I thought perfect. Doubled the recipe, had some sweet potatoes so threw those into the mix. Will make this one often.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!! Used the squash. I put 1/2 the spices and oil on the chicken and let that marinate for about an hour before cooking. Used the rest of the spices/oil on veggies. Then spread it all onto a very large pan. I baked it for 50 min because the squash wasn’t done and my husband likes his chicken really browned. Everything was cooked perfectly. The lime juice, cilantro and sour cream were a nice touch. Didn’t have any pepitas. Easy and delicious!!! Will definitely make this again!!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Subbed carrots and diced sweet potato in pace of squash - this was delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe and followed it to the letter. Delish! A real keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: The chicken crisped up beautifully. I used sweet potato and added  it and the beans 1/3 of the way into the cooking time. Even so I thought the beans were a little dry, but the sweet potatoes were done just right. I thought I had lime but I didn’t, which I think would’ve been a good addition. I topped with  avocado. The sour cream would’ve been good mixed into the vegetables instead of plopped on top.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I substituted sweet hungarian paprika for the chili powder and it still turned out really good. Although the beans were a little on the crunchy side. Next time I might bake for 15min then add beans and cook the rest of the time.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: And the recipe tip on how to adjust for boneless skinless chicken thighs was spot on. Sadly skin on thighs not as easy to find in my neck of the wood

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious. I cut the thighs up in parallel strips leaving bone in each piece. substituted achiote powder for the chile powder added .5 tsp of coriander, 1t of dried oregano. 
Next time I’ll double the amounts of spice and marinate the chicken for a few hours or overnight. Threw a few corn tortillas in the over after I removed the sheet pan while I was plating. Avocado, micro greens and lime made for perfect. This is definitely on the sheet pan rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I’m a big fan of Ali Slagle, just wondering about the roasting time in this case. Is 25-30 minutes at 425 enough to cook bone-in thighs? That’s normally about how long it takes me to cook boneless thighs. Did the listed time work for people who have tried the recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
@DanaYes it did.  The bone-in thighs I used were pretty hefty.  3 of them equaled a pound and a half (actually a little more).
They were done in 30 minutes.  I also used an actual butternut squash and cut it up and it was done perfectly in this time.  The Beans were a little mushy but had good flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: It's a simple, healthy meal, but less than stellar with extremely subtle flavors I doubt I'd repeat. Salted and seasoned chicken ahead and added smoked paprika to the mix.  Seared skin before flipping but flavors were mild at best.  Used high quality sweet potato in lieu of squash, but can't imagine that impacted dish.  Beans and onions added halfway through.  Served Avocado, sour cream, lime, sriracha cream with pomegranate seeds which helped a lot!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Oh my lord was this ever good!! Great combo of textures, colours and flavours. And best of all: dead easy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Ok- let me start this review by stating that all who know me know that I don’t ever cook chicken in my kitchen. Irrational fear of salmonella despite being an otherwise pretty accomplished cook (we all have our issues!). This recipe struck me for some reason. OMG is it sooo good!! Used avocado oil. Served over white rice with lots of lime, avocado, cilantro and a few sliced grape tomatoes (bc the color was nice in my estimation). The heat from the raw red onion and creaminess of sour cream were killer with this fabulous baked combo. Served with a crisp New Zealand  Sav Blanc. I’ve got enough for a few lunches/dinners - assuming I don’t scarf down the rest of it before it hits the fridge. YUM! Thanks NYT Cooking!  Not sure I’m ready to cave on the chicken outside of this recipe- but wow this was worth it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I used sweet potatoes instead but kept everything else pretty true to the recipe. Garnished with pepitas, avocado, lime wedges and some pomegranate seeds I had leftover from another recipe - would definitely recommend. Will be adding this to our usual rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I was unexpectedly out of cumin, so I used chili powder, Graham marsala, and curry powder. It was delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe! I’ve made it twice, first time with sweet potatoes and the second time with butternut squash. For me, sweet potatoes win -hands down. So much more flavor!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was delicious!  I added Brussels sprouts which went well with the other ingredients.  I par steamed them so they would be cooked through.  I used a mix of boneless thighs and breasts and all was done in 20 minutes.  I added smoked paprika and a pinch of cayenne pepper to the spice mix. I used most of the red onion in cooking and didn’t feel like it needed any of the cold accompaniments. Definitely a crowd pleaser and so easy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Awesome recipe!  Easy and fast to pull together.  Chock full of protein, not much heat, great mix of textures.  I followed the recipe, including the toppings, with just a few substitutions.  I didn't have black beans so I used a can of garbanzo beans,  we used Greek yogurt instead of sour cream, and I added a bit of avocado to top it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was very tasty but I found the beans which are quite small, dried out in lots of places and became hard and incredibly dry such that I had to chase these ones down with water. I shouldn't have been surprised 25mins at 425 is a searing heat.  I'll make it again (with sweet pots as others people suggested) but add the beans half way through along with some crushed garlic maybe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe! It was so good. It did take 40 min to finish, but was well worth the wait!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Simple, easy, healthy, inexpensive, delicious meal. I replaced the chili powder with smoked paprika. My beans didn’t get crispy initially so I broiled for 5 minutes and that was perfect.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: We loved this for how easy it was it make (mostly kitchen staples), how quickly it came together and it’s big flavors. Follow as written you won’t regret it. Yum!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and very easy. Only modification I made was to quickly brown the chicken thighs to ensure the skin would be crispy. Otherwise made as is. Topped with cilantro, sour cream, lime and feta - this is a keeper!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Better than expected... added some olive oil to the mix, the beans got nice and crispy in parts

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Several comments mentioned watery canned pumpkin. The canned pumpkin I used was thick and definitely not watery.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Jane-pumpkin?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: May I ask what chili pepper people used or what proportion of other spices? I buy Indian spice from the Indian grocery store and I know 2 tsps would kill my family, including myself. So when recipe author writes chili powder the brand would be helpful!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: @Nina. I believe chili powder and chili pepper are two different things. I do not find chili powder to be hot at all. I use Spicely organic chili powder but any good chili powder works. I don’t do chili pepper because I’m not good with hot.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I like chicken thighs when the meat is practically falling off the bone. 15 minutes cook time for boneless thighs seems way too short. How well cooked are the thighs?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Easy and very tasty. Wish I had some avocado with it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! If you have any around, adding a few dashes of Kashmiri chili powder makes this spicier, and also works perfectly with the flavors. Just go easy - a little goes a long way.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Oops! So excited about how good this was that I forgot to add that yes- took 18 min longer than noted in the recipe to adequately cook with bone in thighs ( use them- the flavor is great!) and I added some fresh asparagus at the last 5 min. perfect!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: One of the easiest suppers I have ever made, and absolutely delicious! The combo of roasted and raw red onion made the dish. Topped mine with some plain yogurt and a hefty squeeze of lime. Loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was easy to pull together and quick to assemble.  I used skinless boneless chicken thighs as they were on sale at the market.   I did not check the temp of chicken prior to pulling and the chicken thighs had not fully cooked in the time given in recipe.  Ended up having to mircowave to finish them which of course pulled a lot of moisture out.  Next time will use a meat theometer to avoicdthe under doness.  Bump up the spice level, very mild.  Used Greek Yogurt for sour cream great swop out

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: So easy and delicious. Only sub was a yellow onion for a red onion.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I added all the onion because I didn’t have anything else to throw over it after it was cooked. Used all the chili powder and it wasn’t too spicy, just a hint. Really nice meal. Used up different pieces of chicken, thighs,drumsticks and a thigh fillet. Was a really nice dinner served with a fresh salad.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: It was a little dry for my family's tastes but they all enjoyed the flavor and textures.  Next time I might employ some green chiles, add a second can of beans, and maybe reserve the liquid from the beans to add back later.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent! I had most of the ingredients in the house when I saw this; it became dinner that day. This will definitely be on repeat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  I will be making this on repeat, so adaptable and easy to prepare.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: quick, easy and delicious.  I made a litte extra marinade and did the chicken separately.  added some smoked paprika.  Leftovers good too!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: The veggies were SO good. Next time I make it, I will double everything but the chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I can’t get to my recipe box.  Any suggestions

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Like others I subbed in sweet potatoes. I also used half chipoltle chili powder for some extra zazz. To pump up the veggies I sauted some zuchini I needed to use up with red onions and the same spices from this recipe and they all worked together well served bowl style.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Nutritional information isn't helpful - says it's for 2-4 servings.  Which is it?  Has anyone calculated it the nutritional info for the 4 servings that it's supposed to serve?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Made this as described with the exception of subbing boneless skinless thighs. Baked for 30 minutes at 425 and it came out fantastic. I used a quick pickled red onion from another nyc times recipe and I felt like it really complimented this dish wonderfully.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: It's fabulous, and SO easy!!!  I can envision two sheet pans to feed my whole clan, and that is very doable with this recipe.  I also used a mix of butternut squash and sweet potatoes (because I LOVE them!).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Bland and boring. As easy as it was to prepare it wasn’t worth the trouble. The only interesting notes were the raw red onion, cilantro and lime added at the end. I made it as the recipe was written.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Final 5 under the broiler made all the difference. Crisped black beans was a new one for me.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: If you don't eat the skin but like to cook it, rub the marinade under the skin to make the chicken more flavorful.  Since we are a small household, I usually like the skin the first night when crispy but not as leftovers when it is soggy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Easy and delicious!  Cooked as written except I added some chopped kale (oiled and salted) to the sheet pan the last 10 minutes of cooking for some greens.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Really, really good and super easy! Made exactly as the recipe suggested. Next time, however, I think I’ll try boneless/skinless thighs just because they’re easier to eat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night and it was a winner!!!  I will most certainly be making this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
This does not disappoint!  Classic sheet pan, amazingly simple but tastes like a treat on a weeknight. 
For those with chili spice aversion (we have allergies) ground ginger and a small amount of smoked paprika provide a pleasant punch, and pairs well with lime and cilantro.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I usually try to do the recipe exactly, but for this like a lot of other commenters, I found sweet potatoes more doable for convenience and family preferences. I did also add garam masala because I end up not eating the chicken recipes that I cook (family eats) and thought extra marinade might change that. I made 2 trays with 5lbs of chicken thighs, bone in and skin on. I didn't have red onion and used shallots. The outcome was tasty even for me. I ate it wrapped in a tortilla like a burrito. I made slits in the chicken because I was worried about the spices not getting into the meat, not sure if it's needed but I wanted to be sure I found it flavored enough for myself. One thing that happened I didn't see anyone else mention, is some of the black beans dried up and got crunchy, not an issue for me but husband won't eat those but there were a good amount of black beans that didn't dry out. I also followed one the comments and mixed the spices and oil and chicken in a bowl. I probably went easy on the chilli powder too because we have heart burn issues. I can't give feedback on the recipe as is for the spices as I made some changes but the method and cook times I followed and it gives wonderful results and I am going do this nice easy, tasty and healthy recipe a lot. Thank you Ali Slagle!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I served this over Spanish rice and added roasted pepitas and fresh avocado.  i liked it, but the hubby and kids were not.  IF, I make this again, I will have to make a sauce to pour over it.  Perhaps a creamy cilantro lime sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was great - used 2 chicken thighs and half the other ingredients - + pepitas, sour cream, cilantro and pickled onions. For one - another meal for me - really delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: My husband doesn't care for onions, and I don't ordinarily eat beans. (Their texture is gross.) But we tried this recipe anyway and the combination of beans, onions, and squash was so good! The two of us ate it all in one sitting. I used garam masala in place of the cumin and boneless thighs; other than that I followed the directions exactly, including the note for boneless thighs. This will definitely go in our regular rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I’m low FODMAP. Can I use pinto beans or will they get too mushy?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: if using  boneless, skinless thighs, what is the equivalent amount in lbs?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Loved this!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Cooked this yesterday for my Book Club Crew.  Pre-seasoned the chicken lightly before as was a bit concerned that there was nothing really added to the chicken and as we know, chicken tastes of nothing if not seasoned properly.   It went down well-ish, and even with the seasoning I added the chicken was still a little bland, for my liking.  And I also thought the whole dish was a little dry?  Interested in what others think.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
Love these low bang for high buck recipes...so easy to make! Delighted that any bitterness of the squash baked away in the oven. Can't always find NYT Cooking recipe ingredients here in northern Portugal, but all were available in local grocery stores. Next time might brown the skin on the chicken first so it comes out of the oven a little crispier.

But it's definitely going into rotation at our house as other commenters have said!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Simple and tasty. I tried the boneless, skinless thighs version of the recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I love NYTIMES recipes and trust the commenters so implicitly, so I was disappointed by this recipe. It felt slightly random in ingredients, and not cohesive as a meal. Cooking it all on one pan prevented the butternut squash from crisping and led to a more soggy sheet pan than I was expecting. The teaspoons of seasonings were not strong enough to keep this dish from being overly sweet. It improved after a minute under the broiler but overall was unimpressed.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: 425°! 25-30 minutes?! Veggies were black/burnt in 15 minutes, house full of smoke. Chicken raw. Maybe I have a super hot oven but I knew I should have kept it no hotter than 375°.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious as written! Kids and adults raved about it. Would double recipe next time for my family of two adults and two kids. Used frozen cubed butternut squash which worked great. Will make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! We had extra rice so used that along with some green,s since we didn't have squash, and it turned out great. Who knew I needed crunchy black beans! They are so good. The recipe served just the two of us so I'm doubling for company tonight. We used avocado, tomatoes, plain yogurt, lime and onion for toppings. Tonight I'll use a sweet potato and some rice. This recipe is a new favorite at our house.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: chicken was totally under cooked. oh man....super gross. everything else was okay-ish.... still have a bad taste in my mouth. yikes. My gas oven has never let me down before. hope my mom and dad are okay. They're in their mid 80s. Gross

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I used sweet potato and reg potatoes.  Used white onion, instead of red and then added sliced green onion on top at the end of cooking.  Also used Gongujang paste and garlic powder with all the other seasonings, including smoked paprika.  served it with rice and or corn tortillas.  Delicious!!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Swapped sweet potatoes for the squash, just as delicious

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Meh.  Followed recipe except added more chili powder & cumin cuz I like spice.  Used scarlet runner beans from Rancho Gordo - dish was just too starchy with the squash & beans served over rice.  Longed for more than toasted pepitas (great addition), sour cream & guac to lighten the load.  And my chicken thighs didn't give off a lot of drippings for some reason.  Maybe make a light gravy of some kind?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent.  It was fragrant and tasty and easy!  I substituted cauliflower florets for the sweet potatoes to lower the carbs and it turned out great.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Solid as is.  Could easily add garlic powder, paprika, or other herbs, but not really needed. Add a nice green vinaigrette salad to this and you've got a great protein + greens combo with a ton of flavor.  Keeper for sure, and super-simple to prep.  I could see pomegranate seeds in a salad next to this really popping some sweetness next to all of the spices.  Might try that next...

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Easy, great, new favorite. Squash or sweet potato, equally good

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Something went wrong here.  The chicken came out rubbery and the squash didn't cook through.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This is so easy and delicious!!! Love it!!! On the regular dinner rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Subbed in sweet potatoes, skipped the pepitas. Wonderful served with warm, soft tortillas to scoop all the amazing flavors into. Will add more onion next time. Roasted four pounds of bone in thighs so the meat could be used in another meal but then my ratios were a little off and I had extra veggies. Turned them (and all the pan juices) into soup and topped with crushed tortillas - could have totally topped that with the pepitas. Yum (again!)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: I made this with carrots because that is what I had and it was good, but pretty dry, especially the black beans when reheated as a leftover. I wanted to keep it healthy and don’t like sour cream so I didn’t use any. Any tips to make it less dry?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody:
Made this dish with a mixture of chicken breast and bone in skin on thighs. Also cut back on chili, and added paprika. Worked perfectly!
Question for the crowd - I have vegetarians visiting next month - they would love the taste profile - what changes do I need to make this with no chicken? Add some broth? Add tofu?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I doubled the recipe and adjusted spices and oil accordingly.  But I only had one can of beans and i wish I would have added another can.  Husband loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous recipe! Perfect as is. Thanks Ali! I used Rancho Gordo New Mexican Chili Powder and it was just the right heat and flavor profile for me. Also, I just threw the pepitas right on top of the sheet pan about 5 minutes before everything was done and they came out nice and toasty. Another hint - I checked the internal temp. of the chicken thighs - America's Test Kitchen suggests that you cook thighs to 190-195  - sounds high but apparently it break down the connective tissue. Worked for me.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, this was easy, this was healthy, this was in the oven in 20 minutes on a Monday night! Add 1 tsp of oregano in addition to the spices listed here. Cut up an avocado and lightly mash with a fork while the chicken cooks, add lime juice, salt and pepper to taste to serve with the vegetables. Squeeze more lime juice on the squash and beans right before serving and top with crumbled feta cheese. My chicken needed an extra 10 minutes or so in the oven than the recipe stated.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you have leftover tomato paste, you can put it in a ziplock, roll it out, and put it in the freezer. Then you can break off whatever you need.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @AndoverDan I do something similar. I like to measure little 1 tablespoon balls, freeze on a plate, then pop in a baggie. I find the pre-measuring helpful.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @AndoverDan, I do the same thing and mark the paste into smaller squares where I only need one tablespoon—easy to drop in the chunk already divided for multiple uses

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Bridget I do this also

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe received a ringing endorsement from my 7yo who said the recipe should be named “lights” because “it’s lit.” Made some meatballs separately and served it with a side salad… will be making “lights” again!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I cut the recipe in half to (generously) serve two and added about 1/2 a finely chopped chipotle and 3/4 t of the adobo in addition to 3 oz of tomato paste.  We were very happy with the results - deeply tomato-flavored with some chipotle Smokey spice and cheesy to boot!  Excellent side to grilled pork chops, but I can see it as a satisfying main with green veg or salad.  Great comfort recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @marksmans did you adjust the amount of water in half as well?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Chris honestly, I rarely measure out water for pasta- I’ve been cooking for almost 70 years and can eyeball a sufficient amount of water and salt.  Also, I follow the Alton Brown tip to cook pasta in a wide skillet rather than a deep pot.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @marksmans thank you for the chipotle adobo tip! made this super flavorful

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The recipe was easy and pantry-friendly.  The sauce had a nice depth of flavor. Toasted panko should be added to every pasta dish!  Made the dish exactly as written except I cut it in half.  The dish was good and comforting.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I was quite surprised to read about the race to use up leftover tomato paste. For many years I have done the following...  
- Scoop out leftover tomato paste 1 tablespoon at a time
- Put the mounds of paste on a small plate with a tiny amount of space between mounds
- Freeze until solid
- Pop frozen mounds off the plate and into a small plastic bag. 
- Remove from the freezer as needed in pre-measured tablespoons

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Followed the recipe but seared 2lbs of 90/10 ground beef prior to adding at the end of the dish…oh, and mustard power in place of the cayenne. Teenage age monsters loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jeff right on!  Was thinking this would be a good hamburger helper 👊

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It is now 1:04. I read the push email with this recipe at 12:05. OMG what a great lunch on a gloomy day. Hope the leftovers reheat well!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this per the recipe. Liked the hint of heat, but it needs a little something. Thinking that adding some canned tomatoes (fire roasted diced?) would be interesting. The panko topping was a nice addition and adding it when serving ensured it remained crunchy which was a nice textural element.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @kirbic Agree. The next day we added some aged cheddar and tripled the garnished breadcrumbs and it was still lacking. I like some of the thoughts to add ground sausage, beef, etc.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @kirbic I think a béchamel would have made this a better textured Mac and cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @kirbic  Rotel works great

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Is this recipe confessing that the double-concentrated tomato paste I've been buying is really only 1.5 times the strength of regular tomato paste?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The point is to use an entire package of paste without any leftover - tubes are typically 4.5 oz, and cans are typically 6 oz. The difference in a recipe like this is negligible

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Somewhat contrary to its name, the double means it’s been concentrated a second time, not that it’s concentrated twice as much.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Bob in Tennessee
I just pop the can with left over paste in the freezer and then pop it into a small plastic bag and take out I need. No mess or dribbles of unused paste or anything to clean, not even a skinny spatula. And never any waste

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: When I open a can of tomato paste for 1 tablespoon of paste, the rest of the can is scraped into a small food container and stored in the freezer. A short time on the counter is enough of a thaw to spoon out another spoon or two. It took me until I was about 50 and had thrown out many moldy cans to learn that.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @CaroleW I just turned 38 and recently threw out the moldy remains of a can of tomato paste.  You have shown me a different way - thank you.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These comments always offer great tips, like Alton Brown's tip to use a wide skillet for cooking the pasta that marksman shared. The water would evaporate quicker, so just watch your heat level. We added spiced, ground chicken at the end for some added protein. Maybe next time, we'll use italian chicken or pork sausage for added flavour. I love how the tomato paste and butter, along with the starchy pasta water replaces the traditional roux from classic Mac n Cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could you add ground beef to this recipe to increase protein?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jdubbs would you have to do the ground beef after the first step?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sierra I would add ground beef, brown it, drain it then start with step #2!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jdubbs liking this idea! If you try it do share if good

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Definitely a comfort recipe, but I can’t figure out what it would take to make it delicious. I found it pretty bland. The tomato subdued the cheese too much. Adding a bit of balsamic definitely improved the dish, but really, if I’m going to consume that much cheese and butter, I want delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: in regards to tomato paste. I buy a large jar of the stuff. I get a large ziplock bag and throw it into the bag. I spread it out (about 3/8 - 1/2 inch thick) into a sheet inside the bag. After it's partially frozen i take it out and press a chopstick lengthwise and make squares that are partially pressed into it so i can break them off easily later.  like tictactoe squares.  Never waste another partial can of paste!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meh.  The Panko topping actually detracts and so it’s a completely unnecessary first step. I also don’t usually associate cayenne with comfort food, but I dutifully, and regrettably, went along. I’m not all that comforted right now.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Kent
I often make Julie Moskin’s creamy mac & cheese and it calls for cayenne as well; just adds a bit of a kick to the recipe (which along with the sour cream and Kirschwasser that I use gives it a nice tang…)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Kent I’m reading the comments after having made it. It’s easy, which is a plus, but otherwise disappointing. I followed the recipe exactly and wound up with a boring, runny mess. When the pasta was done, it was still way too watery. I pushed on. The hand shredded cheese turned into tiny balls. It did firm up after sitting (much longer than 3 minutes). Given the raving reviews, I’ve no doubt I messed up some basic cooking rule, but regardless, it was tasteless. Out of sheer stubbornness (and being a big fan of “easy”), I’ll probably try it again. But once I figure out the liquid aspect, I’ll definitely add more … something. 😉

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I also followed directions exactly and it was kinda soupy. Taste is ok, I’d use less water next time and add some if the protein and spicy additions mentioned here.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Freeze leftover tomato paste. I measure 1 tablespoon portions and freeze them.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made the recipe exactly as written. My husband, the real mac and cheese lover, and I found this recipe a bit bland.  The advantages of the recipe are that it is easy, has common ingredients and only requires one pot. I'd rather spend the time and make baked mac and cheese and stewed tomatoes which is far more satisfying.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Patrice Totally agree! During dinner we started talking about what could be done to enhance the flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this tonight and it was EXCELLENT. I seldom leave a recipe as it’s, but I kept my tweaks to a minimum (for me at least). Had to do it gluten free so I left off the bread crumbs (too much work tonight to create some). Added some chipotle in adobe as suggested, as well as a drained can of Ro-Tel chopped tomatoes with chilis. Cooked the tomato paste mixture at least 15 minutes in all that butter. Used slightly less than 1 pound GF elbow macaroni (the box had 400 grams) so reduced water to 5 cups. I added two cups of cooked broccoli florets before adding the cheese.  Cheese was cheddar and the grated ends of whatever else in the fridge. A few dashes of liquid Maggi (a GF version) at the end tied it all together. A definite keeper!!!!!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: No reason to waste tomato paste. When I have a partial open can, I scoop spoons onto a plate or tray, freeze them, and put them into a plastic bag in the freezer. When I need a tablespoon or two, I just take one out of the bag and add it to the pan to melt.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I think it was an Italian restaurant owner or chef who told me to reseal my unused tomato paste with a layer of olive oil, which conceals in the fridge. If the exposed part of the can is well cleaned I've never had a problem with mold. I'm not sure what a health inspector would have to say about this, but I've been doing it for years without any ill effects.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After reading some of the comments stating it was bland, I decided to add some beef base to the water instead of salt and I added some diced sun dried tomatoes. I will make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’ve been vegan-izing NYT recipes for a couple of years. Made this one with Banza chick pea pasta and kite hill chive cream cheese in place of cheddar and it was amazing. 5 stars, no notes, please don’t come for me meat and gluten eaters. I’m positive you’re right that the regular recipe is better, but the trick is, I’ll never know and I loved it!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @jDubbs, yes, I believe Hamburger Helper built a rather large business around this idea.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
OK, folks. I see some of us have had gummy, tacky cheese that sticks to our teeth. From what I'm reading on the internet we did nothing wrong, but there are a couple things we can do to NOT have that happen:

- Stir the pasta less so less starch is released into the water while cooking. 
- Don't just turn it off and add the cheese — wait a little bit (especially if you have a nice hot stove + cast iron Dutch oven like me), and add cheese gradually. High heat makes the proteins seize. 
- I haven't tried this one, but the internet says to add a little milk to help, if it happens.

Good luck!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I feel completely gaslit by this five-star rating. Bland, soupy, sad. I made as directed (and I'm a decent cook! I can't remember ever messing up a pasta!) and the cheese would not properly incorporate, much less become glossy. Avoid.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What's the magic of sharp yellow cheddar vs sharp white cheddar? Colour? Taste? Melting?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Lee color. For sure.  Only thing about cheddar is age and sharpness!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I loved that the recipe description (in the NYT) said “uses a whole can” of paste.  I recently started saving leftover paste - in single tablespoon quantities, pressed between sheets of waxed paper - in the freezer!  Works great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Ali Slagle commented in the email I received: "it feels like a race against time to use up the tomato paste before it goes bad."

Tip, scoop the tomato paste out of the can and portion out into baggies- I usually divide into fourths - and freeze. See? Easy peasy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this recipe with a can + liquid of white beans blended into the remaining amount of 6C water and added the dregs of my cheese drawer. Served with a side salad and some sausages. Amazing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Looks like a gut bomb in the best possible way. Gonna make tonight but I will use 1/2 Tillamook medium cheddar + 1/2 aged white cheddar since I have both & don’t want to go to the store for an 8oz. block of “sharp yellow cheddar.”

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Banshee I made it and just adding the cheese, you barely notice it- it mixes in without adding creamy gooey texture. doesn’t feel like a mac and cheese- so I’d add a béchamel!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so easy for a fast weeknight dinner. It was a bit boring, but fine for a dinner with kids when served with some veggies on the side. My main complaint was the mouthfeel- the cheese stuck to our teeth.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Note that tomato paste in a tube is typically way more expensive than in a can -- even if you toss out half the can.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: With all of the high ratings, I was expecting something AMAZING. This was, in my opinion, the definition of mediocre. Its fine. Its not in any way exciting, flavorful, or something I would write home about. I tasted it at the end and added more paprika, cayenne, garlic powder, and salt. And it was still boring. (and also a little watery - the pasta was cooked and there was still too much liquid) I likely won't make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was tasty but not great. Made it exactly as written; it seemed missing something. The sauce is thick so the toasted panko topping gives it a gluey texture, especially when reheating. I like the cheesy tomato base so if I make it again, I’d skip the panko, start with cooking bacon or smoked sausage, remove from pan, make the rest as directed, then stir the protein back in before serving.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
One tip for leftover tomato paste - put it in a ziploc, flatten it and freeze it. Easy to break off and throw into recipes as needed. 

I’ll try this recipe and report back!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: To keep extra tomato paste, drop it by tablespoonfuls onto wax paper, put in freezer. When frozen, put the one tablespoon globs of tomato paste into a ziplock bag and store in freezer. Take out and use one tablespoonful at a time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: TO SAVE LEFTOVER TOMATO PASTE, place the remaining paste in a sandwich size ziplock bag, flatten it with your hand, seal and freeze. When you need it for a recipe, open the bag and snap off some of the frozen paste. It’s easy to estimate the right amount.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hoping that I would see a solution to my "too much liquid" and "why did my cheese seem to curdle" but I found only one comment that seemed related.  In any case, I quite liked the recipe but would definitly make some changes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Am I the only one to feel like this is tomato soup with grilled cheese dish in a bowl? It’s easy and yummy. Not earth shaking but a sweet little dish that I’m certain the little ones will love.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I was heavy-handed with the paprika but otherwise made this as written, and it made for a tasty and cozy winter weeknight meal. The next time I make this I'll make extra panko.

When making a one-pot/skillet pasta, the amount of water required varies with the circumference of your pan; it will boil away faster or slower depending on the surface area. I recommend starting with less water and topping up as needed as your pasta cooks. After a few batches, you'll learn the right amount for your pan!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was like a so-so, bland tomato pasta and not much at all like mac & cheese. I doubled the garlic & spices and even added a chopped chipotle in adobo and it still came out kind of meh. It smelled so yummy during the tomato paste and pasta boiling steps, but the cheese I grated never really melted and though I stopped cooking the pasta well ahead of done, in the end it was just mushy. The panko crunch helped a lot, and I wound up adding some grated parmesan too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’m normally not one to make a ton of changes, but I had to use what I had. Added in cream cheese to get up to the right quantity of cheese, chipotle powder instead of cayenne, and crispy onions instead of bread crumbs. I do not regret any of my choices. Fantastic and easy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Saucy! Followed recipe exactly (even using full 6 cups water), except that i added a little pecorino with the cheddar, and a little red pepper flake for added oomph. The generous liquid made for a proper saucy sauce. Don't know about anybody else but i like it when my sauce has plenty of lip.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We loved this! It was soupy at first, but letting it sit w/lid on as instructed allowed it to thicken. I added parmesean to the panko before serving, which made it even better.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I followed the recipe.  I did not like this.  I love mac and cheese.  Thought this would be a great spin on a favorite. Nope!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: One thing I like to add to dishes like this to easily up the nutrition without significantly altering the flavor: frozen riced cauliflower. For this, I'd do it after step one. Cook the cauliflower in the butter until it's at least lightly browned and its water has evaporated, then continue with the recipe. I usually eyeball the amount of cauliflower rather than measuring, but maybe 2 cups in this?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Matt
I always add riced cauliflower when making lasagna.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: tomato paste can also be frozen...

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Until I was about 18, I thought mac and cheese was ALWAYS made with tomato sauce because that's how my mother fixed it--1 lb. elbow macaroni, 2 cans of tomato sauce or condensed tomato soup and 1 lb of sharp cheddar. Period. This version adds a few nice touches to enhance the flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Can’t wait to try this!
FYI - I have taken the extra tomato paste and put it in 1 Tbsp dollops in a pan and frozen it. When the dollops are frozen solid, I put them all together in a Ziploc bag. Then I just take out however many tablespoons I need for a recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For recipes that use less tomato paste: freeze the remaining paste in a small plastic food container with tight fitting lid. When you need to use it, either defrost it fully in the fridge, or run warm water under it. Scoop out what you need and refreeze the remaining tomato paste.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Or scoop by Tablespoon into wax paper, fold up in a packet, and put in a freezer bag.  Remove and thaw as needed tablespoon by tablespoon.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I absolutely loved it. I made it the night of a snowstorm and it was so comforting. I added a pound of good quality ground beef to make it a little more hearty and up the protein. The breadcrumbs add a lot. We had it with a side of green beans.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I ended up with a VERY salty pasta soup. The flavor, under the salt, was nice although not particularly cheesy. I used Barilla 'snowflake' shape pasta, which are a mid-sized short pasta. I used Tillamook sharp cheddar, grated it myself, and even had DK crystal salt. I put the cheese in after the pasta was cooked (with the top open to reduce liquid), quick stir, covered 3 minutes...then...Salty pasta soup. It's quick, so I REMADE it thinking I'd mis-measured. And voila! TWO POTS of salty soup!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Despite the warning not to use pre-shredded cheese, I was lazy and it was in the fridge, so I threw caution to the wind. This recipe was delicious! My only changes were the addition of ground mustard as per another commenter, and substitution of the advised-against cheese. My household is only two people so I used slightly less pasta than called for. We were still able to enjoy leftovers, heated in the oven with extra panko and cheese and dotted with butter.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this for my Girlfriend (who honors Lent no meat Fridays) It was very good, I probably  overcooked the pasta just a bit as leftovers were a little soft. Served the pasta with fish-sticks for a nostalgic Lenten Friday dinner

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly but had a meh experience and my teens voted not to make it again. The flavor is nice, particularly if you are a tomato soup fan. But for us the texture was off. Because the cheese was just melted into the saucy pasta, as opposed to making a bechamel, it left a thick unpleasant coating on our teeth.  The cheese was freshly grated from a block, not pre-shredded, and I followed the instructions.  Maybe it was our pasta brand? Glad others had a better result.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not a fan.  Just a gigantic pot of glop.  Wasted Tillamook sharp cheddar on this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: A friend described this as "like fancy Chef Boyardee" (possibly because I used orecchiette), which is 100% delightfully accurate. I didn't have smoked paprika so added a pinch of cumin for more depth, and also some steamed kale as a nod to virtue. 10/10

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After reading lots of comments about how this was only "meh," I followed some suggestions, and it turned out great! I used some veg broth I had made, with a 1/2 tsp of beef-flavored "better than bouillion," and doubled the spices, used only 4 C. of water, and added some spinach at the end. It turned out yummy, and I do think the addtion of some sausage would up the game. Quick, tasty, and comforting. I used Tillamook sharp cheddar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After sifting through all the comments about how to freeze tomato paste, this turned out really good! Added a chopped up chipotle to the tomato paste mixture and some garlic powder to the panko. I only added 5 cups of water and it came out perfectly. Will definitely be making this again!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meh.  There wasn't a lot of flavor to this dish.  It was easy enough to prepare and a little reminiscent of spaghetti oh's but there was just little to no flavor.  I will not make again.  Wasn't really worth the effort to improve in my opinion.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I rated a three because I wish I’d added less water to begin with.  I had read the recipe and it said you may need to add extra water but it didn’t say you may need less.  Consistency was not what we would have wanted ideally.  But flavor was great.  Fun recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Based on the soup comments, I started with just 4 cups water and ended up adding one more during cooking, for just 5 altogether. The cheese incorporated beautifully. I didn’t have garlic cloves so had to sub garlic powder. It was good, my 8 year old liked it, and there’s plenty of room for improvisation to jazz it up. Keeping this one!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Use only 3 cups of water for the pasta if using small elbow macaroni. I did this and it was immeasurably better than the first time I made this with the full 6 cups. I turned off the heat before al dente, and they steamed with the cheese step to perfect texture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wish I had read from all comments and not just most helpful. Then I might have added less water, and gotten less sticky cheese soup, and had less of an enormous pot of mediocre leftovers. Tomorrow I may double down and brown some ground beef to add in, plus more seasoning. Of any kind.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very tasty, but certainly less cheesy than a typical mac’n’cheese dish. It’s more like a very cheesy, tangy, rich marinara with very little seasoning. I consider a good base for all sorts of other ingredients. Add some meat and veggies to make it a meal, season it with different spices and herbs to change its profile, add extra cheeses to make it richer and more complex. Possibly it could be a more typical mac’n’cheese if the tomato paste was replaced with onions or shallots.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I haven’t eaten macaroni and cheese for about 60 years, but this recipe sounded interesting. It definitely counts as comfort food. I put my serving over some kale, so it didn’t feel so degenerate.  It’s pretty good, but I added a little freshly grated parm and would have liked a little more heat….

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this as written and I ended up adding 2 slices of American cheese to the sauce (for the sodium citrate) and letting it sit and melt, covered, for a couple more minutes, since the sauce was looking a little grainy and I was afraid that the kiddo would protest. I think this helped and didn't drastically alter the flavor, if this happens to you. I think I'd also add a pinch of MSG next time since I felt like it was lacking a little depth. Overall very good, quick, and pantry friendly.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I found this rather bland, despite using aged cheddar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you’re going into this expecting gooey cheesy mac, you’ll be disappointed. I LOVE mac and cheese; it is my number one comfort food and I have a lot of opinions about it. If I went into this blind without having read comments I might have been disappointed. But I went into it expecting “it’s like tomato soup and grilled cheese as a pasta” as someone else commented and I would say that’s pretty dead on. The bread crumbs are a MUST; they really bring it all together. Make sure you’re using a salty cheddar because despite cooking in its own salted pasta water, I had to adjust salt at the end. I also think your consistency is going to heavily depend on the type of pot you use and evaporation rate, but this does not become a stretchy gooey dish. It really does end up saucy and its own thing. We used cavatappi because structurally I find it has the biggest range of tolerance for overcooking compared to elbows or fusilli. This actually reheated surprisingly well as leftovers the next day; just added a little melty cheese on top to bring a little life back to it and more bread crumbs again when serving.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Want to make this for a party. Can I make a day ahead and reheat?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I think this would be good with a can of Rotel or chopped tomatoes and roasted peppers. I'm going to experiment with it a bit!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I freeze tomato paste when I don't need the whole can. Dollop a tablespoon at a time on a flat surface, stick it in the freezer. When frozen wrap individual pieces and store in an airtight container. Handy to have at the ready.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I store leftover tomato paste in 1-tablespoon portions wrapped well, in the freezer. Then when a recipe calls for just one tablespoon, I remove just one blob and toss it in the pot. It takes very little time to melt. Voila.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Maybe everybody already knows this but I now keep a little (4x6) container in the freezer and plop extra tomato paste by tablespoons.  Once in a while I have to open a fresh can to keep it full.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So, you have some left over tomato paste from a partially used can?  Just spoon it into a quart sized zip top freezer bag, flatten to remove air, and freeze flat and thin.  You and really break off a piece of any size when called for in your next recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone tried to reheat leftovers in a microwave?  As there's no milk or cream like in regular mac and cheese, the sauce shouldn't break when reheated...?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
This is teenager firepower. Big grins and nods from my boys when they saw the pot.

I  cooked 1-1/4 lbs of ground turkey in the butter in step one and increased spices accordingly (eyeball). I had a bag of frozen broccoli florets that had been languishing, so I threw those in at the end after the cheese was melted. It needed more salt, but otherwise a total win. I used shell pasta.

Re: mouthfeel. I run into that when topping chili with cheese shreds, but here it was no problem—I think the starchy pasta water acts like a roux and evens it all out.

Oh and since it seems to be a pre-req for comments on this one, my method on tomato paste leftovers is similar to another poster:
Plop the remainder into a sandwich size ziplock, squeeze down to the shape of a roll of quarters and freeze. You can slice off as many quarters as you need.  They melt fast in the pan but keep your hands clean.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The tomato flavor overpowered the cheese flavor, even though I used extra sharp cheddar. It was tasty but I missed the cheesiness of Mac & Cheese. I do like the technique of building the sauce out of the cooking water... will experiment with that!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Three cloves of garlic & a pinch of cayenne sounds bland for 1 lb. of pasta, a 1/2 lb. of cheddar & a full can/tube of tomato paste.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this last night according to the recipe and it was delicious! Hubby added hot sauce to his and I have a list of things to try next time (not because it needs it, but because it seems so versatile). I microwaved a bowlful for lunch today, and it was even better second time around.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Thought the basic recipe was uninteresting, but quick to prepare. Added some chili crisp to liven it up some.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Loved! Followed the recipe, but added home-smoked, shredded chicken breast at the final stir for protein, and some dried basil as garnish (because winter). Yummy. Think we’ll add a bit of freshly ground pepper and maybe a little freshly grated Parmesan next time for garnish too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I also halved the recipe for the two of us.  I did half the amount of water and added some Calabrian chili paste that I had on hand in place of the cayenne. Nice one pot upgraded mac and cheese. Will make again!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
As my 20yo son would say, "this slaps!"
Made it for my 64th birthday; I would say this was a wonderful treat! Will definitely make it again before my 65th.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Don't buy tomato paste in cans, go for the tubes!  It lasts almost forever, and no need to roll out the paste in ziplocs or on wax paper and freeze and whatnot.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very nice and I liked trying out the one-pot method for mac 'n cheese. I made half. At high elevation I needed all of the water since the pasta takes about 50% longer to get tender. Down at sea level I might start with less than called for and add the rest as needed. I used 1 tablespoon of gochujang paste (Trader Joe's) instead of cayenne which gave it just the right amount of spiciness with a little extra depth. I also added a handful of grated carrots 3-4 minutes before the pasta was done.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Based on some other comments, I added 1/2 t chipotle powder, 1 can of fire toaster tomatoes and reduced the water by 1 cup. Also serves some meatballs on the side. Delish!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I was hungry last night and stumbled upon this recipe, and thought, 'I bet I have everything I need to make this.' Well, I didn't. I was missing the tomato paste -- the key ingredient. I did have a can of Rotel Tomatoes and Green Chiles. So I cut the recipe in half and subbed the can of Rotel tomatoes for the paste and the cayenne. I cooked and smashed it until it cooked down somewhat. Then followed the recipe as is. It was delightful, fast, easy and tasty. My son loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Browned a pound of lean ground beet after cooking the tomato paste and it was perfect.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Trust me, do not worry about tasting for salt and cayenne….just add a hefty drizzle of sriracha before topping with the panko. It was perfection!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wanted to like this, especially cooking in a tomato paste water, but the flavor doesn’t really bloom. The cheese at the end gets completely lost, and it’s hard to call this a Mac and cheese. If I were to make Asian, I’d make a béchamel, mix in the cheese and and toss the macaroni in that at the end. I’d also add a lot more cayenne!!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: A tip for leftover tomato paste - freeze it! I buy 14oz jars of paste. When I open a jar, I freeze the leftovers. Line a medium baking sheet with grease-proof paper. Dollop tablespoons of paste onto the tray and freeze for a couple of hours.  When hard, remove the paste mounds and transfer to a freezer zip-lock. All my recipes use tomato paste by the tablespoon, but it's easy enough to cut one in half if I'm making a smaller recipe.  They will keep for several weeks in the freezer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I love this recipe. I’ve always wanted to use a whole can of tomato paste (because I wondered what would happen!), so this allowed me to do that—and the result was delicious. It’s terrific with or without the breadcrumbs, depending on your mood. And it’s perfect on a snowy day. This is something I’ll make again and again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It was very rich, and I would cut back a little on the Tomato Paste next time around.  It is however, pure comfort food.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used an extra sharp white cheddar that I had on hand and the sauce broke. It was still delicious, but because it didn't look that great, I saved it for work lunches instead of giving it to the kids with dinner (poor me). Next week I will make it using the sharp yellow cheddar as specified!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Between the breadcrumbs and spices step I browned sliced kielbasa and onions in the pot and set them aside until after the cheese was added. Did a green salad with some fried garlic. Perfection.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used this recipe to fancy up a humble box of instant mac and cheese that I had in the pantry, and, wow, did it ever make that little box into a star!  I made a couple minor changes (4 cups of water instead of 6 (less pasta, so less water), and no garlic or dried red chili (I can't eat them), used the Indian spice "hing"  (asafoetida) and a mushroom umami seasoning instead), and had the most delicious, fanciest little box of Instant Mac and Cheese you've ever seen. This recipe will be on forever rotation in my kitchen!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hmmmm, maybe British tomato paste and cheddar are different.  This tasted mostly of tomato paste, even though I followed the recipe. Usually love NYT Cooking recipes but it's a pass on this one for us.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As someone who grew up happily eating hamburger helper because it meant my mom didn't burn something, this was yummy! Had to be gluten-free, so used Kikkoman GF panko, Jovial GF elbows (12 oz box), 5.5 cups water, and added a can of diced tomatoes, about a pound of browned carne picada (diced beef), plus an extra cup of cheese (3 total). I cooked it in a wide pan as per other suggestions, to excellent effect - definitely needed most of the water this way. Tangy and creamy! A hit with everyone in the house :-)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Thank goodness I only made half the recipe!
I am an experienced and pretty successful cook but must agree with those who found this very bland. I am not a fan of very spicy food,but this was a bummer! I was very hungry after working late so I ate some; don't know what to do to make the leftovers palatable.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My toddler (and spouse) loved this. I added ground beef, upped the garlic, added mustard powder, omitted the cayenne (because toddler). Oh and I burned the breadcrumbs, so didn’t use those, but it was great without.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Someone define “stewy” for me. I reduced down the water to what I thought was an acceptable level,  but I think there was too much as after adding the cheddar it really lacked in flavor. It was good, just bland and I can’t decide if it’s because i didn’t simmer off enough of the water.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delicious on the first day.  However, the pasta absorbed the sauce overnight, and the leftovers were disappointing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yes, definitely on par with SpaghettiOs... if you are craving the taste of canned pasta and are too lazy to run home to your mom's house like that Italian kid in the old TV commercials, then make this recipe. Horribly bland. What a waste of good pantry ingredients! After a few bites, I ended up dumping mine in the trash.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Made this last night…a big hit with my carnivore loving spouse. The only change I made was I used broth instead of water. The panko bread crumbs made this a keeper..so easy (except for shredding the cheddar cheese).
Next time I will increase the garlic and smoked paprika

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After reading the reviews and a friend also remarking on it being a little bland, I purposely over seasoned and added ground beef! It’s delicious! This will be a family go to this entire busy weekend and hopefully moving forward!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as directed.  We found it flavorless. Even after adding more paprika.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this dish as written, used 1 tsp smoked paprika and 1/3 tsp cayenne pepper. Loved that it was easy ad delicious. Thank you for sharing!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I mean this in the best way possible: this is adult spaghettioes

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delicious, will be making it again. Can’t beat an easy yet delicious recipe! I loved the smoked paprika and tomato with cheddar, and the toasted panko on top was a nice touch. It felt like a sophisticated mac and cheese!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: definitely not bad but not outstanding. I had to add quite a bit of salt and shredded parm to make it more flavorful. the toasted panko is lowkey genius and something I will prepare for more pasta dishes from now on

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Was okay. I can’t see me making this again. There are better quick pasta recipes on NYT cooking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This really is an incredible dish, but definitely use only 4 1/2 cups of water and cook pasta for a minute LESS than al dente

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The tomato took away from the cheese-iness and there is A LOT of cheese and calories in this dish to not taste good enough. Would not recommend.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I altered the recipe a bit, so I won't rate it, but commenting with my don't do suggestions. I added maybe 3/4 cup pureed sweet potato to add more veggies,  but I don't recommend that. I also found the garlic was a bit off in a Mac and cheese. I used extra old white cheddar that I had and it just didn't incorporate well. stayed separated from sauce in a sort of split way. So yeah, maybe try exactly as written first lol.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Sometimes you just need a comforting, easy meal. This one ticks all the boxes. If the description speaks to you, just make this dish. Oh, and I roasted some broccoli on the side to make it feel more...ahem...healthy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was a nice one to mix it up a tad but not throw off the kids. I wanted more flavor so needed extra salt and red pepper flakes at the end. But the toasty tomato was nice.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this for lunch today and it really hit the spot! Unfortunately, I have celiac disease, so I had to make some adjustments. I used one 12 ounce box of Barilla gluten free elbows and gluten free panko. Since the amount of pasta was less than called for, I reduced the liquid to 4 cups (and I used a carton of chicken stock instead of water, at that). I reduced the amount of shredded Cheddar to about 5-6 ounces, too. It came out perfectly!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is lwky so gd. My lil brother loved it! But i added extra bread crumbs on the top and it tasted peak

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Did not do anything for me. I thought it was quite bland. Give me good old regular cheesy Mac any day.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was pretty good. Flavor profile is tomato soup with cheesy mac. The toasted bread crumbs make the dish. I like other suggestions of adding a protein or chopped up chili adobo. For more kid friendly you could even chop up hot dogs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
It’s good, solid 4 star
Next time I make it, bacon crumbled up is being tossed in

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I never comment but am involved in this community. Made the recipe as printed. Video is very inspiring. Added pre- sautéed Italian chicken sausage to the end with the panko. First 3 bites were delicious. Then something about the texture? Was it the Trader Joe’s sharp cheddar? It didnt feel as smooth. 3/4 of the people at the table cleaned their plates. Flavor was good but missing something. Should I use broth instead of water? So much to learn.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Both my kids took a bite and said "yum,, this is so good!!" Thanks for an easy weeknight meal that two picky eaters loved!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This reminds me of an elevated take on my Mom’s Mac n Cheese, which was a box of macaroni cooked, 2 cans of tomato juice and a block of cheddar mix together in a pot. Top with extra cheddar and bake in the oven.  All the kids always fought over the crispy top.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so delicious. Like super high class Spaghetti-O's. For some reason the 6 cups of water was a bit too much for my 1 lb of macaroni though, was soupy and therefore the cheese got clumpy. The only thing I changed was making my own bread crumbs from whole wheat sourdough, they were so delish and added a great texture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Other than the cooking times being underestimated (to be fair, we had doubled the recipe), this was a huge hit in our home. (Might toss in some meatballs next time.)

The only vaguely negative comment I received was that next time we make this, to please use something other than elbow macaroni that will hold onto the "amazing" sauce better.  (I ordered a couple of boxes of rotini pasta for our next go-round.)

Served this with Air Fryer Green Beans, which our whole family loves.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Kind of bland. I didn't really like it. No standout flavor, even the 8 oz of cheddar didn't make a statement. I added meatballs, so that helped a bit.

author: Ali Slagle
