commentBody: This was just okay, I thought it would be better. The tomato paste pretty much overpowered any sharpness of the cheese. It felt like it was missing something- had the leftovers with sausages which was better, but I still wouldn't make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hey Ali...you can buy tomato paste in a tube which allows you to use what you need and keeps in the fridge for quite awhile therefore not wasting any.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you can't use the contents of your container of tomato paste in a single recipe, you can freeze the leftover portion in an ice cube tray.  one cube = about 1 Tbsp of product.  I tend to buy tomato paste in larger than 6 oz containers and freeze it - very handy for recipes that only use a small amount of tomato paste.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great dish!!.  Doesn't really solve the leftover tomato paste problem.  Tube tomato paste is too expensive.  Freezing is the answer.  In tablespoons (freeze on a tray or in an ice cube tray, then wrap). I do a no-no but it works for  me: in the can, top sealed with plastic wrap, then foil. Depending on how much is needed, I scrape it.  Bad home economics I know

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I will make this, and I will absolutely use the *pre-shredded* sharp Cheddar cheese from Aldi, which melts wonderfully well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My daughters got us using a zip lock plastic bag to freeze whatever remains from a partially used can of tomato paste. It works well, still I will try this recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Does anyone have advice on making this ahead? I’m thinking of a double or triple batch to take the next time I could for our family shelter.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can you make this ahead? How is it leftover?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What do you do (or add ) the scrapings (from Step 1) back into the mix?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: See the last sentence of Step 5. It's the breadcrumb topping.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For those that cut the recipe in half, how many tablespoons of tomato paste did you use? I have frozen tbsps of paste in my freezer that I could use up...

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really good - Also great with the addition of cannellini beans and made with Bionaturae sourdough pasta!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I freeze my unused tomato past can and soften it in hot water bath prior to reusing.
Also substitute olive oil for butter (or with butter).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any thoughts on what type of gluten-free pasta might work best? Thinking one of the corn/quinoa type, maybe with a supplement of potato or tapioca starch to thicken.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
So just read the recipe and then made it. Scrapped what I was going to make. 
Changes I made: cooked ground Turkey with chopped onions. Drained. After step 1, then continued. Added a can of fire roasted tomatoes when I added meat back in. After step 2. 
Used white cheddar and TJs Quattro Formaggio  

The panko breadcrumbs are what make the dish delish.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I didn't have elbows, so I tried bow-ties.  They must not absorb as much water.  The taste was good, but it was a bit soupy.  I will try again with elbows next time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Would this freeze well? I've never stored anything Mac & Cheese adjacent that way. If not, I'll just make a half-portion.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with “veggie pasta” that is made with pea flour, lentil flour and cauliflower flour? I imagine it would work the same as regular pasta?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was lovely but I had to pour off at least a cup of liquid after the pasta was finished cooking. What did I do wrong?? Too small of a pot possibly so not enough water evaporated?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Kate I had the same experience. I used rotini noodles and a large pot.  I thought that in my case, the type of noodle could have had something to do with it.  I am not sure about the small pot vs. large pot.   I am curious to hear other thoughts on this too

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Foolproof deliciousness. I improvised with a tiny bit of leftover tomato paste and a handful of leftover penne pasta, Italian seasoned breadcrumbs in the absence of Panko, and overlooked the paprika...and it was still an amazing, easy meatless meal. Can't imagine how good it will taste when I follow the directions.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So easy and yummy! The large amount makes for great leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This dish was delicious I made it with GF corn pasta for my granddaughter who is gluten intolerant. She loved it - I cut the recipe in half - it worked out fine. I love the breadcrumbs. Thank you NYT Cooking!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made tonight and loved the taste, but the 6 cups of water was way too much for me. It didn’t reduce enough and was essentially noodle soup, so I had to drain about half of it to be closer to mac and cheese consistency I was hoping for. I added Calabrian chili instead of cayenne and served with meatballs. Super tasty and I’ll make again with less water!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Decent,  but a lot of hands on attention. Regular Mac and Cheese with stewed tomatoes on the side are easier and just as tasty.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As other reviewers have mentioned, this was very easy but somewhat bland. I even added some minced salami and sauteed diced onion and bell pepper. Not sure what else it needs to add depth. An aged cheese like asiago?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We did this gluten free easily with banza brown rice elbows (we usually like jovial but the banza are a better size for this). No adjustments other than making it about 2/3 the recipe on acct of the 10oz pasta packet. Added cooked ground meat as a topping for the non vegetarian.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is basically what you wish hamburger helper was, with just slightly more effort!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you hate those cans of tomato paste, buy it in a tube. It stays fresh and you will find all kinds of ways to use just a little, like in salad dressing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We had this for dinner tonight along with a mixed green salad. It wad edible but I won't be making it again. Or saving the leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this exactly as written and it was the perfect easy comfort food I was craving! My husband and daughter also loved it and we are looking forward to the leftovers today. Going to pop it in the oven like a baked mac & cheese

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe ended up needing a bit of tweaking for us - added A LOT more salt and an extra brick of cheese to get it where it needed to be. We added ground beef + onion for our own personal preferences.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This fit the bill for us! Cooked the recipe exactly. Simple. Great food for hunkering down in a blizzard.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was good but lacking something. I'm thinking some onion or onion powder would give it a little more flavor. Maybe a can of crushed tomatoes? Some chili powder?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I cooked the full amount crumbs and the tomato paste mixture.  I put half the tomato mixture in the freezer and continued at step 3 with half the amounts specified.  That amount was enough for 4-5 small appetites.  
  Next time I'd use olive oil in place of butter to cook the tomato paste and I'd cook the garlic in the oil for 30 seconds before adding the other ingredients.  I'd also consider trying gorgonzola in place of cheddar and adding fresh basil or fresh spinach with the cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was the perfect comfort food for a winter day. I threw in some chopped broccoli about 4 minutes out from the pasta being done, and I used crumbled olive oil and rosemary bruschettini (https://asturifoods.com/our-products/bruschettini-rosemary-olive-oil/) instead of the panko breadcrumbs since my breadcrumbs smelled stale. The bruschettini worked well and I think I'll just stick with them in the future to save a step.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly. I've been looking for a pasta recipe with adjustable quantities of spices as my spouse had digestive issues. This worked perfectly. I followed the recipe exactly other than omitting the garlic and skipping the cayenne (I added a little powdered mustard and pinches of onion and garlic powders); I also used smoked paprika. The pasta was pleasantly al dente and the sauce was creamy. I topped mine with very garlicky and spicy black beans; the spouse got chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fantastic. I actually cooked a lb of chorizo sausage in the pan, drained it, removed from pan then added the garlic and spices to the "oil" left behind.  I left out the cayenne due to the heat in the sausage.  I only added in 1/3 of the sausage back to the skillet, with the pasta and water.  I would add less water next time, as I do prefer more al dente pasta. ( I'd start with 5 cups) I used a mix of sharp cheddar, mozzarella, jack cheese and a sprinkle of parmesan.  Adding to the weeknight mix!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Gotta say, I followed the recipe TO THE LETTER, and there was way too much water .... very soupy and uninspiring. Really hated this, as did my grandchildren and grown children. I have to confess, I'm baffled by the kudos here.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Watery slop

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So delicious!  I cut the recipe in half, reducing the butter a bit and adding browned ground beef at the end. I also threw in little chipotle powder.  Otherwise, cooked as written and loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy and good.  But I used 2 cups cheese without paying attention to the ounces.  I think it needs more cheese than 2 cups.  and a little salt, although more cheese might have fixed that.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Super good kids loved it. I used medium white cheddar because that's what I had on hand, but next time will be sure to use sharp.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Does it matter if you dont use YELLOW cheddar?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Owen Andean Dream Quinoa pasta, either the fusilli, or the turmeric penne!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: All 4 of my young adult kids and I enjoyed this!  Instead of cayenne I used 1 TBSP of adobo with one diced chipotle pepper, added just a hint of smokiness without too much heat.  I added 1.5 LB of a hamburger/mild Italian sausage mix (cooked) after step 4.  The elbow pasta took ALL of the suggested water plus a bit.  (I did sub 8 oz tomato juice for 8 oz water)  I dusted my store-shredded cheese with a bit of flour and it did not clump.  This absolutely going into the rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is delicious AND it must be noted, is like an adult sophisticated version of Spaghetti O’s!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I think more cheese makes it better.  I didn't add salt and the kids thought it was too salty already.  (I don't add salt to most recipes so it's definitely just our household.) They both said it tasted more like lasagna than Mac n cheese.  They both wanted more black pepper.
I enjoyed making this but don't think I will add it to our regular rotation.
The roasted broccoli that came with this dish was amazing.  The kids liked it better than the steamed I usually make.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I found it bland as well. I have not decided if I can will make it again. The texture from the breadcrumbs was very nice. It needs umami. @Mary

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After reading other comments, I added a can of rotel and some chipotles in adobo sauce. I also added 3 links of spicy Italian sausage, 7 cloves of garlic, and Better Than Bouillon beef and there were no regrets. Excellent, easy dinner!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have thrown away SO MUCH tomato paste over the years! I'm so glad I read the comments here for the tips to reduce the waste, and at the same time embarrassed I didn't think of them myself.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy and delicious meal!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meh. The best thing about it was the panko.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Definitely a pantry friendly recipe. Made exactly according to instructions and it is was good. Not great, but about what I expected. And easy. As others noted, adding greens and/or sausage would take it up a level.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So easy and oh so good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: flavor was OK. But preparation was ridiculously hands-on for something that’s billed as a one pot meal. In my experience, the amount of pot watching and stirring required to ensure the pasta didn’t stick to the bottom was pretty much constant monitoring. Even so, I’m now dreading a pan that’s going to need a lot of hearty scrubbing due to the pasta sticking to the bottom. Would’ve been much better just to make the sauce in one pot, separately boil the pasta and bring everything together at the end. This will not be going on a “keeper“ list.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My kids and husband liked this dish. I did not.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was a very easy weeknight meal. Per the comments, I did add about 2 tablespoons of chipotle in adobo and thought it was a nice touch. Overall, the meal felt like it was missing something. Perhaps adding some ground beef would have helped or even serving this as a side with a more substantial main. Easily made 6 servings- put the leftovers in a metal tin so I can freeze it and pop it in the oven another in a busy night.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as instructed. Lacked depth of flavor. Pretty bland. My family was disappointed. Needed something.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For anyone new to cooking, it is NOT recommended to use a metal spoon when cooking with a Dutch oven as it can wear off the enamel.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is like a 5-rung step up from Chez Boyardee and a new staple if I'm looking to eat nicely without the expense.  Will try with meatballs next time.  Classic 5-star recipe right here.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It was good but needed something. I used a lot of hot sauce :) It was also better cold the following day.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not a fan.  Pros: Makes a lot.  Cons: You don't want a lot.  Can't think of anything to add to make it better.  Glop.  Next....

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Another perfect recipe from Ali Slagle.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy to cook, recommend

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Made for the first time, but based on the recurring comments about blandness, I took some prophylactic measures:
-Doubled the garlic
-Heaping tablespoon of gochujang with the tomato paste
-50/50 mix of Tillamook cheddar and smoked young gouda
End result was VERY good, not bland at all, plenty depth of flavor and umami. Would add a little less water next time and treat the pasta step more like a risotto preparation. Also added shredded sous-vide chicken breast for pRoTEin, by spousal request.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'm Italian, so cheesy pasta is definitely a "delicate topic", LOL! This is a decent Mac & Cheese for Americans, BUT even for a less fussy pasta palate, I'd suggest (as did my husband) adding more cheddar. Perhaps up it to 12 oz? Also, we found that -- typical Italian addition -- some grated Parmigiano/Parmesan added a nice touch to the mix of flavors. It was a tasty & warming Sunday lunch on a cold day after some hard work!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’m making this easy and tasty recipe for the 2nd time. My only variation is that I use a 6 ounce can of tomato paste.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This dish comes together quickly with pantry staples. It would please finicky kid eaters and most people. My only gripe was how difficult it was to get the pot clean after. I added 5 cut-up all-beef hot dogs. My men-folk wolfed it down!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Added bulk Italian sausage, which I sautéed separately and drained, before stirring in cheese. Was yummy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I loved this! the starchy water helped create a thin cheese glaze, that was very light and absolutely yummy as heck.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Anna I had a similar issue. I don't think I had too much liquid (it was rather perfect in that sense), but my cheese kind of curdled. It was goopy, but not liquidy — the kind of goopy that sticks to your teeth. The flavours in the recipe were good, but I might try next time to make a separate cheese sauce and add the tomato-garlic base to it, and the pasta to that. It takes away from the "one-pot"-ness, but so it goes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was great! I used less water, around 5 cups. I also used habanero powder instead of cayenne. We ate it with grilled chicken thighs and slaw. Looking forward to the leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you throw in some ground beef and taco seasoning, this becomes taco mac!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Excellent technique and easy to customize. It can be made ahead up to adding the cheese.  Consider adding hot sauce, milk and something herbal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My three-year-old (the person I really made this for) loved it! Would recommend this to other parents trying to get their finicky toddlers to eat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Love this but I’m not a big fan of tomato paste so would marina work?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My kids said “this isn’t good… it’s great!” And it is! My one note is that the meatballs fell apart in the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Justine
As did mine. I thought it was may have been because I used a whole pound of spinach. The sauce was too monotone made with just garlic;ic and tomatoes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The egg does help, but it seems to me that coarse, crispy panko crumbs would increase the chance of the meatball cracking, since they crunch and fracture. Finer breadcrumb particles would integrate better. And if you add a bit of milk to the crumbs before adding to the meat, or even try a hack I read and now always employ for salisbury steak; use a box grater over the breadcrumbs on a plate, and add the onion by way of finely grating it and its juice into the crumbs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Sometimes a little overworking of the mix is beneficial. There's a sweet spot where they're still tender but cohesive enough to stay intact. A slow simmer with very minimal agitation helps them to set up, then you can stir a bit more. Baking until done works, too. Then transfer to the sauce to simmer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are fabulous. Added some red wine, more garlic and more basil to the tomato sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I sometimes use fresh tofu (I grind it with a potato masher) to stretch both meatballs and meatloaf. No one can tell it's there, but unlike the spinach, it has a binding effect.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can ground chicken or turkey be used instead of beef or pork?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@David B
Why not? It may be slightly drier but with the spinach that shouldn’t be a problem (I am not a professional cook)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @David B Rachael Ray has a great Florentine Meatball recipe that uses ground Turkey.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fresh organic spinach is better. I have been using 1 1/2 cups of chopped spinach for every 2 pounds of meat for decades

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Thomas
Here’s AI’s take on it: Frozen spinach is generally considered better for cooking due to its high nutrient retention, convenience, and cost-effectiveness, often containing more iron, folate, and fiber per serving than fresh. Because it is frozen at peak maturity, it avoids the rapid nutrient degradation fresh spinach experiences during transport.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Thomas so right now I have fresh not frozen spinach. My question is how fine did you chop the fresh. Thanks

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'm still going to use fresh spinach. It is more convenient for me than thawing and  squeezing the frozen.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have had that problem with leaky spinach and they fell apart so I used a bit more binding the next time in the meat. Also squeezing the spinach to death of course. Make the spinach mixture w a bit more cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Spinach and meatball connect I place thawed spinach in metal colander and press hard with a large bowl. Then wrap in paper towels and dishtowel and roll to the point I sometimes have to pick apart as it’s so dry.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Reviewers are complaining about the meatballs falling apart. I'm not surprised at all. Skip the liquid. I was dumbfounded when I read that a 1/2 c of spinach water is add to just one pound of (fatty) meat. Holy mushy meatballs!! Yuck. Also, cut the panko to 1/2 c. A full pkg of frozen spinach + 3/4 c bread crumbs = too much filler.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: any thoughts on using ground turkey or chicken here?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Michelle Great idea!  I have come to prefer turkey in meatloaf and meatballs.  Sometimes on its own, sometimes in a mixture with other ground meat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Michelle I was thinking the same thing, but worry they will be too dry. Please post back if you try it and let me know how they turned out!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: That was my first thought! My husband is supposed to avoid red meat, so I'll use a pound of ground chicken along with the pound of ground pork and halve the exposure.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Why hasn't someone invented a frozen spinach that does NOT have to be squeezed???

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Max is this a serious question? Frozen spinach is minimally processed and highly nutritious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not sure how the nutrition reading for this one ended up with nearly your entire day's supply of sodium in one serving... looking at the ingredients it shouldn't be nearly that bad. I've come to believe that the NYT nutrition estimates are way off, unfortunately. Anyway, this should be easy to make as low-sodium, just look for canned tomatoes with no salt added, because the rest of the ingredients look pretty sodium-friendly.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @trinkd i’ve found that many Italian brands of canned tomatoes, tomato sauce and tomato paste contain very little sodium.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any reason not to bake the meatballs? I am partial to a firm meatball that keeps its shape in the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Deb I have not made this particular recipe yet, but I’ve made very similar dishes where I baked the meatballs instead of frying as called for in the recipe, and it’s worked well if not better. It may mean dirtying a baking sheet, but it results in a firmer, better formed meatball overall. In the case of this recipe, I would bake them at 350° for about 30 minutes (slightly under baked, in the case of my oven) then add them when making the sauce, just like the recipe advises, along with whatever drippings or fond has rendered off on the baking sheet. They will finish cooking and add their flavor to the sauce without falling apart.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Deb when you cook the meatballs in the pot, some of the meat’s drippings wind up being the base for the sauce… yum! That doesn’t happen when you bake the meatballs. (It’s definitely a personal preference thing, so you do you! But I like keeping the fat in the sauce. And thought I’d share since you asked.)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not sure why there isn't a separate recipe for making the sauce.  A can of crushed tomatoes with parmesan, while OK, should be jazzed up with the traditional ingredients (IMO). Regardless, will try the meatballs in this manner.  Assuming substituting ground turkey will work as well?  (Yes, I understand that it's not a traditional Italian meatball!)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I put them in a 400 ‘ oven first for about 10 minutes to set them up-when they come out, I pour the juices/fat into the pan then sauté them in the pan to crisp them and to render more fat. They never fall apart!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Minette Love this idea!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I keep some cheesecloth available to "wring out" cooked spinach - fresh or frozen.  If the recipe calls for liquid, I will supplement with the water that was strained out to capture any benefits that might be lost in the process!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Do you think the recipe would work if I used "Impossible" sausage and meat in place of the beef and sausage?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I very much appreciate calling out Diamond Crystal vs. coarse Kosher salt. It makes a difference and so few people specify.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Disappointing. I usually love Ali Slagle’s recipes and have made many of the NYT meatballs recipes but this one didn’t work for me. The meatballs formed together well while shaping but they disintegrated while frying in the pot. I see many comments saying the same. I’m guessing it’s because of the water in spinach (although thoroughly squeezed out) coming out when heated? I don’t know how this passed the recipe testing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used to make this over 40 years ago to get more veggies into my kids. It was one of their favourite dinners. Used all beef then; but I like the idea of adding ground pork too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Instead of going through all this trouble, I just add spinach (fresh or frozen) directly to the sauce. I usually just use frozen meatballs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I do this as well, but I go a step further: I don't bother with meatballs. Just brown the crumbled meat and throw it in the sauce with the spinach. Then you don't need the panko at all, and if you use pasta made from chickpeas, you have a super-fast, low-carb, high-protein meal. You can sub seasoned, pressed tufu crumbles for some or all of the meat if you like. Cuts the cost way down considering the current cost of beef.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The best binder is a bit of coconut flour. It's sticky - holds meatballs, meatloaf and so on. . . together really well. Just mix in a Tablespoon or so. You won't notice a change in flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I love spinach and often add some to meatballs and meatloaf. When thrift is an issue, frozen is better, and for the same reason, I buy inexpensive "Wonder" type bread, which I wouldn't eat as bread, but makes perfectly acceptable breadcrumbs, and is much cheaper than panko. Onion is a big plus. If you need more bulk to hold the meatballs together onion powder is preferable.
I like to add pulverized mushrooms, too, but that isn't particularly thrifty.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could anyone advise on why panko, specifically, is called for here? I make my own breadcrumbs as part of my bread-baking routine. Buying breadcrumbs usually seems wasteful and unnecessary, but I realize that a recipe occasionally depends on a specific texture. Has anyone who's used whatever breadcrumbs they've had lying around comment on how that worked? (And NYT: could you stop specifying panko when it's not really necessary, please?)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Su panko is just crustless bread made into crumbs and I make my own (many packaged ones have junk in them) - there are recipes out there but I doubt you need one. That pioneer woman has one.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Of course use your own bread crumbs - it's a recipe not the law. If you read the NYT comments you will find a plethora of commentary with so many substitutions it doesn't even resemble the original recipe. Go for it!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The trick is to NOT flip the meatballs while they simmer. Just leave them be and they will hold together.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’m considering baking the meatballs instead. Has anyone tried this with success?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Laura please read the intro to the recipe please

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone tried making this with ground turkey or chicken?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Winner with Ground Turkey Breast.  Lite and Lower Fat.   Also baked meatballs in a FAN ASSIST Oven for 30 Minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Stuffed the meatballs with fresh mozzarella pearls. Yummy

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My husband is a gourmet foodie and he, along with the rest of my family thought this one of the best meatball dishes EVER! I carelessly didn’t read the directions correctly and dumped all 6 cloves chopped garlic into the meatballs….oh well! I just added 4 more to the crushed tomatoes. Also used fresh basil and parsley and rigatoni. This recipe is a KEEPER! Thank you!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My husband says there is no such thing as too much garlic. If the recipe says 2 cloves, he will double (or sometimes triple) the garlic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I read all the notes and comments before starting this recipe. Here are my comments: first, my husband wanted to know when the rugby team was coming. 2-4 servings? More like 6-8 or even 8-10! 15 minutes prep? Not likely!  My 12 oz package of frozen spinach turned into 5 oz once thawed and squeezed, but it was plenty for the meatballs. I had no problem with using panko - no broken meatballs. I let the meat balls rest as suggested in the frig for 10 min. Then I used the oven method including broiling.  They stayed nicely shaped and firm, even after simmering in the sauce for ~20 min. After tasting it, we added red pepper flakes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I had fresh spinach in the house, so while I read the comments about frozen being more nutritious and moist, I went with fresh, used more chopped garlic, probably 8 cloves and a "meatloaf" mix of ground beef/pork/veal.  We did them on the stovetop, gradually browning each side.  I use two forks to turn them.  Tomato sauce doesn't sit well with my husband, so since we were using the spinach, I served  with pesto.  Very tasty and ours didn't fall apart (only used the 1 egg and panko as suggested).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wondering if these can be done with ground chicken instead? Or if there is a reason that wasn't suggested as an option.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone tried baking the meatballs in the oven?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Shelagh I have not tried it with this recipe, but I regularly make a similar recipe that bakes the meatballs on a cookie rack over cookie tray (so they do it just boil in the juices) and it’s delicious. If I make these that’s how I’ll do it.  I think it’s 20 min at 350. No need to turn. 
I often substitute pork sausage for ground pork, to up the seasoning, and add onions and peppers to the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe is absolutely delicious and will become a favorite in my house. I made minor changes with the seasoning, but everything remained the same. I added dried oregano, basil, and crushed red pepper to the sauce and it was perfect.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Will this work with beef instead of the pork listed in the recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Seren was literally about to ask this

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can it be make with ground turkey

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My meatballs were delicious and firm; you must really squeeze out all the water from the spinach to ensure a firm meatball. However, the sauce was 'meh'. I would make the meatballs again but use my own sauce style which is full of flavors.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Using 2 eggs helped properly bind the meatballs. Added basil and parsley to the sauce. Made it in one pan and then baked at 350 for an hour. Delicious. Can’t hide the spinach from my husband though;)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Also good to add frozen spinach to meatloaf (I do turkey not beef).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I doubled this but followed as written and it ended up becoming a meat sauce. The majority of the meatballs held together through browning but fell apart once put in the sauce with some stirring. I’d recommend baking the meatballs like others have suggested.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Several noted the meatballs fell apart when they browned them, but my experience was they held together well.  For size context, I got 16 meatballs, the recipe spec's 18, so close enough.  I made these per recipe except for subbing in finely chopped cooked kale for spinach.  These are tender and I wonder if cooks who report them falling apart didn't let them sit long enough on the first side when browning, that is tried to turn them too soon.  I used tongs to turn and it worked well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so delicious. No complaints, no fixes. Loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this last night and it was soooo delicious! I left out the spinach liquid and only used ½ cup of panko, and added some chilli flakes. I fried the meatballs for about 5 minutes on each side to get a really nice crispy edge. Perfection! 😋🍝

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Sounds great. Has anyone frozen the meatballs for later use. Just two in our household.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @MaryCC you can always refreeze meatballs and I’ll be perfectly fine

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can you use impossible ground beef instead of pork or beef?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’m adding some to my next meatloaf and Salisbury Steak patties. Love adding veggies to anything I can.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any thoughts on baking the meatballs instead of pan frying?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can you freeze these in the sauce  for later consumption?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I shall make these tomorrow. I bet if I baked the meatballs in the oven for a bit, on a piece of parchment, they wouldn't fall apart in the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hi! Any suggestions/modifications for making a turkey or chicken version of these?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I would favor the ground turkey over the chicken for the meatballs, as I’ve used chicken in Albondigas (Mexican meatball soup) and they do tend to fall apart, even at lower temps. The ground turkey has better flavor and holds up better in meatballs and meatloaf. Just use the higher fat & a good quality product when not combining with ground pork.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Add more garlic and basil!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I add spinach to meatloaf, too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: what is the type of noodle in the picture?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Maria
It's called 'fusilli con buco'--Rustichella D'Abruzzo brand makes it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: How about a meatloaf--can spinach be added to that?--instead of meatballs?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Red pepper flakes… the spinach is a great addition! Never thought of that one before.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Really no need to put them in the sauce to disintegrate. Sauce your pasta then lay em on top and sauce again:)
Old restaurant hack!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'm still puzzled about why recipes call for panko when the panko is just going to be soaked and softened.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's being used as a quick and easy binder here, not a topping.  Personally, I'd use a bread and milk panade, and save the panko for breading or pangrattato.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The fix for meatballs that fall apart is to cook them in sauce in a large frying pan.  Arrange them similar to making rolls - no meatballs on top of each other. Don't stir - just gently turn over halfway through cooking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We used to get a "Joe's Special" somewhere between Sacramento and the Bay Area - ground beef, spinach, and eggs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really, really delicious. Cooked 12 oz of fresh spinach instead of frozen. Sub mild Italian ground pork sausage instead ground pork. Generous 1/2 cup of Parm. Skip the parsley. Fridge is key to holding them together. Recommend baking at 425 15 min flip another 5 min and boom.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Costco had huge packages of brats on sale, so I took them apart and used them for the meat. worked out well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Metric equivalents as well please!  I am an international subscriber, as are many others, so please provide a metric toggle on all recipes, not just some.  Thanks

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Step 6 where we are to gloss the pasta in sauce seems unnecessary to me. I could skip it, but what am I missing here?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added some garlic powder and powdered oregano to the sauce, which kicked it up on par with some of the best sauces I have enjoyed from various Italian families who have invited me to dinner.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
For my spaghetti sauce, I’ll add:
1 large onion chopped, 1 red bell pepper, chopped, 1 lb mushrooms sliced, sautéed for aprox. 5 minutes.  Then add an additional 1/4 cup of chopped basil, the remaining garlic and crushed tomatoes. 
Continue with recipe by adding meatballs and drippings to sauce, then simmering till meatballs are cooked about 25 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After reading the other notes, I made the meatballs a little smaller and baked them in the oven a bit to firm them up before adding to the sauce. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious and and they didn’t fall apart. I added an extra egg, skipped the spinach water and used a few tablespoons of milk instead. Air fryer the meatballs about 8 minutes before adding to my own sauce which I  cooked for awhile before adding the meatballs

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: i use my potato ricer to dry the spinach. Awesome results. Squeeze over separate container to save the liquid. (I know seems obvious but I have found out may people have no idea what I am talking about (such as friends of grandkids - no experience in kitchen)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used to add spinach to meatloaf and the kids loved it.  I can, and will, try that for meatballs as well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meatballs came together fairly easily.  However, they tended to break apart during the Browning and even more so after added to the sauce. Speaking of the sauce it was kinda bland. I'd give the recipe a 'C' , 'C+' at best. Won't make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made the meatballs and they were great! I baked them before adding to sauce and they held together beautifully.  Much moister and tastier with the spinach added.  A keeper for sure and I froze some for the future.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wanted to like this, getting protein and veggies in one. But the steps were baffling. Remove the spinach water and then… add it all back? 56 Oz of sauce?? My meatballs held okay when browning and I tried to keep them together in the sauce. But I checked after 25 minutes and there was still raw pork. It was so much sauce it barely fit, and the pasta had to be in a separate pot. Not gonna make this one again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone tried using this meatball recipe as a filling for stuffed shells?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As Wendy B already noted, there's really no need to make meatballs of the meat/spinach/garlic/parm mix.  I chopped up some chicken thighs (instead of using beef and pork), then browned that with the aforementioned ingredients.  Added a jar of our favorite spaghetti sauce and served over noodles.  Delicious and easy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For those who don't like the simplicity of the sauce or don't have the time to make a more nuanced or complicated one, I suggest using your favorite jarred marinara sauce. I used Rao's. It is OK not to be perfect! I also cooked the meatballs in the oven and then put them in a large saucepan with the already-heated sauce, adding the pan juices from the baking. Next time I would pump up the flavor of the meatballs with some dried onion, maybe. Make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I used one reader's suggestion to bake the meatballs for 10 minutes @ 400 to firm them up a bit before browning. Ignore the "don't overwork" advice -- I pack them tight as golf balls. They hold together just fine as long as you're fairly gentle with them. The frozen spinach makes the mix very moist and keeps the meatballs tender.

The nutritional information is bogus. The yield is "6 servings" and the nutrition guide is for "2 to 4 servings." So which is it?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: A delicious recipe, but because I felt it needed some help I added a little flour to the meat/spinach mix and that helped keeping things together. To lift the taste a bit, I added a little pepper flakes and fennel seeds. To break the sharpness of the sauce, I added 1/4 cup of sour cream and a little tomato paste - and it was really good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used 1 lb. Italian bulk sausage + 1/4 lb. ground beef (because I had it left over). Whole Foods didn't have a 10 oz. package of spinach so I used about half of a 16 oz package of whole leaf spinach that was in the freezer. I chopped it up while it was still partly frozen, microwaved it and squeezed out the water. I did not add back any of the spinach water to the meatballs.  Meatballs did not crumble but I was careful with them. Made 24 meatballs. Delicious with the sauce and pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: S PMwould turkey work with pork as opposed to beef?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If pork is never on the menu, could you use all ground beef or substitute ground turkey for the ground pork?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Julie she says in the recipe that you can use all ground beef if you prefer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Just thought a quick clarification on the instructions would alleviate questions about "parsley" (see step 2) vs. the spinach and panko mixture (see step 1). Can't wait to try them!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can I use fresh spinach?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Coletteim sure you can but it has to be cooked to get the water out. Microwave or sauté it and then squeeze and chop

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If fresh spinach keeps the meatballs from falling apart, that's an improvement in my book even if there is a slight difference in nutritional value.  Does it help?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can the meatballs be frozen after cooking and saved for later?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can you use fresh spinach?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Spinach and meatball connect did you add more bread crumbs or egg or both?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I will use this recipe to make vegan "meat"balls by substituting TVP or lentils for the ground meat. Seasonings and the addition of spinach will make all the difference. I make similar ones without spinach, so I am eager to enjoy them with spinach, one of my favorite vegetables.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any thoughts on trying this with vegan ground meat substitutes???

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can these meatballs be frozen if prepared in the oven? I love the idea of extending the nutritional side of meatballs by adding the spinach!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could you use canned spinach?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: May we sub ground turkey or chicken?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Five cloves of garlic is overkill.  In fact saute' the garlic for flavor then get rid of it.  This is what my mother from Calabria used to do.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As a garlic Lover , I say the more the better!! I will be adding another 5 cloves to my sauce with more herbs as well! ;-)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can I use fresh spinach?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Maybe, but that would definitely be more labor-intensive, since you would have to clean, cook and chop the spinach first.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The sauce needs a lot more. I think a jarred marinara would be much better. The meatballs also did fall apart but were yummy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I found this to not be so good.  It's edible, sure, and it will be consumed, but by *following the recipe exactly*, I got a sauce that was one note (tomato) and meatballs that basically fell apart so the one note sauce became a meat sauce.  I found the meatballs bland, too, so the sauce wasnt helped when they fell apart.  Justine below also had this issue.  And someone else talked about the tomato sauce blandness, too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We added onion and mushrooms to the sauce. I felt that the sauce was too acidic so we added some sugar. We didn't have any trouble with the meatballs, but I used ground pork only. Also, we browned the meatballs on all sides so that might have helped keep them together. In the future we may add some anchovy so give some more depth to the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
These were delicious but a little more labor intensive than practical on a week night. We made these for a weekend lunch with the following comments:
1. Didn’t take my spinach out early enough for it to thaw so I ended up using a double boiler method on the stove top to get the spinach to thaw. 
2. Uses a combination of Pomi chopped tomatoes and some chopped fresh cherry tomatoes I had on hand. I added some leftover Pinot noir that has been open for awhile and a couple of Tsps Pomi tomato paste with splash of good balsamic vinegar. 
3. I ended up with 15 decent sized meatballs that are quite filling and make great leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
My meatballs fell apart too. I bought a packaged ground meat blend (beef pork and veal) and wish I’d stuck to just beef and pork. I also should’ve minced the garlic and parsley to avoid larger pieces that disturbed the structural integrity of the meatball. 
The sauce and pasta need sufficient salt. 
At the end of the day I’d prefer ground meat and veggies in a sauce, over attempting this recipe again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Did you chill the meatballs before adding to sauce? Helps to firm up. I have used this method for years.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The spinach is good for you, and much easier to use when it's chopped and frozen. Just make meatballs and add the spinach to the sauce. Works with just garlic and oil as well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are delicious! Super tender. I made them in the oven as my toddler was getting a bit hangry and we couldn’t wait for a 35 min simmer. Also used Raos sauce but next time will try the homemade sauce option. Toddler and adult approved!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We had half an onion and some mushrooms leftover from another meal so we added them to the sauce, along with a tablespoon of sugar.  The meatballs come together easily and quickly. This is a great weeknight meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really -- two 28 ounce cans of tomatoes. I wanted to check but since no one has said anything I guess it's correct. This is a time consuming recipe with a lot of prep.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
These meatballs are really great for a week night dinner but like someone commented, are a little bland. I added soup stock to the sauce, which improved the flavor. Next time I will add sautéed onions to the batter for more umami. 
Regardless, our family enjoyed the leftovers in the form of meatball sandwiches.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this tonight. Like the concept but flavor wasn't there. Next time we will sub Italian sausage for the pork. Served it with polenta instead if pasta and that was a good decision. Bery comforting.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The meatballs fell apart for me and the whole dish wasn’t that tasty.  I’d cut the spinach in half, or maybe just used chopped mushrooms instead, possibly with ground chicken. Add milk, substitute plain bread crumbs for panko, increase the parmesan cheese.   Keep the parsley and garlic and egg.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I should have read the comments before attempting this. Same issues with meatballs breaking apart while cooking.  I added my own herbs to the sauce as I usually do, so that part tasted very good. We did not eat the meatballs because they weren’t even meatballs. They were particles 😆  
Figures the first time not reading comments…. Dummy me.  But I’ve had great experiences with many other recipes of Ali’s.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
The meatballs are good. I used regular bread crumbs and the mis seemed dry so,I added 1/2 c of ricotta. They held together nicely and tasted good. 
The sauce is very bland and not worthy of the meatballs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meatballs fell totally apart even thoughI chilled them as suggested.   Ended up eith s beef/ spinach ragu

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: very good. very tender meatballs. added a bit of Worcestershire sauce and ketchup  to boost the sauce

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tastes fine, but very wet and is more like a meat sauce than meatballs

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I loved the idea of having somethng green in my meatballs.  But my store didnt have frozen spinach, so I took 14 oz. frozen kale instead.   I was too lazy to squeeze water out of it or wait for it to thaw.  I threw in 2ibs of ground pork and turkey to absorb the excess water along with all the other ingredients listed.  I baked about 50 meatballs at 400 for 25-30 min.   Then, I simmered diced onions and garlic, threw in 6 cans of tomatoes and red wine, and added the meatballs in.  Yummy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: In the frying, the panko in the mix burned the first side of the first batch of meatballs so I had to pick off the burnt stuff.  My meatballs are in the sauce right now, mostly falling apart. I have them on low and are just letting them simmer, no stirring.  Should still taste ok.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Would definitely recommend good whole canned tomatoes (crushed by hand) instead of crushed tomatoes.  In order to completely cook the meatballs it needs to be cooked a while and by the time they're cooked, the "sauce" is more like a paste.  Also, the sauce is very bland.  I would brown some onions, then the garlic, then add the tomatoes and spices.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could this be made with plant based meat???

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Lisa M. I made with 2/3 impossible beef and 1/3 beyond breakfast sausage patty. Worked great. Omit extra liquid in first step.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I took Jojo's advise and left out the spinach water, reduced the panko to .5 cup. It was perfect. Meatballs held together fine. Two cans of crushed tomatoes, one with no salt and basil. I didn't make any other changes. Delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you want to get exactly 18 meatballs, they should be around 47 grams instead of 50 grams. A #24 disher / red cookie scoop will get you pretty close, but you might need to add a few extra grams worth to each scoop.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wanted to like this. I read many of the reviews and took note. In particular, the comments about the meatballs falling apart. I thought I had this with minor alterations, to avoid the meatballs falling apart. But alas, they did. Though not entirely. My assessment is there’s too much spinach in this for it to be tasty and to hold together.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I think I will try this recipe but use all beef and basil pesto in the meat mix and use no canned tomatoes. Instead of canned tomatoes I think I will use one tomato peeled and seeded, chopped and simmered in butter.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really good- modifications I made was I added mozzarella cubes. It froze really good in my Souper Cubes and I brought to work

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Soft, but kept their shape. Challah crumbs; no panko. Put only a little squeezed-spinach liquid into the meat mixture. Made the sauce in a large frying pan by browning salted onions, adding garlic, then a dab of tomato paste, deglazed with about 1/2 cup pinot noir, stirred in the tomatoes and remaining spinach juice, then basil, oregano, and red pepper flakes. Roasted the meatballs in a rimmed sheet pan lined with parchment, which absorbed the grease, before adding them to the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not bad, but overall pretty bland. Made as directed, but  the sauce and the meatballs both felt like they were lacking in flavor.  Only one meatball fell apart for me, so no issue there with making them in the pan rather than baking first. And while I am a huge spinach fan, I almost felt the meatballs had a tad bit too much spinach. If I can figure out how to give it all a bit more depth I would make it again. My husband, not a spinach lover, liked it a lot though so that was a win!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tripled recipe with turkey, pork and bison. Roasted meatballs at 400 on an oiled sheet pan. Also used matzo meal instead of panko. Came out great.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We really liked this! Used 1 lb ground beef. 1 x 28oz diced tomatoes + 2 cups beef stock. They didn't fall apart. Served over sautéd mushrooms, peppers and zucchini noodles. Spinach in the meatballs - genius!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and my beautiful delicate meatballs instantly stuck to my le creuset and were destroyed…did my pan get too hot?? Spinach too moist?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: delicious. we mis-read and also put chopped basil and parsley in the meatballs which was great. also cooked the tomato sauce down for an hour before adding brown balls back and added chopped onion, anchovy paste, wine, worcester. really good and would cook for friends. meatballs did not fall apart but we were extra nice to them :)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Added two eggs to help with binding and used only beef ~1.3lbs. Meatballs still kinda fell apart.
Delicious and moist meatballs with a subtle spinach flavor. We had to put the lid back on the Dutch oven in order to get the meatballs up to temp (165F).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made almost exactly as written, sooooooo delicious and a great toddler friendly meal! Baked the meatballs per the note and added pan drippings to the sauce. Also added 1/2 tsp sugar to the sauce

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made just the meatballs with a few  modifications—used ground turkey and fresh chopped spinach, and put them in the oven at 425 for 20 minutes, rotating halfway. Moist and flavorful meatballs! Great way to use up some spring spinach!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I make this recipe with ground chicken and I use fresh spinach.  Much easier that way and I don’t have to squeeze out the water.  The spinach is finely chopped.  Results are yummy. I also add more Parmesan cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As others have stated, the meatballs fall apart.  They are so delicious, what does it matter.  I added  chili flakes, bay leaf, oregano, and cumin to increase the flavor based upon comments of others.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Don’t know what the raves are about. Meatballs didn’t fall apart but they were bland and 2 28oz cans of crushed tomatoes made abundant sauce that lacked flavor or depth. Would not make again (from someone who likes Ali’s recipes 99% of the time).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Surprisingly (to me) good. I used the drained frozen spinach and all beef and when fashioning the balls, dipped my hands in cold water between balls. That way you get a nice rounded meatball. I cooked them in a large frying pan: heated the oil, placed the balls in the hot oil, and then put them into the oven. It worked. Then poured a 15 1/2 oz jar of marinara around them. Heated up on the stove and poured over the spaghetti. A crowd pleaser.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used only half the called-for frozen spinach and no liquid but kept all else the same, except used turkey sausage instead of plain ground turkey (cut back a bit on salt as a result). Mix made 15 meatballs. Baked meatballs at 400F for around 25 min and skipped the simmer. Also made Julia Moskin's classic marinara (on NYT) instead of this one--called for only 1 28oz can of tomatoes. Made 12oz dried pasta rather than an entire pound, to feed three people.  Tasty; would make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any ideas for substituting the egg? Will it hold up with extra panko/breadcrumbs? Egg allergy in our house

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The recipe was good, but needed a little hel to not be too bland. And the time estimates were pretty off for me… it took way longer and was more of a project than expected from the instructions. I’d give this three stars.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: How long am I supposed to preheat a 12” stainless steel sautee pan and how many cups of oil do I need so the meatballs don’t stick? This became a bolognese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I just made this with a tweaks. I used ground chicken, and added tabil spice blend to the meatball mixture. I baked for 30 mins at 375°. I added a heaping tablespoon of Onion Achaar to the crushed tomatoes. I used orercciette instead of spaghetti or other pastas mentioned. Delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My toddler and I loved these. Used a whole box of fresh spinach (wilted in olive oil then chopped), garlic powder instead of fresh, and simmered in Rao’s marinara with a sprig of basil. Get a good sear on them first and they’ll hold together. Finishing them in the sauce is key! Makes them so tender and flavorful.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made with cottage cheese since I had no ricotta. Wonderfully moist meatballs which stood up to simmering. Very good, simple sauce using Muir Glen crushed tomatoes. Definitely a keeper of a recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wonderful meatballs and sauce. I used fresh spinach and 1/2 cup 4% fat cottage cheese instead of ricotta since that’s what I had. Opted for my homemade bread crumbs vs Panko. Did not need to add liquid. Muir Glen Organic crushed tomatoes used for a delicious sauce. I will be making this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The sauce is a little bland as it is. I added some more herbs: rosemary, oregano etc. aka “Italian seasoning” and probably should’ve sautéed an onion too to really pump up the flavor. Overall a good dish. Would also recommend baking the meatballs (I believe Smitten Kitchen does this for many of her recipes) to avoid them falling apart in the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I liked the meatballs with the thawed frozen spinach really tasty.  I used the .5 recipe widget and added dry basil and a touch of oregano in the beginning to the sauce as well as a small chunk of parm. used barilla protein spaghetti as I couldn't find any dreamfields.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meatballs fell apart in the sauce but flavor was good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I made the ssamjang from scratch, and boy, was it good*!  

As was the entire dish.  For so little effort, it was an impressive hit for my dinner guests.  I expect it would also be terrific subbing shrimp for salmon . . .

Served it with Eric Kim's miso soup, which also got rave reviews from my guests.

*so good, in fact, I'll be making another batch to keep in the frig.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: so simple. so satisfying. so ssamjang.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My spouse had bought too much salmon. So I turned to this recipe and invited two good friends over for a Friday night dinner. One of our guests remarked that he had to slow down eating so that he could savor every bite because it was just that good. I made a quick cucumber salad with rice vinegar, sugar, and ice, cold water and had some kimchi on the side along with cheater version of gamja jorim. Such a super easy meal and so rewarding.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: A new favorite way to make salmon! This is one of those recipes where the "whole is greater than sum of its parts." The rice vinegar in the marinade truly cuts that sometimes strong salmon taste.  Super easy to make, went excellently with the rice and lettuce leaves. I had mine with perilla leaves too, and mixed some ssamjang+sesame oil+rice vinegar+green onions into a dipping sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So simple and fast to make. I baked the salmon at 375 for 10 minutes because I don’t have a broiler and it was perfect. You can really decorate your lettuce wraps with condiments, too, which is fun — furikake, sriracha mayo, soy sauce, etc — in addition to the ssamjang

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: amazing recipe. so good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yummy and easy to make and customize for a family with lots of different food preferences.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I liked this, but my husband didn’t. Will try same recipe with chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So Flavorful for such little  effort!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: really, really tasty! definitely worth making the ssamjang yourself. very easy and quick. i would recommend using a full tablespoon of gochujang in the ssamjang if you like spice!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wow. Super easy and beyond good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone tried using an air fryer instead of a broiler? I don't trust my broiler. How did it turn out for you?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used the air fryer 400F for 8 minutes. Great easy recipe

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We enjoyed this very much.  When we watched the video, there was a flat circular thing on the lettuce that the salmon and rice, etc were placed on.   There was no mention of that in the recipe?  Seems like that would make eating this recipe easier.  Anyone know what it is?  Rice paper or something?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Broiled on 2nd rack for 4 minutes and skin burned. Next time will bake or air fry per other comments.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Two questions, please: In the recipe video, what is the white disc that sort of looks like a small tortilla, sitting inside the lettuce wrap? I don't see it mentioned in the recipe itself or the introduction. And, are we supposed to leave the salmon skin for eating?  Thank you.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Ashley daikon or similar radish

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: it's been asked before, but why do these pasta recipes seem to always call for 12 oz?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @WTR because 12 ounces is four servings. (Technically, 2 ounces is a serving, but almost everyone eats 3 per serving.)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Because so many NYT Cooking readers don't seem to like pasta, so they always says 16 oz is "too much pasta." Also, the full bottle of wine will work best with 12 oz. Otherwise, you would be asking why the recipe calls for 1-1/3 bottles of wine.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I always scale the 12 oz. recipes down to 8 for just the two of us and up to 16 for a guest or two. The calculation for this one is a little trickier than most to get the right consistency. We don't always have a suitable white on hand but do have Noilly Prat vermouth in the fridge that works well for this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I thought it was really terrific. I used dried pasta and cooked it a bit longer so the sauce would thicken up. I added scallops and the resulting dish was full of flavor. Will definitely make it again. (Might squeeze a lemon over it for some brightness).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @David C Scallops are a great idea!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Don’t use the best wine in your cellar but there’s a rule: “If you wouldn’t drink it, DO NOT cook with it.”

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Peter W.  Ha!  I like a good wine but I save a lot of money cooking by being happy to drink almost any old plonk.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Peter W. Maybe I’m just a philistine, but I often make beef stew with old open bottles of red wine I wouldn’t drink, and it turns out great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jo I recently watched something about cheap wines having sugar, pesticides, etc.  might be worth it to up your game.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Didn’t have anchovies so used several tablespoons of Vietnamese fish sauce with a little Worcestershire sauce for good measure.  Tasty pasta.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made it without any modifications.  It is horrible.  A mucky mess of highly reduced wine and gloopy pasta.  Don't bother with this recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Michael, I agree. Made it tonight and it was tasteless.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Michael
I also followed recipe to the T, and while my pasta came out quite nice in texture and the sauce was creamy, the flavor was acidic and bland. I added sautéed garlic and grated cheese after tasting how bland it was, and while that helped, the acidity from the wine was off-putting. I think adding some wine while deglazing makes sense and adds some nuanced flavor, but boiling pasta in that much wine is gimmicky and not for me. That said, cooking pasta with much less water than the standard process is a good method and one that I have been using for a while now.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Michael same. I threw it all away!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This this is the craziest thing i’ve ever heard. Cook the pasta in a whole bottle of wine? No. There are so many better ways of doing this. Firstly, if you really must cook the pasta in wine, then use a wide shallow pan instead of a pot, you’ll be able to use 1 cup of wine and 1 cup of water and get the same effect. But it’s completely unnecessary anyway. Just follow the same basic recipe (which is just a typical midnight pasta recipe), cook the pasta in water as always, then move the  very al dente pasta to the pan, add a half cup wine and let it simmer until soft enough to eat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I think I will try it your way!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: could you substitute something like capers for the anchovies

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Pat In a word - NO. There’s no “taste” of anchovies left - only the umami which they contribute when used in this fashion.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Pat I wonder though how a white miso as the umami contributor would do with the white wine

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Pat i’m going to try a good amount of garlic instead of anchovies to make it vegetarian.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I don't understand this recipe at all - cook 12 oz of pasta in 5 cups of liquid for 8 to 12 minutes and if it's looking dry, add more water? How can it possibly look dry? Five cups of liquid isn't going to boil away in that amount of time. What am I missing? I'm imagining spaghetti and anchovy soup.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @CS Wine reduces much faster than water, because the alcohol evaporates into a gas state much faster than water.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @CS The water/wine isn't boiling away as much as it's being absorbed into the dried pasta. When the eight minutes have elapsed the liquid has transformed the dried pasta into the pliable pasta you eat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Darby Doo wine is only between 10% and 14% alcohol, so that’s not going to affect the amount of liquid that much, reducing it by only ~90 ml if all the alcohol boils off. I

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I often make red wine spaghetti, so I used the same preparation technique. Boil the pasta until a couple of minutes before the doneness you prefer. Drain the pasta and save some of the pasta water. Melt the butter and anchovies (I use a wok but you can use the same pot). Toss the pasta with the anchovy butter. Add the wine and cook/boil until absorbed by the pasta. Add some of the water if more liquid needed. More of the flavour of the wine is absorbed this way. A nice dish. (LOVE anchovies.)

author: Ali Slagle

It is a conundrum - rather like why hot dogs come in packages in different numbers than rolls. Go figure.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I've made this recipe twice now and it's always a hit. I do agree with the commenter below who questions why NY Times recipes always seem to call for 12 oz. of pasta, when dried pasta is ALWAYS sold in 16 oz. packages, at least in the US. You end up having to either do complicated math to adjust the recipe for 16 ounces of pasta or, more likely, end up with a bunch of open packs of pasta with 4 oz. leftover, an awkward amount that's too little for a single serving.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Do you actually add dry pasta and wine to the same pot with the suace? Do you ever need to drain it before you serve?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Brother Bluedream you shouldn’t need to drain it. This is one of many “one pan pasta” dishes. The proportion of liquid to dry pasta is usually something like 1 cup of liquid to 3-4 ounces of pasta, and once the pasta is cooked you have a fully cooked pasta dish without the need to cook the pasta in a separate pan of boiling water, saving you time and clean up. 
This one is 5 cups (2 water, 3 wine) for 12 ounces of pasta, so it’s probably intended to be a bit saucey once the pasta is fully cooked.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Dry pasta will take longer to cook, giving the wine time to flavor the pasta and for the alcohol to simmer away. Fresh pasta won't do either of those things.

As for the question of whether the quality of a cooking wine matters... 
I used to make Marsala chicken with a pretty low-end Marsala... then I switched to something far more drinkable, and it was transformative, totally improving the dish.  

But... I have also prepared other dishes with unimpressive, long-opened bottles of wine... and the results have still been delicious.  Could they have been even better?  Maybe.

2/ We added carrots and celery. We diced 3 carrots and minced 3 celery sticks. Boil for 15 minutes, rinse with cold water. Prepare ahead of time, so

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Axel, thanks for your suggestions! As so many soup recipes start with mirepoix, I wondered why this one didn't have carrots, celery, and onions. I will add them, and leave out the bacon or pancetta. Then the recipe can be vegetarian if one prefers.

When making a big pot of soup, I cook the pasta separately. That way, when there are leftovers, you can add cooked pasta to the leftovers so it stays al dente. Or, add another grain the second day.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @ Karen Lee: the headnote to this recipe explains why we don't start with diced carrots and celery.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Richard X My local store (like many others) sells prechopped mirepoix, which makes it easy to boost the flavor and nutrition. Or you could just chop an onion, which takes two minutes and is a basic kitchen skill. Or you could toss in a bag of frozen onions (either chopped or pearl$.

I basically lived on this for months in a difficult time and place (don’t ask) and still make it regularly. If I have time I simmer a half dozen peeled garlic cloves and a chopped celery stick or two in olive oil to add in at the end; and if I get “fancy” I use chopped pancetta instead of bacon.  Like others, I will also cook the pasta separately as time and space permit.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This what we grew up calling "Pasta Fazoole". Sweat some onions, add a can or 2 of beans, including the liquid, maybe a cup or so of water, season with oregano and/or whatever else you like for flavor, add 5-6 tablespoons of spaghetti sauce, simmer until it thickens a bit. Cook the macaroni separately, then add to the soup, finish with grated cheese. Here's the most important part: Put it in the fridge, and eat it the next day. Nothing tastes better than leftover Pasta Fazoole.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jim that’s what we called it, too! Grams always had a pot on when we’d come to visit.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I find that letting garlic brown produces a bitter taste. I add the garlic with no more than 1 minute before I add liquid.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@crabby kotzmann
I do that too. I also smash my garlic before mincing it up. Something about breaking down the fibers on the cloves makes it sweeter as well. I always mash my garlic into a paste for salad dressings.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this first of all, there’s no such thing as “Italian seasoning”. Italians use herbs such as oregano, rosemary, thyme in most savory dishes. Secondly the base of onion, celery and carrot is a MUST no matter if it’s a slow cooking soup or a quick 30 minutes weeknight soup. Never skip it. Always short pasta such as ditalini are what my Italian in-laws always use. Fresh or canned tomato can be used. Either is fine.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Lost In A Space I totally hear what you are saying but some cooks (novices, those who have limited kitchen space, or those who are trying to get dinner on the table in a hurry) prefer to use seasoning blends such as Italian seasoning, which are made by most of the large spice companies and usually contain all the herbs you mention). Personally, I prefer to stock individual herbs and spices but there are a few blends I like that contain seasonings that I would probably not use in enough quantity before they’d get stale, making them a more economical choice.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Skip the carrots, celery, onion.  Just use lots of garlic. A quick tomato sauce.  ditalini  goes best with the bean size.  Add some water from the pasta and you have a dish as soupy as you like.  Yum.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I make a very similar version but use a 15 can of tomato and more broth. This is such an easy recipe and you can use what you have in your pantry or freezer.

For a more American version use 1 lb Italian sausage. I like to add onions, carrots and celery. Frozen veggies work well and most grocery stores carry  mirepoix in the produce section.

You can cook the pasta separately and add it separately to each serving so that the leftovers don’t soak up too much of the broth and get mushy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My Italian grandmother's depression era recipe.  Crush a garlic  clove, fry in olive oil till golden, add 1 qt. crushed tomatoes (home canned).   Cook elbow macaroni.  Add navy beans (canned or cooked) to either sauce or pasta before draining.   reserve some pasta water.   add pasta to sauce with enough water to thin to taste.   grated romano cheese.   bread.   salad.   dinner.  My father refused to eat it as he said he had his fill in the depression.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: In my opinion, the traditional ditalini works just fine. It's what my family always uses. The pasta soaks up flavor as it cooks. We only use as much pasta as we will eat. For leftovers, we cook more pasta... also in the soup.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Excellent recipe !! 

Just cooked it this evening (we live in France). We loved it, that's the first thing ! Now, a few (positive) comments !

1/ Prepare more broth : we prepared 5 cups of broth, because some types of pasta soak up more water than others and you don't want to find yourself with a dry meal. Add more broth if you feel it's necessary.

2/ We added carrots and celery. We diced 3 carrots and minced 3 celery sticks. Boil for 15 minutes, rinse with cold water. Prepare ahead of time, so

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delicious! I did take the time to add a mirepoix (a cup total) and swapped orzo because it was in the pantry, and added 3oz  baby spinach. Wonderful!  Thank you!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy! Cut recipe in half except for the broth, used not quite 4 c. I also did the mirepoix of 2 small carrots, 2 celery, small leek, sautéed in the bacon fat and a little olive oil for 7 min before 1 min for the garlic. Simmered more like 11 min. Served with a swirl of pesto but it wasn't really necessary, this is so comforting and good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Learn from my mistake: do NOT make this with whole wheat pasta for your kids and spouse. No one will like the taste and you'll be stuck eating it for a sad office lunch all week. One of those instances where traditional semolina elbows are the best way.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this exactly as written. It's a fast, satisfying weeknight meal. I cut the whole tomatoes in half while they cooked with kitchen shears. It seems a little flat to us. Will double the Italian seasoning next time. Maybe try ground Italian sausage?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: While Pasta e Fagioli often starts with a bit of pancetta, it traditionally does not include meat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @SBGo I followed the recipe. I thought it tasted very “light”. I’m used to a more robust taste. My husband suggested some red pepper flakes but on the whole, it needs some umami flavor. I used pancetta and very good tomatoes, seasoning and added spinach at the end. Not sure what else to add!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For the vegetarians out there: replace the bacon with onion and smoked paprika fried in butter. EXCELLENT weeknight meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wonderful! We are vegetarian,  so I substituted some chopped mini sausages for bacon. We had celery and scallions so I chopped one of each and added it to a Tb. of olive oil along with the chopped garlic and vegetarian sausage. Amazing flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
The 20-minute "reddy in" time for this recipe is misleading. For example, it calls for "3 slices bacon, chopped (or 4 ounces diced pancetta)." This means you chop or dice BEFORE the method begins. Since that takes a few minutes, we're up to 22 minutes. Ditto for "thinly sliced" garlic. Another few minutes, maybe 3. Now we're up to 25 minutes. (And. why slice? You get much more garlic flavor from finely chopping. Also,why not a can of chopped tomatoes?) 
Sam Gugino "Cooking to Beat the Clock"

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Time estimates do not include chopping, peeling, etc. Also, the recipe already calls for a can 0f tomatoes. (Good San Marzano tomatoes are always sold whole.) Why chop garlic? We want a hint of garlic, not a slap in the mouth of garlic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: To follow the recipe as printed (1/4 teaspoon seasoning?), ends up with little flavor to lift with the vinegar.  I will start with a mirepoix next time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Nice, easy recipe!  One hint: I would brown the bacon first and then add the garlic. Otherwise your garlic might burn.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I followed the recommendation of adding mirepoix while my diced guanciale browned. I also added much more Italian seasoning. Yummy and quick weeknight meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this today for lunch. I will cook  it again. I followed recipe exactly. It goes into rotation of soups. Next time I will try it with the mirepoix

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Nice and quick, but needs more depth. Next time I’ll add a Parmesan rind, smash in a few anchovy filets and toss in more herbs, dried and fresh.

author: Ali Slagle
