commentBody: Don't need chicken broth,use pasta water instead.Dont drain the beans,it will thicken the broth,This is an simple soup don't over do it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We use chicken broth to add the flavor that was sacrificed by omitting the sautéed celery, onions and carrots. We don't drain the beans because bean juice is yukky.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Pasta fagioli is my favorite soup, so I was excited to try this recipe. I followed the recipe exactly (except added more oregano, basil, rosemary and dried hot pepper) and it’s delicious. I ate 3 bowls and my husband is on his 4th. Thank you NYT!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It’s either Pasta Fagioli or Pasta Fazool - depending on which region of Italy you are from.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For "pasta fazool" the region is probably Brooklyn.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was really easy and so delicious on a cold winter night. Made as directed except added 4 oz baby spinach with the vinegar after taking off heat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fantastic and quick! Perfect for a rainy winter night. Will definitely add half cup more broth next time,

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I probably won’t make this again and I don’t say that often for NYT recipes. It’s basically adult Mac n cheese. It really needed a TON more veggies (we did 5oz spinach and it still ended up light), less pasta, and the sauce was lacking flavor

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Do you put vegetables in macaroni and cheese? Are you maybe thinking of minestrone?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I inadvertently added TWO cans of tomatoes and so had to double the recipe. However, instead of doubling the pasta ( elbows) I added only 8oz and found this to be more than enough. I also added hot chicken sausage  (  1 package)  and a few pinches of raw sugar. We like this soup a lot, and obviously will have plenty of it  for the freezer! Today is Blizzard Day in CT, and the soup is perfect for it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Trader Joe’s has chopped Pancetta and adding some of it to the chopped onions etc. some like using will bring another note to the dish with little effort.  Trader Joe’s also has frozen garlic cubes as well (and also ginger). A quick stir of jarred Pesto wouldn’t hurt, either.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I like the idea of adding a mirepoix but that makes it less easy. It is delicious. 

I added extra vinegar to combat the bacon fat, red pepper flakes and some celery seed. Let the seasonings cook in the fat to bloom. I also used orecchiette which cupped the beans so well!! Next time I’ll add kale or spinach for a nice pop of green & veg.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Added spinach and onion. Served with bread. Very good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My momma , first generation American w a southern Italian mother,  always put chopped celery in her pasta beans as we called it . I do as well and feel it’s an essential ingrédient. Try it

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not great - 4 cups of broth is not enough at all.  Taste was bland and would strongly suggest adding onion, celery and carrot for texture and taste.  This simplified version just doesn’t work.  Stay traditional.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Traditional vs Quick: on a busy weeknight, Quick will win.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really great recipe! Quick and easy! Hope you have some leftovers for lunch!!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I thought about adding onion and celery but decided to try to stick to the recipe. I used prosciutto and added 2 tbsp olive oil. I also increased garlic to 6 cloves. Delicious! Will definitely make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Just returned from vacation - no food in the fridge.  This recipe popped up on the NYT Dinner Tonight newsletter in my email while I was on the plane  this afternoon thinking about dinner.  This was perfect! Such easy home cooked comfort food using pantry ingredients.  Made as written.  Topped with lots of parmesan, chilli flakes and flaky salt.  Nothing to complain about here - thanks NYT!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious!  Don’t forget to add a Parmesan rind - extra lovely flavor!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This has way too much tomato in it.  My Italian mother in law made this probably once a week.  No meat at all.  A few cherry tomatoes, or one large one, or maybe a couple of spoons of leftover sauce.  Celery leaves instead of chopped celery.  In Italy it's the leaves that are valued for flavor.  This soup should be almost white, not too garlicky, always with a parmesan rind and some peperoncini for a touch of heat. Peasant food at its best.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Pretty simple recipe for an authentic tasting pasta fagiolo. I would make a couple minor changes: 1) Cook the pancetta a little longer before adding the garlic - the garlic crisps up so quick and the fat on the pancetta didn't even render 2) crush the tomatoes by hand before adding to the pot - adding them whole made the soup a little too chunky for my liking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Bobby G. As everybody knows, canned soups usually have way too much sodium. I like shortcuts too, but not if it's going to send my blood pressure up.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Add about 1 tablespoon of anchovy paste, for a serious yet subtle umami boost.  While you’re at it, crumbled Italian sausage goes well, too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Do you drain the can of tomatoes?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Recipe does not say to do that, so no.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meh. Lacked flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Is the bacon cooked first before adding?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The bacon is cooked in Step 1. It isn't "added"; it's the original ingredient.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jim  you have some good shortcuts, like using spaghetti sauce that has the seasoning already built in, and using the bean liquid for a nutrition bump that also thickens the broth. Nice!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Pbasile you need chicken broth for flavour! to compensate for missing soffritto at the beginning!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is a delightful recipe to follow and it yields a scrumptious, soul-warming dish! In addition to dried oregano and basil, I added some freeze-dried chives as well as a pinch or three of Burlap and Barrel’s Silk Chili. The chives imparted their lovely mild oniony flavor and some pop of color, and the Silk Chili added some extra depth and a bit of heat to the dish. Really delish!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used smoked sausage instead of bacon/pancetta. And carrots and celery. This is wonderful.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I add some chopped chard leaves.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Try it with ceci cooked or canned and drained with rotini or cavatelli.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Pasta e fagioli is best when the beans and pasta are about the same size.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I've been making a version of this once a week for over 40 years. Now I add some carrots and spinach, possible making it more like minestrone. Try it with baby shell pasta!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
No matter how you make pasta fazoole,  I can only add that for me,  I always try and use a
ham bone with some ham on it. If i dont have one handy I can  always go to Honey Baked Ham and buy one. Last resort is Italian sausage or even Andouille! Skip the carrots etc

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I make something like this but I use chicken sausage - maybe 2 or 3 removed from skin and browned with garlic. Also do Mirepoix, a can of fire roasted tomatoes, small can tomato sauce, & some homemade (or jarred) pesto. I cook the pasta separately and store leftovers separately so it doesnt suck up all the broth & get soggy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was quicker and good. I did add a mirepoix after sauteeing the bacon and garlic slices, stirred and sautéed five minutes more, then proceeded with the rest of the ingredients. Garnished the soup with parsley and parmigiana, I don’t think that’s optional. I have a favourite Pasta e Fagioli soup recipe that’s delicious but this one is really good too and I’ll make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this this afternoon for lunch  with a few changes and it turned out well. I used turkey bacon and added small dice carrots and celery to brown with it before I added the garlic. I had made some sauteed Tuscan kale for dinner last night and I dumped most of the leftovers into this dish. I also put in a pinch of dried red pepper, enough to just give a warm feeling as the dish goes down your throat, but not be too spicy. It was altogether delicious and I'll definitely make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What a fantastic recipe! Followed as written with the addition of a sautéed onion at the beginning. Questioned the need for two cans of beans and am very glad I followed the recipe. One of the benefits is multiple meals. Expect to add more water on day two and three. Looking forward to making this again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Please use an extra cup/can of chix broth… this way you have enough liquid in 2nd day and you are not watering down. 
Remember - one CAN (15.5oz) of broth is two cups!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delicious just as is!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is a baseline soup. It needed flavor so I added: one-half lb. of cooked, crumbled Italian sausage; one diced onion; 1/4 c. red wine; 1 tsp each dried basil, Italian seasoning, and oregano, plus 1/2 tsp. each red pepper flakes and fennel seed; and a sliced and quartered zucchini. Perfetto!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I can’t eat legumes… any substitute suggestions?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really appreciate a quick recipe that IS quick! Very hearty & delicious, especially when you use a small amount of duck pancetta!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this as written except that I added what was probably close to 2 TBS of dried Sicilian oregano because I agree with a previous comment that 1/4 TSP would be meaningless.  Delicious and filling.  Will echo another comment about having more stock on hand if necessary.  I used Whole Foods' brand of elbow pasta.  The dish turned out closer to a stew than a soup.  When I went back for seconds, it had thickened even more.  Will reheat leftovers with another cup of stock.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made with a mirepoix, a splash of white wine, lots of fresh grated parm and two nice parm rinds. Took others’ suggestions to follow the mirepoix with 4 crumbled chicken sausages (removed the casings). Delicious weeknight meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We found this so bland. I was trying to make it vegetarian so I did the mirepoix, no bacon. I added a potato… we ended up adding a bunch of lemon and additional seasoning stuff. Not sure I would make this again. Extremely hearty though.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Quick, easy, and delicious! This recipe also works well with chickpeas. And add arugula at the end.

I'd omit the bacon and use olive oil instead. My favorite pasta for a recipe like this, where the pasta cooks in the sauce, is Brami protein pasta curly macaroni. And if I know there will be leftovers, I cook the pasta separately so it doesn't get mushy when reheated.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Growing up, we also called this "Pasta Fazoole"...very, very thick, never noticed tomato, and my grandmother would give it to us in Mason jars.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so easy to make on a cold rainy day. I did substitute wheat berries instead of pasta because they keep their firmness, but you do need to simmer it a lot longer. Tasty, quick, a keeper!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Solid base recipe.  Needs more spices.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Delicious as written!

I used boccoli, like in the picture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Cooked as written and it was delicious!  I'd probably add a little more broth next time - it wasn't very soupy (I know other reviews said the same thing but I generally make the recipe as is the first time).  Definitely a keeper!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What a great recipe!  I love making a nice dish with items already in my pantry.  I upped the garlic and spice to my taste.  I used parmesan packets from my last take pizza.  It was delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This soup turned out so beautifully!  I followed the recipe exactly, and I'll definitely include this in our rotation of dinners that come together easily.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I adapted this recipe to being a vegetarian - so no bacon or chicken broth - and what I had in the pantry. The liquid was two, 14-ounce cans of stewed tomatoes with onions, celery, green peppers and 2 grams added sugar, and 4 cups vegetable broth. Celllentani, the pasta joined by 2 cans of low-sodium Great Northern Beans and 1 teaspoon of apple cider vinegar. Quite tasty.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy to prepare and very yummy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Good enough, but my bf certainly didn't love it.  Tbh neither did I. I even added carrots, celery, onion, and some parmesan rind - but it still lacked some level of complexity and umph.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very nice! Like others, I added some carrots and onions while frying the pancetta. I also added 300g of Italian sausage. I needed closer to 5 1/2 or 6 cups of broth. Will definitely make it again. Lots of possible variations.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: In step 3, when the recipe says to mash some of the beans against the side of the pot, I instead used a handheld immersion blender to puree some of the contents (which included carrots, onion, and celery), just as I do when making minestrone.  That thickened it and made it more to my taste (though I see from other readers' comments who added more broth, that they must prefer a thinner, soupier product).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: added 1/2 lb of sweet italian sausage, used full 16 oz of pasta, pre-cooked the pasta to al dente which worked out great, went cray-cray with spices, fresh-ground fennel, cumin, oregano, thyme. used onion and celery. next time (sans 20-month-year-old grandson) will add some red pepper flakes. diced sweet peppers would be a nice addition. the toddler loved it for dinner AND lunch the next day, parents loved it because the toddler loved it.  big hit all around. and yeah, i liked it too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Savoury dish! Made a mirepoix as I had the time. Did a 50/50 on chicken and beef broth. Added laurel, thyme and rosemary. It really gained a ton of flavor after two days in the fridge.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Solid recipe. Easy to make and nice flavor. I did add some mushroom I had lying around and some bok choy to add more vegetables. I would make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Excellent, forgiving base recipe and perfect for a rainy day. Added broccoli and tofu. Will make again and again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Simple and satisfying! Omitted the egg but would probably add next time, along with more veggies. Used homemade chicken broth instead of water for the soup, and I think that helped provide more flavor/body. Did not need the gochugaru. The gochujang and kimchi provided enough spice as is.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Super easy & fast. I used 2.5 c of chicken broth (next time I’ll use 2) instead of water and went light on the soy sauce. I didn’t cut up the kimchi, I like a substantial chunky broth. I added both the egg and tofu. Five stars! Can’t believe it had such great flavor but came together in minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: DO NOT DISCARD THE SEASONING PACKET. Shin Ramyun packets specifically (and ramen packets generally) make an insanely good popcorn seasoning. You're welcome.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Broth was a little thin, even with the addition of oyster mushrooms, bok choy and eggs as recommended by Greta.  Will try adding beef or chicken broth in the future to round out the spice.  Otherwise quick and flavorful.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really fantastic recipe—I used way more soy sauce than directed and added sesame seeds, garlic, and mushrooms. The broth was great for me but I plan to add a bit of corn starch to thicken if necessary in the future.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The temperature dropped 30-40 F here in 2 days.  This recipe is fixing all of that. It's perfect for this day!  Will add to the rotation.  Yum!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very good base of a recipe. I added Shrimp, carrots, mushroom and garlic as well and it came together great. I'd recommend first sauteing the shrimp in the pan and then pulling them once cooked. Then the carrots for 2 minutes, then the mushrooms and garlic, then starting step 1.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Good. But not any better than regular Sapporo Ichiban with egg and kimchi added as a topping. Doubled the recipe. No need to cut up kimchi. Add egg as soon as noodles separate from their form or they'll be overcooked by the time the eggs are done. Added bok choy cooked w noodles and then tofu and salad greens in the bowl w ramen poured over. Didn't use gochugaru, didn't need it for spice level. Mushrooms would be a good add.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Veggies is a must, not an option

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anybody made this with rice noodles to make it gluten free?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious! Definitely will make this again! I did not cut the kimchi, which added some body to the broth.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Comes together quickly and tastes excellent. I got a rotisserie chicken and shredded up the meat to add as a protein. Also, feel like I easily doubles all the spices. We had sliced limes at the end and dipped some baguette bread in the broth. 10/10 kid review and the adults liked it, too!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: gochugaru is red pepper flakes in Korean. When I was growing up, my brother and I debated a lot over whether egg yolks should be stirred into soup or left whole.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What would be wrong with doubling (or more) this recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Delightful! I was fortunate to have  a quart worth of homemade kimchi from a friend (I tripled the recipe). I tripled the recipe, adding a full bunch of scallions + garlic + many shitakes—and followed users' recommendations for chicken broth over water, leaving no need for soy sauce. Add baby bok choy added alongside the tofu cubes for a 10 min simmer, so the tofu becomes somewhat saturated with flavor.

Condiments I lined up: more fresh scallions, sauteed corn, soft boiled eggs, nori sheets!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fantastic - 30 minutes start to finish and excellent flavor. My only suggestion would be to back off the water a little - 2.5 cups was fine, but 2 would have made for a slightly more intense broth. Add fresh vegetables toward the end if using.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This one wins the Old Person Hippie Food prize for the month of May! Made as written with egg, Britt's Back Market Kimchi and a handful of broccoli florets, but with this one parlor trick: cooked a small fillet of salmon in the toaste,r oven and poured the noodle soup over it to serve. OUTSTANDING.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Substituted de-stemmed chopped Kale for chard and used chili crisp oil with the coconut saute instead of just red pepper flakes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious! Cooked exactly as written. Substituted kale for the chard as mentioned in the note. Don’t forget to squeeze a little lime on the finished dish. Will definitely be making this one again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Pleasantly surprised! I used Tuscan Kale, but otherwise followed the recipe. Served it with wild rice. Will make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Outstanding!  Doubled the chard and used steelhead trout.  This will be a "go to" recipe for me.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Farmed steelhead is a great budget-friendly swap for salmon. It’s always available at our local co-op for a lot less than wild salmon.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any substitute for chard?  It's not available around here except during the summer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Recipe note suggests kale

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used lacinato kale and it worked really well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We used the chard, my husband didn't really care for the chard and next time I will try the kale. The recipe was really good. Followed the instructions and it came out perfect. Served it with roasted sweet potatoes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Forgot to get shredded coconut at the store so used unsweetened coconut mild we had on hand.  Still very delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Very good! 
I found that 10 minutes skinside down and a quick flip for two minutes was the right formula for my desired doneness. I might try popping the lid on next time to get a little bit of surface steam and avoid flipping.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this with kale and followed the recipe as recommended. I did end up sprinkling a bit of water over the kale to soften it up and steam a bit. The coconut kale was really flavorful and delicious. I found that the salmon was a bit overcooked in the oven - I may consider steaming it next time? Or maybe cook it in parchment? I think a silkier texture would go well with the chewy kale.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Would it work to cook it in a covered pan the whole time?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is an outstanding recipe I will put in heavy rotation. I made it with white chard and doubled the chard, and used canola oil instead of olive oil. Otherwise I followed the recipe exactly and wouldn’t change a thing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was really delicious. I used kale instead of chard because that’s what I had in the fridge. Otherwise, I followed the recipe exactly. Love Ali Slagle’s recipes!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used the Swiss shard recipe on its own since my garden is heavily producing it now. I made one batch exactly as is and it was great. Made the second batch and added some yellow raisins for fun. Love the coconut.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This turned out fantastic. Many readers and cooks have shared how they made adjustments to recipes in the past. I’ll say I’m in that camp as well to this recipe. What I realize about recipes is they are a base unless one wants to follow to the rule. Isn’t that what cooking is all about. Anyhoo, I modified this by adding  a few dry spices to the pork and added Spanish sofrito and in the end finished it off with cilantro (parsley too can add a touch of green freshness) and lime to cut the heat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I added an onion and browned some sausage I had on hand and added for the last hour. I also stirred the pot a few times during the process to brown more bits and wouldn't change a thing. A keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, layered flavors, with a delightful array of different textures! Scaled down for just two, used some white wine to supplement fluid while braising, otherwise followed recipe. Served it with German spaetzle pasta (dried), a fine pairing. Will be making it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
Tasty and versatile. Served with Caribbean style red beans and rice, but can see this with pasta/in a taco/on mashed potatoes.

I also let it sit overnight to remove the fat cap. Maybe was just my pork but it was crazy fatty. Must have removed at least a full cup. The residual broth was fantastic and not too rich.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I added an onion and used beef broth instead of water to give it some depth. This could be done in the slow cooker but you lose out on some of the roasted components by baking with the lid off. Ate this over polenta which was good, but made a sandwich the next day on soft French bread that was the best version of this.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Loved this! I made a big pan of slow roasted tomatoes that I mixed in at the end which was an amazing addition - could be substituted with sun dried tomatoes. Ate with some homemade garlic bread. Next time I might double the amount of bell pepper because the cook down a lot and are so good. Perfect Sunday dinner.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Easy & delicious; perfect for a cold winter day!  And it doesn't break the bank.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I made this as directed (I usually try that first unless a recipe looks off or else later goes sideways in the cooking process).  This one worked, was easy and delicious. I used 2 large fresno peppers and I like the combination of heat and sweetness (from the peppers).  Given all the meat juices and vegetable (garlic, peppers) rendering into the sauce I don't think any extra broth addition is needed.  Like the French bread idea and my son is looking forward to leftovers on tacos.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Can someone explain to me how “prep time” is defined???? I usually add about 15 minutes to the recipes in this app. But this one? It took me 45 minutes to prep the peppers and pork. Browning in batches, four, took about 20 minutes. Dinner is going to be eaten  pretty late tonight.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @Chris-SFO i agree! If you don’t have your own sous-chefs, add 15 minutes for every three ingredients listed. 😂

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delish!  Husband who made it (yeah!) put only one pepper in as I can’t take spicy food.  Next time we’ll add two to try it out - one added a hint of great flavor w low heat.  Looking forward to tomorrow’s leftovers!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made in a crock pot?  Asking cause my husband really dislikes crock pot meals.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @Lee Greenwald and you want to make him unhappy?🤣🤣

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @AC Wisner. Thanks for asking!  No, I don’t.  And this is delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor on top of polenta. Made as directed and is now one of my favorites.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I ended up adding 1 jalapeno and 1 yellow onion, skipping all the stovetop steps and throwing it all in the crockpot on high for 6 hours. Very tasty and tender! Even if there was fat that wasn’t trimmed it didn’t end up chewy or stringy. Put it on Hawaiian rolls for sliders. Very easy for the Super Bowl party!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Super delish! I’ve got a lot of weak taste buds in my house so I only added like 10-15 jalapeño rings from a homemade jar of maple jalapeño cowboy candy and it gave just a TINY bit of spice. Still too much for my kids apparently. 😆 It looked too watery so I let it go another 30 mins. Don’t do it! Once I shredded the pork there was basically no liquid left. 😩 I will definitely make again but stick to the 2.75-3 hour cook time. I can totally see myself eating this in a taco, rice, noodles, on top of cooked veggies… yum! Makes a bunch and probably freezes great.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: The peppers and braising are game changers for what is otherwise just a pulled pork recipe. Delicious and so easy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I would make this again but reduce the liquid and add onion. Made as written except added some Jamaican/Louisiana spices. Used bell peppers and Pablano peppers. Also added roasted tomatoes. It does need more depth of flavor. Makes a lot of liquid as written. Used beef broth instead of water, but would reduce to 1 cup.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Very similar to pork chilindron, a Spanish classic: to convert, char and peel the peppers, add paprika and sauteed onions, and use olive oil rather than a neutral oikl

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This seems ideal for a slow cooker, too. How would one go about converting it to a slow cooker?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I haven't done this yet, but am going to try it in the slow cooker. I'll reduce the water a bit, maybe to one cup, and will cook it on low for 6-8 hours. Once it's done and shredded, I may transfer it to a sheet pan and pop it under the broiler a bit to crisp it up.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I resisted the urge to add onions and was pleasantly surprised with the depth of flavor without them.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @Portia R IKR? Since I found out, I actually need to avoid onions, I’ve been doing a ton of cooking without them. I thought they were a secret ingredient to everything but it turns out most recipes are just fine without.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
Don't add any extra liquid (you'll be tempted). When I first put it in the oven, there was very little liquid and a pile of peppers / meat just sitting on top. Within about 30 minutes, both the meat and peppers released enough liquid that it was properly braising. 

At the end, we didn't have crispy pork, but we had a really delicious sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I made this today and followed the directions exactly. OMG this is so good! I was worried about all that vinegar but it really smoothed out. I served it as a hot sandwich but looking forward to serving it with mashed potatoes.  I added 2 hot peppers- next time I will use 3. So good, make this and thank you Ali.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: My lumpy mashed potatoes worked really well with this.  I used a 4 lb pork butt instead of shoulder as that is what the butcher had. Used white wine vinegar.  3 hours was perfect.  So so good. Will repeat this for sure.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: One of my new favorite meals in the Dutch oven. Added an onion, replaced the water with beef broth, and added a few chipotle peppers in adobo sauce to give it a smokey heat flavor. Poured over Zatarain’s dirty rice to finish it off. Incredible.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Had to make this almost immediately after seeing the recipe and it didn’t disappoint. Next time would kick up the heat with more chilis. Since the pork gets shredded anyway, I’d sear in larger pieces as that’s the most tedious part. Otherwise super easy, flavorful and versatile!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: It’s a keeper, but next time I’ll add onions.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone successfully done this in a slow cooker?  Absolutely deliciously.  In steady rotation here!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This was great! Followed the recipe with the addition of parsley before serving.  I don't think it needed anything else. Terrific depth of flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I love recipes like this but I would sauté the chicken with the onions first, wilt the greens in same, then add liquids & beans. Due to dietary issues I need greens like kale & chard to be well cooked. And I like the added bit of flavor that comes from browning the meat first.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Give it a squeeze of lemon too!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Had chicken in my fridge that I didn't want to deal with and then found this - all I had to do was throw the chicken in the pot which was exactly what I needed.  Super easy prep.  Made as written, with plenty of salt and pepper, minus the herb sprigs (none on hand) and used spinach instead of kale.  Smashed beans made it somewhat creamy.  Served over some leftover rice with a shred of parm on top.  Delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Wicked Good.  If I had made it as-is, it would’ve been perfect. But…. I had a sick wife that wanted extra broth. I doubled the broth to two quarts.  I also added 4 sliced up medium carrots. It was perfect. Oh, I also added in a parm rind for the last 1/2 of the cooking.  She loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Joe Pro Adding the parm rind! Thank you for this tip. Never would have thought of it and it is a great way to not waste that last but of parm AND flavor soup.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Joe Pro was just going to comment that a parm rind would be perfect here.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Joe Pro parm rind is always delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Added 2 tsp grated ginger.  Lovely lighter flavor, a great alternative to traditional chicken soup. Can easily adjust other add-ins, add spice, chilies, as desired. Next time might add 3rd can of beans and more as it there was a lot of chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Liberty adding grated ginger was great!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: look up the NY Times recipe "Braised White Beans and Greens With Parmesan" for a more tasty version

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @KM Ah, yeah, but that recipe does not have chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @KM When I saw this I thought “hmmmm, think I just made this!” But yes, it was the one you posted. That was very good, maybe just add the chicken, if you want.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @KM thanks for the tip! I used this same recipe as a base then added in rotisserie chicken  and more chicken stock afterward. It smells and tastes fantastic.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and incredibly easy. I used ingredients on hand and made some modifications: I didn’t have fresh herbs so used a heavy hand with dried rosemary, probably twice as much garlic for extra flavor, and a bit of red pepper flakes. I used the four cups of broth and probably added at least half that again (or more) in water. I used four cans of Great Northern Beans and after removing the chicken I dipped out and smashed probably a can and a half’s worth to thicken the broth up and transform from soup to stew. I added several handfuls of shredded collards instead of kale, which cooks up beautifully and went well with the flavors, I added more red pepper flakes at the end which really punched it up. I think, for me, the recipe as originally written could lean towards a bit bland if you don’t add a bit more zing in the way of spices to the cooking or a drizzle of olive oil and/or lemon, etc to the finished bowl. Start to finish in under an hour and a great recipe where you can pull chicken from the freezer and don’t have to wait for it to fully thaw out first - just have to adjust the cooking temp and time a bit to compensate.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I’m no cook but I wanted to try making this. Unfortunately, I misread the ingredient list and only bought one can of cannellini beans instead of two. Being snowed in, I couldn’t just run out to the market. Luckily, I asked my new buddy electronic Alexa if I could add a can of corn which was in my pantry. She said go for it but it would change the taste (sweetness) and texture (crunchiness). OMG was it fabulous. Added to make it again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Ken AI is the best when it comes to helping with recipes, or creating new ones! Glad you found a new friend!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Really great easy dish, super warm and fuzzy for a cold night. Used some dried cracked rosemary because I had all the other ingredients. I also used frozen chicken tenders, just defrosted in the microwave. Half recipe was perfect for two.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: It was a bit thin; I thought it could have used another can of beans, allowing for more mashing. And, just judging from the picture I decided to throw in some casually chopped baby Bellas. I took the suggestion to add the chicken early, but it still took a long time to get the chicken up to temp. And the breasts resisted shredding. We agreed that next time we’d add the beans, keep the Bellas, use thighs, and not hold back on the spinach.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
This was very easy and delicious. I made a few changes:
I had chicken thighs on hand so cut those up and browned them with the onion. I let the onion cook for five-ish minutes first. 
I didn’t have any herb sprigs so I added some dried rosemary and sage to the onion. 
I added a few sprinkles of crushed red pepper with the broth. This really added a nice hint of heat and flavor. 
I used spinach as that’s what I had on hand.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Really nice for a cold night.  I used an immersion blender to mash up some of the beans and added a Parmesan rind as suggested by another reviewer.  Plan to freeze half.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was super easy and delish!  I used sage sprigs, low salt broth, and generously added salt and pepper

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Like KCartire I had chicken in my fridge and I was uninspired last night so I made the recipe as stated subbing baby spinach for the Kate and using fresh sage from my garden. Despite the five star rating i feared it would be bland.  It wasn't.  I was deiighted with it.  There are lots of suggestions here for how to improve it, add lemon, browin the chicken first, add red papper flakes, and I'm sure they would be good. However, this is delicious as is.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was super yum! I wanted to make it a bit more peppery so I added lots of black pepper and tumeric for color added flavor and hopefully some anti-inflammatory benefits. Will be making this again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Would love a filter on the comments where I could choose between "made as written" and "made with modifications."

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Teresa very few people actually make the recipe

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
Sorry bur I always find this hilarious, especially the way this is worded:
melt the (unsalted)butter over medium. When foaming, add the onion, garlic and herb sprig and season with salt

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: If I used dry beans, how would I change this recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Used both fresh sage and rosemary. Added crushed red pepper flakes with onions and garlic, further to suggestions from others.  Next time I will roughly chop/slice garlic. Also, further to others’ suggestions, I added Parmesan rind in last 10 minutes.  I don’t know that I will do that again. Also squeezed in half a lemon right before serving. I agree with others that it would be advisable to add a third can of beans. Served with warm baguette. A real hit with the whole family. Five stars!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: One of, if not THE, best soup/stew I've ever made.  Added a parmesean rind with the liquid.  So comforting and delicious, rich without being heavy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Made almost as directed, but low butter had me opt to render two thick bacon strips, saving crumbles for garnish (highly recommend) and no fresh herbs made me sub a rosemary sea salt which worked well with both unsalted broth and low sodium beans. Satisfying comfort food but more brothy than expected. Next time more beans, and immersion blender to partially puree before adding kale. And more garlic. Measurements for kale, garlic, and onion would have helped.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was a really yummy dinner, very clean tasting.  I added a teaspoon of red pepper flakes, cuz it was cold here yesterday.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I added carrots and mushrooms the 2nd time I made it. It added to it. Also, it's better the 2nd day (like most soups, stews, & chilis).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: made this almost as written, the only alterations I made, and method where I browned all of my chicken first, and then cooked just the onions and the butter for a while until they had really begun to caramelize, then I added the garlic. I did need some acid, so I add a couple teaspoons of soy sauce as well as white wine vinegar, and just a dash of Worcestershire sauce. I didn't have any fresh herbs on me so I wound up using time, rosemary, and a few Bay leaves. all of this made inside of a homemade chicken stock. I double the amount of kale because we enjoy kale. this is great on night one, however this made even better leftovers night two.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: How to modify for a pressure cooker?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I think for me I would reduce the broth by a cup and add an extra can of beans. But I enjoy a thicker consistency. I also butterflied the chicken breast and seared it in a pan before adding it to the pot. All in all it was an excellent winter dish that will be making again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: After reading through the comments, I added chopped carrots to the onion garlic mixture.  The grocery store only had a pkg of "poultry herb blend".  Several people said the stew was a bit bland so I used 2 sprigs each of rosemary, sage, and thyme.  As suggested, I used 4 cans of cannellini beans, mashing 1 1/2 cans. I added parmesan rinds from the freezer with the spinach and about a  tablespoon of lemon juice.  Served with parmesan cheese.  Delicious and easy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I added a parm rind at the same time as the broth and removed it with the chicken and it made it a little extra creamy. Nonna tricks for the win!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Very nice on a chilly, rainy winter night. I used boneless thighs, added two sliced carrots with the onions and a Parmesan rind with the broth as suggested in the comments. A couple shakes of hot flakes suited us. Other than that followed the recipe. Fast, easy and good. Served with salad.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious recipe! The squeeze of lemon to the bowl of stew was a nice touch.  I added some chili crisp oil because I love spice.  Some sourdough bread is great with this stew too.

I think next time I’ll add some ginger because #1. I love ginger!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Yummy but a little bland. Unlike other bland things, it still really had the warm yummy rich feel. Agree that adding some zing with some chili oil and lime helped quite a bit for me.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Don’t trash those chard stems.  Dice, sauté in a little olive oil or butter and sauté till soft about 15min.  I made the recipe as printed and tasted - very good. Then added the sautéed stems - added a nice crunch and did not diminish flavor.  A keepers.  Would make a ice breakfast meal too.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I had bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs that I needed to use so I made this recipe. When I threw the chicken in I kicked myself that I hadn’t thought to brown the chicken first and use the fat for cooking the onions, but I was still amazed at how much flavor this soup had with basically no spices. I also used dried great northern beans cooked in the Instant Pot—I can’t find dried cannellinis around here to save my life (and yeah, I could have used canned, which I often do, but I had the time to make dried). I cooked them to the slightly mushy stage, which gave the soup a nice creamy texture without having to mash the beans. Will make this one again. Nice comfort food for a blizzard!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Natalie cooking time for instant pot please?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I used olive instead of butter and used three can of beans instead of two. Whenever I use canned beans I soften them up a bit before adding by boiling for 5 minutes with a half tsp of baking soda. I love this recipe and will make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Super easy, delicious and even better with a parm rind!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Will try that next time, thanks!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Add a cored and sliced fennel bulb, and forget the chicken breast. Use a quqrter-pound of Italian sausage for flavor. The small amount of fat in the sausage will be offset by the kale and fennel.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Mary Quirolo thanks for the fennel suggestion! Such an amazing addition!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Title says "stew"; blurb/instructions say "soup."  Which is it, please?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a winner. Strong ROI compared to the effort required.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
Quite good! Even the 9 and 13 year old approved.
1. Used chicken thighs. Browned them in the pot first. Removed to plate.
2. Cooked the onions, garlic, and butter in the chicken fat.
3. Did not add herbs until chicken, beans, broth were added to the pot. Let herbs (sage) infuse the broth.
4. Simmer longer to add depth and reduce liquid. 
5. Serve with hot sauce. I liked it with Tobasco, my partner liked it with Dave's Gourmet Carolina Reaper Hot Sauce. And of course bread and butter.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Disappointed. I didn’t love this like I thought I would. It seemed to be missing something—maybe some spices other than the sprigs of sage or rosemary. Adding lemon juice helped and so did Parmesan cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Comforting and easy. I didn’t have all the same ingredients but followed the recipe mostly  w black and white (great northern) beans; cooked chicken separate to add my son’s favorite seasonings (paprika garlic and onion powder salt and pepper ) and used Swiss chard. Crushing the beans a bit was a good trick to learn! Thank you Ali Slagle.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding! Made almost exactly as written. Used spinach. Use more pepper and salt than you think!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
From other’s comments, I cut up chicken first, fried it in 2 Tbsp oil, 2 Tbsp butter and seasoned heavily with dried thyme, rosemary, Zatar, S&P.  Removed chicken, added more oil to pan and continued with onions and garlic, (cook 5 minutes stirring frequently), added two cans of drained and rinsed white beans (cook 5 minutes stirring frequently), added broth (5 more minutes, lid on), remove lid and add escarole, just to wilt. Off heat, add cooked chicken to soup, ladle to bowls, add Parmesan per bowl. 

Next time I will try garlic, ginger, turmeric for spices, and coconut milk instead of broth and have escarole salad on the side instead of in the soup. Not crazy about cooked greens in soup.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: To make this vegan, use soy curls and vegan butter.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I consider this recipe to be a diamond in the rough.  It's very good, but it needs polishing.  I made it close to exactly as written but added some sliced mushrooms and a cup full of mixed frozen vegetables to the spinach.  For extra seasonings, I added a tsp of za'atar.  In spite of the additions, it was "tasty but bland" my spouse claimed.  Be generous with the salt and pepper and kick it up with whatever spices you think would compliment it, and finish it off with a squeeze of lemon juice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I like to substitute bone-in chicken thighs since the bones impart more flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is basically the same as one I’ve been making for years. But, along with the addition of the chicken broth, I add a piece of the rind from Parmesan Reggiano and allow it to simmer in the soup along with the onion, garlic and greens, for about an hour. Adding the beans along with 1/3 cup of sherry before serving.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Great, easy, highly satisfying dish.  As others suggested, added celery, carrots and a parm rind.  Served with farfalle pasta.  Two teaspoons of balsamic boosted the umami nicely!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I made a half recipe pretty much as written, bit I used Great Northern  instead of cannellini beans.  This dish is absolutely delicious.  I added the kale at the same time as the chicken so it would be more tender and I added a squirt of lemon juice at the very end.  The next time, I will add another can of beans so I can smash more.  It eats more like a soup than a stew, but who cares!  This is a definite keeper… so quick, so easy, and so healthy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: The flavor of this dish was terrific but the chicken texture became very tough!  The two fork shredding method was new to me and perhaps I did it incorrectly?  The chicken was inedible.  AND, I promise I had a low simmer and did not overcook the breasts.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
Kim, 
Sometimes this happens to me even when I know I didn't overcook.  I think it's the "luck of the draw" with purchasing chicken breasts.  Once in a while they're so tough and rubbery, they're inedible.  Cut up and feed to the dog or throw outside for critters.
One theory is "wooden breast" - the chickens are raised so fast that the muscle and/or soft tissues don't mature properly for weight gain time-line.  Sad state of our modern day factory farmed chicken.  
Id say give it another try.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Super good. I took the advice of others in the comments and pre- braised the chicken (in the same pot as the onion mix), tossed in some mushrooms, added a dash of cayenne, cut down on broth and pre- braised the kale. Will definitely make again. Next time I’ll add a second can of beans as I added only one.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this exactly to the recipe, except that I substituted 1/2 cup of chicken stock with 1/2 cup dry red sherry. It really amped up the flavor!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed except: used thighs, added some diced pancetta and sauteed with the onions, and squeezed some lemon in each bowl when serving to my family. As a monster snow and ice storm approaches, it made for quite a cozy and delicious meal!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Super simple and delicious. I seasoned and browned the chicken first, then removed it and proceeded with the recipe as written. A generous squeeze of lemon as a finish added a lot. Will definitely make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Really loved this - easy to make. Agreed like the kale a little softer.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Not sure how this earned so many 5 star ratings. Mine is an overly garlicky, rubbery chicken breast, otherwise bland mess. No evidence of the rosemary sprig I used. I added some powdered ginger to try to dial back the garlic, but nothing about this is 5 stars. Would likely have been far better with a shredded rotisserie chicken or a mix of chicken breast and thigh, not to mention more than just salt and pepper as seasoning. Will not make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Great easy recipe. I liked it with adding additional rosemary and beans.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I was so excited about this recipe and very reluctantly disappointed in the outcome. Yes, it’s easy, but honestly a bit too simple. We added lemon, Calabrian chiles and Parmesan at the table, but also considered soy sauce & chile crisp. By itself, it was pretty boring, I’m sad to say.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Is there a crockpot version?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: LOVED this. Couldn't be easier. I tried once to dress it up with some saffron and it was better as is. That'll teach me.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I think boneless thighs would be even better.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night.  Tasty but needs a little goosing.  Next time I'll add some red pepper flakes, and at least three rosemary sprigs.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: My husband made this day before the snow storm.  He used leftover rotisserie chicken, spinach instead of kale, an extra can of beans and a bit of lemon.  Perfect snow day meal!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: One of my recent time-saving hacks is to buy the pre-packaged, pulled breast meat from a rotisserie chicken(s). They have this at my local Kroger and at Costco. I'm going to use that in my freezer to make this tomorrow night.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was the best comfort food that I’ve enjoyed in a while! I added a few cut up already cooked mini Yukon gold potatoes, and I used a rotisserie chicken, I also only used half of the broth stated, as I did not want a soupy soup. Fast, easy and delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: How do I make this in a crock pot?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I've been stuck in a hotel apartment during this recent storm.  Here's the cheap version: used boneless chicken thighs sauteed with Mrs. Dash and olive oil, can of canelinni beans, and can of low salt Ol Glory greens, dash of minced onions.  I daresay on a day with 0F windchill, my version tasted pretty good.  Oh, did I mention the Tabsco?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I made it pretty much as-is, except I used leftover chicken that I roasted yesterday. Next time I make it, I may add more veggies, and finish it off with a squeeze of lemon juice. Of I had it, I’d pair it with a slice of crusty baguette. It’s perfect for this sub-zero weather we’re having ❄️

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Super easy, but unfortunately, very disappointing. I found the stew extremely one-note, even after adding a squeeze of lemon juice and grated Parmesan on top. I appreciate it for its simplicity but it won’t be making it into my regular rotation. If I made this again, I’d seriously up the spice amount (paprika or Italian spices), or add a glug of white wine to deglaze the pan. I wanted to love this, but it was a miss for me. Such a bummer!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I like to brown chicken first in olive oil for extra flavor before I use it in soups or stews. I just have a hard time putting raw chicken into the mix. Browned the chicken and did everything the same - except added some celery. Came out great.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I agree with several comments that the soup/stew is rather bland as offered. I added the parmesan rind (I often do that to a soup) and some lemon juice. I also added more chicken stock. I still think it is missing something to take it to deeper level....not yet sure what that is. Maybe a splash of Worcestershire? Please offer advice if you have found a way to boost the flavor/

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Gail try the Better than Bullion reduced salt chicken flavor. We add this for a bit of depth to lots of recipes.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: subbed ghee instead of butter. used dried thyme and oregeno instead and it was delicious

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Explodes with flavor!  I cooked with a quartered lemon that really enhanced the flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: It felt like too much broth…I would start with just enough to cover the chicken and keep adding as you see fit. Also, salt and pepper to onions and garlic in the beginning. Still great though. Added tons of lemon and parm at the end!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to like this but found it very bland. Added fresh dill and some lemon juice for a bit of acidity. Won’t make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I had some chicken breasts that were getting close to their best by date, and zero inspiration or motivation. I’m so happy I found this! This recipe punches way above its weight - it was super flavorful and very easy! It was a hit with my type-2 diabetic husband and my toddler. I used shallots instead of onions, and spinach instead of kale, because that’s what I had on hand. Otherwise followed the recipe closely. This is going in the rotation for sure.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This dish as presented is over the top for someone with diabetes. A serving of beans is a 1/2 cup, so a single can of beans is plenty in my opinion.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Made as written. The rosemary sprig adds wonderful depth and reduces the need for salt. The only change I'll make next time: add a third can of beans to increase the creaminess. The broth is a touch too watery.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
LOVED THIS!! My husband couldn’t stop raving. My tips:
- must use good homemade stock, it really makes the dish.
- Make a 2-3 hours before serving to let flavors meld, if possible 
- 12 oz fresh spinach worked great
- Boneless thighs were what we had on hand, would use again next time. Brown the chicken first.
- 1 small sprig rosemary next time - 2 large was a little too much
- 3 cups (not 2) Rancho Gordo Cassoulet beans with some of their liquid - these were in my freezer and perfect in this soup 
- add 2 sliced carrots after stock comes to a simmer
- add lemon juice (1/2 large lemon) toward the end
5 stars Fabulous especially on this snowbound Saturday.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Used a rotisserie chicken, plus some skin for flavor (can remove later). Used dried sprigs of rosemary, sage and thyme and left them in longer, which added depth. Used 2 cans of rinsed beans, and added a 3rd, lightly rinsed, to help thicken. If you have an immersion blender, use it! It will puree some of the beans along with the garlic cloves and spices - yum! Topped hot individual bowls with baby spinach leaves so they wilted but remained bright green. Served with lemon wedges and parmesan.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I’m wondering if substituting olive oil for butter would work. I’m trying to limit my butter intake. Anyone tried this?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
@olive oil for butter 
Should work fine. Optional: For buttery taste, add 1 T as the last step before  serving.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely the easiest meal and most delicious….

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I took a chance and brought a pot of this delicious soup to a gathering of friends, where we ate it with a loaf of good, crusty bread.  Everyone loved it . . . but we all thought it had way too much chicken.  Next time I'll just use one breast (and take the advice to add some red pepper flakes).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Wow, much better than I expected, very tasty!  I used mirapoix as I always have that on hand. I put the chicken in before the broth and beans and let it sear just a bit for flavor.  Took a bit to get it up to a simmer in my dutch oven.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: massaged the kale first and covered the dutch oven to make sure it very soft.  wonderful.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: After reading some comments I used chicken stock, an extra can of beans and thighs instead of breasts, but otherwise followed as is. Wonderful satisfying meal.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Threw the chicken breasts atop the onions, garlic, and herbs ten minutes before adding the stock -- and used dried rosemary, because it's winter and who wants to pay for a package of sprigs of which half will go to waste? Excellent result, will make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: A little bland but won't offend anyone!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I puréed most of the beans since my husband thinks he doesn’t like beans!  lol. Was delicious and he loved it. Super easy and very creamy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Made this in my Instant Pot, followed recipe as written with escarole but added 2 tsp chopped fresh ginger with the garlic,because I had some and love it in some versions of chicken soup. Credit to previous commenter for the idea

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was quite easy, didn’t take too much time more than the recipe stated (one perennial issue with Times recipes), and was quite delicious. I left the rosemary in, with no ill effects, and fished the stems out at the end. Made 1.5 x the quantity; looks like we will get eight servings from it. And three hours later the kitchen still smells great!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I used less butter and added pepper flakes. I also cut the chicken tk bite sized pieces first to save a step. Browned chicken slightly with onion

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Good! Made it pretty much as written, using Spinach and Rosemary rather than Kale and Sage because that's what I had on hand. I followed a suggestion or two in the comments, such as adding an extra can of beans (excellent suggestion) and lightly browning the chicken breasts (not sure.) The chicken was a little rubbery, maybe from the sautéeing then stewing? Served with rice and squeezes of lemon, with salad and hot rolls and butter on the side and everyone enjoyed it. Will tweak and try again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I made this stew this weekend and my husband and I loved it.  I took the herb sprigs out as directed (I used rosemary and sage) but ended up chopping the leaves and adding them back in as I wanted a more savory herb taste.  The nest time I make it I will add the herbs chopped from the beginning and also toss in a parmesan rind.  We served it over rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: So simple and so good. A new favorite in our house.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Agree that browning the chicken (thighs) first adds a lot of flavor. An old-fashioned potato masher makes smashing the beans easy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I browned the chicken a bit before adding the beans and broth and used a mix of ground thyme, rubbed sage, dried tarragon, and crushed rosemary instead of the sprigs. Added a whole lemon's worth of juice at the end (and honestly could have added a second). Topped with parmesan and served with a side of sourdough. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Used a whole rotisserie chicken, minus the skin and bones of course! It was about 1 pound of cooked chicken.  Used sprigs of rosemary and thyme, and a 5 ounce package of pre-washed baby spinach and arugula.  Added the chicken after mashing some of the beans.   Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was a great use of spare rotisserie chicken!  Just sautée the cut up chicken with the butter & onions, otherwise the chickens often too bland. And grab some sourdough!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I left my sprig of rosemary in until I added the greens, Swiss chard of which the stems were chopped and added with the onions. Tossed in a piece of Grana Padano rind and, because this reminded me of white chicken chili, a can of diced Hatch chiles.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
Made this one my own a bit -- it was delicious!

- Doubled the chicken
- Added 1 shallot 
- Added 3 green onions
- Added 2 cups Costco wild mushrooms (dried & rehydrated)
- Added 2 additional cups chicken broth, plus 1 cup water
- Added cayenne, cumin to season
- Topped with Parmesan

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This is very good for the combo of ease and taste and healthiness   I used rotisserie chicken and threw it in at the end after having used an immersion blender to emulsify the beans somewhat.  I used Parmesan rinds and a little bit of lemon at the end.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. A few minor changes: I seared the meat first just to brown it on both sides before cooking the onion and garlic. I added the kale with the beans and chicken instead of at the end. I tripled the rosemary, doubled the garlic (minced, not smashed), doubled the broth (homemade bone broth), and added a Parmesan rind that was hanging out in my fridge. I stashed ~8 cups in my freezer and can't wait to pull some out on a rainy day.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I gave this four stars instead of five because I think the chicken should have been prepared differently.  It would have been better if the chicken breast was pre-seasoned or brined prior to adding to the broth.  As written, the chicken comes across like an afterthought - some plain unseasoned chicken breast thrown into an otherwise delicious stew.  The rest of the flavors are great. I'll make it again with modification to the chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Running late one night, so I used chopped breast from a rotisserie chicken. This is a quick, delicious and filling meal. Chopped spinach also works well as a substitute for kale. Delish!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This is my new go to chicken soup! It’s quick and easy and uses ingredients that I usually have on hand. I added an extra can of beans and it was just right.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Made as posted. Easy ,delicious and nutritious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Has anybody froze the leftovers and if so, how did it?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: marvelously.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I added some Parmesan rinds into the broth as it simmered and then removed when I took out the chicken for shredding. It added a little something to the broth. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Very good recipe. The last time I made it, I felt it was a little bland and added about 1tsp of ras el hanout. I really liked this addition in case you’re looking for a bit of a twist.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is delicious and easy! I used lots of spinach instead of kale. Had only one can of white beans, and it worked. Served over rice with some grated parmesan. Very good!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Why is this recipe so high in sodium? It's so high I can't consider it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: It's the canned beans and chicken broth. If you use low or sodium free beans and low sodium or homemade broth it will be much lower.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @WB maybe because of the chicken stock and canned beans? Use low-salt stock, rinse the beans or cook your own, be judicious with how much salt you add, and you should be fine.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: You could control sodium level by adding as much or little salt as you like, and choosing low/no-sodium broth?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: think I'll try this with boneless chicken thighs. I don't mind the added fat, but does anyone think it'll hurt the flavor or texture of the soup?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I tried this with chicken thighs.  The only difference I could see is that they don't shred in quite the same way. But didn't affect my enjoyment at all

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I used thighs and it was fine.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Michelle I’m going to do the same. I think it will taste better :)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: 1.10.26. Used thighs & Thyme. Browned the thighs as suggested by others; good idea. However overall good but not great.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This is excellent and so easy. I add sauteed ground chicken or turkey (which I tend to keep in the freezer for quick access) to boost the protein content.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: How about using turkey thigh meat? It’s what I have in freezer. I’d also finish with some parm, as another suggested.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This is THE BEST main dish recipe I have ever made from The Times. Many opportunities for modifications, including the type of beans, type of greens, type of onions. Even add red bell peppers for more color and flavor. Great over flavorful brown rice. Easy to clean up preparation bowls and the rest of the kitchen during the final simmer.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This is genuinely very easy and healthy. It’s going in the week day rotation. When I have my kids I like to start with a soup the can eat all week. This meets the needs.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Flavor was excellent, but I didn't like how the chicken turned out. Next time I will try chicken thighs with 2-3 cups chicken stock, 1/2-3/4 c white wine, and simmer that all for around 20 minutes or so, as in Sarah DiGregorio's chicken artichoke stew

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe and made the soup mostly by the book. I doubled the recipe and used both rosemary and thyme. I also left the herbs in until the chicken breasts were cooked.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Used chick peas and cannellini, all I had.  And savoy cabbage. All good on a January night. !!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Simple, easy but wonderfully delicious flavor and texture. I made exactly as written (used rosemary sprig). Sautéing the aromatics in the first step is key to building a delicious and flavorful base for the soup. I served it over rice with a squeeze of lemon. A perfect cold weather/under-the weather meal.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Very easy and minimal prep. Can be halved easily if it’s one or two of you. I served it with a ton of parm on top and that put it over the edge. 4 stars no parm, 4 1/2 stars with a lot of parm.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Simple and delicious. I added carrots and celery with the onions and garlic. I chopped the chicken into chunks instead of shredding. I didn't have fresh herbs so I added a very small pinch of thyme and cracked rosemary. I used 8 oz. of fresh spinach, which seemed the perfect amount when eating the stew. I love beans, and white beans for some reason remind me of my Italian heritage. It almost took longer to write this review than it did to make the stew. Thank you!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Used 2 cups broth from cubes and 2 cups homemade. Used  chicken from cooked breast of whole bird. Added splash of white wine and a bit of balsamic vinegar. Omg yum

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: @Sarah also - Tarragon

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty lots of flavour I used spinach instead of kale.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Use escarole and add some crushed red pepper - now it's Italian ;-)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This is SO good and garlicky! The  Boursin gives it a super distinct flavor, so choose which one you put in wisely.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: What Boursin? I don't see it in the recipe, or is that your own addition?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I don't see any Boursin in this recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: I'm confused - where in the recipe is there anything about Boursin a semi soft cheese spread?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: A wonderful meal for a winter night. Served with a fresh, crusty bread and a mixed greens and slaw salad. A robust red wine and enjoyed by all.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looked really good for a cold winter day. Yesterday I bought some chicken-spinach-feta sausages so I used them by squeezing out balls of the meat from the casings. I browned that first, then added mirepoix as I wanted the extra veggies. Lots of garlic and red chili flakes went in next along with the herbs. I did buzz one can of the white beans in the blender with a bit of broth before adding it to the soup. It’s wonderful and satisfying!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious served over rice. I added turmeric to make it a little more pretty and nutritious, as well as carrots and celery with the onions, and loaf of parsley to finish. Delish!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody: Sorry I wasted ingredients on this.  Bland, kale undercooked and chicken tough.  I followed directions, so 🤷‍♀️.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
commentBody:
This is a nice recipe made exactly as is, but it came out more brothy than stew-like and a little subtle in flavor on the first attempt.
