author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Agree that this recipe is a keeper. But it is a bit too sweet for my taste. I always follow a recipe exactly the first time but next time will reduce the honey or omit it entirely.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really delicious!  Made it with blackberries, dried dates and pomegranate seeds and topped with torn mint leaves.  One of the best breakfasts in recent memory!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This sounded weird to me at first, but now I can’t stop making this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The tahini with a touch of honey adds a creamy texture to smooth out chunky curds in the cottage cheese without overpowering the natural sweetness from fruit. (I only used a couple drips of honey.) I will enjoy this again!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Was craving something warm, so made a variant on this with homemade ricotta (NYT recipe!), some Bob's Red Mill 10-grain hot cereal, lightly baked apple chunks with tahini, honey and a dash of pumpkin pie spice. Heavenly!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For the US cottage cheese - Good brand.

commentBody: @Lee Rogers Good Culture is my new favorite here in Northern California

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I can't wait to try this. It would make a great dinner or lunch as well as breakfast. But I will ease up on the almost 4 teaspoons of honey and skip the flaky salt to keep sodium in check.

commentBody:
Or just get some good quality halvah and crumble on top!
Tahini- honey is a pairing made in heaven.  I make cashew cream weekly and then make the family a cashew milk, honey, tahini shake with whats left in the blender to applause.

commentBody: Genius that the article mentions persimmon. I eat a breakfast of oatmeal and cottage cheese with a Hachiya persimmon blobbed over it whenever I can get it, come December and January.

commentBody: Outstanding! One serving: 1c cottage cheese. 3/4c mango chunks, 1tb tahini, 1tsp maple syrup

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Loved this recipe, love tahini, and love Ali Slagle's recipes and her cookbook. My dietician has been wanting me to get more fruit in my diet, and this really fit the bill along with the protein in the cottage cheese. The tahini really makes this simple dish sing. The sesame seeds may seem insignificant, but they really do add crunch. Look forward to trying this recipe with many different fruits and appreciate all the suggestions in the headnotes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wow, some things really don't photograph well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @KatieC I also noticed the good stylist left the leaves on the strawberries. She was definitely reaching to make it look good

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Surprisingly delectable!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Strangely delicious! Especially with fresh cherries.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Take the following facts into consideration, before strongly advocating for cottage cheese.
While cottage cheeses is an excellent source of protein, it lacks dietary fiber and is high in dairy components that can slow down digestion.
Dairy proteins like casein, combined with fat, take the body longer to digest, which can slow down bowel movements.
If one have trouble digesting lactose, dairy products can cause gas, bloating, and constipation .or worsen existing symptoms.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I always check the AI generated nutrition info on New York Times recipes that seem healthy-ish. They’re almost always super calorie dense but this one is under 300. Must be all the fruit. Well done.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Cut-up strawberries dusted with Splenda, then tahini and cottage cheese added for breakfast.  Forgot the sesame seeds, maybe next time.  But this is delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As soon as I came across this recipe I had to add the tahini and honey! Yum!!! I pile up all kind of fruits on the cottage cheese. For crunch and extra protein I like to add hemp hearts.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
This came out really good.  Faintly Asian because of the soy. Only a few points: 
1. Not enough sauce as written. Next time, I will double the sauce liquids and/or add some chicken stock. I also added some sherry.
2. Don’t slice the chicken before adding it to the sauce after cooking. Dried it out. And deglaze the pan before cooking the broccoli. I use sherry.
3. If you want some zing from the jalapeños, leave the pith with the pepper. I took it out and the flavor was good but no zing. 
Enjoy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: @BobInBonedale I agree on the chicken. Used breasts, cut in half height-wise. Once done, chopped into 1” wide by breast-width pieces and added to the sauce. I used Aleppo pepper flakes because I had no fresh jalapeños. Great recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: After reading the instructions, I decided to change them. With the changes, this was utterly delice! Here’s what I did. I cooked the chicken thighs - did it in the air fryer for convenience, and chopped them into bite sized pieces. I put the vinegar, soy and Clem juice in a jar and added 1 tablespoon of corn starch. I chopped the ginger and jalapeño, adding 2 chopped scallions and put in a separate bowl. Then, I heated my skillet. Added oil, then the ginger, jalapeño, scallion mix. I let that cook for a couple of minutes then added the chopped broccoli, chicken, salt, pepper, a splash of water and a splash mirin. I added the lid and simmered till the broccoli was crisp tender. Then, I removed the lid, stirred the soy slurry well, added it, and cooked for a few seconds till the sauce thickened. Finally, I seasoned with toasted sesame oil dribbled on top.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: @Essie disappointed with this one.  I do like your take on the corn starch.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This seems an overly complicated way to make what is essentially a stir-fry. Made the sauce, set aside. Heated a tablespoon of oil; sauteed the chicken thighs, which I diced and s & p'd. Remove from the pan; added another tablespoon of oil, and then added chopped garlic and red pepper flakes, then the broccoli. Sauteed until tender-crisp, then added the sauce (sans peel) and a wee bit of cornstarch slurry. When it thickened up a bit, added the chicken and combined. Served with brown rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: @CYW this is exactly what I did! Except I did not add cornstarch so sauce was too runny. Also I did not find the juice from the clementines added the sweetness I expected, so maybe add a teaspoon of sugar to sweeten it up.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This was not terribly good.  Kind of boring and salty.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I read all the notes and it turned out very well for me.  Once I cooked the chicken I put it in a warm oven with the sauce.  Then once the broccoli was cooked I added the sauce to the skillet with the broccoli and after slicing the chicken added that, mixed it all up then sprinkled the cilantro on top.  I served it with rice and served the chicken/broccoli on the rice and spooned sauce on top.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Double the sauce. At the end, slice the chicken and put  the sauce and chicken in the skillet with the broccoli, warming over low heat for two minutes. Otherwise you combine just cooked broccoli with chicken and sauce that has been sitting around for 15 minutes and is room temp. Serve with brown rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
I like the dish, great citrusy acid flavor. But it felt like it needed rice to soak up some of the sauce.

I would consider thickening the sauce too so it sticks on better. It’s really where all the flavor is coming from.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I liked this, but modified the instructions after reading the comments. I marinated the chicken in the sauce before searing it. I put the tangerine rinds in the skillet and smashed them down to caramelize them--I love the tangerine rinds in classic restaurant tangerine chicken! Next time I'll cut the rinds into strips after squeezing the tangerines, or perhaps peel the zest. I agree with others about quickly sautéing the spices and chili. It was good enough that I'll try it again soon.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This recipe turned me into the person I usually find mildly annoying…someone who changes the recipe. Despite the high stars, the comments were brutal and made it sound so blah. So I am sorry to say, I succumbed but it was delicious. I didn’t do anything too crazy…followed the recipe except for the following: for the marinade, I just used a large glass heat resistant bowl. I used 1 big orange, all I had, zested a bit but did not put the peels in. A little bit of cornstarch-1/2 teaspoon- and warmed in microwave for 1 minute. Then instead of taking the broccoli off and dumping in the bowl, I dumped the chicken and marinade in the pan after the broccoli was finished. I also used frozen broccoli, which worked great. The marinade thickened up nicely and was hot and not cold, which sounded unappetizing to me. I served it on a bed of cauliflower rice and my husband doesn’t like spice so I eliminated the peppers and just sprinkled sesame seeds only since I didn’t have cilantro. It was delicious- we loved it and will make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Haven’t made this yet but seems like sauce should be warmed

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Rather boring, not enough sauce and weird to create a room temperature dish

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: @Will Exactly. Very disappointing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe was ok. I won't make it again. Usually when I make orange chicken I coat the chicken in corn starch and use ginger and garlic in the pan then heat and thicken the sauce. This sauce was just kind of a soup that made the chicken cold. 
I kept the clementines peels in the dish. I bought them organic and washed them really well other wise I wouldn't think it would be a good idea to add them.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
This was a waste of time the saucecwas never cooked, ginger not perfumed, a cold weak dressing over cold cooked chicken and greasy broccoli.

Now, if the works had been instructed to return to pan, and after heating and bubbling a cornstarch slurry was added it might work except the ginger wouldn't have been tempered, the pepper wouldn't have been temoered/cooked and the vinegar not tempered.

Ditching because it is not properly constructed and a waste of time and ingredients. Zest tangerines.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Not bad but, IMHO, not as good as i’d hoped given the effort involved.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Good, but needed a little something. I grated the ginger which I think was helpful. Next time I’ll put the pan with the sauce and chicken in the oven on “keep warm” while the broccoli cooks. And will add the whites of 4 green onions to the sauce, or a grated clove of garlic.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is delicious with some modifications. I added a bunch of spring onions (White parts first in Green parts later). I also added a cup of shredded carrot and instead of the jalapeño I added a tablespoon or two of Sambal for some heat. Tamari instead of soy to complement the orange. Juice of 1 full orange instead of 3 clementines. Sweetened Mirin instead of plain old white vinegar and 1 cup of vegetable broth.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I heeded the earlier comments.  Increased the sauce liquids, and heated the sauce.  Poured warm sauce over unsliced chicken.  I was pleased with the results.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: the chicken should not be overcooked. I followed the recipe for the first batch of chicken but shortened the time for the 2nd and it was definitlry jucier. I also should have followed my instinct to blanch the broccoli before sautéingto get tender aflorets w/seared on outside. But the sauce is the main issue-thin and bland, needs  garlic, onions and deglazing. I ended up adding bottled teriaki sauce for flavor and thickening but I don't think I'll make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: It’s ok. Edible and gets better the more you eat likely because of the jalapeno/serrano pepper in the dish. I thickened (with cornstarch slurry) and heated the sauce once the chicken and broccoli were seared.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the other users that the sauce should be heated in some way. Here’s how I prepared it: Seared the chicken on both sides, not cooking all the way through. While searing the chicken, cooked the broccoli and sauce and written in a 9x13 for 10 min at 375. Then sliced the chicken, cooked at for 10 min at 400 to continue cooking the chicken. Turned out very delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I have been using my NYT cooking several times a week for several years and generally love the outcomes.  Occasionally there is a meh, but this was a disaster.  I wish I had read the suggestion to cook the chicken in an air fryer.  It should at least have been cooked at a medium low temp.  Even with a screen, my kitchen is filled with grease, and the thighs had a tough exterior from being overcooked on outside to get inside at temp . Hoping for leftovers, instead I am tossing the whole mess.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: So, I've quadrupled the amount of sauce, halved the amount of vinegar, added some chopped garlic and ginger to the sauce and some honey. With those adjustments it turned out way better than on the first try.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I used peeled and sectioned "cuties" -- i.e. small tangerines-- plenty of them, and added orange juice to create more liquid.  I did not use any of the fruit skin-- not sure if this might have changed the flavor, but it was delicious!  It's helpful to add a slurry of approx 1 tablespoon cornstarch stirred into 1/4 cup water toward the end of cooking to achieve a thicker sauce with a nice glazed look.  Good with Thai or other small hot chilis.  I also added some fresh garlic.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
The ingredients are all these - the technique is not. 
I completely re-worked this dish according to the notes (thank you, fellow cooks).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night, carefully following the instructions of commenter Essie. The only changes I made were to cut the clementine skins into strips, throwing them in to cook with the broccoli; and adding rice vinegar in place of the mirin.  The dish came out great, with a taste very similar to Orange Chicken from a Chinese takeout. That said, next time I will add carrots, red and yellow bell peppers, and mushrooms. The pairing of chicken and broccoli just wasn't enough to keep me interested.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Could someone advise as to the amount/weight of tofu to use instead of meat?  Thanks.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: @Kathleen Minder, think you could use same amount as the protein in the recipe

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Would it work almost as well using left over roast chicken? I'd like to avoid the step of having to pound and then slice chicken.  Sauce is different, good!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
