author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Intriguing recipe until I saw that it has 29 grams of FAT per serving.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: You don't have to use that much oil if you don't want to. That's where the fat grams are coming from. Use the minimum needed to sauté, and a splash of sesame oil at the end for flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I would have liked a more clingy sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I added the sauce back into the seared veg, then added the sliced chicken and brought back up to temp. It was too acidic for my taste. More orange juice and less vinegar next time?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except turned the sauce into traditional stir fry sauce, thickening it with flour and cooking til bubbly before tossing meat and veg back in.  Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
I use some of the sauce with brown sugar to make a deliscious citrusy sauce for the dish. 
In the pan sauté garlic, ginger in butter, add a good couple spoonfuls of brown sugar and mix over medium low heat in the pan.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: double the sauce

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
Turned out great! After reading a few comments (and based on what I had on hand) I made the following modifications:
1. I used the pre-minced ginger in the tube. Probably not as spicy/fresh, but it did combine with the sauce very well. Swapped white vinegar out and used rice vinegar. Also used broccolini instead of broccoli and it was still great.
2. After cooking the chicken, and while it rested (unsliced!) in the sauce, I deglazed the pan by squeezing a couple extra mandarins in, and added that to the glass dish with the chicken/sauce.
3. Since the chicken was primarily for my S.O. And aim not big on poultry, I crisped up some baked tofu in the pan for me, and threw that in the sauce while I prepped the broccoli. Delicious!
I loved that I could use one pan for this, and it came together quickly. I’ll absolutely be making it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Vinegar was over powering. Way too much in this recipe. Would not make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I thought this could be fairly customizable. I ended up using venison and cauliflower for the main ingredients. My husband isn't a huge fan of sesame oil, so I dropped it down to 1T and added 1T of chile oil. I also added corn starch to the marinade, and let the sliced venison marinate in the sauce before cooking. Then added the remaining marinade to the cauliflower to cook and thicken up before adding the cooked venison back in at the end.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Agree that there wasn’t enough sauce. I halved the recipe and quickly realized I shouldn’t  have. We loved the flavor. Had to use cuties because couldn’t find clementines and it was fine. I will def thicken the sauce next time. Otherwise it’s a good salad dressing?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Made this with soy curls in place of the chicken.  Worked perfectly.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this but diverged from the recipe a bit. I used oranges and to emphasize the fruit flavor more than the salt I added some hibiscus juice, apple cider vinegar, and chopped dried apricots which I sautéed. I didn’t want to buy what I didn’t have in the kitchen already so instead of the suggested pepper I used a chili sauce packet I had from Panda Express and some spicy red salsa. I added chopped onions & garlic with the broccoli. everything back to the pan to simmer/heat up, serve w/rice

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as a stir-fry with a little cornstarch added to the liquids, which were added at the end.  Will use orange or mandarin in my stir-fry in the future!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is more work than it seems. Doesn't make much/enough sauce, which isn't really sauce, as much as a marinade. Tastes ok, not great. Doubt I'll make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: To make this more flavorful, don't put the citrus skin in the sauce and then have to remove it - peel the skin off with a vegetable peeler and finely dice and add that to the sauce - you can just eat that and it makes for a more fragrant and flavorful sauce...

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Why not roast broccoli while everything else is being prepared and cooked? Then add hot broccoli to hot chicken and sauce?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I had my doubts about this recipe because of all the competing flavors, but it was a knock out! I cooked it as directed (except I used tamari rather than regular soy sauce because it is gluten free) I used 5 fairly large mandarins, so had quite a bit of juice for the chicken and broccoli to swim in. Next time, I will use more ginger, as that flavor got a little lost in the mix. One inch of ginger can vary a lot- it would be nice for an actual amount to be specified.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Labor-intensive but flavor was good and we enjoyed. I marinated the chicken first, increased the amounts for the sauce (as well as chicken and broccoli). Next time I would up the spice, marinate chicken, cook the broccoli first, then cook the chicken, remove to slice while the sauce cooks, add some corn starch. and then return everything to the pan to warm.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Agree, cornstarch should be considered as well as more sauce.  Used Serrano chile with a left over shallot to add to garlic/ginger.  Worked well and distributed the heat well.  In a perfect world, I’d use the little peppers found in traditional orange chicken.  Also, used thighs.  Though thighs are great, this might be a chicken breast recipe…cleaner flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I took everyone's suggestions to double the sauce, but I still used 5 tangerines (locally-grown!). I used 2 serrano peppers, seeds and all, and about 4 cubes of pureed ginger. I had some Trader Joe's Aminos sauce so I used that to sub in for the soy sauce. I couldn't find my toasted sesame seed oil, but I did have Korean Perilla Seed Oil, so I used that (a Korean friend said they can be used in similar ways). I added a splash of maple syrup for extra sweetness. We thought it was great!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful! Loved the tanginess of the citrus offsetting the richness of the sesame oil. Next time I'll cook it in my wok, and cut the chicken into smaller chunks before cooking. (I used chicken breasts, and they weren't quite done when I sliced them.)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: We really wanted to like this recipe. With ingredients we enjoyed mixing, we were looking forward to what we thought would be a pleasant combo of vinegary sharpness, jalapeño heat, orange acid, and nicely browned chicken, but it disappointed. Carefully working through all of the comments helped, double the sauce, add some cornstarch and chicken broth to thicken enough to coat the broccoli,  don't add salt, add sherry. Ended up being an overly complicated way to turn out a simple stir fry.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Maybe I did something wrong?! Chicken was super dry and bland. Would not make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Really good. Made half recipe but would better with full recipe of sauce and broccoli even with half the meat. Used thighs. Served on jasmine rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Seeing as how a certain percentage of folks don't like cilantro (it tastes like dirt to them, guess), does it make sense for you to include parsley or some other tasty green in your recipes?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Although I was underwhelmed by the flavor when eating this for dinner, I enjoyed mine greatly as leftovers the next day. It was like delicious leftover Chinese takeout.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: 1/4 cup of to heavy avocado oil?  Wowser!  Perhaps spray pan with Pam ans substantially reduce oil?  Suggestions for subs, dear readers?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: It was ok. I have decided that I am really not a fan of ginger. It was all I could taste, and I did add some aleppo pepper flakes as the sauce (that I had thickened a bit) was a little too sweet. And gingery.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I made a mistake with what I grabbed from the freezer - ground chicken instead of boneless chicken.  Decided to go ahead anyway - stir fried some onion and the broccoli - added the ground chicken until almost cooked, then added the sauce - Delicious last night, then again as leftovers.  Maybe the ground chicken countered dryness concerns!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Agree with previous reviewer who said to double the sauce. It’s flavorful as written but a bit dry. Double the sauce and serve with rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. I used sour Chinese oranges (sour like lemons) because that's all I had, added a couple of tablespoons of sugar to the rinds and a teaspoon of agave sweetener to the juice. I also had kumquats on hand and they made a superb addition. Quartered, seeded and tossed in whole. You can eat them whole with the chicken and broccoli and if you like kumquats they are quite wonderful that way. I agree that increasing ingredients for sauce is in order. But chicken stock would dilute the citrus.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t care for this one as the sauce was never thickened up and kind of bland despite the relatively large amounts of ingredients. The NYT roast orange chicken recipe is better and the same amount of work.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: We liked this a lot, but we also did plenty of modifications based on comments here. One thing I did differently than anyone else I saw was sear the chicken and then bake it in the oven in the sauce after adding a bit of a cornstarch slurry to the sauce. I’ll roast the broccoli next time alongside some onion. Zest the mandarin oranges. You know, basically make a completely different dish!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but I'll make the following changes next time. I used very fresh, ripe mandarins (green leaves were still attached!), but still couldn't taste much orange; I'll zest them first, and just add that to the sauce and toss the peels. Did use a cornstarch slurry, maybe a small splash of sherry as well. The chicken was good, but this might be a good recipe in which to velvet sliced chicken beforehand. Good base recipe with tweaks.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
This was delicious! the sauce to me IS the recipe- all the other ingredients could be changed. What i love about the sauce is its bright, fresh flavors - i ended up adding more orange juice to taste so that the orange flavor was just becoming a bit more noticeable. 
I do especially enjoy that the sauce is NOT thickened. i feel it's a great Spring recipe: simple, clean, bright and fresh.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: may be better with less vinegar

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I prefer healthier dishes so for this, I grilled the chicken in a grill pan with only 1 tablespoon of oil. I steamed the broccoli. Then sliced the chicken and added it all together with the sauce (I also cut the toasted sesame oil in half). I found it pretty flavorful AND healthy. Will try the cornstarch idea next time though.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I loved the flavours, though agree with many comments and will change things up a bit next time.  Did anyone find the orange instructions confusing?  What exactly goes into the sauce? The squeezed juice? The orange rind? The smushed clementine pulp? All of it?  I put all three into the sauce in the end.  Gave recipe four stars because I thought this was unclear.  But will definitely make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I saw a lot of people complain that there wasn’t enough sauce, or they decided to stir fry everything with the sauce. I followed the recipe as directed, but with a little less chicken, because all I had were two large chicken breasts, and I thought this was fantastic! I love how the sliced cooked chicken and broccoli soaked up the sauce. I agree that this is Asian inspired, but I don’t feel like it’s mimicking a saucy stir fry at all. I would advise people to try the recipe as it is before making modifications. Also, I tried this on my Blackstone grill and it worked out perfectly! This dish was light and refreshing! Definitely adding this to our rotation of simple and yummy dinners.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I'd take a couple of cues from Pailin of Hot Thai Kitchen: 1) use a quality red curry paste such as Mae Ploy; and 2) start cooking the curry paste in a small amount (~2/3 c) of good coconut milk, such as Aroy-D. The coconut milk will emulsify with the curry paste and then eventually the coconut oil will break out of the emulsion and the curry paste essentially fries gently. Use the rest of the coconut milk, thinned out with the chicken broth, for the cooking liquid. Lastly, I'd be inclined to use boneless thighs, as they do cook through in the given time, but failing that, pounding the breasts to a thinner depth seems the way to go.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Contrapunctus I took your suggestions and it yielded the best rice I’ve ever made. The slaw is trash but the chicken and rice are soooooo good. Going to add onions to the rice next time.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoy Thai food and I have overwhelmingly positive results from the NYT cooking app. But this one was a miss. The chicken takes longer to cook than the recipe states. But cooking the rice longer results in the rice becoming paste. Would recommend cooking chicken and rice separately. The zest of two limes was too much and overpowered the slaw.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Max I agree about cooking rice and chicken separately.  But I loved everything about the slaw! I didn’t use a prepackaged slaw, just savoy cabbage with everything else.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I found this dish to be quite dry. I had to scale it for 6 people but it still could have used more of the lime juice dressing. Would consider some flavor added to the slaw as well otherwise it simply adds another dry factor. Overall taste was quite nice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Dave Holland I found the chicken to be dry as well. Perhaps there’s something else I could’ve done to keep the chicken from drying out. However, I found eating it with the slaw It didn’t matter too much. I personally would’ve added less lime and more of the tangerine or orange flavor. Overall I really liked it!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Dave Holland You could always use thighs instead of breasts, if breasts are too dry for you

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Dave Holland couldn’t agree more!!!!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve recently learned the importance of “blooming” curry paste from chef/author Kenji Lopez, so I added that step first with the oil and curry paste. I decided to use mussaman instead of
red curry and it was delicious. As other said, the chicken was a little dry. I might pound the breasts next time or filet them so they’re thinner. The dressing for the slaw was delicious, with mint as the option. I added some green onion to it as well.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Chicks took far longer to cook than recipe said. I would recommend slicing the chicken first. I would also season it as the curry paste alone did not season the chicken throughout like salt would.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Brad I will either cook the chicken separately or slice the thick breasts horizontally. I will also salt the chicken first.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Thought it was pretty good - I had very thick breasts so I cut them in chucks - salt and peppered them, used the whole jar of curry paste, didn’t remove the breasts when resting , doubled the peanuts in salad.  Not a big cilantro or mint fan but used mint- the salad grew on me . For the citrus I used canned no sugar added mandarin oranges

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Tamra D.  I see the size of chicken breasts these days and imagine that the chicken must have looked like a monster when alive!   Ridiculously large.  I no longer buy them, regardless of recipe ingredients.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @PhyllisJL They make me think about hormones.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I’m confused.  What do you do with the dressing you make in step 3.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Dan M the recipe says to toss the tangerines, peanuts, slaw, and herbs with the dressing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Dan M it’s for the slaw - add the herbs, peanuts etc and toss.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: "Add the slaw, peanuts and herbs to the dressing and toss to coat."  So the dressing is what completes the slaw.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Love Ali Slagle’s recipes, but this was very bland and very dry. I tried to spruce it up to no avail. Will not be repeating.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Meh could not agree more. My wife made coconut curry sauce to give it some life.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Alright, I just made this despite the negative comments because the flavor profile seemed fun. I also changed a lot as I went and it ended up delicious. Main changes: 
- use chicken thighs
- salt the chicken 
- bloom the curry in coconut milk and oil before toasting the rice 
- use the remainder of the coconut milk from the can instead of the chicken broth. Throw 1/2 cup of chicken broth in there. 
- add a generous splash of seasoned rice wine vinegar to the slaw 

With these changes, I cooked covered for 20 minutes and it was pretty much perfect.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I love this so much! Buy

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Definitely one of the worst recipes here. Too spicey and missing more depth in the flavors.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if this would work in a pressure cooker, which would yield juicier chicken?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Ukiedancer  A pressure cooker would work well, I think. I cooked the chicken separately in the air fryer which always yields a moister chicken breast, in my experience.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Ukiedancer did you try the pressure cooker? I was wondering about using an instant pot.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone try doing this in a rice cooker?  I would definitely use more broth as normal ratio for cooking rice is 2:1.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Mistakenly bought “red curry sauce” instead of red curry paste—which differs as paste already mixed with coconut, but still “pasty.” Probably used closer to 1/3 c than 2T—but remember the paste was already diluted a bit. Left the chicken in the pot with the rice while both rested; had moist chicken and cooked rice. Would definitely make again

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Chris…I will say I needed to add more liquid to cook the rice (1c), and about 10 min more to cook the chicken and rice. The chickeb on its own was a bit dry, but as others noted, very good with the citrus salad

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I loved this. Fresh, clean and non-fancy. Easy to prepare.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This was fresh, tasty and easy to prepare. You can easily use up the small jar of Thai red chili paste.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Just don’t…

After cooking, throw out the rice and chicken and enjoy the slaw with the second round of meat you prepare.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: How would this be with thighs be more juicy?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: For those that tried it and it was too dry, do you think you could swap out the breasts for boneless thighs and it would be better?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Quite delicious!  Next time I will use more curry paste, more fish sauce, add some curry spices, and make a bit more dressing.  But, we like more spice :)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I might tinker a bit next time - yes, more juice for the dressing, and more curry paste - but it's a clever recipe, hearty and flavorful and easy to pull together.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I think this needs some modifications

I chopped the chicken to bite size pieces first, and added to the dutch oven with some salt. I also added a lot of red pepper flakes and ginger powder. I started with 2 cups of stock but that started running dry so i added more as needed like a risotto and it came out with that lava flowing consistency. 

I just ate the slaw and tangerine on the side and glad I did

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Delicious with the slaw served right on top of the chicken (which was otherwise a bit bland). Lightly salted the chicken breasts before cooking. Makes way too much rice. Will halve the rice and broth next time, and double the slaw.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This recipe needs to be tested again n. Rice stuck to the bottom of the pot but chicken breasts were raw in the middle even after I cut them in half before cooking. Just overall really poor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: My rice wasn’t cooked through (kind of crunchy) but the chicken was juicy and delicious. We all enjoyed the recipe. Will make it again but will cook the rice a bit before adding the chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The flavour is good but it doesn't look anything like the photo due to the way it's cooked. Not as pretty. I didn't like the dressing as written so added some soy sauce and brown sugar to balance the acidity of the fish sauce and lime juice - that made it quite tasty. Overall, I would do again as it was easy and tasty.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: It’s always tough to cook rice and leave it over the heat without it scorching. I’d recommend bringing the rice to a boil while stirring constantly, then adding the chicken, covering, and reducing the heat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: It has potential. My rice burned on the bottom. Too much rice left over as well. Chicken should be thighs. Salad was watery so couldn’t be stored for leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Easy "5" - exceeded expectations (perhaps from reading some of the comments)

Made exactly to the recipe with a couple of minor variations, cooking for two:
- 8 oz CH breast lightly pounded to 1/2" & divided
- cooked rice + chicken per recipe maybe < 20 min (adjusting for 1/2 recipe)
- chicken aside, cooked rice a bit longer with 1/2 C coconut milk
- sliced and served

The rice was perfect; a bit spicy but my wife ate it up well
What surprised me was the chicken. Possibly the most tender ever!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Nice, muffin-like texture. However one can clearly taste the bitterness of the stout. I usually cut the amount of sugar in US recipes (generally way too sweet for European palates) but here I feel the sweetness is needed to offset the Guinness. Next time I'll try adding some chocolate chips to go with the muffin theme or cover the cake with chocolate frosting as I feel it would benefit (but loose the delightful Guinness exterior)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: We loved the Red Curry Rice bowls last night. I used the full 1/4 cup of curry in the rice in a Dutch oven. Added chicken thighs and cooked for 25 minutes then let it all rest covered for another 15 minutes. The coleslaw was the bomb!  Added and finely chopped jalapeño. We couldn’t stop eating the salad.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely amazing. So many good flavors cleverly mixed to allow for sweet and savory, soft and crunchy.  Immediately added to our regular rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The slaw is the star of this recipe! Truly inspired. My chicken was juicy and tender but needed salt. Other than that, a nice, easy, and delicious dish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I read them all. Took notes, bloomed the curry paste, added salt. Cut the breasts into cutlets. It still turned out just like the reviews mention. I added a makrut lime leaf. The rice was mushy and the chicken was dry. We put it all down the disposal and we NEVER waste food. This recipe simply does not work.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Dan M:  See step 3 - slaw mix, peanuts and herbs are added to the dressing and eaten as a side dish with the chicken and rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Ok, there is a big difference between 2 tbsp of red curry paste and 1/3 cup.  For those of you who have made it how much did you use?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I used the small jar of Thai chili paste sold in supermarkets with no problem. Spicy but not overbearing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I'd really like some of the little peppers by the recipe so you know how hot it is - does 1/3 cup equal 3 little peppers?  Some of the NYT recipes are just too hot for me to eat. 3-4 little peppers  = very hot, 1 = mildly hot etc.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I've bought the red curry paste at the grocery store and used a whole jar and it's still bland.  

Then I bought a similar looking jar at the Asian market and a TB made us sweat... so I can see why there would be a big disparity on how much to use. 

I'm EXCITED to make this recipe - will be using the legit Asian curry and adding some Gai Lan or BokChoy for additional veggies...

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Maybe “slaw blend” is an American thing, but I’ve never heard of it — what do you put in if making your own? Cabbage, carrot, anything else?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Guy yes, it’s a widely available American thing. Typically a mix of shredded carrots, red cabbage, and green cabbage.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: @Guy. It is usually a mixture of shredded green cabbage and shredded carrots.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for an acceptable substitute for fish allergies?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The chicken is boring none of the sauce stays on it during the cooking. Surprisingly the rice is also kind of boring really liking in flavor.  The only thing that saves it is the slaw and the dressing, which are really good.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I thought the chicken was bland. The slaw is what saved the dish. It was AMAZING. I’m going to try the recipe again but think of ways to season the chicken more because it would be a great weeknight meal.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I didn’t look at comments until after making it for dinner tonight, following the recipe. I agree it was underwhelming and my chicken breast was dried out. The rice got mushy trying to cook it longer. 
Next time, I’ll use cut up chicken thighs that have been salt and peppered. I used the small jar of red curry paste available at regular super market… but a better quality red curry and subbing a cup of coconut milk instead of all chicken stock could improve it. 
For my family, the slaw was too sour. (I liked it ok.) I’d make again but use less lime zest and juice. The mandarin oranges and peanuts were a delightful touch.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This dish was pretty bland and the chicken was dry. Definitely needs a quality red curry paste. What we got from the grocery store was unsatisfactory. Even with good curry paste, however this dish doesn’t seem like it would work. The addition of a good coconut milk would help and blooming the curry pace. Definitely don’t do this dish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I should’ve read the comments before I cooked. We also had a hard time getting the chicken breast cooked at the same time as the rice. And we felt like the rice could’ve used more liquid. The slaw is fantastic. Both really need some salt and perhaps a bit more flavoring. I’m going to try a different brand of Thai red curry, and cut my chicken breasts in half or thirds next time. But I wanna give it another shot. It is fast and easy. Very little prep very little cleanup.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Two tweaks: cooked rice and chicken separately; used full amount of curry paste (mixed into cooked rice) but added a half can of coconut milk to cut the spice. 
Result was delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have any peanuts so I added some peanut butter to the slaw mixture. Not many people know it’s made from peanuts!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I liked the recipe, but was wanting a little sauce with it. I’ll fix it for the next try, because I will try it again, it hit thumbs up and yums all around! Thank you.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Yeah, hey, my first comment. I took this recipe and made my family happy. Appreciate all these chefs giving us foundations to work with! Trust me, I work with the 30/45 minute recipes first. 

Anyways, I swapped out chicken breasts with chicken thighs. Tastier, but probably defeats the original aim of the dish. That's OK. I poached them in coconut milk, slowly. And made the rice as is. Added more juice for the slaw and definitely more heat: serannos and side hot sauce for my teenagers. Love!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I don't know what other reviewers mean by dry, except maybe overcooked chicken? I sliced my chicken breasts in half to cook them in the ~20 min timeframe. Turned out perfectly and very flavorful. The slaw is a delight and a good use for a few of my toddler's Halos.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This was very, very good.  The slaw, peanuts, fish sauce and I added some avocado were a great addition. This was a light comforting winter meal.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Brine the breasts,  and they won’t be dry

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: High level comment - yum and would make again with following changes: When I started the recipe, added a larger chopped shallot to some ghee in the pan. Sautéed until translucent, added rice, red curry paste and sautéed as directed and then added 1 cup broth and 1 cup coconut milk. Proceeded as specified.  I made this with boneless skinless chicken thighs. They were not cooked when rice was done so added splash more chicken broth to the rice and brought it back to a boil, covered and cooked on low for another 5 mins. Put chicken in separate pot with some coconut milk and simmered for 5 more min. Made slaw as directed but added in some chopped ginger I had on hand left over from another recipe. Also added a teaspoon of sugar to the dressing based on comments. Used only mint as that’s what we had. Some of the rice got crispy on the bottom but that was actually yummy. Served with lime wedges and sriracha.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I found the slaw to be the star here. Everything else was a bland mush, I used more red curry paste than called for and it still tasted awful.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
I added a fresh herb blend (that paste you get at the store)- Thai spice. I rubbed that and the chili paste on the chicken (boneless skinless thighs) beforehand. I forgot salt which it could have used. My chicken cooked in 23 minutes but not my rice. I used short grain and I’m at altitude, that could have been my problem. The slaw was excellent, I used more of the citrus and peanuts and less slaw. 

All in all would make again, with some adjustments to address the rice issue. The slight marinade/rub was good, and the spice blend addition was great.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Made fundamental modifications based on the reviews and it turned out a weeknight hit. The herby peanut slaw is a flavor bomb.
--Jasmine rice in the rice cooker
--Chicken breast in the Instant Pot with red curry paste and 1 can of coconut milk (definitely needed salt), shredded in the curry sauce after cooking
--Chunky peanut butter in the dressing to give it some body, agave syrup for balance

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I’ve got a go-to red curry with chicken thighs recipe. But wanted to make something a little different. I did bloom the red curry (1/3 cup of Mae Ploy) per someone’s else’s suggestion, because I do this with the red curry & chicken thighs recipe. Using coconut milk for this is preferred, but some heavy cream and coconut oil work, too. I butterflied the chicken breasts, and used a size 30 (le creuset) Dutch oven, and found the chicken breasts sink in, so no need to add more red curry paste. I found that boiling and simmering low covered did not dry out the chicken breasts, but didn’t cook the rice. I had to continue cooking the rice by adding more broth, boiling, and the simmering again. The cabbage slaw is delicious with this. I didn’t think the rice needed additional fish sauce. I didn’t have scallions but wish I had used these to finish the dish. I’d make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I thought this dish was awesome. Following someone’s recommendation, I added a small can of coconut milk to the curry in the beginning before adding the rice. My chicken breasts were large so I cut them in half before placing on top of the rice. The rice and chicken cooked well together. With a lot of lime juice and citrus, the slaw was the perfect acid needed to balance the dish. Family loved it; will make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Got the ingredients to make this but this is the most confusing set of "helpful" comments I've ever seen on this site.  Totally not sure what to expect.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Bland and dry. I foolishly followed the recipe with no changes. I don’t know how the chicken is supposed to have any flavor without any seasoning or salt beyond the curry paste. Will not make again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I would make the slaw on its own for other recipes; really tasty (but I added more lime juice, cilantro and crushed peanuts than what was called for). The rest of this recipe is nothing special, but it wasn't bad. I followed the comments about lack of flavor and added far more curry paste and additional seasoningnfor chicken. onion or shallots would have helped but I didn't have them on hand.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: About half the rice was uncooked. Something very wrong with the recipe

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This was not our favorite. I sliced the chicken vreasts in half, as reviewers suggested (they were big), and they were overcooked. The rice was undercooked. It had a strong flavor, but not a great flavor. The slaw was decent- I especially liked the peanut bites.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Very disappointed in this dish. I followed the recipe completely…chicken was dry and rather tasteless in spite of the curry paste. I will not make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is average. You will also have to add more liquid so the rice will cook and not be chewy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: loved the slaw...the chicken was fine as was the rice but wasn't amazing- will make the slaw again though

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Too much curry in the rice. Rice did not cook all the way through. I would try less curry paste next time and cook the chicken separately.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
It's fair, but hardly a stellar Thai (sic) recipe. Made as instructed – though the instructions are a little spare in the prep – I didn't find the chicken to be dry at all, quite the opposite. But a really good curry paste is key here, and without it, there's nothing there. Would likely have benefited from coconut milk. And I suspect I didn't "bloom" the curry for long enough at the start.

Also, it works with a brown rice substitute if you double the cooking time (removing the chicken first).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Hot tip to make the slaw even better: double the lime and fish sauce plus a good squeeze of honey and olive oil, to taste.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The dish sounds awful like a banana split served over stuffed cabbage.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Ugh! I wanted to love this dish, but it completely fell flat. The slaw needs more moisture or an actual dressing  - Would recommend preparing the slaw with your own dressing and flavors. Hope this helps and happy cooking!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
We enjoyed this recipe. I read the comments and pounded the chicken breasts to an equal thickness and sprinkled them lightly with Kosher salt and let them rest while I toasted the curry paste in a little avocado oil before adding the rice. 
I served it with the slaw and the flavors of everything were excellent together.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: The flavors here were quite good. I substituted red cabbage in the carrot and some green onion and added everything else. The amount of dressing is sparse you gonna double up on the lime juice and the fish sauce and the rice needs cooked with a little bit more liquid. Everything needs chopped to precision.  It’s very light and I love how there is no oil other than in the peanuts.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night and followed directions mostly as written. I used more broth than the recipe called for. I used a whole jar of the red curry in the rice and on the chicken breas. My chicken was juicy. I made the slaw as directed but added a bit more of the lime juice to balance the fish sauce. It was a bit one note but that being said, I found it to be tasty when all the components were eaten together.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I can’t comment on the cooking technique as I used some leftover grilled chicken breast, but I thought all the flavors were delicious. The rice had an excellent level of spice - I recommend ensuring you let it toast in the curry paste on the longer side, I got better flavor results that way. I skipped the tangerines in the slaw as I just wasn’t feeling it and imo it was still so good. The amount of dressing was perfect, it flavored the slaw well without producing any excess liquid. The best part: it’s quite easy and fast to cook. Perfect for weeknights!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I love garlic, but 3 grated cloves is too much. I would reduce the garlic and add a sweet element to the slaw. The chicken took way longer than the rice and was dry and bland. Either brine the chicken and then cut in halves, use thighs instead, or even swap for brown rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was a bust. The flavors were poor together. I like garlic cooked rather than raw so my mistake.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: This seems like a recipe that could work being made directly in a rice cooker. I generally have luck with proteins and rice being done at the same time within the rice cooker. Going to test it out. Has anyone already tried?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I just saved this (I think!) recipe after seeing it on Instagram reels. That post mentioned papaya, but I don't see papaya here! I would love some guidance about how to include it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Can this be prepared in a rice cooker?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: Many commentators remark that the chicken seems dry and takes longer to cook than the rice. I have taken to slicing chicken breasts horizontally in half and velveting them before cooking (Chinese-style). This results in faster-cooking, more tender chicken.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I love Thai food and all curries, but wow this recipe dried out the chicken AND rice, and has very little flavor. The slaw was also over powered with lime. Maybe I had some very juicy limes on my hands. But unfortunately this was a disappointing dinner.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly. Chicken and rice cooked perfectly. The salad is delicious as part of the ‘bowl’. Definitely cook again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: So glad I read the notes first! Grateful for you all, as always. Took the advice on using coconut milk with the rice and butterflied my chicken breasts. Used about a quarter cup of chili paste from H-Mart. Cooked chicken and rice for 25 minutes. Only zested one lime, added a splash of soy sauce, and navel oranges for the salad. Put the chicken pieces under the broiler for 2 minutes at the end just to add some texture. Absolutely delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody:
Using chicken breasts in the recipe is a big mistake, it was very dry and tough!!!!
if and that's a if, I was to make this again I would use chicken thighs.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
commentBody: I have never written a comment on a NYTimes recipe before, though I have cooked many successfully. This just didn't work for me. The rice cooked unevenly -- and, worse than that, the rawest rice was under the chicken (which was cooked, bordering on overcooked depending on thickness); I had to remove the chicken and then try to scrape raw rice off of it. At minimum, pound the breasts and cook the rice and chicken separately. But probably just try a different recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My non-stick oan is my beloved cast iron skillet. Instead of flipping the eggs, once the edges are done I stir slowly with chopsticks from the center out. It’s a wonderful ritual.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Minus the vinegar (I know, a big omission) this is what my grandfather taught me when I was a little kid.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Gave eggs more flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious and simple. Cooked and eaten in less than 10 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I questioned the vinegar, but I say go for it!  I used a dash of red wine vinegar with no regrets.  I had farm fresh eggs, so my dish was brilliantly orange.  Ate these out of the pan!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tried this today just minutes after reading this recipe. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: White vinegar?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Guess what I had for dinner! The vinegar is important: tried a few bites without, then added just the tiniest schpritz using my fingers. It transformed the eggs from overwhelmingly unctuous to sublimely balanced. All that butter is keeping it off my list of weekend go-tos, but when having guests over for brunch, oh myyyyyy, yes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
The eggs had a heavy cooked butter taste. Not
What I like for scrambled eggs

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
The eggs had a heavy cooked butter taste. Not
What I like for scrambled eggs  I personally prefer a lighter, fresh flavor.
As for the vinegar,  I found it added nothing   Why add something to cover up the flavor of another ingredient?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I am sure glad I read this - my eggs today were so much more flavorful - without any extra work or ingredients!   Thank you.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Is there some reason not to just add the vinegar to the eggs before they're cooked?  Seems easier and more evenly distributed.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Recipe seems great. Followed to a T. Medium heat. Chocolate color browned butter. Less than 30 seconds off the heat when edges started to cook. Rubbery eggs. No real Curds. Not shiny. Will try again but bummer

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My Greek Grandmother always let the butter get slightly brown before adding the eggs.  I've been doing the same for 81 years.  That nutty flavor is a winner in our home.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I've made a version of these for years and bring them to Christmas parties. I use goat cheese instead of blue cheese and mix the cheese with a touch of honey and cinnamon. They look festive arranged on a round plate.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I make a version that combines the bleu (or goat) cheese with cream cheese and it topped with a toasted walnut. No need for the nut to be candied; the date is plenty sweet.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I agree. Candied nuts on top of dates sounds cloying.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jennifer I use candied pecans with goat cheese, and it is actually a good balance.  Can’t say for bleu cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Seems like starting with pitted dates would make this faster.

author: Ali Slagle
Ali Slagle
commentBody: Debbie - Re your comment that starting with pitted dates seems as if it would make this faster, it might but pre pitted dates are never as fresh and good as ones that have pits in them and pitting a Medjool dates is less than a finger snap of time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Debbie Agreed!  I make a similar date appetizer and use pre-pitted Medjools that I find in “sundry” area of my favorite grocer’s produce section.  So easy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I stuff them with blue cheese and wrap a small piece of prosciutto around them.  Then I bake them in a 400 degree oven for 8 minutes.  The salty prosciutto and melted cheese make a lovely combination.  Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made a few of these a while back, kinda too sweet for my taste. So I made some with lightly salted toasted nuts, all kinds. I think the macadamias and the pecans were best.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These are always a hit... The sharp tang of the blue cheese balances the sweetness of the date.  I tuck a toasted pecan in with the cheese.  I keep these ingredients on hand and at the ready!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have made this combo for years, after having eaten them at a small French cafe in Victoria B.C (of all places!).  I use sliced almonds because I like them better.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: i do these with gorgonzola and roasted walnuts…and often a bit of prosciutto or bacon

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These look great, but I don’t understand the 2-hour advance limit.  I would think these could be made far more in advance - I really don’t want to be making anything two hours before a party!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The only thing I can think of is the salt of the blue cheese might make the dates weep a little, but I'm not sure dried dates even have enough moisture to weep! I've made them wrapped in bacon and Frozen them with no issues so who knows 😁

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious. For a variation on the theme, would recommend omitting the pecans, wrapping the cheese-stuffed dates in bacon, and baking them in a 400-degree oven for 30 minutes, until bacon is done. Break out the toothpicks and enjoy the acclaim.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My mother made these in the 1960’s when we lived in Portugal.  She use fresh pecans, not candied.  I make these all the time and people really like them if you like blue cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Elaine Uh…my family lived in Portugal from 64-66, in Estoril, outside Lisbon. When and where were you?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My mother made these every Christmas, with walnuts instead of pecans and cream cheese in place of blue. Dates are so sweet that the cream cheese tasted like a sugared cream filling.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: ...add a drizzle of honey, as well

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as written.  Easy.  Yummy.  I think candied walnut halves would work too if you don't have pecans handy.  As serving size comment, the recipes says 14 but although it makes 14 dates, I'd say plan on 2 per person.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: They're also delicious without cheese and simply stuffed with two whole almonds and one walnut half all standing upright like soldiers!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have done a variation on this, using brie cheese and chopped mixed nuts. What a great combo! The dates are so sweet that the honey isn't strictly necessary, but it doesn't hurt, either.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yummy! I used goat cheese as that's a little more palatable than blue cheese (depends on your crowd) and a hit.  Drizzled with honey and people kept going back for more.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Loved them just as the recipe called for.  So did everyone else I made them for.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The BEST dates come from Rancho Meladuco in California.  Once you’ve tried their dates - there’s no going back.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy peazy and delicious appetizer! I disagree with others who say the candied pecans make it too sweet. The blue cheese and pecans are a wonderful mix of sweet and tangy goodness!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Taking these to a party tonight. Will report back!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Stellar. These have been the hit of every event I’ve taken them to this season. And they’re so easy to make. Double win

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: These sound great!  I like a simpler variation.  Pit the date and open it up, add about a Tablespoon of whipped cream cheese and add 2 walnut halves on top.  So quick and so good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I was worried about the spiced nuts being too sweet so I used plain, which I regretted. The flavour was still great and people gobbled them up, but I think the spiced nuts would have added an extra kick.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wow!  This is a delicious dish!  A nice dessert that is easy to assemble.  10/10!!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The Medjools I bought were quite large. and we found that halving them and using less cheese to fill made it easier and neater to eat as an hors d'oeuvre. The candied pecans I bought were not a pretty topper and added too much extra sweetness. Next time I'll make my own very lightly sweetened candied pecans or just use a roasted pecan! However, I'm happy to have discovered this recipe and all the good suggestions. Thanks everyone!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this recipe for Thanksgiving using a similar NYT recipe with olive oil and black pepper instead of honey/chili.  It was gone in a flash (I doubled the recipe). The one comment that was spot on is to use a mini processor, not a hand mixer.  The results are simple yet outstanding.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written (with Calabrian chili paste as the spice), and the moment I tasted it, I regretted not doubling it! The whipped ricotta becomes so light and airy, and the swirl of sweet and spice is sublime! I served it with baguette, and everyone from the family foodie to the pickiest eater adored it! It disappeared in moments. I’m going to make it again tomorrow. Thanks for another wonderful recipe, Ali Slagle!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Even extremely "creamed" Ricotta can still have a grainy texture.  I suggest other cheeses, especially cream cheese or a blend of cheeses instead.  Also, hot honey is good, but try Sweet Chile sauce purchased from an Asian grocer, because many of the brands from regular markets are flavorless.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jeanne Bishop The graininess of ricotta varies by brand.  My favorite is Galbani.  It will get smooth following this recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jeanne Bishop definitely agree with the sweet chili sauce, for a simpler version of this one of my go-to snacks is plain whipped cream cheese with Thai sweet chili sauce with some sort of buttery club cracker, it’s delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Hamish MacBeth I do the same and sprinkle with chopped coriander, even more delish

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it just seemed very bland. It was fine with a crudite and crackers but I think it might be better with an addition like pesto or raspberry puree or something to dress it up.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
What if you don't have a food processor? Can you use something else?
@Dan

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Caitlin If you have one, try your blender. I do have a food processor but my blender is on the counter so I often use that instead. It works just as well. I should note that my blender has a small, bullet-style container. The standard blender container might be too big for this recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wanted to love this, but it didn't quite get there for me. The ricotta did whip up beautifully in my mini food processor, but it was also runnier than I prefer for a spread/dip. I wanted the flavors incorporated throughout, so I stirred them in fully then topped with some blobs that I swirled with a toothpick. This is what I used for flavoring: Calabrian Chili Orange spread (jam), roasted red pepper chili paste, and a smidge of Sambal Oelek for more heat. It was good, but not fantastic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could one just use hot honey?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I had something similar at a roadside diner that blew me away - on toast with sunny side eggs on top.  HEAVENLY.  Missy's Main Street in Rockaway, NJ.  Hope it's okay to share the restaurant!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Nicki OMG, that’s my hometown! I’ll need to go to Missy’s when I’m back there to visit.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you want a variation that's sweet and a little spicy (I can't tolerate spicy foods), use jalapeno raspberry jam. Simply heat in the microwave for 30 sec to make it more pourable. If you don't have a food processor, use a whip and mix it with softened cream cheese. Another alternative is adding lemon zest from 1/2 lemon. And if you want a sweet version, add the lemon and 1/2-1 c powdered sugar. The possibilities are endless!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I've made this with 1/2 mascarpone and 1/2 ricotta. The honey chili flavor from the chili crisp I used--hot!--was delicious. The texture was light and airy. The mascarpone makes it a touch sweeter than plain ricotta.   Replacing the chili crisp with jarred pesto is also delicious. Having both options, with a tray of freshly-crisped baguette chips, makes a pretty plate.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Is there any reason all of the ingredients couldn't be incorporated in the food processor?  Also, why "serve right away"?  Couldn't this be made in advance of a party, say, early in the day?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have the same q. Why not whip the honey and chili paste into the ricotta?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @DS this was our question an and the swirling technique left picked of no flavor other than whipped ricotta which is bland. IF we made this again I would 100% just put it all in the processor and maybe just add a drizzle of honey between the two layers and on top.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Fred I tried out the suggestion of putting everything into the food processor - though I haven’t done the original preparation to fully compare, I’m quite pleased with how it turned out. I think I’ll always do it this way :)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used an immersion blender to whip the ricotta. On high speed, the ricotta turned into lovely fluffy creamy texture with no grains.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This received loads of compliments from my book club but next time I'll get regular Calabrian. I used Chile Spice, which kind of overwhelmed the flavor and made it taste more Asian-y -- like something you'd smear on rice and hot chicken? I ended up redoubling the sweetness to calibrate it and at the last minute stirred in a bunch of agave and cream cheese. Still a lovely and super easy treat and good on a plate of vegetables and apples!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: You can also make this with the Friendship Whipped Cottage Cheese for a healthier and higher protein option.  It's delicious.  Adding a tablespoon or heaping teaspoon of apricot preserves is another adding touch - the sweet and hot honey combination - so good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I do not have a food processor, but I do have an immersion blender with a whisk attachment. Would that whip the ricotta as well as a food processor does?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I don't have a food processor anymore - instead I have a Vitamix blender.  Could I use it instead?  Will it work?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can you substitute cottage cheese for Ricotta?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If you want to make a totally sublime whipped ricotta dish just as simple as this one use whipped ricotta, honey and fresh basil.  Optional addition is to top it with slivered almonds.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Well, I had high hopes and I followed directions exactly, but this just didn't hit the spot for my family. My ricotta (whole milk) never became smooth and fluffy, and the flavor was underwhelming. Given all the positive reviews, I'm not sure where I went wrong, or if this is just not for us.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very good.  Takes minutes and you can prepare it in minutes. Note instructions state "serve immediately" I layered it (first layer) and refrigerated that plus what remained in the mini processor 2 hours prior to and finished it right 10 minutes or so prior to serving. Did not seem to impact flavor, did not separate, was not grainy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My girlfriend just made this and said it was awful. She threw it all out. Boo!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe did not work for me. It was bland and too sweet for crudites or crackers. The combination just tasted odd. Very few misses with NYT recipes, but in my opinion, this was one of them.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I tried to make whipped ricotta last weekend and it didn't whip it was very liquidy. Would I have better luck if I strained it first? That step is not in these directions... I used a ninja food processor and I felt like I did it for way longer than 2 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: In the IP it will take unsoaked beans between 45 - 70 minutes, depending on their age. If you pre-soak it will take 20-ish minutes. My suggestion (and what I personally plan to do) is to soak the beans and cook the whole thing together for about 20 minutes. I would use the same amount of water to ensure the beans cook properly. If there's too much liquid at the end, just uncover and set to "saute mode" to boil it off.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sis I used the instant pot  to make this dish.  It worked great and only one pan to clean!. I Started by browning the meat on the sauté setting, deglazed the pan, then used the slow cook setting (low) for 6 hours. I used Rancho Gordo Corona beans. did not soak in advance. After 6 hours on the low slow cooker setting I opted to  switch to  10 minutes high pressure to finish cooking the beans. Beans perfect and meat was tender and delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Question:  What is a "garlic head halved crosswise"?  Is it a head (bulb) of garlic sliced in half so that the slice is parallel to the bulb's growing position?  Or what?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It means cut the entire head of garlic (containing all of the individual bulbs) straight across the middle, horizontally (perpendicular). You’ll essentially be slicing each clove in half, while keeping the head of garlic together. When the two half-heads are added to the Dutch oven in the first step, they’re placed cut-side down, allowing the now-exposed garlic to make contact with the heat at the bottom of the pan. It’s also easier to remove the garlic (and its peel) this way before serving.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I apologize for being a bit unclear in my first response. The entire head of garlic is the bulb. The individual cloves inside are not bulbs, although I mistakenly called them that at one point. However, if you plant an individual clove of garlic outdoors in the fall, it will grow to an entire bulb by next summer and can be harvested. Garlic is very easy to grow.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I typically soak dried beans overnight unless they are lentils or split peas. I often use my pressure cooker to cook them for 10 minutes or so in stock suited to the recipe. I then add them to the sautéed vegetables and protein if using. I can’t consume spices the way I once did. I find I’m enjoying the flavor of the ingredients more with a subtler seasoning than my younger self’s heavy handed peppering. It’s been a process. Yes to smoked spices/ additions and savory friends such as olives and anchovies, as well as the brightening powers of interesting vinegars or citrus peels and juices. 
I love adapting recipes with what I have on hand- stretching my resources rather than rushing out to buy ingredients.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Kaz - Cannellini beans are a type of white kidney bean and like red kidney beans they do need to be boiled to remove the lectin.  Corona beans are a runner bean but most people will likely think of the Royal Coronas, the giant one, sold by Rancho Gordo and they take a LONG time to cook plus a pre-soak to get right. Apple cider vinegar on its own best matched the flavor profile but the addition of an equal amount of boiled cider rounded the acid notes.  Do this to taste.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The recipe doesn't say but definitely soak the beans overnight or use the quick method (sort & wash beans; put in large pot & cover with 2" water; heat to boiling & boil for 2 minutes; take off heat, cover, & let sit for 1 hour; drain). Dried beans won't cook in only one or two hours, or they won't cook enough. They'll be semi-hard & undigestible. Also, skip the garlic bulb & use minced garlic instead---easier, less mess & same result.

commentBody: Made this tonight:  Did a fast boil for the beans, soaked for an hour, drained and put everything together as directed. Roasted the garlic separately, used 2 pounds of pork shoulder since most of my meals are intended for 2 dinners (for the 2 of us). No salt until beans were nearly done, which took about 45 minutes. Meant to fry some sage leaves to add at the end but forgot, so topped off with Romano.  Better than I expected, and I’m sure the leftovers will be even better. Great recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I converted this to an InstaPot prep; very flavorful prep. One nit: "cover with 3" of water"; that presumes everyone is using the same size pot. Why not determine an actual measure instead?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: On the contrary, depending on what size pot you use, covering with 3" of water could be a wide variety of measurements and not one fixed measurement. Step 2 is correct.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: How long the beans take to cook depends on a lot of factors. I'm able to buy freshly harvested dry beans at my farmers market. I cooked a pound yesterday on the stove top in about 90 minutes without soaking. I salted the broth pretty early--another factor which is said to impair softening of the beans. I was able to pull the papery husks of the garlic head out of the pot well before the beans were done. Garlic was also from farmers market. It's all highly individual.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @CB Where are you with a farmers’ market with freshly harvested beans? I’m jealous.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: There is a ton of research and articles out there now to show that salt does not impede softening of beans. Serious Eats has a good one: https://www.seriouseats.com/salt-beans-cooking-soaking-water-good-or-bad I always season at the beginning and the beans always soften.

author: Ali Slagle

You could substitute country style pork ribs for the pork shoulder-might be easier to find in a 1 pound package.  Squeeze out the garlic bulbs into the sauce!  Enjoy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Love trying new recipes- this one was well worth it! Used vegetable broth instead of water- delicious.  Put my slow cooker to work, with dried beans (Pinto) - no overnight soak.  Cooked perfectly after 8 hours on low. Pork fell apart so easily, and complimented the creaminess of beans so well.  Can’t wait to share with friends for a “Fall” get together this weekend.  Thank you Ali! 😊👍

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Did this with lamb and Rancho Gordo corona beans and added crushed fennel seeds and a sprinkle of red pepper flakes. Started on the stovetop using a bit less water then baked covered at 300 for about 4 hours until beans were silky and meat fell off the bone. So delicious! Be sure to season with plenty of salt, even early on, the beans need it (and it will not prevent them from softening, that's a myth). Will be serving with crusty bread, lemon for squeezing, and a side of greens.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this on Sunday for lunches and it's absolutely delicious! I probably let it go a little longer than two hours because my beans needed a bit more time, but the flavor is incredible. The garlic and fennel melted into the broth and the pork is so tender. I will definitely make this again for easy lunches to bring to work.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I couldn’t find dried cannellini beans at any nearby grocery stores so I tried canned cannellini beans. Also couldn’t get pork shoulder unless I wanted a very large roast so I decided to try some pork stew meat. The stew meat turned out to be tenderloin which was ok but not as good as shoulder.  I did cook everything much less than the recipe stated but the beans still turned out mushy. Tasted pretty good but the texture was lacking. If you have to use canned beans then definitely reduce the cooking time and add the beans  towards the end. Also, my personal preference would be to double the fennel next time. 
In the last couple of years, grocery stores in my state have been allowed to sell liquor so that means they have reduced their grocery offerings. It just never occurred to me that a staple like dried  cannellini beans would get dropped!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: To soak or not soak largely depends on the age of your beans. If you are buying beans from a store or supplier that regularly moves their bean product and turns it over so that you’re getting beans within a year of harvest, you generally don’t need to soak them. If you’re cooking beans that have been languishing in your pantry or the grocery store shelf for a long time, a soak will help with hem. I find, however, that larger beans like Coronas always need a soak, even better if they are soaked in salted water (1 TBS Kosher salt per pound of Coronas in 8 cups water works for me).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
The recipe says to put the garlic in halved, unpeeled then to add the remaining ingredients. The recipe never says to remove the garlic after it browns.

You can't stew this recipe with halves of unpeeled garlic especially with the root end.

I've been using Times recipes for years and they have been great. I've been running into problems like the with The Times recipes many times in the last few months. Disappointing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Problem with this recipe -

I’ve cooked many braising recipes that include garlic prepared this way. It ends up adding lots of delicious flavor. 

Keep the garlic bulb whole. Remove some of the outer papery husk, but leave enough to keep the bulb intact. Cut the garlic bulb in half horizontally so that all the cloves are cut in half. 

When the recipe is finished, there is yummy garlic flavor throughout the whole pot. Depending on your preference about how garlicky you like things, you can squeeze the softened garlic cloves into the pot, or onto crusty bread, or not.

I disagree with your premise, though: this site provides recipes and directions, but it relies on the idea that we already know how to cook. To keep directions on the shorter side, it doesn’t usually include every single detailed direction (although they sometimes provide great helpful videos). When I need more information, I look elsewhere online, or I read the comments.

commentBody: This was extraordinary. Threw in a Parmesan rind and wow, everything came out rich & decadent—real stick-to-your-bones stuff. My Alubia Blanca beans were done in a little over an hour; if your beans are still chalky after two, I’d suggest finding better beans!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’ve noticed NYT tends to have recipes that say to add water instead of adding broth. The solution to this is to always have at least one jar of better than bouillon in your fridge. Also, miso paste, both of which I added to this recipe (along with a Parmesan rind and some lemon peels added with about an hour left) to enhance the flavor!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is a wonderful, easy meal, satisfying and not ordinary.  Really great for a winter evening.  Prepped and put in pot around 3:30PM, ready to eat by 6:30PM.  Comments about being bland - you’re allowed to adjust seasoning elements to taste.  I added 1 t. Better than Bouillon chicken to the water, and  1/4 C of dry white wine (French Sauvignon Blanc), 1/2 of a white onion, sliced, and a couple shakes of crushed red pepper about 20 minutes before finish.  Also juice of half lemon at the end.  Used Rancho Gordo cassoulet beans - cooked to perfection in about 2.5 hours, but let it go a bit longer for extra flavor.  I cut the pork into smaller pieces, more like 1/2-3/4” bite-size cubes, and no need to shred at the end.  Did not have whole sage leaves, used dried crushed leaves from CSA.  Would be very nice with a green salad but didn’t have anything on hand so just the stew for s.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have been cooking beans for 45 years and during this time I’ve lived in four cities. The time it takes to soften beans when cooking depends greatly on the mineral content of the water used. My first house had well water and beans would *never* get soft. City water improved softening, now I have whole house water purification that removes most minerals and beans get soft very quickly with no soaking. You might try using different water if you are having trouble softening beans. BTW I love this recipe!!
