commentBody: A drizzle of high quality flavorful olive oil makes it extra yummy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Good breakfast fuel option before my morning swim. I subbed chives from my garden (as that's what I had), and spread it on toasted bread. So maybe it's better described as a spread instead of a dip.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can we just drain the cottage cheese to make it more firm?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Charlotte
Bingo!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Excellent recipe!  Tried with Mary's Gone Crackers super seeds and it was delicious.  Will try with cukes & celery next.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was quite enjoyable!  I was limited on my cottage cheese selection when I decided I needed to make it - 2% was the highest fat content available with larger curds. I do think the higher fat thicker cheese would be even better - next time.  However I did whizz the 2% through the food processor to "thicken" it up and added a heapin' dollop of sour cream. It made a nice lunch with crackers and cucumbers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe has me convinced not to hate cottage cheese anymore. But you have to use the 4%.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Is it a healthy dip? Absolutely!
Does it taste good? Not really!

I think with some tweaking and twisting other recipe it could be decent. But then it wouldn't be healthy. And I'm assuming that was the point of this recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I use 2% Good Culture cottage cheese and find this a very satisfying substitute for higher-fat and calorie dips.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Such a great snack. I only had caper berries, which are a milder flavour profile than capers so I chopped them up super fine and added some lemon juice to brighten the whole thing up. Game changer!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: 4% cottage cheese on toasted bread or a bagel, topped with tomatoes, or chopped olives, or chives, and later everything bagel seasoning was a thing I copied from my Mom. I saw her doing it in the 70s and beyond!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'd like to make this.  I wonder if mixing in Greek yogurt would give it a little more smoothness (so it doesn't just feel like someone dumped in a quart of cottage cheese) would make it more creamy and with more tang?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could I use ricotta for this?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Boston Gal after reading the recipe, I was wondering the same. I think it would work; maybe drain it in cheesecloth first, especially if it’s part-skim.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Boston Gal nope. The curd police will come and get you.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Friendship cottage cheese is the very best.  I make a vegetable cottage cheese recipe handed down by my family.  It’s prepared with diced cucumber, scallions & radishes incorporated into a mix of cottage & farmer cheese, with a spoonful of sour cream.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My Polish parents would appreciate this

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Also good for a zero effort breakfast: an ounce of cottage cheese (Daisy, Trader Joe’s, or Knudsen in California) on toasted sourdough with the Everything seasoning.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Friendship 4% California style cottage cheese is my favorite.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have been doing a version of this forever but seasoned differently; sesame oil on cottage cheese is AMAZING; freshly ground black pepper alone with radishes and arugula; another great thing on cottage cheese is tajin or chaat masala.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can anyone please tell me why the recipe for the spice blend suggests that when kept in an air-tight jar, if will only be good for several weeks? Thanks.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I didn't think this was all that great when I first made it, but the next day the flavors were much stronger and way more interesting.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Friendship brand is very good, almost as good as the now disappeared Good Culture was in its day.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: To make this more of an actual dip, buzz the cottage cheese in the food processor until smooth then add the other ingredients. Helps for those who don’t enjoy the consistency of cottage cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I like the concept, but am trying to figure out how the arugula can work--short of a bagel.  I see that the recipe says "bite-sized pieces" (vs. photo).  So, in theory, this should work with the suggested dipping implements?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: White this is stellar with great cottage cheese - Nancy’s or Good brands are my fave, it also is delicious with Trader Joe’s 4%, even TJ’s low fat or Daisy in a pinch. It’s a wetter texture with those but I gobble it down no fail. This has become my new favorite easy dip! With the sturdier cottage cheese I love it with cucumber slices, and the wetter stuff is great with crackers.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe as directed, except I used 18 year balsamic rather than red wine vinegar as that’s what I had on hand. I also tossed some baby yellow potatoes on the sheet pan with everything else.  Easy, delicious weeknight dinner that we loved.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I had everything home to make half this recipe, which is perfect, since I live alone and deeply appreciate leftovers. It is so easy and incredibly delicious! I did use the pork tenderloin from Trader Joe’s, which is already seasoned with garlic and cracked pepper, so I only salted and peppered the tomatoes and garbanzo beans before roasting. I roasted everything for 20 minutes, and after 5 minutes of rest, the thickest part of the pork was 150 degrees. I think if I were serving this to guests, I’d sear the tenderloin before roasting, but especially for a weeknight easy dinner, this was great. My cherry tomatoes veered on the larger side, which meant a smaller tomato to pork ratio, but crunchier garbanzo beans, since there was less juice across the pan. I’m out of couscous, so I served it over tri-color quinoa, drizzled with TJ’s creamy vegan dill dressing (SO good), alongside a Greek salad. I can imagine setting down a platter of thinly sliced pork, tomatoes, and garbanzo beans with piles of pita bread and sauces, etc., and let my guests assemble sandwiches. This is a keeper!!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Maybe the best effort to flavor recipe I’ve made in a long time. I will use the oregano vinaigrette in many other ways. I did give the pork a quick sear but I don’t think it impacted the flavor.  I might add more cherry tomatoes but other than that this is a perfect recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I loved this the second day as leftovers. The first day the chickpeas had not had enough time in the vinaigrette to have much flavor. Next time I will marinate the chickpeas in the vinaigrette for awhile first. And there will be a next time as I really do like this dish!!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Easy one-pan recipe. The tenderloin did turn out a bit grey. Would recommend browning the tenderloin in a pan first

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Very easy to make and tastes great.  I only had one tenderloin about 1.6 lbs, but I kept the rest of the ingredient amounts the same. I am looking forward to the leftovers and making this again. I did need to roast about 30 minutes but that can change with the thickness of the meat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I just made this today using just one tenderloin but kept everything else the same.  At the same time, I had a separate sheet pan with russet steak fries and pole cut red onions that I first put in a bowl with garlic salt and a tablespoon of olive oil.  I put that in the oven first.  That part was done cooking about 5 minutes after the pork sheet pan. When it was all done, I mixed it all together and added the oregano vinaigrette to all of it.  I like having more veggies and it turned out great!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: We aren’t crazy about chickpeas, anyone try substituting cannellini beans?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
I used canned cannellini beans and also did the few minute broil at the end. Beans were fine. Creamy texture. I’ve made this before with the chickpeas and liked that too.

I sliced leftover pork as thin as possible and packaged it in the leftover tomato and bean mixture. With some feta, that leftover mixture made delicious pita sandwiches a day or two later!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Easy one-pan recipe. Pork turned out a little grey IMO. Would brown it in a pan first next time. Would also cook the chickpeas and tomato a bit first before adding pork if you like it slightly pink.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: @Zoey just made this for dinner tonight as well. 100% agree With comments about grey meat, out it under broiler for a few minutes in each side-gave the meat and the tomatoes a nice char. Definitely going into rotation

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: *0* to 6 servings?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic, easy meal. I seared the pork first, to good results. I also put the sheet of tomatoes and garbanzos in the oven for about 10 minutes before the pork. Also good. The oregano chimichurri-like vinaigrette really makes this dish shine.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I really wanted to follow the recipe, but I the cupboard did not support me. Not enough tomatoes, and some of my garden ones were still a little green. That started the revision. I added a few halved caltrava olives, and a tablespoon of green capers. I had only fresh oregano, so minced that with double the garlic. Not sure if my oven ran hot, but the pork was cooked and the chickpeas too crunchy. Added and some leftover crushed tomatoes to pan and cooked while the pork rested. Yummy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I married this recipe (sans vinaigrette)  with the sheet pan tomatoes, chickpeas, feta dish and it was fantastic!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This might be my all-time favourite NYT recipe. I have made it with Trader Joes' seasoned pork tenderloins when holidaying in California, and at home in Alberta with PC Greek seasoned pork loins. Cooking times change, but the essential recipe, the oegano vinegrette drizzled over the pork, chickpeas and roasted tomatoes, is amazing. Tonight I served it with Melissa Clark's Potatoes Anna, and her Broccoli Salad with Garlic and Sesame. Our friends loved it! Tomorrow's leftovers will be sublime.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I must have done something wrong because this didn't have much flavor.  And several reviews say that people are using already seasoned pork loin. Definitely need to brown the meat...either in a grill pan or under broiler.  And cooking time depends on the size of the pork loin...mine were large and took almost double the time in the oven.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. Made one small change. Rather than searing it in a pan on the stove as some folks suggested to give it nice color. I sprinkled the tops of both tenderloins with paprika to give it a nice color before roasting.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: The tomatoes weren't bursting open so I switch to the broiler for 5 minutes. This was perfect for the tomatoes and added some color to the tenderloin and the vegetables. Oh, I also added an orange bell pepper to the chickpeas and tomatoes...very nice addition.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Really easy recipe to make as directed. I tossed the veggies with some spinach so it would wilt/cook down a little, resulting in a little extra balance and color. Would probably do as others suggest and brown the tenderloin before putting in the oven Will definitely make it again since it's so easy and hands off.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly and I’ll be honest- I didn’t love this it was a bit plain for me BUT it was super easy and def came out pretty good. I added some fresh mozzarella after reheating it and that really really helped. I think I would make some tweaks to taste if I make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
Really enjoyed this.  Quick and easy.  Like some others, I used cannellini beans rather than garbanzos because I prefer them, and added halved small potatoes to the beans and tomatoes.  Next time I’ll cook tomatoes and beans 
10 minutes before adding the pork so the tenderloin doesn’t overcook.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was a great easy meal to use up the last of my summer cherry tomatoes and some cooked chickpeas I had in the freezer. The chimichurri-like sauce was great, too. Definitely a keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I added some mustard and a tiny bit of honey to the glaze. Perfect! I could see a squeeze of lemon at the end being lovely too. Served with mashed potatoes and a simple aria salad. Easy and delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Like another commenter, I am not a fan of chickpeas. Any suggested alternatives? I would love to add something green.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
Nice dish—brown pork on grill first
Then brown under broiler at end
Had to cook 20-25 mins
Served over couscous—everyone enjoyed this 11/25

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: No chickpeas or white beans in the house, what’s a chef to do? Combined this recipe with Kia Damien’s Roasted Shrimp, Okra and Tomatoe. No shrimp but did sear tenderloin then roasted it with 50% more tomatoes, a cup of cubed ube,  1/2 the oregano in the vinaigrette, okra tossed with garlic and olive oil, za’atar, at 400 degrees for 25 minutes. Colorful and tasty!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Very good! Next time i'll probably season the pork more, and double the vinaigrette. very easy and tasty!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
This a winner, delicious, healthy, fast, minimal
mess, affordable. I would use a little more tomatos next time!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: The pork was just a little meh. The dressing was great, but we were really missing the feta from the famous tomato feta chickpea sheet pan recipe...

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Tasty recipe and easily executed.  I used can of drained chopped tomatoes because it’s winter here and balsamic vinegar as that’s what I had.  Added some spinach to make a full meal. Last I also did a quick pass under the grill to brown the top.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Adding 4-5 tablespoons of Kalamata olives levels this dish right up. So good, very easy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Trader Joe sells chickpeas in olive oil with cumin.  I used those.  I halved the recipe.  We are the entire thing.  Too good.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was super easy, and I had everything on hand. *Personal preference* — I don’t mind oregano, but it’s not my favorite by flavor. If I make this again I’ll 1) brown the loins beforehand (if you use a roasting pan it goes from stovetop to oven just fine) and 2) reduce the amount of oregano (the dried oregano reconstituted in the vinegar just fine, but I wasn’t a fan of the resulting texture — it’s a lot of little oregano leaves) and add a little more garlic and maybe some thyme and or dill to the vinaigrette.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
soooo good! very adaptable recipe. 

used canned corn, tomato, and bell pepper instead of shishito, because that's what i had. also tossed some chili powder with the veggies. ended up having to bake at a lower temp for longer because things were getting burnt. 

served over brown rice with barbecued pork and avocado. the quick pickled onions & cilantro are a must! definitely adding this to the regular dinner rotation.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @alyssa what temp?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @G. I used the Sheet Pan Feta with Tomatoes and Broccoli method— lower third of the oven at 400 (maybe on convection setting, can’t remember!). And then a watchful broil. Worked out well.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Extremely easy weeknight dish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This was a lovely weeknight dinner. The ingredients that were used are Easy to require and  the end product was savoury and delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Easy to prepare and absolutely delicious; followed recipe exactly

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This is so easy to come together at my family loved it. I love how quickly the broiler can transform a meal like this one.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I don't have a broiler but seem always find broiler recipes that sound yummy. Can this be made well in a hot oven, or should I just move on?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: If you like more heat then add some red pepper flakes after it comes out or add some more chilies

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: It was just ok to me. I sautéed a seafood pack from Trader Joe’s at the end and added it to the sheet pan with the onion slices and additional lime juice. My advice if you’re adding additional items, make sure the temperature is consistent with the other components.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @Michelle H. came here to say .. i’ll be adding some green king prawns for a few minutes at the end

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: If you like spicy, add some pepper flakes or another pepper

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: My market doesn’t sell shishitos. Is there a sub?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Trader Joes

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I used red bells cut up. I think any pepper you like would be great too

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written, it was delicious!!! The pickled onion and jalapeño slices really brought everything together well. I will be making this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was devoured in our house.  The whole dish took 20 minutes from start to finish.  I served it on a bed of fresh spinach and added sliced tomatoes.  My family would have licked the bowl if no one was watching.  The cumin was freshly ground in the mortar and pestle which made a big impact.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
This is a great way to use up veggies! Used canned white corn, cherry tomatoes sliced in 1/2, shishito peppers, & left over, uncooked snow peas. Used blue cheese crumbles (I don’t like feta).  Broiled veggies for 5 min, then put the crumbles over the top for 1 minute.  Turned out well. Had over leftover brown rice.  Snow peas were excellent for this.

Used yellow onion, instead of red (didn’t have red) & it was just meh.  I can see how they would add a lot.

Please tell us how thin slices onions should be, by a measure, in recipes. Mine were probably too thick.  I would appreciate 1/4” or 1/8” so I can use my mandolin   I think the size of the slices is really important to me, maximize the flavor balance.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Quick, easy, and flavorful. Could not find shishito peppers so subbed a mix of jalapeño and serrano. They mellowed out under the broiler and gave a nice flavor without being too spicy. Used a block of regular feta since I couldn’t find goat, and it worked fine. Otherwise I followed the recipe and it was delicious. Would serve with rice next time to soak up the yummy pan juices.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I used half the amount of feta and added a can of navy beans. It was really good! The beans toasted nicely with the corn. I’d add a bit more cumin and lime next time though.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious and easy to make.  Followed the recipe entirely except I used some jarred, pickled red onion that I had on hand already rather than slicing up an onion and adding lime juice.  I will make this again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This looks really good.  I enjoy all the ingredients.  But - it's October!  (Actually, I'm reading it in November). Why now? Corn is gone; shishito peppers are gone; fresh basil is hard to come by....this is a late August recipe - I will calendar it for 8/26.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I used frozen sweet corn from Trader Joe's and they also have shishito peppers.  I used cilantro instead of basil and it was fantastic!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: just got shishito peppers and goat feta at my local farmers market in atlanta. i have corn in the freezer - gonna make it tonight with swordfish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic!  Don't skip the onions and the lime juice.  We will make this again.  Super easy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: fast and easy. I also made some substitutions and additions and baked. Flexible for what you have . Although I wouldn't recommend  anything canned.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Trader Joe’s shishitos didn’t cooked enough in my broiler. Trying to sauté them first this time. And may try to roast rather than broil

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This was super easy and had good flavor but left my wife and I a bit underwhelmed as the main show for dinner.  I'd make it again as a side dish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic. Prepared as directed (except used feta crumbles instead of a block) and served with a platter of pita chips for “nachos” at a potluck. Big hit

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I added some sliced fennel and cherry tomatoes and they worked well. Will try zucchini and a few red peppers next time around. Overall, a great, easy, flexible recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: really tasty! no shishitos today at the store so subbed in some sweet peppers and it was great. served with a yogurt sauce and pita. so easy, will definitely be throwing this together again in the future for sure!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
So good!  Great side dish for roasted salmon (with lemon bars for dessert).  Also makes a great taco filing.  I imagine it would be wonderful as a topping for avocado toast. Don't skip the picked onion.
Watch closely so you don't incinerate the shishito peppers.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: This was so yummy! I did lettuce, sugar snap peas and radishes as the veggie base. Udon noddles and salmon with sesame oil and everything bagel seasoning. Who knew you could buy ponzu sauce at the store. What a delight.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: Quick ponzu is easy to make from equal parts soy sauce and citrus plus a bit of mirin.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: My husband made this dish, it was fantastic. It took just 30 minutes which totally impressed him

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone have recommendations for the best store bought ponzu sauce? I haven't met one yet that I liked, relative to homemade versions? If anyone has a from scratch recipe I'd love to see that too. Thank you foodie community!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: @LD any local Asian supermarket should have it! Trader Joe’s does too.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: @LD. Very good question. I have used “Kikkoman® Ponzu Citrus Seasoned Dressing and Sauce” and it was GREAT!”

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: @Millette I believe homemade ponzu is just a few ingredients. Soy, lime and something else?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: I used pork belly because I had it on hand. I made a dressing of ponzu and sesame oil. I rinsed the udon and tossed it with sesame oil to prevent sticking and let them cool.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: @Sophie If you go to the NYT cooking app and search "fish" you will find more recipes for fish that isn't salmon than you will ever have time to make.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: I lightly tossed the cooked noodles in sesame oil. I used leftover salmon, cucs, scallions, sprinkled with sesame seeds and furikake. Drizzled Trader Joe’s Soyaki sauce lightly over the top. Was very good!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: WOW. Outstanding recipe— and visually beautiful too. I made as directed, using soba noodles. For toppings I used thinly sliced cucumber, radish, Serrano pepper, and scallions. A mandoline makes light work of this. Each of us dressed our bowls with Kikkoman ponzu, a squeeze of lemon, and more sesame oil. The whole process took 30-40 minutes and dinner was on the table. Will make again and again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was really good and was incredibly easy and quick to prepare. I didn’t have all of the ingredients so I subbed a few things by default. I found a quick ponzu recipe online and it was surprisingly good. It required boiling so I made it first so it could cool down (I may try this dish warm with the homemade warm ponzu). I roasted broccoli and cauliflower quickly at high heat to keep the crunch. I did add the peppers, like the tip suggested. I watched the video attached to the recipe and noticed she used cherry tomatoes so I threw some in too. It turned out amazing. This recipe is incredibly versatile. I’m definitely adding this to my rotation, and am going to grab the recipe ingredients and try it as written.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: Pretty basic but healthy and flavorful. Made my own ponzu which kept the salt content down. Added cilantro and avocados to green onions, radishes and cucumbers. Served on a bed of spring greens.

commentBody: I used extra firm tofu for the salmon and cooked it in the air fryer. I think salmon would be great too, but the tofu was fantastic. I used an avocado, carrots, and yellow bell pepper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
commentBody: Quick, easy, delicious. For veggies I used radishes, cucumbers, shredded cabbage and cilantro, along with some shelled edamame beans I cooked with the noodles. The recipe calls for 2 tablespoons of ponzu per portion but this would have been too salty for us; I’d suggest starting with 1 tbsp per portion and letting people add more to taste. The adults also added some chili crisp to their bowls!

commentBody: As someone who likes to meal prep a week worth of lunches, over the years I've come to hate burrito bowls. For some reason I decided to give this one a try and was so pleasantly surprised. It's delicious! I used frozen corn. Also happened to have some leftover avocado lime cream from the crispy tofu taco recipe and it's the perfect topping, and it doesn't brown: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1026900-crispy-tofu-tacos

commentBody: I wanted to make this but didn’t want to go to the store, so I made a bunch of substitutions (sorry, I know this is annoying) and it still came out very yummy, so I think this is probably a very robust recipe. Also super easy and quick! I subbed a minced red Thai chili for the jalapeño, a diced roma tomato for one of the cobs of corn (only had two cobs on hand), half a lemon for the lime, and omitted the avocado. It was still great. I look forward to trying the proper recipe next time. The cheesy bean rice itself is very tasty, so am also planning to experiment with other toppings after corn season ends

My husband decided to prepare the dish exactly as the recipe suggested, and he paired it with some mouth-watering mojo-grilled chicken breast. The combination was delightful, creating a deliciously balanced meal that we both enjoyed immensely. I will definitely be making this again in the future!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I highly suggest two alterations to the recipe:

1) Reserve an extra 1/4 cup of water while the rice is cooking. Stir the rice about every 3 minutes while it’s cooking, and if it starts to stick in the last 5 or so minutes add the water. I’ve tried this with a few different brands and varieties of beans and some have needed this extra liquid and some have not.

2) Lightly pickle the raw red onion at the start of your cook by adding the lime juice and a teaspoon of sugar to your 1/2 chopped onion. This gets rid of some of the less pleasant raw onion taste while preserving the freshness

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I always have trouble making rice dishes on the stovetop. Not enough water, not long enough boiling, I’m not quite sure but I usually end up with at least 1/4 of the rice crunchy. Sometimes I panic and add more water but I usually do follow the recipe. Any tips would be appreciated.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@cookin Perhaps your pan is too thin or you haven’t stirred enough? 

My method for stovetop rice: Mix 1 cup Jasmine rice and 1.5 cups water (ratio is per package instructions) plus 1 teaspoon salt and 1 Tablespoon butter in a small nonstick pot with a lid. Turn burner to high, pot uncovered, stir a few times, scraping bottom to prevent sticking. When the liquid reaches a full boil, stir once more from the bottom, turn the heat to very low, and cover it. Set the timer for 15 minutes. When the timer goes off, turn off the heat, but leave the pot on the burner for another 10 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I always cook my rice separately and then mix it in. I use brown rice and it never cooks in the time allotted, and then everything either gets mushy or some of it gets mushy and the rice is still crunchy. I've also started cooking rice like pasta so I don't have to think about water to rice ratios, like this https://www.thekitchn.com/pasta-method-rice-23719020 Brown rice takes longer than white rice and I've never had an issue with over- or under-cooked rice.

commentBody: This is muy bueno. Subbed brown rice and used the same amount of water, and added a few more minutes to the cook time to cook the rice through. The corn salsa is what makes this dish sing - I used frozen corn. Topped it with sour cream, green Cholula, a touch of Trader Joe's taqueria salsa - eat your heart out, Chipotle.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Threw some rotisserie chicken in...husband not a huge fan of rice but this one is a winner. Great for lunch leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Healthy delicious meal. I used 1 can of corn and 2 cans of beans. 1 black and 1 light red kidney bean. Great for meal prep!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: easy to make, healthy and tasty

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I didn't expect this to be as good as it was!  My husband recommended we use the rice/beans as our standard side for Mexican meals instead of rice and refritos.  I made the salsa with canned, fire-roasted corn. It needed help. I added Mexican oregano and a little granulated garlic and let it sit, stirring occasionally, for a good 30 minutes. That made a huge difference!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy and yummy, grounding and filling-- great for a healthy (and satisfying) weekday meal, with leftovers for lunch.  Thumbs up!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious, just as recipe is written!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added a can of beans - one pinto and one black and I’m glad I did. I was going to skip the corn salsa and just use some store bought pico but I’m glad I didn’t! Easy, economical, and delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Normally I make NYT recipes as written, but this time I used frozen brown rice instead of the uncooked white rice. I made the rice separately, per package instructions, for about eight minutes, when it was al dente. When I added it to the beans, of course I didn't add the can of water, just a couple of tablespoons during the eight minutes I heated the beans and rice at medium for a bit, then at low.  I added one-half teaspoon of kosher salt to the corn salad but not any to the rice and beans.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was fantastic and came together quickly.  I'd use two jalapeños next time.  Microwaving the corn in the husk was great and made it so easy to shuck.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is an excellent recipe. I used black beans for the rice. Will be adding to my rotation!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious!  Summer, fresh, bicolored-sweet corn made the dish.  Used a whole jalapeno but could have used 2.  Next time.  Garnished with lots of cilantro, radishes, cherry tomatoes, black olives and extra cheese. Looking forward to the leftovers ;-)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Reading the reviews, this sounded great. Maybe I had the world’s least spicy jalapeno pepper, but mine was bland, bland, bland. After adding a chopped tomato and a lot of salsa, it was pretty good, though not enough to make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: An update: my daughter loved it & wants me to make it again. Go figure

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was so easy and delicious! Will definitely make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious! Added some grilled chicken and peppers for extra protein and veg. All three kids devoured it, including my four year old who said it’s the best thing she ever ate and requested that we have it for dinner every day. Very high praise!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
So so tasty! Luckily I doubled for my family. Added a shallot and lots more sliced garlic. Cooked the basmati rice in veggie broth instead of water so did not need to add salt. Did not use broccoli stems, just added lots of florets at the end. Sharp cheddar was perfect, broiled until slightly browned in spots. Huge hit with everyone, will definitely make again!

Reminded me of a less fussy, more cheesy Enchanted Broccoli Forest from Moosewood.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I loved this..used Jasmine long-grain white rice, .added some left-over cubed , cooked sweet potato..nice touch..and accented with Fly By Jing Sichuan Chil Crisp...brought it all together with flavor bursts..I wanted easy and delicious...got it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Fancy Nancy , excellent hacks! Thanks for sharing. My wife and I are chili crisp junkies. We like chili crisp in chili crisp. So when I make this I intend to make it with your suggestions. I’m guessing the marriage of the sweet potatoes (sugar) with the chilies (and spice) brings home the flavors in a real big way, yeah? Can’t wait to make it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Glenn A.
Try Mama Teav’s Hot Garlic if you haven’t already…it’s my absolute fave chili crisp! :-)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I think I need to add some little chunks of Velveeta into the rice. 

What can I say? I grew up Southern and the only cheese I knew was Velveeta. There's not many casserole-ish dishes that aren't improved with some melty, creamy Velveeta.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: That broccoli will not be bright green after 8 to 10 minutes of cooking unless you consider olive green "bright".

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @P mine was very nicely green and I added the extra 2 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Judy Forkner Like yours, mine too was still bright green at 10 minutes

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can I substitute cauliflower for the broccoli?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: No, that's not allowed.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not under any circumstances, that would never work.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @JL I'm sure you can, it just won't be as colorful.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Deeply browned the onion and used chicken stock instead of water. Incredibly delicious! My 20 month old devoured it. It would be great with thyme, I think, or most any herb. Personally I felt that 4 oz cheddar was the perfect amount, and I love cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is just what the doctor ordered after a long road trip vaca, a pantry recipe. Going to make this for dinner tonight but using froz broccoli & roasted instead. Might use brown rice with chicken broth, fresh herbs too. Thanks for this easy & comforting recipe, Ali!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's a little bland. I think the commenters who suggest swapping chicken stock for water and using more garlic and cheese are on the right track.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this tonight and added cannellini beans and used a Mexican cheddar we get here in Ireland that I use on Nachos. Brought a little heat and spice to the party. The next time I will put some chopped mushrooms in with the onion and garlic. I think that would add some bottom notes to the dish.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great easy comfort food dinner for an autumn night!  I used local farm broccoli florets, basmati rice, and Speck Alto Adige IGP on top of the broccoli before Dubliner cheese.  The Speck was a delicious addition!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@srlopez
Thanks for your comment. I don’t have easy access to speck but you inspired me to add diced ham steak that I browned a bit before adding to recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any adjustments suggested for basmati or other types of rice?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @jtk I used basmati and didn’t need to make any changes to cooking time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great dish but wanted more substance so added a can of white beans when adding florets. Also threw in some feta with the cheddar instead of adding more salt. Delish!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Probably requires a full 8oz block of cheese to get the look shown in the picture. I feel like the version we made with the 4oz it called for looks like the diet version.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So…recipe doesn’t say to remove broccoli before adding rice and water but indicates to add the florets on top of rice after cooking rice for 10 minutes have a bad feeling the broccoli is going to be mushy after all the specified cooking time. Any insight from those who have made this dish?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It looks like you just cook the stems (not florets) w/ the onions etc., and yes, you leave them in w/ the rice. They'll get mushy, but it will be like broccoli-cheese soup. Florets stay out until the end. I think I'm going to make this tomorrow and will report back.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I haven't made this yet, but, the broccoli stems and florets are divided. 

Only the chopped broccoli stems go in with the onion and garlic. The more delicate florets are added after the initial 10 minutes of rice cooking.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The recipe says to cook the stems separately, adding the florets after cooking rice for 10 minutes. Hope this helps!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wonderful riffable recipe.  My own riff was to use orzo instead of rice (using Ali Slagle's shrimp scampi with orzo method), toss the orzo mix when cooked with about 1 TBSP of chili crisp, before adding pre-cooked broccoli and cheddar topping.  The result was nicely saucy, which I think would not be the case with rice.  A terrific alternative to the usual broccoli dishes.  Goes with anything.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Easy, simple. Delicious. To make a full meal, I added canned salmon and corn then topped with pickled jalapenos under the cheese. Would have served with corn chips if I had any.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’d like to make this with brown rice.  Does anybody have any recommendations on how long to cook brown rice before adding the broccoli?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sara Lynn brown rice typically cooks for 45 minutes or so.  Subtracting the 10 minutes of cooking after adding the broccoli florets means you should cook the brown rice for 35 minutes before proceeding as described. Hope this helps.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This recipe has become our go-to comfort food. I just feel better knowing I have the ingredients on hand to whip this up at a moment's notice. Other than being the best thing in my fridge, it also works great as a heat-and-eat drop-off for friends who are sick or struggling with a loss. I label it "Magical Rice & Broccoli: Heat, eat, heal."

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It’s a very mild, gentle dish, and there’s a time and place for that. I used weak chicken stock instead of water, and decided at the last minute to stir in chunks of chicken and mozzarella, cheddar and Parmesan. Very satisfying.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is a simple and low-budget recipe made with items likely on hand. It's mild, as you can tell from the list of ingredients, and sure to be kid-friendly (my teenager was not impressed.) I like the suggestion in the intro about serving with a rotisserie chicken or something. I'll keep this in mind for future weeknights when I have no idea what to cook.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I know I know. It’s not quite the recipe but- cut up 1 and 1/2 onions and threw it in the bottom of a class casserole pan with a bag of frozen broccoli and some cut up carrots which needed to be used. Rinsed brown rice, brown rice, and quinoa, and added some red lentils and stirred it around. I added a bunch of zaatar, salt and pepper and vegetable stock and water. I covered it and cooked it in the oven for a couple of hours. Dinner for a week!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: love this, Lucy.  thanks for sharing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
This is comfort food! I added a can of tuna as the intro suggested and it was a one pot meal. I think this is a recipe where using enough salt is really going to make a difference between bland and satisfying Next time I might add a splash of white wine or lemon juice towards the end of Step 1. I sprinkled a pinch of salt on the chopped broccoli right after chopping and let it sit until I added it. 

I wish the dimensions of the skillet used while developing the recipe were included rather than just 'large'. The diameter and depth are going to affect cooking time and the surface area that the cheese will cover. I used a shallow cast iron Dutch oven (11.5"/29 cm diameter, approx. 2"/5 cm depth) and I wouldn't want to use anything much larger. The layer of cheese looked thinner than in the photo.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I mixed in a can of white beans right before the florets and it was an excellent decision.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is now one of my favorite meals. It takes one pan. Everything is done in successive stages. Pretty much any vegetable can be used. I have used asparagus and broccoli. I preferred asparagus. I added crushed roasted bacon once and it was great. I have used brown rice and white rice. Both are good. I’ve used five kinds of cheese and they’re all good. I will make this often as comfort food.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very nice. I loved the use of broccoli stems—to added flavor and nutrition. Most recipes have you throw them away

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Andrew Oh! I never throw them away, regardless what the recipe says. They always taste good, no matter what!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very tasty.  The rice took longer to cook than as written.  Next time I would use 2 cups of liquid and 12ish minutes then check to see if the rice is starting to soften.  The broccoli was beautifully cooked, slightly al dente and yes, still bright green after about 12 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Trees and cheese with rice!  Now my kids won't think I'm nuts because it's in the NYT Cooking app!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I’d say this would work with basmati rice, otherwise you’ll probably overcook the broccoli (which needs about 5-7 minutes steam cooking).  Don’t put the broccoli stems in, just peel and chop into chunks. Once peeled, they require even less cooking than the florets. 

If you other tires of rice, just add the broccoli toward the end. 

If you must use frozen broccoli, add it in the last two to three minutes of cooking— it’s already cooked and flash frozen.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this with brown rice and just eyeballed the cooking time which was longer. Accidentally threw in all the broccoli at once because I did not read the instructions well enough.  Turned out great!  Served with buttered crusty bread.  Will try to add cooked chicken in the future.  Really lovely and easy meal. I might consider using chicken broth instead of water as well since I do not have vegetarian concerns.  Love a nostalgic inspired recipe that does not require condensed processed soup.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Shallots and onion good. Used 1 c TJ brown basmati rice and 2 1/4 c water.  Cooked 30 min then added broccoli flowerettes for 10, then broiled for 4-5 min. Very good!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: American-grown rice has arsenic because there is arsenic in the soil.  Use rice from elsewhere, such as Thailand, and there is no arsenic.  I hope this helps you.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Susan. California grown rice is also free of arsenic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Is that a deep dutch oven? It is hard to tell from the photo--the photo looks more like a braiser or I guess a 2 or 2 1/2 quart dutch oven?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hi Ann - It does resemble a Dutch oven, but it's shallow, like a skillet. I think it's a saute pan.  Weighs a ton, so the two handles are great.  I am dedicated to the George Carlin mentality ...I don't want "more stuff."  But last year I caved in and bought one; absolutely love it; use it constantly.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Seems like a great way to do something with the leftover rice after a weekend splurge of take-out Chinese Feast.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I skipped step 4 and just added the cheddar to the pot of (jasmine) rice and broccoli. Also skipped the hot sauce. Even TPH (the Picky Husband) liked it very much.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I'm sooooo embarrassed. I followed the recipe exactly, including the Diamond salt and a cast iron brazing pan. It looked like the photo and tasted great with a splat of hot sauce. Yes, the rice was done.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Christian  - Absolutely!!! Made mine EXACTLY per the recipe, and it looked like the picture and was absolutely delicious!! I used a Le Creuset braising pan

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Do not use a Dutch over. 
You cannot get it close enough to broiler elements to brown cheddar and broccoli. 
While very tasty, it lacks any  kind of complexity. 
Need to add other flavors to give it some punch: olives, anchovies, various herbs, capers, pepper corns, cherry tomatoes, black pepper.
Anyway you get the idea. 
Great base for riffing to elevate it to gourmet status

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I had high hopes, but this wasn’t a 5 star recipe for me. I used stock instead of water and added fresh herbs and plenty of hot sauce but I still found it very bland.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Excellent. Add a little heavy cream, chili crisp and canned beans for a filling comfort meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Would quinoa work?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My kids said it's the best thing they've ever eaten.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this last night for my family, which includes 2 fairly great eaters. Feedbacks from the 11 year old was “I liked it, it was surprisingly good. But I don’t want to eat that again.” 8 year old thought it was a little weird and only ate half of the serving. I concur. Was okay, fairly tasty, a little mushy, definitely isn’t a full meal so needed a main protein- we added meatballs. Might have chosen salmon instead if I had some on hand. This recipe is giving Midwest risotto. I think maybe we’re not huge cheddar fans. We may have preferred actual broccoli risotto with parmigiana. All in all, it was fine. I like trying new recipes. Made this with ingredients I had on hand so it was low pressure and happy to have tried it. No need to make that again though.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I needed to add 2 more cups of water during the time the rice was cooking.  This isn't the worst thing I have ever eaten but it does taste bland and I agree with others about adding spices and other types of cheeses.  I used a 12" cast iron pan and everything fit inside.  Feel free to experiment but just add more water if need be.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yes, this took me a lot longer being a beginner cook, but this was amazing. It tasted silky from the butter and I followed it exactly as a recipe stated this is definitely a recipe. I would make my 10-year-old son loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
The broccoli florets did not fully cook, I would suggest cutting them smaller.

I added shredded rotisserie chicken at the last step when you gentyle stir - was amazing, especially with some Franks Red Hot dashed on when served.

Also, I believe mixing some cheese in would enhance the recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great with a fried egg!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious recipe. I highly recommend using a broth of your choice instead of water (and therefore no salt) and doubling the cheese!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tastes good, but it is missing something acidic. Vinegar-y hot sauce works. I also added sundried tomatoes which helped add an acidic kick.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Sprinkle some lemon pepper in the mix. That should brighten the dish up a bit.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Agree. Hot sauce at the table was a must and made the dish perfect. I like Louisiana brand.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this with wild rice to add texture and fiber!  Used a little white wine to soften and flavor veggies.  Sub veg broth for water.  As suggested by another,  just tossed in the whole 8oz of cheese, half mixed in the rice when almost done cooking and the other half on top.  Amazing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: What an amazing meal. I added cubed cooking chorizo: heavenly. Bless NYT!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For any fellow allium intolerant/allergic sufferers, here are substitutions I made. Two bunches of green onion tops for the onion, 2" piece of grated ginger for the garlic, allium free chicken broth instead of water and I added a heaping teaspoon of curry powder. Right before adding the cheese I stirred in some unseasoned rotisserie chicken. I offered pickled jalapenos for topping and served with a green salad and then NYT pumpkin chocolate chip bread for dessert. Everyone loved it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I found it to be a bit bland. Won't make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tasty and easy weeknight dinner. We will make this on the regular during winter's coldest months. Be sure to add the right amount of salt - I think this is the difference between commenters who said "meh", and those who liked it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Amy Exactly! I was wondering why people are saying this is bland. It is extremely flavorful and delicious. But, I am not afraid of salt. The salt grinder is one of my favorite items on my kitchen stove.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this the first time as written and found it tasty, but a bit bland. After an addition of plenty of chipotle tabasco, it came alive. Now I’m cooking it again, but I’m using stock instead of water for the rice, exchanging the cheddar for a blend of Italian cheeses, and will brown and stir in some fennel salssiccia and chili flakes somewhere along the line. Yum! This seems like a recipe that you could vary in all kinds of ways.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was awesome! Used brown basmati rice from TJ’s and added 15 minutes to the simmering time, but otherwise followed directions exactly. Family loved this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used brown rice, took longer, excellent result.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added 2 cups chopped, roasted chicken I had from the freezer, used chicken stock instead of water, and toasted 1 t. yellow curry powder with the onion/garlic mixture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My 1.5 year old ate it, no one else did. Very bland. Needs more seasoning and maybe cream of chicken. If I wanted dry rice I would make cilantro lime rice which is much more flavorful and everyone in my family eats it. Not sure why this is a 5 star recipe

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Disappointing. I added red pepper flakes to the onion, but it came out with just a hint of heat. I also added the breast meat of a rotisserie chicken (chopped) and 4 oz. shredded cheddar along with the broccoli stems, and splashed in some chicken stock to keep things from sticking to the bottom of the pot. Also sprinkled 4 more oz of cheddar (mixed with turmeric, as my cheese was white) over the whole thing before it went in the oven. Used brown rice. But bland, and didn’t come together.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I substituted chick stock for the water but other than that stuck to the recipe. Husband, who is somewhat of a food snob, said we needed to keep this recipe  He added of all the rice, cheese and broccoli recipes he has eaten over the last 50 years, this was the only one he liked.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: When I read this, I immediately got week night soccer mom vibes. Which I thought, why not try it? We added two cans of tuna at the end when we stirred the rice, and it was amazing! The two of us ate almost the whole batch, which I just finished what was leftover from last night for lunch. Can’t wait to try it with beans!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added edamame for vegetarian protein and the kids love it

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Bland. Added chicken for substance, used chicken stock instead of water and it still came out bland and leaving me wishing for real risotto next to roast chicken and broccoli.  Though I supposes this was "one pot" easy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Make it with quinoa instead of rice. delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I was hesitant to make this based on some of the reviews but it's become a go-to meal, especially when we have a broccoli on hand. We add a bit of chicken stock to the rice as it's cooking for a little more flavor. If we have left over chicken we'll throw that in, too. Easy weeknight meal that the kids enjoy.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Did anyone try the rec to stir in a can of tuna? I'm curious what you thought.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Totally delish, I will make again!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If I wanted to add chicken would I put the cubed raw chicken in the pan and cook until golden then follow the recipe? Will it cook all the way through?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added 1-2 Tbs of curry powder for extra flavor. Turned out mellow, but delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yum!! Made as is, with a small bit of broc crown I had left over. Sautéed some fake ground beef and added towards the end before covering with cheese and broiling. Rice was cook perfectly! Will be making again with the proper amount of broc!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used this as a jumping off recipe and it was quite good! I chopped up 3 strips of bacon and pan fried it in the Dutch oven, took it out, and then sautéed the broccoli stems, onion, and garlic in the leftover bacon fat. I used jasmine rice and two cups of homemade chicken and veggie stock. When I stirred the rice I added in the cooked bacon, some cheddar, and some finally diced chives. Served it with grilled chicken breast and sriacha sauce. The broccoli and rice were just a little mushy, so I will cook it for less time next time. I’ll also add more cheese in the rice and on top. A good base recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Stellar easy side and very satisfying. I used frozen broccoli florets that I always keep in the freezer. I cut them a bit smaller and added them in about the last 5 minutes of cooking the rice. Definitely a keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Now that I’m single, I’m excited to eat this dish and not worry about having someone in the house to experience the aftermath it will cause.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very nice recipe although I think NYT cooking has star inflation.  No way it's 5 stars.  I will say amounts and cooking instructions are spot on and it's a very visually appealing dish.  Based on other reviews I added one full teaspoon of chili crisp and cooked the rice in chicken broth - with those two changes it had a mild savory flavor with a little kick.  Otherwise, I think it would be quite bland and would require lots of hot sauce.  Will definitely make again though.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was really bland. I'm not sure if I did something wrong, but it... just tasted like broccoli and rice? Not really sure why it has such a high rating, unless people are going really off-recipe and adding a lot of extra flavoring to it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I needed more water and time to cook both the rice and get the broccoli soft (for the kids eating), but thought it was good and my picky kids ate it, so that was win. I used veggie bouillon to add a little flavor.  In the future, will use more broccoli and add some herbs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So this recipe is probably fine as is for some people, but just reading through it.....it sounds bland.  I sautéing onions and garlic, so it had some color because that's where the flavor comes from. I added a little chicken base for more flavor. I did not use stems (although I do use stems in salad and stirfry).  Steamed broccoli florets separately as I didn't want to disturb the rice cooking.  I broiled the finished product in a shallow pan.  It was good, but definitely needed extra love.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Needs more seasoning, lacked any sort of flavor before adding salt, pepper, and spice blend. Would not make again with recipe as is

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I didn’t expect anything mind blowing, and it wasn’t, but considering the ingredients and ease this is truly a 5 star recipe.  Only adjustments I made were to use chicken stock instead of water and finely chopped shallots instead of onion.  Oh, and some msg.  My kids made a face when they asked what was for dinner, but when the time came they cleaned their plates and asked for seconds.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My husband made a face too, but he like it very well.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Huh?  Won’t the broccoli be overcooked?  Like WAY overcooked?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Alibre I followed the directions and the broccoli was just right. I felt the dish needs to have some additions to up the flavor, but the broccoli was perfectly al dente.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delightful and nostalgic! I followed as written but swapped in chicken broth for the water,  used dairy free Daiya cheese, and stirred in about 1/4 cup canned coconut milk at the end before popping in the oven. Makes for great leftovers too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added the tuna as suggested and it was a wonderful weeknight meal.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used unsalted chicken broth instead of water. I also rinsed the uncooked rice several times in cold water to remove the excess starch. This is an easy recipe that could easily become a one pot meal by adding leftover cooked chicken or pork just before step 4. It would also be delicious topped with a fried egg after Step 4.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The rice was undercooked despite using the longer times. Otherwise very tasty. Next time I’ll use a slightly higher temp.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jean our rice was badly undercooked as well. One of the top five most frustrating things to happen in the kitchen is to end up with 50% rice that's cooked well, and 50% rice that seems like it was cooked for 5 minutes. Have no idea how this happens. Always dubious of cooking rice with stuff in it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I had to add a little more water to the rice, but this is New Mexico and everything is very dry.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meh. Not really worth it in my opinion. I added 6 oz of cheese compared to the 4 in the recipe and it still seemed pretty light on cheese and definitely didn’t look like the picture.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Meh. It was pretty bland. I won't bother with it again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Add 1 extra cup water to rice. Used 1 bag frozen broccoli, defrosted in microwave. Great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I couldn't remember this morning whether there was any of this left. There wasn't. I added veggie sausages and some chile crisp. Made it a meal and a gave it a little oomph. Otherwise, chopping up the parts of the broccoli I usually throw away was genius. Easy, fast, cheap and yummy. What more can you ask.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious!  Added the broccoli stems in with the onion and softened them.  Garlic then rice and used chicken stock with my Jasmine rice so did not add salt.  I added my broccoli flowerets the last 5 minutes of cooking so they would retain their vibrancy.  Broiled with the cheese until lightly brown.  Served with a rotisserie chicken and grilled small peppers.  A complete, easy and very good meal.  Will definitely make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used Morton kosher salt instead of Diamond. Tasty and less expensive

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: use a low salt chicken broth instead of the water.  no added salt needed.  totally improves the taste.  mix the cheddar in, no need to broil.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Has anyone used brown rice instead of white?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: PR: Haven't made this yet, but would like to with brown rice. In Step 2, I'm going to try cooking the rice for about 30 minutes, waiting til 20 minutes in to add the broccoli stems. I might try adding some chopped roasted pecans under the cheese, too. Best of luck. P, I tend to agree.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Any ideas for yummy topping to use on 1 serving for a dairy-free family member?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Vegan or lactose-free cheese? Or maybe seasoned bread crumbs.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very nice formulation

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this tonight and served it with roasted bone in, skin on chicken thighs. I cut the quantities in half for two of us. My little skillet doesn’t have a close fitting lid so I had to fiddle with the amount of water because of evaporation. Since I had the oven at 400° convection I put the pan in it to melt the cheese. We liked it a lot!  Next time I’ll put aluminum foil under the ill-fitting lid which should solve the water problem.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I have a feeling we will be making variants of this all winter. Used diluted homemade chicken broth rather than water, which gave the fi al product a risotto-adjacent yumminess. Lovely comfort food. Might try a little Pandora on top next time for a little crunch.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used several cheeses including pecorino romano, and finished with some chili crisp. Otherwise it would have been incredibly bland. Even with the onion and garlic, I would recommend some onion and garlic powder. And other spices you like.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as written. Threw a couple of chicken sausages on the griddle while I finished the rice & broccoli. Cooking times were perfect. It was nice, not amazing, definitely wanted the hot sauce!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made a half batch for lunch today.  Easy to follow recipe.  Definitely don’t skimp on the salt and pepper as this is the main source or flavor.   I might use more cheese or a more strongly flavored cheese (pepper jack or mix of cheddar and parm) next time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Great! Added can rinsed cannellini beans and large can Sirena chilli tuna at the stir part - and topped with combination smooth ricotta and the grated cheddar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: After all the positive comments I was disappointed. It was very bland. My husband and I added salt and pepper and I added balsamic vinegar. It was okay, but I won’t make again.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Far more flavorful than expected. A keeper to be repeated!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Fabulous! Didn’t add the chopped stems (lazy) and mixed in maybe 2oz extra cheese when stirring all together, but otherwise followed exactly. Going to make again this week with leftover rotisserie chicken and chicken broth instead of water.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: i only had half a cup of rice so i used quinoa and added 2 cups of veggie stock. i also added diced carrots and celery. it was delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Didn’t have any rice so used farro instead and also added mushrooms. Used half chicken broth and half water. Yummy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Just tried this. Followed the recipe to a T. It was okay. But, I did have thoughts. I suggest use chicken or vegetable broth instead of water. Double the cheese, perhaps stirring half in when you stir in the florets.  And it was hard to judge the quantity of broccoli when the description is “a large head”. I had too much broccoli. (If that is really possible).  But, I’ll give this one more try.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Added cubed ham while stirring, before adding cheese and putting under broiler. Everyone loved it. Ate it the next day with a fried egg, pickled red onions and chile crisp for lunch... superb! Keeper.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I cooked this in a braiser. I substituted chicken stock for the water, and added a sprinkling of capers when I added the broccoli florets. For the broccoli stalks, I peeled them, and then chopped them into coins. The rice and broccoli cooked perfectly, but broiling the cheese on top at the end brought this dish to delicious. I put a fried, over easy egg on top of each dinner portion. My husband loved it and asked to have it again. Next time, I'll add toasted sesame seeds along with the florets.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I really liked this - simple, easy and tasty....and I don't even really love broccoli.  Did add 2x more cheddar than called for and next time would do the same except stir in some of it to the rice and the rest on top before broiling.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used pepper jack cheese instead of cheddar and it was REALLY good.  No hot sauce needed.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wonder about the cooking time for the rice...seems too short.  I buy rice in bulk at the food coop and it takes considerably longer to cook than the 18 to 20 minutes stated in the recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I would love to make this dish, but I cannot "arrange a rack near the broiler" (near it??). My apartment's broiler is in a separate section below the oven. I'd have to remove the rack entirely. And, the Dutch oven would be too hot to handle, and burn being so close to the flame.

Anyway, I'd probably use instant rice and other shortcuts, like the frozen steamable Green Giant Broccoli with Cheese sauce, or microwave in the bag broccoli and use the oven to melt the cheese..

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: you can stack on top of a cast iron skillet to raise the surface closer to the broiler

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Boring recipe. wouldn't make this again

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added a can of tuna as suggested in the recipe note. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: In our opinion, this meal fell somewhat short of its 5-Star rating. It seemed like just eating broccoli, rice, and cheese would be quite bland so we added a block of cubed, marinated tofu after the rice was finished cooking for a little protein (would recommend). Not trying to discourage anyone from making this though, it fed four well and was pretty tasty.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious recipe that the whole family loved. I added shredded leftover rotisserie chicken in with the rice and used chicken stock instead of water. I did notice there wasn’t enough liquid for rice to finish cooking when adding the florets, so added more stock to the pot as well. Next time will use typical 1:2 rice-to-liquid ratio from the get go.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The methods here just don’t work.  Rice and cooks unevenly - rice gets mushy, broccoli doesn’t cook. Just trust one’s instincts and blend everything together and simmer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used brown-wild rice mix - more water and cooking time.  Cooked in broth, but maybe not enough (?) Rice and broccoli came out well - added broccdolli the last 8 min of the rice - which needed 1/2 hour.  Have no broiler so just put it in oven with cheese.   It was very bland indeed - meh.  I will try again if our CSI has broccoli but put in dried Iranian 'sabzi' herb mix, and a much sharper cheese - a blue cheese might be good. ???

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Can someone explain to me why NYT recipes frequently do not include the water in the ingredients list? Thanks in advance.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: For the same reason they don't include pots, pans, spoons, or knives in the ingredient list. It's often considered a cooking medium, rather than a structural ingredient.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: this is good and easy -- I dressed it up with a little smoked paprika, dijon mustard and Worcestershire sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This one was a dud. For all it's simplicity, it's fairly fussy with all the steps. Which wouldn't be a dealbreaker, but it's also just not very good. I followed the recipe, but after it should've been done (just before adding the cheese), I discovered the rice and broccoli were both still crunchy. Other similar recipes that involve baking cooked rice, broccoli, and cheese are less hands-on and more delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Having read all your comments:  Added a handfull of persian dried herbs used to make Sabzi polo or such.(cilantro, fenegriek, chives, parsley) which lends a lovely aroma to almost anything.  A handfull of dried dill, a handfull of leftover favabeans (double skined) and some extra butter when adding the broccoli flowers.  Cooked in vegi-broth. I didn’t add cheese tonight but will put the rest in the oven tomorrow with cheese.  Eaten with cucumber yoghurt (Persian style) it was very good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Yes, Mollie Katzen's Enchanted Broccoli Forest--but without the poetry.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is delicious and easy to make.  I added extra butter at the get-go, a bit of white wine as the rice was softening, and a tad of extra cheese.  It was scrumptious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Use romiano cheddar is amazing in this recipe

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this as written tonight for dinner.  I should have followed my instinct to use a full 2 cups of water for the stated 1 cup of white rice.  I knew better and followed the recipe anyway.  The rice was a little crunchy — which ruins an otherwise decent dish.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
This was amazing! My vegetable resistant wife gobbled it up and had the leftovers instead of pizza when I went out to dinner with a friend. 
I added minced jalapeños (half of a pepper) along with the garlic and broccoli stems and the heat was perfect—no need to add hot sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I do not understand Step 4 after finishing Step 3.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: You want the sprinkled cheese to melt with some brown spots from the broiler heat.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @webalc broiler is a known as a grill in the 4est of the world. and I've always called it an oven rail rather than a rack. otherwise the recipe is clear. which sentence didn't make sense?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This dish sounds wonderful, but, when I try it, I think I'll add some deli chicken for a one dish meal to serve with a tossed salad.  Has anyway tried adding chicken?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Simple but much tastier than the sum of its parts. Used a leftover cup of chicken broth for some of the liquid but otherwise followed the recipe. Comfort food!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Would Arborio rice work in this dish?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Shades of chicken divan, a 70’s casserole staple with cream of mushroom soup. This has the same elements minus the chicken and processed soup. Try pearl barley instead of rice. It’s so good. Next time I’ll add mushrooms, too.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: -Great as is and can be dressed up/down depending on your guests

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Added a Teaspoon of Turmeric for funsies. So good.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this for my boys and they went back for seconds! I did add boneless skinless chicken breast browned in bacon fat. Perfect!!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this as written except substituted vegetable stock for the water and used a bit more than 2 cups (in Colorado you always need more liquid). I was sure it wouldn't work, but it did -- the rice cooked, the broccoli was perfect (I broke it into very small pieces)... but it was so bland! The family ate it, but asked me not to make it again, it was so boring. How can I spice up this otherwise great recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Kids and parents loved! Hot sauce was a great idea

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: My broccoli took so much longer to get cooked- and I needed to add more water, twice. Wondering if this was because I added beans and maybe needed to increase the water beyond the 1 3/4 C recommended.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Added very little salt and instead tossed in a veggie stock cube when it was time to cook the rice. Skipped the broiler & put shredded cheese on top for the last 2 mins of steaming the broccoli. White aged cheddar was lovely in this recipe. Will try adding chili crisp next time!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I added a can of chickpeas per the recipe suggestion. Used a combo of sharp cheddar and Parmesan for the cheese. Otherwise cooked as written. It’s good and easy. Hot sauce adds flavor. I would add some red pepper flakes next time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very good recipe. I didn’t bother with the broiler, just added the cheese to covered skillet for 3 minutes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: a little dry; sauce a good option

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I tried this with wild rice with the added cooking time. Must have used too much water, because even after such a lengthy simmer, there was far too much water left. And even after doubling the cheese (!), something was lacking. I suspect the water was the problem, because the cheese seemed to sort of vanish; there was no thick, gooey cheesiness as implied by the photo. It all just didn't come together.

author: Ali Slagle
