commentBody: I was really disappointed in this. Made exactly as directed and it just came out SO SO mushy. Taste was there I think but could not get past texture. Water to rice ratio way off I think.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: When the broccoli is under the broiler is the Dutch oven covered or not covered?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Judith leave it uncovered. You’re broiling to brown the top of the dish.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Really tasty! My rice browned a bit too much under the broccoli, so next time I’ll try roasting the broccoli separately and mixing it in right before adding the cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: As written, it seems that you are supposed to remove the broccoli florets before Step #2, and then re-add florets during Step #3. Is that correct?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @CEA No don’t add the broccoli florets at all until step 3! Just sauté the chopped broccoli stems with the onions and garlic in step 1.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: note: chopped stems in step 1 vs florets in step 3

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: How can we scatter the brocoli on top of the rice since the recipe asks to cook it with the rice ?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @KrysaliaH, read again. You cook the stems of the broccoli with the rice, then sprinkle the more tender florets on top nearer the end of the cooking time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was very good, I did add part of a bouillon cube to the rice cooking water, but otherwise no changes. However, the 45 minute time estimate could only be correct after the veg are chopped and the cheese grated, which I always include in my dinner prep-time estimates. Hard to predict, I realise, given people's different skill levels etc, but still.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Instead of broccoli stems, I substituted the kohlrabi that comes in my CSA that I never know what to do with, and I felt like a culinary genius! Not as eye pleasingly green of a dish but texture and taste worked great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: So so easy and so so good.   I sprinkled Tabasco for a little zip.  Delicious 😊

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I thought this was a bit dry so I tried adding a bit of ranch dressing with fresh herbs.  Great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
My broccoli was overcooked after 8-10 minutes. 
Also, I used chicken broth for added flavor. The whole dish was a bit bland in my opinion.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
This is far and away the most boring thing I have ever eaten.  When my kids finally put me in a home, I will be served this twice a week.  When I complain to them, they will say, “oh it’s not so bad dad. It has broccoli it has cheese. You like those.” And I’ll be forced to say, “then you fraking eat it. Get me a cheeseburger.”

Seriously, there is no where near enough cheddar to make up for the complete lack of umami in this.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this with chicken Better Than Bouillon instead of plain water and added a healthy pinch of MSG. With these changes, it's not bland at all.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was nice, but it definitely feels more like a side dish than a main.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Pro Tip for any grilled sandwich - Don’t butter the bread. Melt the butter in your skillet or griddle. Lift up sandwich before turning and add another pat for the second side. So much easier.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I'm not a pro, but I've been using that method for decades.

This recipe is way too much trouble for a gooey mess of beef and cheese. Running back and forth to a gas grill? It's just a good if you do it all inside. (Might be worth it if you used charcoal.)

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@sandiom
Even better use softened butter to completely cover one side of each slice of bread. Put cheese on dry side. Sauté butter side out flipping when browned

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Naw... use mayonnaise for the nicest crispist grilled bread

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Out here in Middle America we put a sauce made with Mayo, ketchup and Dijon mustard, pickle juice on our Patty Melts. We also do American cheese on one side, Swiss on the other. Gives it a kinda Rueben vibe, maybe. Since our state lays claim to the invention of the Reuben, perhaps that’s why.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this with Fage 2% greek yogurt. I added an extra swirl of honey and it was perfect.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Lovely combo of flavors and textures! I used balsamic vinegar and was generous with the pepper.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Thanks. I am a good cook but I really love these easy recipes.  I would never have added pepper to strawberries so  it  is a great easy recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Judith Add some balsamic with the black pepper, berries, and basil and spoon over vanilla ice cream for a fab dessert!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I used balsamic vinegar instead, more honey, generous on the basil leaves. Bed of baby spinach in center of bowl. Sliced avocado around the edge. Served strawberries around the edge with slotted spoon. Blueberries on top of spinach. Added a bit of olive oil to remaining balsamic vinaigrette and drizzled over spinach. Big dollop of greek yogurt AND cottage cheese. Generous with mixed nuts. I probably won't be hungry for the rest of the day!!! YUM!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I think this would also work with ricotta.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Maggie. It’s absolutely delicious and much better with Ricotta!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Marianne @Maggie Yes, I can hardly wait to try this with ricotta!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Cottage cheese, good/sweet strawberries, basil, and pistachios are great together. The honey, vinegar, salt and pepper…not so much I’ll keep it simpler next time.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I like everything, but this didn’t land.  I see folks have substituted yogourt for the cottage cheese, and I would highly recommend that if the cheese/vinegar combo doesn’t work for you!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’ll have to try this. My husband and I enjoy mandarin oranges over cottage cheese, black pepper optional.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Delicious as printed. Second time around I used almonds and I did up the spiciness using Penzey's Pico Fruita sprinkle. Next I'll try balsamic instead of red wine vinegar. I'm sold on this combo!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made with mint instead of basil and it was good! A nice variation on a high protein snack.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: More black pepper, much more basil, no salt. Balsamic instead of red wine vinegar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Wondering how this would work with peaches since that is what I have in abundance right now… I’m thinking probably not?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@Jeanneco
I think peaches would work so well too, but I’d probably prefer a mix of sticky and white balsamic vinegars to red wine though, in keeping with the fruit’s natural sweetness.  And if you preferred a smoother texture, mix the cottage cheese with a bit of Greek yoghurt.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Jeanneco I’m eating peaches prepared with balsamic vinegar, served over cottage cheese, and it’s as delicious as the strawberries I made last week.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Hmmm... I can see black pepper going with strawberries but honey noooo.. Maybe plain sugar would go better.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @MS I agree. I used half the honey and added date syrup which is perfect with strawberries and basil. Just the right sweet since it thick and sweet almost like balsamic vinegar. And low glycemic.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Very easy and refreshing, and I could see how it would be even better the longer you let the strawberry mixture sit. Had to sub pumpkin seeds for pistachios but otherwise followed the recipe. Thanks Diane & Alex for turning me into a cottage cheese person!!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not usually a fan of cottage cheese, but this is delicious. I did substitute balsamic vinegar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I like this!  Yum!  I know it's sacrilege, but I used frozen, sliced strawberries because it's January and also I'm a lazy cook; super convenient and still good.  I'm thinking maybe to try Szechuan peppercorns next time.  I've tried these with fruit before and the floral quality goes nicely and there's that spicy punch too.  Anyone tried any other sweeteners?  Maple syrup?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: @Sarah yes, date syrup is perfect.  A friend turned me on to it and I use it for a lot of recipes now.  It is thick and sweet almost like a balsamic.  Much better than honey or maple syrup.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Obviously a simple recipe, but the novelty of it was for me an inspiration and a springboard for how to get a new flavor dimension out of summer fruit. I’ll be trying several of the suggestions my fellow cooks have made. In return, here’s my twist: mixed equal parts of probiotic cottage cheese and Greek yogurt, then zested a fresh lemon over mixture, stirred well, then let sit a moment before serving. I think the subtle lemon taste added a lot. As always, I really appreciate NYT recipes and all the helpful comments.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is so so so good. The strawberries with the vinegar and basil are perfect, and such a good breakfast with cottage cheese and some toast.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was really nice on a 100-degree day. I recommend using Trader Joe’s small curd cottage cheese, which is the closest thing to a rich European cottage cheese that I’ve found (though it’s 4% fat, and if you can find a brand with 9% fat, do it; I think Good brand may make it).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Good Culture is amazingly delicious. No garbage in it and it has live cultures. Unfortunately, lately it's been hard to find but I won't eat any other brand.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Could I use mint leaves instead of basil?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: You can use anything you want. Mint would be great, if that's what you like. Basil has such a mix of flavors that I'd be surprised if mint weren't part of basil's profile.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: basil is a member of the mint family.  oddly i love various mints, but hate basil.  i might try some of my chocolate mint next summer.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: There is a a small ice cream chain here in the Pacific Northwest, as well as in Florida, Salt and Straw, that has a rotating menu of very inventive flavors. One of my favorites is Strawberry Honey Balsamic Black Pepper. It’s a GREAT combination! So like others have commented, I would use nice syrupy balsamic vinegar in this recipe. But I’ll have to wait another month or so for the good local berries to arrive.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: If anyone tries this over plain yogurt rather than cottage cheese, let me know. Sounds delicious otherwise

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: We serve this at home as dessert except with the strawberries being the star of the dish and a small scoop of mascarpone on top instead of the cottage cheese.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Simple easy and delicious for lunch breakfast or snack. Pepper and basil harmonize with the berries. Used Sherry vinegar

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’m eating it right now. I’ll lose the basil and pistachios next time. Meh.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I had no strawberries as it is September, so I made this with ripe nectarines.  This recipe made fat free cottage cheese (which is what I had) taste delicious.  The vinegar and black pepper really rounded out the flavor of the sweet fruit.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Ooh, really interesting!  I’m going to make it with homemade vegan ricotta & see how that goes!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made it. After two bites, I strained the basil and pistachios out, washed the salt and pepper off, and had a fine breakfast. I have no idea why anyone would want to cripple the taste of fresh strawberries with that other stuff.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: can you use frozen strawberries?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Would this deliver as much protein if you substituted yogurt for cottage cheese?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Pretty disappointed with this. Too vinegary and the basil doesn’t go well with any of it. Not a fan.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I wanted to like this but it is a no go.  I would rather eat a bowl of cottage cheese and then one of strawberries.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made this as per the recipe and it tasted faintly like pickled herring.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@MerfromMaberly

Wait, so is that a positive or a negative?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I don’t like basil but used chives and that worked well. I did not anticipate liking this as much as I did plus to fits with my online diet app as a healthy breakfast ! It’s a keeper!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I've used mint instead of basil, but I prefer the taste of the basil.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is all the right notes - taste, visual appeal, texture and endlessly adaptable, both in yield and optional ingredients, like ricotta or yogurt for cottage cheese (I recommend Nancy's organic probiotic 2 %), or balsamic for red wine vinegar, peaches for strawberries  I would not substitute fresh basil (be generous) nor the black pepper - both are essential. Ali - thanks for great recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
I was hesitant to make this but wanted to try some new protein options for breakfast. It is delicious! 
If you're on the fence I would encourage you to try it.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was delicious. I used thawed frozen strawberries so that I could use organic berries. The berries and juices were wonderful over Daisy cottage cheese. A very satisfying meal for breakfast, lunch, or dinner.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This has been a real hit throughout my pregnancy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is a bit on the herby side and light on the sweetness...doesn't quite go with cottage cheese as well as tangy yogurt. I could have done without the basil and had a little more sugar.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I personally think the basil funks this up a little bit. And a little bit more honey than suggested (or maple syrup) would improve this. OK, and greek yogurt instead of cottage cheese. I realize this just made a different dish!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I really liked this though I imagine it’s not for everyone. I forgot the pistachios once but still good. A great way to add protein to your diet

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Oh how I wanted to love this dish.  The flavors just didn't come together for me.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this today & it was delicious! Mind you I like cottage cheese, but what a great savory breakfast bowl. I used 1/2 red wine vinegar and half balsamic vinegar, because I knew that would appeal to me. Don’t skip the pepper, it really adds something special.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I loved this, but I also really like cottage cheese. Other members of the family find the texture of cottage cheese a little weird, so this didn't work for them. Regardless, great, healthy dessert alternative. I added some blueberries and reduced the honey to not be too sweet.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Tried this minus the vinegar (I strongly dislike vinegar even in tiny quantities) and thought it was great!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Some cottage cheese has probiotics so try to look for that kind (unfortunately more expensive) but makes breakfast extra healthy!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: this is insanely delicious. made it with white sugar instead of honey

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I blitzed up the cottage cheese til smooth and added some sour cream. My husband hates cottage cheese and he didn't know the difference! I can't wait to do this with other fruits. I see apple pecan and raspberry in my future.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Simple and surprisingly amazing.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this with blueberries instead of strawberries and it was really good. A healthy and filling breakfast!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I love Ali Slagle's recipes and have her cookbook, and this recipe was terrific: pistachios would have been lovely, but I used sunflower seeds as that was what I had.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I’ll cut back a bit on the basil quantity next time (maybe 2-3 leaves only..?) but overall this was a quick, tasty breakfast. It was even better the next day after the leftovers had marinated in the strawberries, so if your schedule allows make this one a day in advance.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Used ricotta and soaked chia seeds as the base. Followed the remainder of the recipe for one bowl, and it felt like almost too many flavors? For the other bowl, I used pecans instead of salted pistachios and omitted salt and pepper. Got a great light, sweet treat. Servings are filling!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This is absolutely delightful!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Absolutely delicious and easy! I use a little extra honey and put it over cottage cheese (good culture and kalona supernatural are my favorites).

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: current hyperfixation breakfast

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
@nandini

And worth every fixate!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I make this with  maple syrup, red wine vinegar and the strawberries. I add the pinch of salt to help make it juicier. Serve it with chia pudding, yogurt or cottage cheese. It's makes the supermarket strawberries taste great.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: The vinegar, honey, cottage cheese combo was not a good one.  Vinegar and cottage cheese is a bit unpleasant.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made as directed, and it was delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Use allulose instead of honey to make this lower carb

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: A little too acidic for my taste. I may try with a different vinegar or maybe lemon juice.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: This was a little too acidic for my tastes - more honey and/or less vinegar might be a good idea. I would also like to try it with white wine vinegar, or maybe with lemon juice.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Not a fan of honey, so I used course Demerara sugar. Did everything else by the book. Delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Made a smaller batch to test. I liked it right in the middle of sweet vs. savory. It would be easy to serve extras so people could move in either direction.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I love cottage cheese, it was a staple growing up in Sweden. I ate it most days as an after school snack mixed with mashed banana and topped with crushed almonds (try it!). I find the American ones I’ve tried much too salty and the curds are harder, not as creamy. Anyone have a suggestion of a creamy, non-salty brand for this recipe?

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this as written (except didn't have pistachios so skipped them)--delicious!  Today I tried a not-quite-ripe peach and threw in a few pieces of diced mango to add sweetness.  I live in Atlanta, so peaches are abundant right now.  Used mint instead of basil with the peach, but otherwise used red wine vinegar, honey, salt and pepper.  So so good!  This is an easy and sophisticated summer breakfast.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this for  my lunch, today it’s 100F in Kentucky! Perfect cool and delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody:
Excellent and easy to make night before. Used balsamic vinegar instead of red wine vinegar. Next time maybe add a bit more vinegar to increase the tartness to our preference. Used lots of basil and it was good. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly. A very nice change for breakfast. Did not have pistachios but toasted up pecans and crushed them and it  was excellent. 
Excited to use leftovers to serve over vanilla ice cream tonight.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
Really darn good. Scaled it down to 1 lb of shrimp, two ears of corn and a bag of locally grown shishito peppers. Two of us polished off the shrimp and half the veggies. Pretty good considering one of us “doesn’t like shrimp”. 

We’ll definitely be making this again. Thanks for a winner!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: 1.  Don’t add extra salt, miso/soy is salty enough. 2. Double or triple the sauce. 3. Grilled two lime-halves to squeeze on the tray before serving. 4. Added some hoisin sauce at the table. 5. Maybe skewer the shrimp.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Made with zucchini instead of peppers. Don’t add extra salt, miso is salty enough.  A little miso heavy for my taste, but husband liked it.  I added some hoisin sauce at the table.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Big hit with the family. I fully endorse the tail-on shrimp, but only had peeled, frozen wild shrimp, so I skewered it. I doubled the shishitos, threw in mini bell/sweet peppers from the farm box, and grilled two lime-halves to squeeze on the tray before serving. Beautiful late-summer spread!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This got rave reviews from my family. I made it as instructed in the recipe except that I didn’t add extra salt per @Judy. It will definitely go in the rotation. Thank you Ms. Slagle and @Judy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Fun dinner you eat with your hands.  Double or triple the sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This dish was waaaayyy too salty for us. The soy miso overpowered the fish. If I make it again I’ll definitely omit the soy sauce.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic. I haven't used miso in a lot yet, so I wasn't expecting this kind of flavor. If I had a real grill instead of cats iron skillet, I feel like the touch of smokiness would have been a huge hit. Go make this.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Way too salty even without adding that extra salt to the shrimp. This recipe is just meh! Not sure I get all the 5 star reviews, I guess to each their own.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and simple. Would 1.5x the sauce so there’s more for dipping.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Really good, just used the marinade for shrimp and zucchini and grilled. Super tasty.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Spouse doesn’t eat corn on the cob so made a charred skillet corn with red onion and jalapeños .  Do not skip corn with this dish! Grilled zucchini with the shishiitos.  This is a keeper. Didn’t salt the shrimp but overall not too salty.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
I have a 6-in-1 Instant pot, and this is what I did.
Note that this recipe makes a lot of food! 
-1lb chickpeas   1 can coconut water, 1/2 the lemon peels, salt, and 1.5 cans of water. The chickpeas need to be completed covered by the fluid with some room to spare or else the top layer will dry out
-seal and cook on bean setting for 20 minutes 
-chickpeas will be al dente, not entirely cooked
-add cauliflower, 2 small yellow onions roughly chopped, and the following:
stock cube
2-3 tbsp curry powder 
2-3tsp cumin powder 
1 can coconut cream
1 jalapeno 
1 inch of ginger 
1 large clove of garlic (mash jalapeno, ginger and garlic in mortar and pestal before adding or roughly chop)
1 can of water
-the cauliflower will release some water, but not enough to make it runny
-seal and put on chili setting for 5 minutes 
-safely open and add 1 cup cashews and 2 handfuls of spinach
-stir spinach (should wilt immediately), add salt and pepper to taste
-add a splash of coconut cream if it's too spicy for you, but remember that the instant pot will water down the spices
I served it with brown basmati coconut rice and it was delicious. The sweetness and creamy flavor of the coconut rice will also help with any spice. The chickpeas are umami and the flavours are great. Cauliflower is perfectly cooked, soft with a hint of crisp. enjoy!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Kind of veers from the author's intended simplicity, but def a good curry recipe.  I also use IP almost exclusively, sometimes agonizing that a slow cook would yield richer textures, deeper flavors.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @SDC not sure why people feel the need to comment on a recipe that differs quite a lot from the original, making it a totally different recipe. I’m going to try the simple original which sounds delicious and so easy.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
@Mo Kevany
I think it is because some of us love to share what new taste delight we have discovered and some of us love to learn what that new recipe is. I plan to save the original recipe and the variation.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Making my first comment because it will be first. This is a dish that will only taste better with time as the flavors blend. And it’s endlessly riffable - add chicken or pork shoulder for the non-vegans. I used to make a variation of this for our office parties. It’s easy to scale up to feed a hungry horde.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
@Bret Bradigan
Making it now. It sounds great!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
@Bret Bradigan
Slow cooking cauliflower for hours sounds awful. It doesn’t overcook?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Anon O. Miss it crumpled into oblivion.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Made this according to the directions with one exception:  I added a tablespoon of smoked paprika along with the turmeric.  I’ve been on a smoked paprika kick lately and couldn’t resist.  It was absolutely delicious!  I think it would have been delicious even without the smoked paprika, but I enjoyed the nice smoky flavor it added to the mix. 

The coconut milk makes it so smooth and silky

The lemon zest and juice really add to the flavor. I probably will add more lemon at the table.

Easy peasy and delish!

Thank you Ali Slagle!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!  Cauliflower held up great in the slow cooker.  I added fresh corn and a teaspoon of cayenne pepper.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I have a pressure cooker but not a slow cooker. Any recos on adapting the recipe for an InstantPot or stovetop method? I have a cauliflower to use up and this looks delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
@Jess

Most InstantPots have a slow cook function, so you should be able to use that without any modification.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Jess You can use your oven at a very low setting. I have put a Dutch oven in at 225, and it behaved just like a slow cooker

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Shelby yes it’s the same but the slow cook setting should be on medium. At 6 hours the chickpeas are al dente and the cauliflower just right. I might add a little time next time. Also considering blending half of the soup to make it thicker.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
This was one of the blandest dishes I’ve ever made.  
So, I started experimenting.  I had added a chopped onion before cooking the garbanzos and waited to add the cauliflower until the end to avoid overcooking it.  Afterward, I added about 2 Tbl Better Than Bouillon Chicken Stock concentrate, about 1/4 c. Worcestershire, a bit of soy sauce, and some Johnnie’s Seasoning Salt.  Ah, much better.  But after a day in the fridge, it’s downright delicious.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Rosie feeling the same way about the recipe and all your adds sound just right. Will taste the original in the morning to see if my mind changes.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Rosie agree 100% this recipe as written is ok but pretty bland. We doused it with chil crisp and added some fresh Thai basil and it brought a *lot* of life to the dish. As written this is a 1.5-2/5 stars max but yes with some additions it can become amazing. Will try your ideas next time (I was thinking about the need for sauteed onions almost immediately as well)!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, except I added a few teaspoons of vegetable Better Than Bouillon because I was worried about it being bland.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written, mainly to see if it would be a suitable vegan dish for my son and family to add to their recipes for those busy days- it was absolutely delicious and the cauliflower had nice chunks to it. Paired with brown rice and a cucumber dill yogurt salad, it was perfect! I would make again and maybe vary the spicing just for the heck of it.  But overall a great recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This is really a lovely, subtle dish,  we served it over white rice. I added a smidge of Aleppo pepper and Garam masala, but not enough to alter the flavor.  The lemony flavor adds so much.  Had only one can of coconut milk so used chicken broth. Less rich and less fattening. We had it as a side dish with grilled pork tenderloin and cooked zucchini but it is a meal unto itself. People who are complaining about lack of flavor are looking for more strongly spiced Indian food, which this is not.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: May be unpopular opinion but it hit for me. I topped it with my choice of hot sauce and I had rotisserie chicken from Whole Foods for added protein

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I too am curious about cauliflower. Also, I suppose this defeats the recipe’s purpose, but how can I make this without a slow cooker?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with coconut milk lite? I have two cans and was wondering if it would be almost as good...lol

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Just ok…I agree with a few other reviewers that this is a good base, but needs some other additions.  To the leftover soup I added chopped spinach and some shredded chicken which made it better….

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Mo Kevany: Many different types or people (and eaters) read these recipes. It would be the easiest if everyone recognized that some comments may land for you and it may land for someone else. If it doesn't land for you...move on. I just read a comment on a recipe that said the chicken treatment works great for eggplant. I thought GENIUS! The very next comment was someone who chastised the writer for making a vegetarian comment on a chicken recipe. Leave space for everyone to enjoy the comments.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I love the sound of this but I really don't like cauliflower. Any suggestions for a replacement veg that won't get soggy?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Maybe chopped - not sliced - cabbage?  If you use a red one, the color would be a pleasant enhancement.  Or cook the chickpeas alone and when done stir in a goodly amount (at least 8 oz) of baby arugula.  Neither option would change the flavors considerably, although the textures would be different.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Yolanda, maybe eggplant? Root vegetables also hold up well, especially parsnips and carrots, maybe kholrabi

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Yolanda I would try sweet potato. Always pair as well with chickpeas in a curry.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made this with the Instant Pot… added all ingredients as directed, set the lid on, used the slow cook function. Time = 7 hours, heat = medium. Was a little watery so I used the sauté function for about 5 minutes then turned the pot off and made brown basmati rice to accompany. Garnished with fresh cilantro. Easy and delicious. My advice if using the instant pot is reduce the water somewhat, it apparently does not need to cover the cauliflower completely.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I added a chopped onion and a diced jalapeno at the beginning. Used about 4C water, not enough to cover the cauliflower. I accidentally cooked for almost 10 hours. Chick peas were good but cauli was overdone. But once I garnished with chopped tomatoes, whole cashews, and some plain yogurt and served over a mix of basmati rice and quinoa, it was pretty tasty. I used the juice from both lemons.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Made this as directed! I'm glad I was home - it was done (chickpeas and cauliflower tender) MUCH faster than written, after maybe 3 hours on low. (These were old, dried beans, not soaked.)

I really like the flavor, but it is pretty subtle, and the cauliflower was bland when it first finished. I added more salt to taste, stirred, and let it sit off heat for a couple hours, and the cauliflower tastes better now.

If you don't like the thin broth, add some split yellow peas with the dried beans!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Elijah B. Or red lentils for thickening and added protein.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I used canned chick peas and cooked on the stove instead of the slow cooker.  Added cilantro before serving.  Hearty but also light and clean.  A new staple for my family.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: The trouble is that the smell of slow-cooking cauliflower is very off putting.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I made a half batch since some of the comments were discouraging, but I like cauliflower and chick peas. It was really good. I soaked the beans overnight. I also used less than 3/4 (coconut milk) can of water because of the comments about bland taste and “soupiness.” I figured the cauliflower that wasn’t submerged would steam. I made sure to salt well, and used juice of half a lemon - didn’t need extra. It was flavorful, easy to make, and I’ll make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I slow cooked this recipe for over 8 hours.  The chick peas were still hard and unsatisfactory.  I recommend soaking them overnight or pressure cooking them first.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
I liked this but here's what I would do different: add cauliflower about 1/2 way through the cooking process rather than at the beginning. Sub chicken broth for one of the cans of coconut milk (less thick and fewer calories). Add some more spices - garam masala? Curry? Red curry paste? Something else. More salt! Also, a can of crushed tomatoes would be nice too. 
I had no problems with the chickpeas cooking through. 
This felt like the basis for a great recipe but as is it's sort of bland.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: The lemon zest overpowered my entire dish. If I read well, 2 lemons, big stripes. Everything tastes like lemon zest, no coconut, no chickpea, no cauliflower. Anyone had this happen?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I had canned garbanzo beans, so I just boiled everything together for about 25 minutes, added a handful (or two) of whole black peppercorns for depth; threw in some garlic, onion, and half a fresh habanero pepper as well. Topped with cilantro to serve. Amazing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Very disappointed .The cauliflower cooked nicely.  I served it with basmati  rice and corn chips.  The soup was thin and bland. Cilantro topping would have helped.   Adding chicken would have helped. I wonder how my friend liked the quart I gave her..(before I had served it to my family).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Did anyone else have issues with this drying out? I needed to add a substantial amount of additional liquid -- an entire third can of coconut milk, and a little water on top of that. Maybe my slow cooker's low setting runs a little hotter... 
Otherwise, we loved it. I did add some extra spices for more depth, but will absolutely add this to the repertoire.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Prepared this recipe as written. It is really underwhelming. It takes so long to cook the chickpeas that it causes the coconut milk to split. As others have mentioned, slow cooking cauliflower will leave your poor dog getting blamed for the atrocious odor until you recall oh yes, I’m SLOW COOKING CAULIFLOWER. Furthermore, as much as I love turmeric, it is not a flavor that is pleasant on its own — it really begs for other spices with which to harmonize. I’m left wondering how in the world this recipe made the cut to be featured in the NYT.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: sorry, this recipe was downright disgusting. the chickpeas were crunchy, the cauliflower was mushy, and the flavor profile was unpleasant. at the very least, you should soak the chickpeas

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except on stovetop & emulsified because I was hiding the cauliflower. Delicious!! What a nice surprise!!! I really enjoyed the lemon. The turmeric wasn’t strong. Hearty yet light, is that possible?! I’ll definitely make this again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this as written and was surprised by the deliciousness of it. Such a simple and satisfying dish that felt almost magical for a dinner party (served with a 5 ingredients baked salmon from NYT cooking, and basmati rice). A winner with all my guests.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Unfortunately, this is one of those recipes that I think vegans who have eaten vegan for a looooong time and forget what other food tastes like thinks is good. And/or if you’re a purist that likes unflavored, ingredient only food? Couldn’t force myself to eat the leftovers and it’s been years since a recipe failed that hard for us.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This dish is really magical in the way it delivers flavor and texture from a few ingredients AND with little cooking. Simplicity at its finest.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Could I use canned chickpeas instead and than cock everything on my stove the old way?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was the most underwhelming dish I’ve made from NYTCooking. Not sure how it left the test kitchen. Made exactly as written and lacked any flavor, texture, umami. Tried to salvage based on other comments with cayenne, a corn starch slurry, and nada. I’ll keep for nutrition reasons, but def not making again. Shame cause I did want this to work. Move along to another dish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
It was delicious, but I used the Instant Pot on slow cook function (high), and my chickpeas weren't done even though I brought them to a boil first to jump start cooking. Ended up pressure cooking for a few minutes to finish it off, which worked but the cauliflower broke down. I would make it again, maybe soaking and/or parcooking the chickpeas first on high pressure. 
It's not bland. Just season it well—and go ahead and use the full fat coconut milk.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Ugh. I love cauliflower, chickpeas, and Ali Slagle recipes, and this is the first time they have disappointed me. Came home to the house reeking of dirty diapers only to discover it's the smell of dinner. The coconut milk curdled and cauliflower turned to mush. Chickpea texture is fine but the recipe is bland and needed a LOT more salt and could use more flavor. Somehow the lemon zest got a gross scent too. I'm throwing this out and airing out the house while I make something else ;_;

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made this with carrots instead of cauliflower since that’s what I had on hand. I also added fresh grated ginger and some dried spices. Turned out great!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made this recently and LOVED it. I found the basic recipe to be a little bit lacking in flavor, so toward the end I added fresh ginger, garlic, and some tamari. For serving, ladled it over rice and added some marinated & broiled tofu, fresh cilantro, toasted cashews, and chili crisp. So, so good and very easy for a nutritious weeknight meal. The next day tossed in some leftover chicken breast. It's a great base to work with whatever else you have on hand.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: We made this today and, as many have stated, it has a lovely taste, albeit a bit bland. I added 1 1/2 tsp of Ras el Hanout and voila! Instant level up! I do think it needs some sort of green - fresh spinach, arugula, Lacinato kale? Unfortunately, I don’t have any of these on hand to try, but that’s my personal note for next time. Thanks to the chef who created this! The lemon is exceptionally lovely.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: If you forget the lemon strips, microplane some lemon zest in there at the end and call it good.  So delicious - the lemon juice is a great addition.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: nice, but a little bland and a little thin. i put about a quarter cup of rice in a bowl and then a couple of ladles of the soup, and that helped body-wise. i also added a little more lemon and that enhanced the flavor.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to like this, it looked so easy and fresh with the lemon. I followed the instructions to a t and it looked like the one in the photo. It didn't smell amazing but I trusted the process. When it was done I took a few tastes of the soup then ordered pizza. It was bland and not too appetizing to look at it. This one was a miss.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: To save time, would it work with can chickpeas? What would be changes to the amount of ingredients (coconut milk, Cauliflower etc)? Thanks

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
The recipe looks wonderful as-is (though I also wonder about slow-cooking cauliflower for 6 - 8 hours).

Wondering about adapting to an IP, I would think that letting the chickpeas cook to about 3/4 done-ness and then adding in the cauli should do it.  I can always simmer longer afterwards, to allow the coconut milk to keep developing.  Maybe also add ground coriander, because.  

L/O's could easily be go into a more complex curry - add sliced onion and chunks of sweet potato.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made this evening. Started with sautéed onion and garlic. Added the Better than Boullion as suggested by others. Still lacking so added Aleppo pepper and a squeeze of lime. Pleased with the result.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I want to cut this in half. How will that affect the cooking time?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: We made this as written. At 7.5 hours the chickpeas were still quite firm. We agree with the description that it is sweet and floral and we just didn’t care for the floral part of the flavor profile.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty and so easy to make! This makes a lot of food - has anyone tried freezing leftovers and have any tips?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I did it with ingredients as above (except subbed 1 c. chicken broth for water to fully cover) in Instant Pot. Cooked 40 minutes with natural release and the chickpeas came out perfectly. Cauliflower only a little mushy. Agree with some other commenters that it's quite bland and hope as someone said that the flavors will meld for tastier leftovers. As to someone else's comment about the consistency, mine came out perfect somewhere between a soup and a stew.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This sure was underwhelming. Even after an extra 10 hours of slow cooking, the chickpeas were still crunchy and the flavor was bland, excepting bites that had large chunks of lemon peel in them. I now have a lot of this left and don't look forward to eating it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Anyone use canned chickpeas?  Its all I have currently, wondering how it comes out.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I found it watery and bland with too soft cauliflower. I ended up doing a quick sauté of garlic and onions added a little red curry paste and white miso then puréed the whole thing and topped it with yogurt and a little green fig leaf oil!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
As much as I wanted to like this recipe, I think it's flawed.   Not enough liquid to cover the chickpeas - so not all cooked at the same rate.  Found them still underdone. Cauliflower fell apart (although I cut in large florets, which I thought would preserve their integrity.  Also, kinda bland - even though I added a healthy amount of fresh ginger (3+ T) and a diced jalapeno.  
Not inedible, just not great enough to make again
Sad.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
My kid doesn't like/ won't eat cauliflower( if he can see it).
If I cut the florets small, won't they disintegrate...meaning the nutrition stays but the evidence is gone??

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Esther the cauliflower dissolved completely in mine. I ended up with a thick mush. I added garlic, a jalapeño and some shallots to mine, along with a little basil. The taste is good but the texture is not. Oh well. Filling and nutritious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
This was indeed an easy, straightforward recipe - the most difficult preparation step was abiding the smell of cooking cauliflower for several hours. The result was interesting - intriguingly unlike anything else, hearty and filling, delightful with small mint leaves, but just not quite enjoyable enough (for me). 

I’ll struggle through the leftovers with lots more salt, mint leaves, and hot sauce. I’m not sorry to have tried it, but probably won’t make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: As it's written, the flavor relies largely on the quality (and age) of the turmeric. Using top-shelf turmeric makes all the difference! Plus I subbed chicken broth for some of the water which didn't hurt.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Great ratio of prep time to yumminess. It is so easy to make that I had my 5 year old help out. You could add stuff to it, sure, but the basic recipe is so good that I'm not inclined to mess with it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Very good!  I used chicken broth instead of water, but vegetable broth would work also if you wanted to keep it vegan.  I also skipped the salt and add some better than bullion at the end to add both salt and umami.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Made a variation on the stovetop:
* cook soaked chickpeas in 6 cups water, 1tsp salt, 5 cloves garlic, zest from a lemon.  ~1hr
* when the chickpeas mostly done, add cauliflower, turmeric.  cook until the cauliflower is falling apart. ~45 min
* add 1 can coconut milk & lemon zest
* salt & pepper to taste.

The chickpeas took longer than usual-- wonder if it's from cooking with the lemon zest.

There's a great dal recipe by George Alagiah of the BBC which has a similar flavor profile.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was disappointing. After 6 hours in the slow cooker both chickpeas and cauliflower were like rocks. I put a bunch in the microwave to make them edible, and added a lot of spices to help the blandness. Then let it all sit over night, simmered an hour on the stove, and applied the immersion blender. At least it’s edible now!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made it exactly like the recipe called for. It never turned into a stew. Even after 8 hours, the chickpeas were still pretty firm, edible but firm. The broth was watery, and tasted fine by itself, but got lost with the cauliflower when eaten together. This will be a pass for me.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made in the instapot on slow cook setting for 8 hours and it was good.  I wanted some other colored veggies on the side so cooked up some broccoli and carrots.  The carrots were especially good added to the soup and I might try putting them in at the beginning next time.  I think I would like it spicier so I will experiment with that (there are leftovers so I will do that before reheating).

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was so easy, and a hit with my husband. I did make three minor changes: I put the cauliflower in two hours before the end; I went with one can full fatted coconut milk and one can lite coconut milk instead of two full fatted cans; and I used Penzey's Hot Curry powder instead of Turmeric because I like a little kick.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Did not care for this at all. Didnt get much flavor and not a fan of texture. Maybe I added too much water because the coconut milk was diluted and this was less of a stew and more of a bland, watery soup. One commenter shared a curry version using an IP. Might be worth testing that approach instead.  Also - note that this makes a LOT of food.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Any substitute for turmeric?  It took forever for the stains to come off of my teeth.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I'm a super taster, and this was a bit too subtle for me. Textures are nice, and the color is lovely.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: The chickpeas didn't cook all the way through in eight hours.  I ended up having to remove the cauliflower and pressure cooking the beans.  If I make it again I would pressure cook the beans first to avoid this issue!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Ok, made this overnight for a work potluck. I wanted to make sure there was a vegan and gluten free option. I had resolved to follow the recipe exactly through to final tasting. My findings: Stew was watery looking and the cauliflower did not break down so it was overly chunky. I solved that by blitzing with an emersion blender in bursts until it was smoother, but still had small chunks. It tasted kind of meh, so I added the juice of both lemons. Very good, but still needed something so I added 1/2 tsp salt and a good shake of pepper. This was a mistake. The salt blunted the lemon almost completely. Sad. I added in more lemon juice but it still was less lemony than before the salt. I also added a healthy squeeze of sriracha to kick it up. I think it’s good. Hope my colleagues agree! Not sure if I will make again, but it was a very good overnight cook option.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Another comment: I wish I had cut the lemon peel into smaller pieces. I kept them in, but they did resemble used bandaids. Not ideal.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This wasn’t a hit in my house. I didn’t like the texture of the overcooked cauliflower.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I'm joining those who feel that the recipe, as is, is bland. When it was finished, I kept tasting and adding: more turmeric, more lemon, then paprika, garam masala, and finally fenugreek. It's pretty good now. We'll enjoy it, but I won't make it again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Easy to prepare and was quite a warming and aromatic meal with good texture as well as taste. I didn't depart from the recipe, other than adding a touch of cornstarch to add some body to the sauce. It turned out well, and paired with some rice it was enjoyable to eat. I cut the lemon zest into thin strips before adding them to the pot.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I made this as directed but did use chicken stock instead of water. I think I put a bit too much in as my result was more soupy than stewy, but it was delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I was so excited to try this even as I was assembling it, as I love cauliflower and chickpeas and curries. I always follow the instructions as given the first time… but I have to say, my husband and I both disliked it. I found the lemon overpowering, maybe I used too much juice but I assume “half the juice” meant the juice of one lemon with the second in reserve; I added some salt, but didn’t have a good idea how much and 3/4 tsp plus more pepper did not help. I put some in the fridge to see if it improves and threw most of it out.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I used my instant pot and set it for 2 minutes. My husband said the 2nd lemon wasn’t necessary, but I love sour and disagreed. Yummy for a weeknight, especially if you’re wondering why you bought that cauliflower in the first place.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: My husband made this and it really has no flavor. I think it would benefit from some aromatics like garlic and onion.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: The lemony flavor is indeed floral and quite lovely, although it makes for something of a one-note dish.  I had cooked chickpeas on hand so I went stovetop.  Cooked the cauliflower in the coconut milk with lemon peel and turmeric.  When done, added chickpeas and simmered for a while.  Since I virtually live on legumes, a welcome addition to my collection.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
I was excited to make this, bought all the ingredients. Then… I read the comments. Although I was looking forward to the ease of making this I did not want my house to smell like farts….

So I made the recipe this way:
-pressure cooked the chickpeas
-air fried the cauliflower 
-then added the chickpeas to the coconut milk & turmeric & lemon zest and brought to a boil and simmered for about 20 minutes then added the cauliflower & lemon juice.

So far so good, and our house does not smell like farts.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: As others have mentioned the original recipe is a bit bland, I countered this by adding a lot of salt, pepper, refreshing lemon juice with each bowl, adding a decent amount of red pepper flakes. it’s an easy recipe and creates a pretty high volume amount. i also added 2 chicken breasts and some barley but that veers off the og recipe a bit more.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was one of the most disappointing NYT recipes I’ve made. It was too rich, thick, and flavorless to be called a stew; it was more like a bland coconut curry(ish). Epic fail. We threw out the leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @Horn Agree. And I’m usually a big big Ali Slagle fan. I managed to salvage it by adding spices and garlic to my taste and using an immersion blender to turn it into a cauliflower chickpea soup.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoyed the flavors here, but wasn’t thrilled with the texture. Liked it a lot better after I puréed it with the immersion blender.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Nice idea - I might try that with my leftovers.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Fried bacon ends and onions and added canned chickpeas and canned white beans with rest of ingredients to that. Cooked an hour on the stove. The shaved lemon was delicious when cooked. Used vegetable broth instead of water. Added half and half left behind by a visitor after I turned it off. Used submersion blender. Sprinkled with sweet curry before serving. It was a keeper

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I was nervous to make this because there were a lot of reviews saying that it was tasteless. But my family really enjoyed it. The lemon peel does add another layer of flavor. And the coconut milk really adds a creaminess. I did add sauteed shallots and garlic as well as chicken broth in place of water. But I find this an easy and delicious recipe.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: What an easy way to make a tasty, special dinner out of an inexpensive bag of chickpeas! Despite my doubts, the chickpeas cooked up nice and tender, and the lemon zest as promised was surprisingly delicious. I served it over rice, offering the garnishes of lemon wedges, cilantro, and almonds, which added interest and complexity. I can see making this dish for company, although I'd add another dish - some kind of green salad I think. Needed  the extra lemon juice and more salt before serving.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: So disappointing. I made this in slow cooker for eight hours and followed the recipe exactly. When it was done I followed the instructions to squeeze in  juice of half a lemon or more. I squeezed the lemon in and the soup curdled.  I have not seen a review reporting this. I threw it out. A ton of soup. Very disappointing.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I don’t think I’ve ever given 3 stars before. Made according to recipe. It cooked properly but had a disappointingly boring taste that was too lemony, and I like lemon. I used 2 small ones for the peels and the juice of one of them at the end. I assumed “half the lemon juice” meant the juice of one lemon but why not just say the juice of one lemon? Or better yet, an approximate measurement with “adjust for taste?”Seems to be a source of some confusion. I may at least use this basic idea without lemon and add red curry paste or a lot of spices to make a good curry.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: So unbelievably bad. I can’t believe this recipe has more than one star. After 6 hours in the slow cooker, the overcooked cauliflower and lemon peels combine to create the most revolting stench. My house was almost uninhabitable until I aired everything out. The smell made my wife and son gag. I chocked down a few bowls and found the taste less offensive than the smell, but it came out incredibly bland. I followed the recipe exactly.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: 💙 Each Thursday brings me such joy to deliver home cooked, love filled food the the Community Resource Center of Mamaroneck.  Sharing this week lemon chickpea + cauliflower stew which has warm turmeric and coconut meal for healing the heart, soul and body.  The CRC shared that each week their members feel the love and that is what Love is.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Be forewarned: six hours of cooking cauliflower requires opened windows. My daughter came home and provided a graphic description of her olfactory experience.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
I liked this quite a bit when I added shredded chicken thighs, but still feel like it’s missing some dimension of flavor.
Does get better after it sits in the fridge a day or so?

Anybody have any simple ideas for enhancement?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Following the recipe exactly, I found this bland, not bad, just blah.  I sauteed 8 cloves of garlic, a giant leek, and an onion, with some red pepper flakes and paprika.  To the pan, I added maybe half the cooked amount of chickpea stew.  I will try other additions to the second half of stew.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Needed a little something, I will add a couple of onions next time. Also my turmeric is old so that probably did not help.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
This was not good.

I’m wondering if it was because I have a crock pot that is rectangular rather than round - which spread the liquid out too much?

The dish was dry, the chick peas weren’t fully cooked. We ended up throwing it out. I’m glad it’s working better for other folks! It sounded delicious!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: My neighbor liked the quart I gave her.  I didn't like the texture or thin broth, After a. night in the refrigerator, it had thickened up; I put it in a blender with some chicken broth and it was. good. Not sure that I will make it again, but trying to include more healthy beans in my diet and garbanzos packed.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: what if I use canned chickpeas? Does that reduce the cook time or coconut milk measurement?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: When I made this, I was concerned about the cauliflower not softening up much after 3 hours, so I increased the temperature to High. While the broth was a bit watery as described by others, the flavor is quite pleasant. (Though yes, the smell is something else!)  I think I will puree this to make a nice lemony soup for these dog days of summer.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Are those mint leaves floating on top?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I used canned chickpeas and cooked on the stove top was still very good.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Can you substitute canned chickpeas for the dried ones?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but I agree with the others that the chickpeas need more time than the cauliflower. If I make this again, I will cook chickpeas for 2-3 hours on high and then turn to low and add cauliflower for last 4 hours. I also agree that this dish is a bit bland and sauteed onions and garlic in the beginning helps. It also helps mask the odor of the cauliflower simmering which is a bit unpleasant.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made half portion of this recipe in a 6 quart Instant Pot set at slow cooker setting for 8 hours.  The chickpeas were still crunchy at the end of cooking.  The is the only recipe from the NYtimes that I have composted after cooking.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Some of the problem was user error (my coconut milk was not the best and I think I left it in the slow cooker too long- 6 hours would have been plenty) but this was not very interesting. We added a lot of extra salt and hot sauce to round out the flavors.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I read all the comments on this recipe before cooking, and made a few changes as a result. 1)Soaked the chickpeas, and only used 1lb of the soaked beans. 2)A large head of cauliflower instead of 2lbs. Amounted to 1.5 lbs on my scale. 3) Only 1 lemon available, and it was enough. 4)Used better than bouillion in warm water to cover. 5)Added: 1tbsp minced ginger, 1tsp curry powder, dash of cayenne, dash of red pepper flakes, 1.5 tsp garlic powder and 1 tbsp onion powder. Took 8 hours in my 8 quart slow cooker on low. Then I purèed about 2/3 of it--incl lemon rinds. It is very good as I prepared, and might add shredded chicken or rice for a nutrition boost on later servings. Take the recipe as a great base, home chefs, and make it to your taste.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Rather bland taste, even with addition of lemon. I wish the recipe would specify how many cups of water to add. I added enough to cover cauliflower and ended up with a soup instead of stew.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I added the juice of probably 1 and a half lemons and a lot of extra salt at the end. I used green beans and zucchini instead of cauliflower. Great and EASY!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made it as per recipe.  While experts may say that the flavors of coconut and lemon go together nicely, I discovered that they do not so that for me.  Won't make this again.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I found it bland.  Others might like it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I individually like all the ingredients in this recipe but did not like the result. There is a bitterness on the back of the tongue. I'm eating leftovers on the second day and the lemon juice (from one lemon) is really overpowering. Other than the lemon, it has little taste.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: i'm concerned that strings of lemon peel will have an offputting texture. Did anyone have that issue, or do they melt into the soup?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: @rcmapp I was concerned about that, too, so preemptively removed the most visible lemon peel pieces prior to serving. However, happily, I couldn’t remove all of them and discovered that when I hd a bite that contained a bit of peel it was not off putting at all. The texture was barely noticeable and the soft hint of extra lemon flavor was nice. Good luck!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was good. It was definitely on the more "subtle" side flavour wise, but the limited, cheap ingredients and ease of method made up for it. I used soaked dried chickpeas because I have an instant pot which notoriously runs cool. My cauliflower was also small with a lot of leaves, so I just chopped the leaves up and threw them in with the rest of the ingredients. It's good with hot sauce, but it would also be a nice meal for a sick day.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was an easy and delicious dish, but we felt it needed a little more depth to the seasoning. We added 1-1/2 tsp of Penzey’s Now Curry — wow! A new favorite recipe!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I only had one can of coconut milk. I used 4 cups of veggie broth instead of all water. At the end I added baby spinach and fresh herbs for greens, and a dollop of Greek yogurt to compensate for my missing can of coconut milk. Served it with red pepper flakes and homemade naan! Will definitely make again!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Ignore the haters and try this. It could not be easier, and the payoff is quite nice and very riffable. 

Made as written, but added garam masala, cumin, and fresh garlic (we love spicy). Used 1 can full fat coconut milk + 1 can lite. Served with cilantro and a dollop of Greek yogurt. Really tasty. 

Next time I'll add a bit more water. The sauce really thickens up in the fridge and you'll want more of it to spoon over your rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I dont have a slow cooker or an instant pot. How can this be adapted with a dutch oven? Thanks!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Ali, this is a lovely recipe. Simple, economical and delicious. Wouldn't change a thing .

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: The finished product had a good aroma, but the chick peas stayed hard. Not edible but soft enough for the disposal to handle it. I’d try it again but would soak over night.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Slow cooking cauliflower for hours produced quite the unpleasant odor throughout the house, and after 8 hours, the chickpeas were still not quite soft. Had to give in and pressure cook for 10 minutes, which produced what I think was very adequate results. If I make this again I will just pressure cook.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Questions:
I do not like the taste of turmeric, plus it gives me indigestion in significant amts. May I omit it from the recipe or is there a substitute?

Many have commented that the chickpeas were still too firm at end of cooking. Could canned chickpeas be successfully used? If yes, should slowcooking time be reduced?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried replacing the garbanzo beans with lentils?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly tasty! Others have said comforting, yet light at the same time and this couldn't be more accurate. Like others, I made some adjustments. Used reduced-fat coconut milk, added cumin, garam masala, and cayenne. Totally missed the part about adding water, but added about two cups halfway through cooking. I kept the cauliflower pieces quite large and they didn't get mushy and the chickpeas were cooked through. In my opinion, both the lemon juice and rind bumped this out of ordinary.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Very good, had leftovers for breakfast with medium boiled eggs on top

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I loved the lemon peels- so delicious to eat the finished peels. I would probably add some spinach right at the end next time to lighten it up a bit, but I also made the mistake of using a few too many chickpeas and not adjusting the liquid.....

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: substitute salt with fish sauce as a finishing touch instead to give a bit more depth.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was surprisingly delicious! I was dubious about slow-cooking cauliflower, but decided to chance it since I had a head on its last legs. I’m glad I did.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I made this because I’m always curious about easy new recipes and so far it’s OK thin I’m thinking of maybe taking my immersion blender to it to get it a little bit more body and some bouillon as stated in some of the other comments.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I made this using the recipe in my InstantPot. After 4 hours on low (=185F)and the chickpeas seemed far from being cooked, I increased to medium (=194F). Now, after 4 more hours, the chickpeas are still not cooked (same texture as biting into an undercooked potato). I tried pressure cooking for 15 min. Since they're still not done, I'm pressure cooking going to pressure cook them in high for 30 minutes and hope that does it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: After slow cooking on low for 4 hours, medium for 4 hours, and pressure cooking for 40+ minutes, it turned out great. Don't skip the final step of lemon juice - it gives it just the right acidic balance.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Also, I used frozen cauliflower rice to make it more kid friendly. That worked well.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Added a raw boneless chicken breast at the beginning. Came out great. Next I’ll going to add carrots.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I was worried about blandness so I used broth made with better than bouillon instead of water, and I also added 1 Tbsp mustard power and 1 Tbsp cumin. It was delicious and still very easy/hands-off — ready in about 6 hours.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a cauliflower fan. Any suggestions for what to use instead?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
the lemon is important to this
i think turmeric alone is sad so added cumin
i tried starting everything at the same time but with soaked chickpeas and the cauliflower still disintegrated by the time chickpeas were done (ok, but not what i was hoping for), so i cant imagine starting with dry.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: It ended up being more of a soup than a stew. Fixed by adding a a tablespoon of corn starch towards the end which thickened it up nicely. The cumin colored the dish nicely but a small pinch of saffron adds a beautiful color profile to the dish.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
