commentBody: I made it is but got worried as I  read comments after it was in the pot. It turned out perfectly. After slow cooking for a little over 5 hours I did 30 minute of pressure cooking and it went from a thinner soup to thicker. It’s delicious. Can I freeze it?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: The coconut milk broke/curdled for me—was that the intended result? Looks like it’s a bit broken in  the recipe photo, but not as badly as mine

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night as wanted a simple meal to host family prior to Thanksgiving. After reading the comments I decided to add 1/8 tsp baking soda to avoid curdling and it worked great. My other edits were: decreased the turmeric to 2 tsp., added 1 tsp. ground curry, 1 clove of minced garlic, added 3 tbs. of maple syrup (please don’t judge), measured the lemon juice and used 2 tbs., swapped out the full fat coconut milk for low fat. I made it on the stove-top in my Staub pot. I did a quick soak of the beans by bringing to a boil for 2 minutes and then covered off stove for 1 hour. I drained the beans, then proceeded with the recipe. I used a quart of water. I brought everything to a simmer, then let it cook covered for 2 hours. It was fabulous!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Really good. Responding to some folks’ concerns: The chickpeas were throughly cooked with a nice firmness. The cauliflower held up well — also had a nice bite to it. The dish is quite mild but tasty. I am adding salt and pepper now. I think I would add a couple of jalapeños or other green peppers next time for some nice heat.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I am making this right now, and following the recipe exactly as stated, including generous pinches of salt and freshly ground black pepper. After two hours on low I looked into the cooker and noticed that the cauliflower had risen to the top and was no longer submerged. I took a spoon and got a little taste (it was delicious!) and also one chickpea that was of course nowhere near done. Still, the flavor was lovely. Should I add more water?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Someone inquired about cauliflower over cooking and yes, the cauliflower was mush well before the chickpeas were fully cooked. That may have been purposeful-it helps thicken broth, but I think I would next time add cauliflower late in the game so you still get the nutty crunchiness of cauliflower.  The lemon flavor paired with coconut though is AMAZING   I  only had 1 can of coconut milk so did use flax milk to make up diff

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Unbelievably tasty for the near zero effort you need to put into making this. I added an extra teaspoon of turmeric and quite a bit of black pepper at the end and it turned out amazing

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Loved it! Simple and easy meal. I liked chopped scallions to finish and the cooked lemon peel tastes delicious. Don’t bother to discard it.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This is quite delicious but does need some time for the flavors to get acquainted. Did blend some for some texture and finished with steamed broccolini. Recipes are a guide!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: It was okay, I found it a little bland overall. Adding MSG helped. Also it will make your entire house smell like cauliflower.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: It tasted ok but not worth the smell of my house when I returned home after 6 hours of cooking cauliflower :(

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I love so many NYT Cooking recipes - but not this one.  It's very (very) bland.  Tomorrow I'll do some significant doctoring to give it more flavor.  I followed this recipe to a "t" and the result was very disappointing. Agree with mc below, my house smelled horrid after about 4 hours in the slow cooker. Had to air it out!

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Can you use canned chick peas?

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: i added two tablespoons of Thai Curry Powder and the recipe turned out quite well

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Ok this recipe didn’t work for me as written, perhaps because I cut the florets too small and didn’t check on it until 6 hours had passed. The main problems were that the cauliflower was way too mushy and the flavor was too lemony-sweet, maybe because I used meyer lemons. 

To salvage this recipe as more of a minestrone, I:
1. Added mushy cauliflower to 7 c. garlic broth. Immersion blend until smooth.
2. Sautéed diced onions, carrots, celery, maybe 2 c. total. Add garam masala (1 tbsp.) roasted garlic from Penzey’s, (1 tbsp.) seasoned salt (1 tsp.) to taste. I did this to offset the lemony excess. 
3. Add 1/8 c water, 2 tbsp. tomato paste, 2 tbsp. minced ginger. Stir until incorporated. 
4. Add 2 c. diced red potatoes and sautee til tender. Adding this starch was another attempt to downplay the lemon. 
5 Add  and Sauté 1 c. Diced zucchini til tender. 
6. Add 2 c. Diced arugula from my garden as well as 2 tbsp. Chives. Sauté till greens have c
hanged color. Add sautee to soup.
7. Add 1 c. Frozen corn and 3/4 c. Frozen peas

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: Like several, I added fresh minced ginger and garlic to bump the flavors. I like a little heat so I topped with szechuan chile sauce and then some finishing salt. We had Rancho Gordo garbanzos, so they were the perfect bean. The cauliflower slow cooked is a wonderful earthy thickener in this stew. So nice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This did not turn out well. there was not enough liquid so after 4 hours the chickpeas were still not done. I thought it seemed light on the liquid side, but I like to follow the recipe the first time. I added water and cooked several more hours until the chickpeas were an ok texture. ultimately though we didn't like the flavor or texture and we eat a lot of chickpeas.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
I can’t believe I found a NYT recipe that takes only 30 minutes and tastes this good! I only made minor modifications:
-1 can coconut milk and 1 can veggie broth (instead of 2 cans coconut milk)
- Added 1 chopped yellow onion
- Added 2 dried chipotle peppers (discarded the skins after cooking)
- increased the turmeric to 1.5 Tablespoons 
- topped with chopped cilantro (good flavor and color)

Otherwise made as directed.
We both loved this. There is so much stew leftover that I plan to try different spice modifications each time I reheat it. Thanks, NYT.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I had some peas in the freezer, so I added about a cup of those at the end! I really just needed to use them but they went well with the stew.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody:
Tasty base, but really bland - our family required a whole lot more spices .... beyond turmeric, we added curries and chilies .... Added kale and served over rice with roasted poblano, fresh mint, chili onion, cilantro, lime, lemon and a side of partha.
For me - not repeatable - but a decent one off.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I made this as directed and it turned out well. Chickpeas were tender without pre-soaking. It’s not a strong flavor but it’s nice. We added some chili crisp when serving to give a little kick. The downside is the smell in the house when cooking cauliflower and chickpeas and for that reason I’m not sure if we will make it again (kind of shame because it is simple and hearty)

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was very good and surprisingly easy! The cauliflower was very soft, but I didn't think it was a problem. I made one minor change -- I added chicken bone broth I had leftover from another recipe instead of some of the water.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: I approached this recipe with some trepidation given the polarizing comments, and I halved the recipe in case I didn't like it. I think this recipe serves its purpose of being simple and quick to put together, but I don't think I would make it again except when in need. The chickpeas were soft enough but not creamy and the cauliflower almost completely disintegrated. (I used frozen cauliflower.) I'd maybe try again with cooked chickpeas in a pressure cooker and perhaps add curry powder.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: An excellent slow cooker recipe! For the spices, I opted for 1 Tbsp red curry powder plus 1/2 tsp turmeric. It’s delicious served with cilantro, yogurt and brown rice.

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was super easy and delicious- I added some crushed garlic cloves and handfuls of spinach near the end which I’d recommend

author: Ali Slagle
recipeTitle: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
commentBody: This was good, and I probably would make it again, but I would probably try to change some things. I thought it would be creamier than it was, so I would probably add some yogurt of heavy cream next time I make it, and I might use a bit less of the lemon peel. I know the recipe said that it wouldn't be super lemony, but more flowery, but it didn't seem to be that way for me. Don't get me wrong, I LOVE lemon, but it kind of overpowered everything else in this recipe for me.
